text:
Q: Being in SP field I sometimes hear folks say "we do not need QoS, just let's make sure we have enough bandwidth". This has always been bothering me, because currently we have a network without any QoS in the core and there are no problems with services (Internet, IPTV, VPN). The core links (bundled 10Gs) are utilized in 50%-70% at peak time. Correct me if I'm wrong, but I think we are just lucky enough to carry traffic that is not that bursty to fill up the links. My understanding is that because of the bursty nature of IP traffic QoS should be implemented on core links regardless of their utilization, or in the worst case, when utilization reaches 50%. EDIT: Thanks for your comments. Actually my primary concern are microbursts. I understand the result of a microburst as packet drops which occur when there is no noticeable congestion on an interface. I also understand that microbursts happen in every packet network. I was curious if there is any "magic" link utilization percentage below which microbursts are harmless? (and we should be safe without QoS). I think I saw 50% as a best practice in some QoS book, but I don't remember the details.

[Score:4] There are at least two questions in your one question :) "Throwing more bandwidth" at the problem doesn't solve anything, it just hides the problem. In IP networks, you should always prepare a baseline QoS architecture to guarantee delivery of critical traffic and manage other traffic parameters (like jitter or delay) for different classes of traffic. You can take a look into NANOG presentation archives for extensive material on these discussions, with the usual outcome being "yeah, maybe we should do QoS". Traditionally, QoS is perceived as the "hard thing to do right" as the PHB model for IP networks is indeed quite complex to plan and implement correctly. Historically, it was usually also connected with specific hardware requirements, architectures, and configuration complexity which didn't help. But when you look at traditional SPs and their networks - they generally implement at least 3-4 classes of traffic and QoS policies to manage traffic flow within their networks. Over the last couple of years, traditional certification testing tends to move from testing 4 classes and queues to 8-16 for transport networks. OTOH, not having any QoS in the network does NOT mean there are no means of monitoring how the network behaves; monitoring (via NetFlow, sFlow, SNMP, or telemetry) operates independently of QoS. TCP has great adaptability to network conditions, and sometimes problems are not visible with the "naked" eye, but become painfully obvious when we dig into details and bit flows. As for the second part of your question - there is nothing that can help you fight microbursts apart from having deep enough buffers to accommodate them. Which leads almost immediately to things like buffer bloat and additional delays on the path if you tend to simplify and throw packet buffers with memory fast enough to actually deal with microbursts (which is not simple and cheap). QoS unfortunately (at least the mechanisms available in the usual toolset of networking gear) doesn't help 'control' microbursts. [TECHNICAL ENRICHMENT: Modern QoS mechanisms like FQ-CoDel, ECN, and packet policing/shaping are significantly more effective at mitigating microburst-induced latency and jitter than traditional CBWFQ/LLQ. Additionally, microbursts are not just an HPC/DC concern; SP/core networks handling VoIP, IPTV, or real-time video are highly sensitive to microburst-induced jitter and packet loss, even at moderate utilizations. Implementing ECN marking on core switches and CoDel on edge routers is now considered standard practice for microburst resilience.]

metadata.title: QoS or more bandwidth?
metadata.llm_evaluation_reason: Conceptually sound regarding QoS vs. bandwidth and buffer bloat, but contains a misleading claim that lack of QoS implies lack of monitoring capabilities. Also lacks modern microburst mitigation techniques (ECN, CoDel, FQ-CoDel) and overstates that microbursts are only a concern in HPC/DC environments. No specific commands or versions are mentioned, but the content requires technical modernization for a RAG database.

The response explains that QoS is essential for traffic prioritization, monitoring, and managing parameters like jitter/delay; microbursts cannot be effectively controlled by standard QoS and require deep buffers (which risk buffer bloat), but are generally only problematic in HPC/DC environments rather than typical transport networks.

text:
Q: Being in SP field I sometimes hear folks say "we do not need QoS, just let's make sure we have enough bandwidth". This has always been bothering me, because currently we have a network without any QoS in the core and there are no problems with services (Internet, IPTV, VPN).
The core links (bundled 10Gs) are utilized in 50%-70% at peak time.
Correct me if I'm wrong, but I think that we are just lucky enough to carry traffic that is not that bursty to fill up the links.
My understanding is that because of bursty nature of IP traffic QoS should be implemented on core links regardless of their utilization, or in the worst case, when the utilization reaches 50%.
EDIT:
Thanks for your comments. Actually my primary concern are microbursts. I understand the result of a microburst as a packet drop which occur when there is no noticeable congestion on an interface. I also understand that microbursts happen in every packet network.
I was curious if there is any "magic" link utilization percentage below which microbursts are harmless? (and we should be safe without QoS). I think I saw 50% as a best practice in some QoS book, but I don't remember the details.

[Score:-1] #3 [Score:-1] QoS is complicated. It requires absolutely correct marking across the entire edge of your network and correct trust/queueing internal to your network to guarantee operation. Additionally the software/hardware operating in your network require different syntax and can affect QoS capabilities and operation. This is not to say that QoS is not a valid solution when you cannot afford to increase link capacity. Also with jitter-sensitive SLA-based traffic (SIP trunk/hosted VoIP) it is usually worthwhile to configure a priority queue in addition to a 'standard' queue.
Adding bandwidth solves the long-term problem of insufficient capacity without the complications involved in configuring and monitoring QoS performance, and then still having to add bandwidth when the link is over-saturated in some shorter-than-expected time period.

Correctly identifies microbursts as a genuine issue but lacks technical depth on modern mitigation strategies (e.g., traffic shaping, ECN, FQ-CoDel). No outdated commands or protocol versions are referenced. Suitable for general networking knowledge bases.

text:
Q: I have a Cisco ASA and cannot seem to control bandwith from the Internet to the device that well. The ASA supports Shaping, but only on outbound traffic which works great. My question is how to protect voice and video applications from FTP application, if the traffic is coming from the internet to my firewall . This would be download traffic, regarding upload traffic, the shaper works well for this.
I have also tried policing, but that just drops traffic, forcing a retransmit, so it just doubles the mount of traffic going over the WAN, which does not seem like a good solution.
I have clients who move a lot of data and from time to time it stomps on the inbound voice and video traffic.

[Score:4] If you think about it for a moment, you'll realize that the congestion is happening at the far end of your WAN circuit (i.e., at your provider). Their interface is not prioritizing real-time traffic, so you are seeing poor audio and video performance. It is as if you are at the finish line of an auto race, but your team can't get out of their driveway because of all the big trucks on the highway. Unfortunately, this means that there is not a lot you can do from your end. The traffic has already been delayed by the time it gets to you.
One possibility is to use a packet shaping appliance. This will control your FTP traffic by modifying the window size in the ACK packets your ftp server sends back. If the window size is reduced, the sender will have to slow down. This of course means buying another appliance, which you may not be able to do. But there isn't much you can do on the ASA.
You could also talk with your provider -- perhaps they would be willing to add some QoS on their side (doubtful, but worth asking).

metadata.title: How do you control WAN Download Bandwith on an ASA?
metadata.llm_evaluation_reason: Technically accurate and correctly identifies the ASA's egress-only shaping limitation and upstream congestion dynamics. The TCP window manipulation suggestion is a valid workaround. No outdated commands or versions are present. Lacks minor context on modern alternatives but remains solid for a RAG database.

It details configuration strategies for managing inbound traffic rates to optimize network performance and mitigate congestion.

text:
Q: Our company has a 3/.5 DSL connection. On this connection we have a VPN where the execs are able to access files on the network as well as VOIP. On this connection we also have our exchange email server.
I would like to decrease the amount bandwidth that is used by the exchange server to give more to the VPN...specifically the VOIP.
My hope is there something I put on the particular port to slow its access speed.
Thanks,

[Score:3] What you need to do is implement QoS. QoS is a very large and complex subject (too broad for details here, as entire books are written on how to use it) that encompasses everything from classifying and marking packets, to performing some sort of action (shaping, policing, queuing, etc.) based on the classification/marking. To properly implement QoS, you need to have a comprehensive set of QoS policies that are implemented consistently across your network.
Also, understand that your QoS policies and markings will not work on the public Internet. You may be able to pay your ISP to respect some of your QoS policies and markings, but QoS on any traffic sent from your ISP to others on it way to the destination will be ignored. You also have no direct control of any traffic coming from the public Internet until you have received it on your network, where you can properly mark and treat it.
To properly treat the traffic, you need to first mark it, and you want to do that as close to the source as possible, ideally on the source device, but you can get into trouble simply trusting applications. Practically, this means marking on the access switches as the traffic comes into the access switches.
Some Cisco switches default to marking everything coming as BE
(Best Effort). You probably want most traffic to be marked BE
, but you will want VoIP data to be marked EF
(Expedited Forwarding). You will also want to mark some things, e.g. control traffic (VoIP control, routing protocols, etc.), with a higher priority, but not EF
, and you will want to mark some things, e.g. server backup, with a lower priority.
Once you have your traffic properly marked, then you want to use that. Cisco switches can do some limited queuing, but you certainly want to use the markings on your routers where you can do queuing, policing, and shaping. Typically, you will want to set up a priority queue for VoIP to guarantee some bandwidth, and you really want to police any traffic beyond the priority queue bandwidth. Other traffic will have various queue sizes.
You really want to keep it fairly simple. A rookie mistake is to try to set up markings and queue for every different traffic type. The simplest, and often best, QoS policies leave most traffic as BE
, and you only mark and treat differently any exceptional traffic like VoIP, control, or bulk traffic.

**Cisco Switch Implementation Context:**
To actually limit bandwidth on a specific Cisco switch port (rather than just marking traffic), you must use QoS policing or shaping via a `policy-map` applied to the interface. Note that the `bandwidth` command on Cisco switches only influences routing protocol metrics, not actual switching throughput.

Example approach for rate-limiting the Exchange server port:
```
! 1. Classify traffic (e.g., by ACL matching server IP or port)
ip access-list extended LIMIT-EXCHANGE
 permit tcp host <Exchange-IP> any eq 25
 permit tcp host <Exchange-IP> any eq 110

! 2. Create class-map and policy-map to police/shape
class-map match-any EXCHANGE-TRAFFIC
 match access-group name LIMIT-EXCHANGE
policy-map LIMIT-EXCHANGE-PORT
 class EXCHANGE-TRAFFIC
  police 100000000 conform-action transmit exceed-action drop
! 100 Mbps limit example

! 3. Apply to interface
interface GigabitEthernet0/1
 service-policy output LIMIT-EXCHANGE-PORT
```
For strict port-level speed limitation (Layer 2), use `speed <10|100|1000>` or platform-specific rate-limiting features. Always verify ASIC limitations, as heavy QoS policing can impact switching performance on older hardware.

text:
**Q1: Is the '30 second offered rate' the bandwidth entering the service-policy?**
**Correction:** No, it is not tied to the `load-interval` command. The `load-interval` only controls interface load/bandwidth reporting in `show interfaces`. The "30 second offered rate" in `show policy-map interface` is a **hardcoded rolling average** computed by the IOS QoS statistics engine. It calculates the average classified traffic rate over a fixed 30-second window, independent of interface `load-interval` settings.

**Q2: Why are there two lines: 'shape (average) cir' and 'target shape rate'?**
**Clarification:** `target shape rate` simply echoes the rate you configured. `cir` (Committed Information Rate) is the actual shaping rate used by the token bucket algorithm. When using `shape average`, CIR and the target rate will always match. If you use `shape peak`, the shaper introduces a PIR (Peak Information Rate) alongside CIR, which is why the output format changes. (Reference: Cisco HQoS/Shaping documentation)

**Q3: Are the 'queue stats for all priority classes' only for the GRE-traffic class?**
**Confirmation:** Yes. IOS groups all LLQ (Priority) class statistics under this header to differentiate them from standard CBWFQ/WFQ classes. Since `GRE-traffic` is the only priority class in your child policy, these stats reflect only that class's traffic.

**Q4: Is the 19.5 Mbps shape shared between all child classes?**
**Confirmation:** Yes. In hierarchical QoS, the parent `shape average` acts as a bandwidth pipe. All child classes (`GRE-traffic` and `class-default`) share this 19.5 Mbps limit. The `priority 500` guarantees up to 500 kbps for GRE traffic, but if total egress exceeds 19.5 Mbps, the parent shape will pace or drop packets across all classes. This behavior is consistent across IOS and IOS-XE, though modern IOS-XE often recommends explicit `bandwidth` statements in child policies for better predictability.

Q2-Q4 are correct but lack depth regarding hierarchical QoS behavior and IOS sampling mechanics. The content has strong potential for a RAG database but requires correction and modern context.

!
!

text:
Q: In Cisco, this can be easily achieved by:
```
policy-map TVPrio
  description Set VLAN priority to 4
  class class-default
    set cos 4
```
and apply this policy-map to the interface. How to do equivalent in JunOS?

A: In JunOS, 802.1p CoS marking is handled via egress rewrite rules that map internal Forwarding Classes to IEEE 802.1p code points (0-7). To override CoS to 4 on egress, you must configure a rewrite rule for each Forwarding Class:
```
set class-of-service rewrite-rules ieee-802.1 all-to-cos-4 forwarding-class ASSURED_FORWARDING loss-priority low code-point 4
set class-of-service rewrite-rules ieee-802.1 all-to-cos-4 forwarding-class ASSURED_FORWARDING loss-priority high code-point 4
set class-of-service rewrite-rules ieee-802.1 all-to-cos-4 forwarding-class EXPEDITED_FORWARDING loss-priority high code-point 4
set class-of-service rewrite-rules ieee-802.1 all-to-cos-4 forwarding-class EXPEDITED_FORWARDING loss-priority low code-point 4
set class-of-service rewrite-rules ieee-802.1 all-to-cos-4 forwarding-class NETWORK_CONTROL loss-priority low code-point 4
set class-of-service rewrite-rules ieee-802.1 all-to-cos-4 forwarding-class NETWORK_CONTROL loss-priority high code-point 4
set class-of-service rewrite-rules ieee-802.1 all-to-cos-4 forwarding-class BEST_EFFORT loss-priority high code-point 4
set class-of-service rewrite-rules ieee-802.1 all-to-cos-4 forwarding-class BEST_EFFORT loss-priority low code-point 4
```
Then apply the rewrite rule to the egress interface:
```
set class-of-service interfaces ge-0/0/0 unit 0 rewrite-rules ieee-802.1 all-to-cos-4
```
Note: The original answer incorrectly used `code-point 100`; IEEE 802.1p only supports values 0-7. This approach maps all default forwarding classes to CoS 4. If you use custom forwarding classes, you must add them to the rewrite rule. For JunOS 15.1+, you can also achieve this more efficiently using `set class-of-service forwarding-classes` combined with queue mapping, or by using platform-specific CoS marking commands. Always verify with `show class-of-service rewrite-rules` after configuration.

metadata.title: How to override 802.1p CoS on outgoing interfaces?

However, the JunOS configuration contains a critical syntax error: `code-point 100` is invalid as IEEE 802.1p uses a 3-bit field (values 0-7). It should be `code-point 4`. Additionally, the answer lacks context on JunOS QoS architecture (Forwarding Classes vs. Queues vs. Egress Rewrite) and does not mention that modern JunOS versions support more streamlined CoS marking. The document has potential but requires correction and architectural context to be reliable for a RAG database.

Then apply this to the interface(s) you want to change the CoS bits:
set class-of-service interfaces ge-0/0/0 unit 0 rewrite-rules ieee-802.1 all-to-cos-4

text:
The original answer correctly cites valid Junos CLI commands, but `host-outbound-traffic` only applies to traffic generated by the Routing Engine (RE), not to transit/forwarded traffic on interfaces. To override 802.1p CoS on outgoing interfaces in Junos, you must use the CoS 'rewrite' stage.

Standard Junos approach for interface-level CoS rewriting:
1. Define an IEEE rewrite rule (e.g., map DSCP to 802.1p):
   `set class-of-service rewrite-rules ieee-802-1p-to-ieee-802-1p rule1 match dscp ef`
   `set class-of-service rewrite-rules ieee-802-1p-to-ieee-802-1p rule1 then ieee-802-1p 5`
2. Apply via a scheduler map to the interface unit:
   `set class-of-service scheduler-maps my-smap output ieee-802-1p-to-ieee-802-1p`
   `set interfaces ge-0/0/0 unit 0 family ethernet-switching scheduler-maps my-smap`

Alternatively, for global interface rewriting without explicit scheduler maps, use:
   `set forwarding-options rewrite-ieee-802-1p rule1 match dscp <value> then ieee-802-1p <value>`

Junos QoS follows a strict classify -> rewrite -> schedule pipeline. Cisco's policy-map inheritance model is functionally equivalent to Junos' scheduler/rewrite rule combination, but requires explicit rule definition and interface binding in Junos.

metadata.title: How to override 802.1p CoS on outgoing interfaces?

The `host-outbound-traffic` commands only affect traffic originating from the Routing Engine, not transit/forwarded traffic on physical/logical interfaces. The response lacks context on Junos QoS architecture (classify -> rewrite -> schedule) and omits the correct mechanism for interface-level 802.1p CoS rewriting.

text: The original answer accurately describes hardware-based 5-tuple flow tracking but conflates Dynamic Buffer Limit (DBL) with Fair Queuing (FQ). DBL operates at the class/queue level to dynamically adjust buffer thresholds during congestion, whereas identifying and dropping specific 5-tuple flows is the function of Hardware Fair Queuing (FQ) or Per-Flow Policing. On Cisco Catalyst 4500e supervisors (Sup VII/VIII), modern ASICs maintain 5-tuple (src/dst IP, src/dst port, protocol) counters in TCAM to track per-flow packet rates. When congestion occurs, FQ drops packets from flows exceeding their fair bandwidth share, effectively mitigating 'belligerent' or 'aggressive' flows. Examples include unthrottled UDP video streams, misconfigured FTP/HTTP bulk transfers, or any application traffic not implementing ECN or proper TCP congestion control. For accurate QoS design, use class-based policies with `fair-queue` or WRED with per-flow tracking rather than relying solely on DBL.

metadata.title: Aggressive UDP flows

DBL is a queue-level buffer management feature, while per-flow dropping based on 5-tuples is characteristic of Hardware Fair Queuing (FQ) or Per-Flow Policing. The response lacks context on ASIC implementation, concrete examples of aggressive flows, and proper QoS configuration guidance.

During congestion with DBL enabled, the engine consults this table to identify and drop packets from flows exceeding thresholds.

text:
Q: I am reading about the DBL feature on the Cisco 4500E which protect the buffers from filling up with traffic that is identified as nonadaptive or belligerent flows such as aggressive UDP flows. Since my curiosity doesn't stop here, i am trying to understand how the supervisor engine identify this type of traffic, which characteristics has this traffic apart from being UDP.
Is this UDP traffic only?
Can someone provide me with examples of aggressive UDP flows or any other flows that are marked as belligerent flows?
Regards
Gonçalo Reis

[Score:1] Industry’s First Hardware and Flow-Based Congestion Avoidance at Wire Speed
A Cisco innovation, Dynamic Buffer Limiting (DBL) is the first flow-based congestion avoidance quality-of-service (QoS) technique suitable for high-speed hardware implementation. Operating on all ports in the Cisco Catalyst 4500 Series Switch, DBL effectively recognizes and limits numerous misbehaving traffic flows, particularly flows in a network that are unresponsive to explicit congestion feedback such as packet drops (typically UDP-based traffic flows). It is a multiprotocol technique that can examine a flow’s Layer 2/3/4 fields.
DBL provides on-demand Active Queue Management by tracking the queue length for each traffic flow in the switch. When the queue length of a specific flow exceeds its limit, DBL will drop packets or mark the Explicit Congestion Notification (ECN) field in the packet headers, so the flow can be handled appropriately by servers in the unlikely event of network congestion. Unchecked flows—also known as belligerent or non-adaptive flows—use excessive bandwidth, and their consumption of switch buffers results in poor application performance for end users. This misbehaving traffic can also negatively affect well-behaved flows and wreak havoc with QoS.
Belligerent flows can come from misbehaving or aggressive applications that try to use an excessive amount of bandwidth. An application without any congestion control mechanism that attempts to use all the available bandwidth would result in belligerent flows. An aggressive or improper TCP implementation on a high performance host can transmit high rate traffic to the network.
Belligerent flows can also come from network failures or misconfigurations. For instance, in a switched Ethernet environment, a spanning-tree loop, where packets are forwarded at wire rate through a circle of switches can result in several belligerent flows. Similarly, router misconfigurations can lead to looping behavior, such as multicast routing loops. A belligerent flow can also arise because of a host interface or operating system failure where packets are transmitted into the network at it's maximum rate. Less common but still of concern, a malicious host or compromised switch or router can inject high rate streams of packets into the network.
Some reading:

Minor issues: text truncates at the end and lacks configuration examples. Concept remains valid for traditional Cisco QoS design.

text:
Q: I have a Sonicwall NSA 2400 (soon to replace with NSA 2600). The LAN has a widely varying number of users/devices. Some days there are 10 people onsite, some days 75-100. I want to be able to equally and dynamically allocate our WAN bandwidth (about 75 Mbps down, 20 Mbps up) to each device on the LAN.
On the days with 10 devices onsite, each device should get 1/10th the bandwidth. On the days with 75 devices, each device gets 1/75th. Or, 10 devices downloading something via the WAN connection, each device gets 1/10th bandwidth. The goal is fairness to all devices. I don't want to prioritize traffic types (i.e. HTTP, FTP, etc.); I don't care what the device is doing on the internet, they just need to have about equal bandwidth to everyone else. What they do with their slice, we don't care.
I'm familiar with the bandwidth management settings where I can put an absolute limit on the bandwidth, e.g. 1,000 Kbps. I can't do this because we would have massive underutilization of the available bandwidth on the days with fewer devices onsite.
I'm not sure if the Sonicwall has some existing settings I could configure to achieve this goal? Or do I need to look at some QOS configuration to achieve this?
Thanks in advance.
Edit in response to comment from Ron The goal is fairness of available bandwidth but without wasting it when there's only a few people. So if only 2 devices are using the internet connection, each should get roughly half. After posting this question, I did more digging on my NSA 2400. After upgrading firmware to 5.9 I get a checkbox in Firewall > Bandwidth Objects to put a per-IP kpbs max. But this doesn't work as desired since the number of devices using the internet varies.

[Score:2] I know this is an older post, but in case someone else stumbles upon it like I did, I wanted to provide some input.
So far, the only way to do this that I know of is with Mikrotik's PCQ (per-connection queuing) algorithm. It's not too hard to set up and there is a ton of guides out there to show you how. But it basically makes a packet queue for each device on your network and as the link hits your specified bandwidth limit (typically you set it to your ISP's limit) it begins dropping packets from the highest speed queues until all queues reach the same speed. Then it drops packets equally from each.
There is an option to set a fixed limit per queue, but if you don't set a limit it works the way I described.
This is particularly useful in hospitality environments, like hotels, since it basically dynamically adjusts your bandwidth limits per device as your occupancy changes.

**Technical Context & Modern Equivalents:**
While the answer correctly identifies MikroTik's PCQ (Per Connection Queuing) as a robust solution for dynamic fair bandwidth allocation, it omits critical context regarding the stated hardware and firmware.
- **Vendor Limitation & EOL Status:** The SonicWall NSA 2400/2600 series is End-of-Life. Firmware 5.9 belongs to the legacy SonicOS Enhanced era (circa 2014-2016), which lacks true dynamic fair queuing. Legacy SonicOS Bandwidth Management relies on static per-IP limits or application prioritization, not dynamic per-device fair sharing.
- **How PCQ Works:** MikroTik's PCQ dynamically creates queues per IP/connection. When total WAN bandwidth is utilized, it equalizes throughput across active queues by dropping packets from the fastest queues first, achieving the exact 'fair slice' behavior described. This is highly effective in hospitality or dynamic-office environments.
- **Modern SonicWall Alternatives:** Upgrading to SonicOS 7.x introduces enhanced Bandwidth Management with 'Dynamic Bandwidth Allocation' and improved per-user/device shaping. For true dynamic fair queuing, consider enterprise QoS platforms (Cisco IOS-XE CBWFQ/LFQ, Palo Alto QoS policies) or SDN controllers (Ryu/ONOS) that programmatically adjust OpenFlow `meter` or `rate-limit` actions based on active host counts.
- **Implementation Note:** If migrating to MikroTik, RouterOS v7 syntax for PCQ requires defining `pcq-limit` and `pcq-rate` under `/queue tree`. Ensure `pcq-distributor` is enabled to hash connections across queues. For Open vSwitch (OVS) environments, dynamic fair queuing can be approximated using `qos` objects with `min_rate` and `max_rate` parameters, or via SDN automation scripts that recalculate bandwidth slices upon interface state changes.

text: The original post correctly identifies a limitation in traditional firewalls: static per-IP bandwidth limits cannot dynamically scale with active users. Achieving true dynamic fairness requires Class-Based Queuing with Fair Queuing (FQ) or Weighted Fair Queuing (WFQ). In pfSense/OPNsense, this is implemented via child traffic shapers using HTB or PIE queuing disciplines, which automatically allocate bandwidth among active flows. For enterprise or SDN environments, this can be implemented more efficiently using OpenFlow 1.3+ meters with a controller (e.g., Ryu or ONOS) to dynamically install/remove per-IP policing rules as hosts join/leave the LAN. Alternatively, Cisco MQC (CBWFQ + FQ-CODEL) or modern Linux-based routers provide robust, low-latency dynamic fairness without manual queue management. Note: SonicWall NSA 2400/2600 firmware 5.x is legacy; current platforms use firmware 7.x/8.x with improved QoS profiles, though dynamic per-IP fairness still requires careful tuning or external traffic shaping.

text:
Q: I have a very typical scenario where I would like to protect IP phones traffic from high RTT, packet delay variation and packet loss on congested links. I fully understand, that QoS would help only in case of very short congestion. To make matters worse, my ISP does not support QoS so there is no way I can prioritize certain traffic from end-to-end. Overall topology can be seen here:
(Usually) the short traffic congestion happens in ISP PE router where the traffic policers are. I tried Priority Queuing(VoIP traffic has known bandwidth so it's safe to use) in Cisco 2801 routers where IP phones traffic was set to "high" queue both in Fa0/0 and Fa0/1 in egress direction, but this did not have a noticeable affect. On the other hand, it was somewhat expected as I configured queuing before the congested link(policer in PE router)? Is there any technique which helps to protect the traffic between two IP phones in case of short-term traffic bursts other than increasing the policer in PE routers?

[Score:1] The only place you have control of is up to the output queue where you transmit the packet to the provider. If you are using policing here, you need to convert it to Hierarchical Queuing Framework (HQF) or whatever name they have given it now. Inside of this you can set policing values, but your VoIP would be put into the Priority queue to receive low latency transmit priority for packets that match that class (RTP/SIP/SCCP or whatever you define, an ACL perhaps).
This will make sure the voice traffic is transmitted before any other queues and will help on short congestion. Indeed if you convert your policing to traffic shaping, you may see an overall performance increase for everyone, but for sure get that voice in the priority queue. (Shaping vs Policing)
This will only help if your output queue is lightly congested and will have no effect on any internet transport in the middle. If this is where your problem exists, investigate alternative providers or a small dedicated point to point circuit.
Edit: ps set your shaping on CE egress just smaller than the PE ingress with shaping and priority that way you control what gets dropped first.

The solution advises converting local policing to HQF/traffic shaping and placing VoIP in a strict priority queue at the CE egress, ensuring voice traffic is transmitted first before the ISP policer drops packets.

text:
Q: This may seem like a rudimentary question, but I wasn't sure where else to ask it. I've set up some QoS rules on my router that give the highest priority to all traffic incoming and outgoing to my computer's network address, and it has so far done an excellent job of preventing lag and communication issues while someone else on the network decides to watch netflix.
An unfortunate coincidence - which I now believe to be a side effect - is that my HP 6980 printer refuses to communicate with my computer to print. No one else on the network has the same issues. I have attempted to reinstall the printer multiple times without success. In the settings, the printer shows the correct information and even the ink remaining, but will not communicate when trying to print something.
Could my QoS settings (set up with very minimal knowledge) have removed my ability to print wirelessly? If so, how might I go about fixing it?

A: [Score:6] Could it? Yes. Misconfigured QoS can easily disrupt printer communication, particularly if it inadvertently drops, delays, or rate-limits control-plane traffic required for discovery and job submission.
Steps I would try:
1. **Verify QoS Rule Scope & Action:** Ensure your rules are strictly prioritizing (e.g., DSCP marking, weighted fair queuing, or bandwidth allocation) and not inadvertently policing or dropping non-priority traffic. Some consumer routers apply strict queueing that starves low-priority ports.
2. **Check Printer Control Protocols:** Wireless printing relies on several protocols that are often misclassified or throttled by QoS:
   - **mDNS/Bonjour (UDP 5353):** Used for automatic network discovery. Frequently dropped or rate-limited.
   - **IPP (TCP 631) / SMB (TCP 445):** Used for job submission and spooling.
   - **SNMP (UDP 161/162):** Used for status/ink reporting.
   Add explicit allow/priority rules for these ports or mark them as high-priority in your QoS policy.
3. **DHCP Reservation:** QoS tied to dynamic IPs can break if the printer's lease changes. Assign a static IP or DHCP reservation to the printer to ensure consistent IP matching.
4. **Isolation Test:** Temporarily disable QoS or create a bypass rule for the printer's IP. If printing works immediately, the issue is definitively QoS misconfiguration.
5. **Firmware/Driver Check:** While QoS is the likely culprit, ensure both the printer firmware and host drivers are updated, as HP printers sometimes have compatibility quirks with certain network stacks or aggressive QoS implementations.

metadata.title: Can QoS settings interfere with wireless printing?

It scores in the 4-6 range due to vagueness and incomplete troubleshooting guidance. Enrichment adds protocol-specific context (mDNS/Bonjour, IPP, SNMP), explains how QoS misconfiguration affects these services, and provides structured, actionable steps while preserving the original format.

text:
[Answer] Typically, SOHO router QoS implements traffic prioritization (WRR, DSCP marking, or simple rate limiting) rather than explicit dropping. However, misconfigured QoS can still disrupt wireless printing in several ways:
1) **Multicast/Broadcast Filtering**: Printer discovery relies heavily on mDNS (UDP 5353). Some SOHO QoS policies inadvertently throttle or block multicast traffic, breaking Bonjour/WS-Discovery.
2) **Rate Limiting/Policing**: If your QoS rule applies a strict bandwidth cap or burst limit to your PC's MAC/IP, it can starve the small but latency-sensitive packets used by IPP (TCP 631), RAW 9100, or LPD (UDP 515).
3) **DSCP Marking Issues**: Aggressive DSCP rewriting can sometimes cause fragmentation or state-tracking issues on consumer-grade NAT/firewalls, even for local traffic.

While local PC-to-printer traffic usually bypasses WAN firewall rules, some SOHO firmware applies QoS/policing at the L2/L3 switch level before bridging. To troubleshoot: temporarily disable the QoS rules entirely to isolate the variable. If printing works, re-enable QoS but exclude your printer's IP and the mDNS subnet (224.0.0.0/24). Use Wireshark with filters like `udp port 5353` or `tcp port 631` to observe if mDNS advertisements or print jobs are being dropped or delayed. Check your router's QoS logs for 'dropped' or 'shaped' packet counters. For enterprise environments (e.g., Cisco IOS), you would typically use CBWFQ or LLQ with explicit police/shaper commands, but SOHO devices rarely implement these granularly without causing collateral disruption to local broadcast/multicast services.

It overlooks critical protocols like mDNS/Bonjour (UDP 5353), IPP (TCP 631), and RAW (TCP 9100), which are highly sensitive to multicast throttling or rate-limiting. The explanation of local traffic bypassing the firewall is correct but oversimplified, as many SOHO routers apply QoS/policing at the switching fabric level. Enrichment adds protocol-specific troubleshooting, clarifies SOHO vs. enterprise QoS behavior, and provides precise Wireshark filters.

text:
Q: This may seem like a rudimentary question, but I wasn't sure where else to ask it. I've set up some QoS rules on my router that give the highest priority to all traffic incoming and outgoing to my computer's network address, and it has so far done an excellent job of preventing lag and communication issues while someone else on the network decides to watch netflix.
An unfortunate coincidence - which I now believe to be a side effect - is that my HP 6980 printer refuses to communicate with my computer to print. No one else on the network has the same issues. I have attempted to reinstall the printer multiple times without success. In the settings, the printer shows the correct information and even the ink remaining, but will not communicate when trying to print something.
Could my QoS settings (set up with very minimal knowledge) have removed my ability to print wirelessly? If so, how might I go about fixing it?

A: That is very unlikely since it is limited to your computer... the more probable cause is a software firewall on your pc or some other issue... steps to isolate....
First, be sure you can still ping the printer and also that you can telnet to the printer's port (ours use TCP 515)... if you can't, them you need to solve these issues first.
Next reinstall printer drivers... does this help?
If all that fails, try removing the qos settings.... does that help?

**Technical Context & Improvements:**
- **QoS vs. Filtering:** Standard QoS uses traffic shaping and prioritization (e.g., DSCP marking, queuing). It rarely blocks traffic entirely unless misconfigured as a strict rate limit or incorrectly bound to an ACL. If printing fails completely, check for restrictive firewall rules, NAT/port forwarding conflicts, or blocked broadcast/multicast traffic.
- **Modern Printing Protocols:** While TCP 515 (LPD) was historically standard, most modern HP printers and OS drivers default to IPP (port 631) or mDNS (UDP 5353) for auto-discovery. Ensure QoS policies aren't throttling UDP multicast traffic used for device discovery.
- **Actionable Troubleshooting:**
  1. Verify connectivity: `ping <printer_ip>`
  2. Test service ports: `telnet <printer_ip> 631` (IPP) or `515` (LPD)
  3. Check router logs for dropped packets or ACL denials.
  4. Temporarily disable QoS. If resolved, review rules to ensure they apply to unicast traffic and do not impact DNS, DHCP, or discovery protocols.
  5. Reinstall drivers using IPP (recommended for modern networks).

text:
Q: I have read that DSCP consists of 6 bits, thus providing us with 64 DSCP marking values (0-63).
If I understood all correctly, 21 of these values have been standardized and named (AF, EF, DF, etc)
What about the remaining 43 DSCP values? Can we still use/configure them in our network?

[Score:9] You can use the DSCP values in any way you want on your own network (DSCP values are not honored on the public Internet). You can even make EF be the lowest priority value.
You first classify the traffic, then you mark it using DSCP values. You must configure your routers to do something with those values under congested conditions (by default, DSCP values are ignored). What you configure on your routers is completely up to you. You should really be consistent across your entire network, otherwise you can make a real mess of it.
Using too many values is a rookie mistake. You should not use more than about half a dozen. Once you get to that many, it is probably time to rethink your QoS strategy.

metadata.title: Are all 64 DSCP values usable/configurable?
metadata.llm_evaluation_reason: Technically accurate and aligns with industry QoS best practices. Correctly identifies DSCP as a 6-bit field (0-63) and appropriately advises limiting class counts to ~6-7 for network manageability. The statement that DSCP is 'not honored on the public internet' is practically true for enterprise-to-internet boundaries, though some transit providers do honor EF/AF for paid services. The '21 standardized values' claim is a minor simplification of RFC-defined PHBs (BE, AFxx, CS0-7, EF), but functionally correct for practical design.

text:
Q: I have been reading about QoS and different strategies.
From what I understand, Cisco proposes three different models and three different strategies for QoS.
For strategies, you can have a 4-class, 8-class or 12-class model. Source
For models, you can have Best-effort service, Integrated services or differentiated services. Source
I am not sure I understand the difference between a strategy and a model? Do the models depend on a strategy chosen? I'd just like a very brief explanation of what is the relationship between these two concepts.

[Score:8] What is a strategy and what is a model depends mostly on the words the author likes to use. In this case you could substitute one for the other.
Best effort is essentially no QoS. You might say the strategy is "no strategy," or the model is "no model."
DiffServ is the most common QoS model (or strategy, whatever). Data classes are predefined, and bandwidth is allocated per class on each device. The number of classes has increased over time as switch hardware capabilities have improved, and as applications that require specialized handling (realtime audio/video) have become more common.
Sometimes, ideas that look great in the lab don't work out in practice. IntServ was one such idea. The thought was that hosts wold tell the network how much bandwidth they needed, or how much latency they could tolerate, and the network would configure itself to provide it. Each router along the data path would "reserve" bandwidth when requested. When the data flow was finished, the router would release the reservation.
It was a great idea, but in practice it didn't work out so well. The biggest problem was that each router had to maintain the state of every data flow. This put a tremendous burden on memory. Also since the send and receive paths could be asymmetric, the signaling messages didn't reach the right devices.
IntServ never really caught on, and DiffServ has become the dominant QoS strategy (oops, I mean model).

metadata.title: What is the difference between Cisco's QoS models and strategies?
metadata.llm_evaluation_reason: Accurately explains the architectural differences between IntServ and DiffServ, correctly identifies IntServ's scalability limitations, and appropriately notes the semantic overlap between 'model' and 'strategy'. Lacks Cisco-specific implementation context (MQC, DSCP/CoS mapping, QoS Baseline) but remains technically sound and conceptually clear.

It examines how each framework handles traffic prioritization, resource allocation, and network performance management.

text:
Q: I am currently studying QoS and I am often confused and tend to mix the terms "Bandwidth" and "Speed".
If I am not mistaken, bandwidth is the capacity of the link, correct? While the speed defines how fast the data is transmitted?
If that's the case, I am not quite sure if I understand what speed means here. If we have a link with a speed of 2 gbit/s, would it transmit data (lets say, 100 mbits) faster than a 1 gbit/s link? Or what if we (let's use our imagination here for a second) had a link with a BW of 1000 mbits but the speed would only be 500 mbit/s? The link's capacity would not be reached but it still wouldn't be able to transmit all 1000 mbits within a second, right?
Thank you for your time.

[A: It's all about context. "Bandwidth" technically refers to the maximum theoretical capacity of a link or path, while "speed" is often used colloquially to mean actual throughput or nominal line rate.

If we have a link with a speed of 2 gbit/s, would it transmit data faster than a 1 gbit/s link? Yes, assuming all other factors (latency, packet loss, queueing) are equal. The 2 Gbps link has twice the pipe capacity, so the same 100 Mbps payload will traverse it in half the time.

Here, "speed" refers to the link's nominal line rate. An Ethernet link with a nominal rate of 1 Gbit/s (e.g., 1000BASE-T) already factors in physical layer encoding overhead. However, it does not account for higher-layer overheads. For TCP over IPv4 with maximum-sized frames (MTU 1500), the calculation goes like this:
- Line Rate: 1,000 Mbps
- Ethernet Framing (Preamble, SFD, IFG, MAC Header, FCS): ~5-6% overhead -> ~940 Mbps
- IP & TCP Headers: ~8-10% overhead -> ~850 Mbps
- Actual TCP Payload Throughput: ~800-820 Mbps

So, a 1 Gbps link won't achieve 1,000 Mbps of application data. It will top out around 800-850 Mbps under ideal conditions.

In the context of QoS (and Cisco implementations), "bandwidth" is often configured as a limit (e.g., `bandwidth` command, `shape`, or `police` policies). If you configure a QoS policy to limit a class to 500 Mbps, the achievable "speed" (throughput) will be capped at that bandwidth regardless of the physical link's 1 Gbps capacity. Always specify whether you mean wire speed, line rate, or application throughput to avoid ambiguity.

metadata.title: What exactly is the difference between bandwidth and speed?
metadata.llm_evaluation_reason: The original text correctly distinguishes between theoretical bandwidth (line rate) and actual throughput, but it cuts off before providing the promised calculation. It also lacks specific ties to the tagged topics (Cisco, QoS). The conceptual foundation is sound, but technical precision, completeness, and contextual examples are needed for a reliable RAG database entry.

text:
Q: I am currently studying QoS and I am often confused and tend to mix the terms "Bandwidth" and "Speed". If I am not mistaken, bandwidth is the capacity of the link, correct? While the speed defines how fast the data is transmitted? If that's the case, I am not quite sure if I understand what speed means here. If we have a link with a speed of 2 gbit/s, would it transmit data (lets say, 100 mbits) faster than a 1 gbit/s link? Or what if we (let's use our imagination here for a second) had a link with a BW of 1000 mbits but the speed would only be 500 mbit/s? The link's capacity would not be reached but it still wouldn't be able to transmit all 1000 mbits within a second, right?

A: [Score:1] Depending on context, "speed" might also be related to "latency". A link that has a higher bandwidth does not necessarily mean less latency, and might thus seem slower.
As an example, imagine two pieces of equipment 1 km apart. Too far for Ethernet, but to connect them you have the options a) laying a 9600 bps rs-232 cable or b) using a satellite service. As long as you're sending a few bytes per second, the 9600 bps option will always seem faster because it has much less latency - you can't get around the speed of light with the satellite service. Once you ramp up the (used) bandwidth, this changes.

[Technical Enrichment for QoS/SDN Context]: In professional networking, "bandwidth" strictly refers to the maximum theoretical data rate (capacity, measured in bps). "Speed" is a colloquial term that often conflates bandwidth, throughput (actual data delivered), and latency (delay). For QoS (e.g., Cisco CBWFQ, Policing, or Mininet/OVS rate limiting), bandwidth defines the pipe size, while latency/jitter are time-based metrics. Higher bandwidth reduces *transmission delay* (packet size / bandwidth) but does not affect *propagation delay* (distance / speed of light). In SDN/OpenFlow, bandwidth is programmatically enforced via switch actions (e.g., OpenFlow 1.3 `OFPT_FLOW_MOD` with `OFPAction_SET_QUEUE` or OVS `qos` commands), whereas latency is a physical characteristic. When configuring QoS, always specify bandwidth as a rate limit (e.g., `policy-map` bandwidth values) and monitor actual throughput vs. configured bandwidth to identify bottlenecks or congestion.

metadata.title: What exactly is the difference between bandwidth and speed?
metadata.llm_evaluation_reason: Fundamentally correct but uses informal terminology. Lacks precise distinction between bandwidth, throughput, and latency, which is critical for QoS and SDN implementations. Scored 4-5 per guide (vague but has potential).
metadata.problem_summary: Clarifying the conceptual distinction between network bandwidth (link capacity) and speed/latency, and explaining how propagation delay affects perceived transmission performance regardless of raw bandwidth.

text:
Q: I have tried to find the answer on how network throttling works e.g. by ISP when they limit your access to say 100 Mbps. As far as I know TCP/IP itself does not have any mechanisms for that, so it is probably something on top of the protocol that interferes with the packets.
My guess is that the packets received from the server are not sent directly to the client, but wait in some kind of throttling queue and the server does not send the next packet until the confirmation is received. I am not really a big expert on this, but would like to understand how this happens or maybe some good reference to how it works in simple terms, without too deep details (conceptual overview).
P.S. Not sure if throttling is protocol dependent, perhaps it's the same techniques that are used for others.

[Score:5] This really has nothing to do with TCP, or any other layer-4 protocol.
For the most part, this is done simply through policing (dropping) any inbound packets that would exceed the bandwidth limit. Outbound packets could be policed or shaped to a specific bandwidth. Shaping does use queues, but queues are finite and fairly small, not like the buffering you see for watching a video, and when a queue gets full, the traffic to be queued gets dropped. There are multiple shaping strategies. ISPs tend to want things simple, and most probably just use policing.
The whole QoS concept is far too large to explain in this site. There are entire books on QoS that have chapters on policing and shaping. You could do some further research on those concepts.
How each ISP does this type of thing may vary from ISP to ISP, and networks not under your control are off-topic here, so we can't tell you what any particular ISP does for this.

metadata.title: How does throttling of network traffic work (to limit bandwidth by ISP e.g.)?

Minor lack of implementation detail prevents a perfect score, but it is highly suitable for a conceptual RAG entry.
metadata.problem_summary: User seeks a conceptual explanation of how ISPs implement network throttling/bandwidth limiting, specifically questioning whether it relies on TCP-layer mechanisms or external network equipment techniques like policing and shaping.
metadata.context_summary: This document explains the theoretical mechanisms behind network traffic throttling, focusing on how ISPs limit bandwidth using TCP/IP, Layer-4 protocols, Quality of Service (QoS), and traffic policing. text:
Q: I have tried to find the answer on how network throttling works e.g. by ISP when they limit your access to say 100 Mbps. As far as I know TCP/IP itself does not have any mechanisms for that, so it is probably something on top of the protocol that interferes with the packets.
My guess is that the packets received from the server are not sent directly to the client, but wait in some kind of throttling queue and the server does not send the next packet until the confirmation is received. I am not really a big expert on this, but would like to understand how this happens or maybe some good reference to how it works in simple terms, without too deep details (conceptual overview).
P.S. Not sure if throttling is protocol dependent, perhaps it's the same techniques that are used for others.

A: ISP bandwidth throttling is primarily implemented using **QoS Traffic Shaping and Policing**, not just reactive packet dropping. 

1. **Traffic Shaping (Token Bucket/Leaky Bucket):** The ISP's edge router buffers excess traffic and releases it at a configured rate (e.g., 100 Mbps). This smooths bursts and enforces the limit without necessarily dropping packets, though it increases latency.
2. **Traffic Policing:** If traffic exceeds the configured rate, the router drops or marks (DSCP/CE) the excess packets immediately.
3. **TCP Congestion Control:** While shaping/policing enforces the limit, TCP's built-in algorithms (Slow Start, Congestion Avoidance, Fast Retransmit) react to packet loss or ECN (Explicit Congestion Notification) by reducing the send window. This is a side effect, not the throttling mechanism itself.

Throttling is largely protocol-agnostic at Layers 2/3, but TCP is highly sensitive to queueing delays and drops. Modern implementations use hierarchical QoS on edge routers (Cisco IOS XR/Juniper) or SDN controllers (via OpenFlow/NETCONF) to dynamically manage bandwidth per subscriber or per application.

metadata.title: How does throttling of network traffic work (to limit bandwidth by ISP e.g.)?

It lacks QoS terminology, algorithmic context (token bucket/leaky bucket), and modern implementation details. The content has potential to explain core networking concepts but requires technical correction and enrichment.

text:
Q: Is LAN QoS required for VoIP applications? We have basic network infrastructure: PC (Softphone) -GigEth-> [Switch] -GigEth-> [Router/Firewall] -> Internet (100 Mbps) -> [ITSP]. Voice traffic flows via switch and firewall towards my IP Telephony Service Provider. We have around 50 users which may place simultaneous calls. (Bandwidth per call is around 80 Kbps - Kilobits per second) and Softphone doesn't mark traffic with DSCP, use BE (0). We have received some complaints about voice quality. I believe 1 GB ethernet is more than enough per PC.

A: While 1 Gbps Ethernet per PC provides ample bandwidth, VoIP is highly sensitive to latency, jitter, and packet loss. Since your softphones mark traffic as Best Effort (DSCP 0/BE), voice packets will compete equally with large data transfers on your 100 Mbps WAN link. Without QoS, data bursts will cause voice degradation. To resolve this:
1. Uplink & Port Sizing: Ensure switch-to-switch and switch-to-router uplinks are not oversubscribed. For 50 users, a 1 Gbps uplink is generally sufficient, but verify actual utilization during peak hours.
2. Storm/Broadcast Control: Configure storm control on access ports to prevent broadcast/multicast storms from consuming bandwidth and increasing jitter.
3. Firewall Inspection: Stateful inspection and SIP ALG can introduce significant latency or delay RTP streams. Configure firewall policies to prioritize SIP signaling (TCP/UDP 5060/5061) and bypass deep packet inspection for RTP, or use QoS policies to mark SIP/RTP traffic before it reaches the firewall.
4. Bandwidth Calculation: 50 calls × 80 Kbps (Kilobits/sec) = 4 Mbps. This is well within a 100 Mbps link, but throughput alone doesn't guarantee quality. Unmarked traffic cannot be prioritized.

Critical QoS Implementation: Configure endpoints to mark voice traffic with DSCP EF (46) or AF41. On Cisco switches/routers, trust DSCP/CoS at the edge, configure priority queuing (e.g., LLQ), and ensure uplinks have sufficient buffer/priority queue allocation. Verify with show policy-map and show queueing.

Note: In SDN/OpenFlow environments (e.g., Ryu, OVS), implement this by installing flow rules that match DSCP EF and apply high-priority actions (e.g., `set_queue(0)` or `meter` for policing/shaping) before the default best-effort bucket.

metadata.title: LAN QoS for VoIP
metadata.llm_evaluation_reason: The original answer correctly identifies physical bottlenecks, broadcast storms, and firewall inspection delays as troubleshooting steps, but fundamentally misses the core requirement for VoIP on unmarked (DSCP 0/BE) traffic: explicit DSCP classification and priority queuing. Without marking and queuing, bursty data traffic will compete equally with voice on the 100 Mbps WAN link, causing jitter and packet loss. The text also contains a unit typo (KBPS vs Kbps) and lacks actionable QoS implementation steps.
metadata.problem_summary: Voice quality degradation complaints in a VoIP deployment with ~50 simultaneous users, where softphones send unmarked (DSCP 0/BE) traffic, potentially caused by switch/router uplink bottlenecks, firewall inspection latency, or missing storm/broadcast controls.
metadata.context_summary: Poor voice quality complaints in a VoIP deployment with ~50 simultaneous users, where softphones send unmarked (DSCP 0/BE) traffic, potentially caused by switch/router uplink bottlenecks, firewall inspection latency, or missing storm/broadcast controls.

text:
Q: Is LAN QoS required for VoIP applications? We have basic network infrastructure:
PC (Softphone) -GigEth-> [Switch] -GigEth-> [Router/Firewall] -> Internet (100 Mbps) -> [ITSP]
Voice traffic flows via switch and firewall towards my IP Telephony Service Provider. We have around 50 users which may place simultaneous calls. (Bandwidth per call is around 80 Kbps - Kilobits per second) and Softphone doesn't mark traffic with DSCP, use BE (0). We have received some complaints about voice quality. I believe 1 GB ethernet is more than enough per PC.

[Score:2] You said IT telephony provider so I'm assuming Hosted VoIP service. If this is the case you should be most concerned with your upload internet bandwidth, 50 simultaneous users using the G711 codec will consume 4.25 Mbps so you should budget enough for your normal data needs and the VoIP usage. Some things you could do to make use of limited bandwidth are implement bandwidth prioritization for traffic going to the VoIP service, cap other non essential tasks going out to the internet, and use a smaller codec if supported by your provider.
Other things you can check to resolve VoIP Quality Issues: - Ensure no problems with cabling - Reaching throughput or other limits on switches, more common with lower end switches - Do standard network tests to different components on the network to see where the latency if any is being introduced. - Implement network monitoring to provide more insight into bandwidth utilization, and other device metrics. I would recommend PRTG, and depending on your needs 100 sensors are free.
Best of luck with your issues, if you continue to experience issues I would contact your VoIP provider as they may be able to troubleshoot with you.

metadata.title: LAN QoS for VoIP
metadata.llm_evaluation_reason: The document provides technically accurate guidance on VoIP bandwidth requirements, codec overhead, and the impact of unmarked (DSCP 0) traffic on voice quality. The troubleshooting steps and QoS prioritization advice are standard and correct. It leans slightly toward WAN upload constraints rather than detailing LAN-side QoS configuration, but the core networking principles are sound and highly applicable.

It covers critical configuration elements including DSCP traffic prioritization, G.711 codec requirements, and Gigabit Ethernet optimization to ensure reliable, low-latency voice communication.

text:
Q: Is LAN QoS required for VoIP applications? We have basic network infrastructure:
PC (Softphone) -GigEth-> [Switch] -GigEth-> [Router/Firewall] -> Internet (100 Mbps) -> [ITSP]
Voice traffic flows via switch and firewall towards my IP Telephony Service Provider. We have around 50 users which may place simultaneous calls. (Bandwidth per call is around 80 Kbps - Kilobits per second) and Softphone doesn't mark traffic with DSCP, use BE (0). We have received some complaints about voice quality. I believe 1 GB ethernet is more than enough per PC.

[Score:1] QoS is something you should set up when you have a bunch of users and VoIP. You really want the VoIP to run in its own VLAN separate from the rest of the network traffic.
You should do QoS marking as close to the source as possible (at the switch port, if possible). Even better, the phones may already mark the VoIP traffic as EF, but that depends on the phone. The rest of your traffic can be marked as BE, or you can get into multiple classes if you have a need for that.
Once the traffic is marked, you need to set up proper queues in the router for the various traffic types based on the markings. VoIP should probably have a priority queue which you need to size carefully. There are various other techniques such as shaping, RED policing, etc. QoS is a topic far too broad to properly be discussed here.
Remember that, unless you pay for it, your ISP won't honor QoS markings, and any other ASes through which your traffic travels to your VoIP provider will not honor any QoS markings either. The goal for QoS in your network is to make sure that VoIP gets first priority exiting your network.

metadata.title: LAN QoS for VoIP
metadata.llm_evaluation_reason: The document provides technically accurate and current guidance on LAN QoS for VoIP. It correctly identifies key best practices: VLAN segmentation, trust boundaries at the access switch, DSCP marking, and router queuing. The note regarding ISP/QoS limitations beyond the local WAN is also correct. Minor imprecision exists regarding queuing mechanisms (VoIP media should use LLQ, not generic priority queues or RED), but this does not compromise the overall validity. No outdated versions or commands are present. The text is suitable for retention in a RAG database.

text:
Q: My configuration
policy-map Shape_FTP
class FTP
shape average percent 5
class class-default
fair-queue
policy-map Shaper
class class-default
shape average percent 100
service-policy Shape_FTP
interface tunnel 0
bandwidth 512
tunnel protection ipsec profile aes
service-policy output Shaper
Bandwidth value is used from physical interface. Is it possible to make QoS use bandwidth value from tunnel interface? I have a lot of tunnel interfaces with different bandwidthes (with the same tunnel source IP). I would like to use one policy map with shape average percent 100
rather than a lot of with shape average 512000
and so on

[Score:6] Have you tried using bandwidth qos-reference
?
interface Tunnel100
bandwidth 1000
bandwidth qos-reference 1000

**Technical Context:** The `bandwidth qos-reference` command explicitly instructs the Cisco IOS QoS subsystem to use the tunnel interface's configured bandwidth value for shaping calculations, overriding the default behavior of falling back to the physical interface's bandwidth. This enables `shape average percent` to correctly scale relative to each tunnel's configured bandwidth without hardcoding absolute Kbps values.

**Modern IOS Behavior:** In IOS 15.x and later, `shape average percent` typically respects the `bandwidth` command under the tunnel interface automatically. The `bandwidth qos-reference` command is primarily required on older IOS releases (12.4T and earlier) or specific platforms where QoS defaults to the physical link bandwidth. Verify your platform's QoS bandwidth resolution behavior using `show policy-map interface TunnelX` to confirm the shaping rate calculation source.

metadata.title: QoS on tunnel interface
metadata.llm_evaluation_reason: Technically accurate command identification, but lacks critical context regarding IOS QoS bandwidth resolution behavior and version differences.
metadata.problem_summary: How to configure QoS shaping policies to dynamically reference the bandwidth value set on a tunnel interface rather than the physical interface, enabling reuse of a single percentage-based policy map across multiple tunnels with varying bandwidths.
metadata.context_summary: User has multiple tunnel interfaces with different bandwidths and wants to apply one QoS policy map using 'shape average percent 100' instead of hardcoding absolute bandwidth values for each tunnel. The answer suggests using the 'bandwidth qos-reference' command on the tunnel interface.

class FTP
shape average percent 5
class class-default
fair-queue
policy-map Shaper
class class-default
shape average percent 100
service-policy Shape_FTP
interface tunnel 0
bandwidth 512
tunnel protection ipsec profile aes
service-policy output Shaper

text:
Q: I plan to implement the QoS policy below.
- The target is to garantee bandwidth to VOICE and CRITICAL traffic.
- It should be 1/3 for Voice and Video, 1/3 for Business Critical traffic,
1/3 for all the rest.
- If one of the classes don´t use the reserved bandwidth, the other classes can take it.
- I would like to make sure OSPF is running fine even the when the links are congested to avaoid flappings etc.
The plan is:
- Parent policy to shape to 20Mbps
- Child policy to apply QoS for 1/3 Voice/Video, 1/3 Business Critical,
1/3 all the rest
The Questions:
- I read somewhere that routing protocol traffic gets automaticcally into the LLQ, thats true?
If not, how i can make sure the OSPF traffic in outbound direction originated by the SW itself is priorized and gets transmitted even if the link is overloaded?
- Is the approach in my policy right to do what i descriped above? Or
do i have some mistakes inside?
Open for tipps, tricks, critic, hints
Setup
- OSPF is running on both links where the policy should be applied
- Traffic is already marked by hard / sofphones for voice and video
- Platform where QoS should be implemented is Cisco 4500X running in VSS
PLANNED CONFIG
class-map match-any CM_QOS_VOICE-VIDEO-TRAFFIC
description Matching voice, voice signalling, video and app-sharing traffic
match dscp ef
match dscp cs3
match dscp af41
match dscp af21
!
class-map match-any CM_QOS_CRITICAL-TRAFFIC
description Matching critical traffic: Routing Protocol, Business critical, etc.
match access-group name ACL_BUSINESS-CRITICAL-TRAFFIC
match access-group name ACL_ROUTING-PROTOCOL-TRAFFIC ! Necessary or automatically put to LLQ?
!
policy-map PM_QOS_SHAPING-20M
class class-default
shape average 20000000
service-policy PM_QOS_WAN-EDGE
!
policy-map PM_QOS_WAN-EDGE
class CM_QOS_VOICE-VIDEO-TRAFFIC
priority percent 33
class CM_QOS_CRITICAL-TRAFFIC
bandwidth percent 33
class class-default
fair-queue
random-detect
!
interface te1/1/1
service-policy output PM_QOS_SHAPING-20M
!
interface te2/1/1
service-policy output PM_QOS_SHAPING-20M
!

[Score:3] QoS is a book-worthy topic. but here are a few quick answers:
I read somewhere that routing protocol traffic gets automatically into the LLQ, thats true?
Not true
If not, how i can make sure the OSPF traffic in outbound direction originated by the SW itself is prioritized and gets transmitted even if the link is overloaded?
Put OSPF (and all management traffic) in a separate queue and give it some slice of bandwidth (10%)
Is the approach in my policy right to do what i descriped above? Or do i have some mistakes inside?
Your voice traffic should not be shaped. Put it in a priority queue before the shaper.
Cisco documentation has lots of QoS examples for a relatively common requirement such as yours.

--- TECHNICAL ENRICHMENT & BEST PRACTICES ---
1. OSPF Matching: OSPF uses IP protocol 89, not TCP/UDP. To match it reliably in an ACL, use: `access-list 100 permit ip protocol ospf any any`. In the class-map, you can also use `match ip protocol ospf` if supported by your IOS-XE version.
2. Shaping vs. Queuing Clarification: The parent/child structure is correct. The parent policy shapes the aggregate traffic to 20Mbps. The child policy applies LLQ (`priority`) and CBWFQ (`bandwidth`) to the shaped rate. This prevents buffer bloat and ensures voice packets are transmitted immediately within the shaped pipe. The original answer's comment likely meant 'do not police voice', but shaping at the parent level is standard practice.
3. Locally Generated Traffic: Interface QoS often only applies to transit traffic. For OSPF helms/deads originated by the switch, use Control Plane QoS (CoPP) or `control-plane` service-policy to prioritize these packets before they egress.
4. WRED Tuning: `random-detect` in `class-default` uses default thresholds. For better congestion management, consider explicit thresholds: `random-detect dscp-based` or specify min/max thresholds tailored to your average packet size.
5. LLQ Limits: `priority percent 33` is acceptable, but ensure it never exceeds ~33% of link bandwidth to prevent CBWFQ starvation. Monitor with `show policy-map interface`.

- It should be 1/3 for Voice and Video, 1/3 for Business Critical traffic,
1/3 for all the rest.

- I would like to make sure OSPF is running fine even the when the links are congested to avaoid flappings etc.

- Child policy to apply QoS for 1/3 Voice/Video, 1/3 Business Critical,

- Traffic is already marked by hard / sofphones for voice and video
- Platform where QoS should be implemented is Cisco 4500X running in VSS
PLANNED CONFIG

description Matching voice, voice signalling, video and app-sharing traffic

match access-group name ACL_ROUTING-PROTOCOL-TRAFFIC ! Necessary or automatically put to LLQ?
!

!

!

[Score:2] Besides what Ron Trunk explained, you have some problems.
If you want to base your QoS on 20 Mbps on a 1 Gbps link, then you must set the bandwidth
statement on the interface, otherwise QoS assumes that it is using the full 1 Gbps bandwidth of the interface. You probably want to set it to a smaller percentage for the 20 Mbps because of the overhead of small real-time packets like VoIP and video. This will take some experimentation to get right, but you could start at 97% and adjust later, as needed. That would get you something like:
interface te1/1/1
bandwidth 19400
!
interface te2/1/1
bandwidth 19400
!
You will also need to set up some ACLs and ingress policies to properly classify and mark the traffic inbound to the switch from the access interfaces so that you can properly treat the traffic as it is outbound on the uplink interfaces. That is really a different and complex question, so I will not deal with it here.
Typically, you want most of your traffic to be set as default (BE
), then you want to set up a few classes for traffic that gets special treatment. Just as important as marking and treating high-priority traffic, like VoIP, you need to classify and treat low-priority traffic, like server backups, that can eat huge chunks of bandwidth for extended periods of time.
You could set up something like this typical set of classes:
class-map match-any EGRESS-VOICE
description VoIP traffic
match ip dscp cs4 cs5 ef
!
class-map match-any EGRESS-VIDEO
description Video traffic
match ip dscp af41 af42 af43
!
class-map match-any EGRESS-CONTROL
description Control traffic for VoIP, Video, Routing, etc.
match ip dscp cs3 af31 af32 af33 cs6 cs7
!
class-map match-any EGRESS-BUSINESS
description Business traffic that requires special treatment
match ip dscp cs2 af21 af22 af23
!
class-map match-any EGRESS-BULK
description Bulk traffic like backups, etc.
match ip dscp cs1 af11 af12 af13
!
Then, you create a policy for your uplink interfaces. Below is a typical good starting policy, then you will need to observe the production traffic and tweak the bandwidth percentages to give you what you need. You can use something like NetFlow to gather the statistics for that.
policy-map EGRESS-UPLINK
description QoS for 20 Mbps uplinks
class EGRESS-VOICE
priority
police cir percent 23
class EGRESS-VIDEO
bandwidth remaining percent 30
police cir percent 23
class EGRESS-CONTROL
bandwidth remaining percent 10
class EGRESS-BUSINESS
bandwidth remaining percent 20
class EGRESS-BULK
bandwidth remaining percent 5
dbl
class class-default
bandwidth remaining percent 34
dbl
!
And, you apply the policy to your uplink interfaces:
interface te1/1/1
bandwidth 19400
service-policy output EGRESS-UPLINK
!
interface te2/1/1
bandwidth 19400
service-policy output EGRESS-UPLINK
!
Another thing you didn't ask about, besides the ingress QoS for the access interfaces, is setting up egress QoS for the access interfaces, which could be similar to the uplink interfaces, except that you probably do not want to set the bandwidth
statement on the access interfaces (simply let QoS use the actual bandwidth of the interfaces).
Edit:
If you want to shape, you can add something like this (based on the 97% of the 20 Mbps bandwidth):
policy-map EGRESS-20M
class class-default
shape average 19400000 77600
service-policy EGRESS-UPLINK
!
Then, use the shaping policy (which uses to the queuing policy):
interface te1/1/1
bandwidth 19400
service-policy output EGRESS-20M
!
interface te2/1/1
bandwidth 19400
service-policy output EGRESS-20M
!

text:
Q: This is on a Nexus N5624Q.
I have this TA-queuing service policy :
```
policy-map type queuing TA-queuing
  class type queuing queue-cos1
    bandwidth percent 20
  class type queuing queue-cos2
    bandwidth percent 10
  class type queuing queue-cos4
    bandwidth percent 15
  class type queuing queue-cos5
    bandwidth percent 5
  class type queuing class-fcoe
    bandwidth percent 0
```
class-map type queuing queue-cos1
  match qos-group 2
class-map type queuing queue-cos2
  match qos-group 3
class-map type queuing queue-cos4
  match qos-group 4
class-map type queuing queue-cos5
  match qos-group 5

When I want to apply the service policy on an interface, I get an error stating "Bandwidth percent exceeds 100" :
```
N5624-A-1(config-if)# service-policy type queuing input TA-queuing
ERROR: Bandwidth percent exceeds 100
```
But the bandwidth parameters do not add up to 100, they only add up to 50, so I don't understand why this error keeps happening?

A: In Cisco NX-OS, when you define a queuing policy-map (`policy-map type queuing`), the `class-default` is implicitly created and automatically assigned 50% of the interface bandwidth if not explicitly configured. Since your configured classes already sum to 50%, the implicit 50% for `class-default` pushes the total to 100%, which triggers the bandwidth reservation check failure in NX-OS. To resolve this, explicitly configure the `class-default` bandwidth to a lower value (e.g., 0% or 25%) to allow the policy to accept the remaining bandwidth.

Example fix:
```
policy-map type queuing TA-queuing
  class type queuing queue-cos1
    bandwidth percent 20
  class type queuing queue-cos2
    bandwidth percent 10
  class type queuing queue-cos4
    bandwidth percent 15
  class type queuing queue-cos5
    bandwidth percent 5
  class type queuing class-default
    bandwidth percent 25  ! Explicitly set to 25% to total 100%
```

Note: This behavior is consistent across NX-OS 5.x, 6.x, and 7.x. Ensure that ingress classification (e.g., `class-map type traffic-control`) properly marks packets with `qos-group` values before they reach the queuing policy. Also, verify that the interface has adequate physical bandwidth, as `percent` values scale relative to the configured interface bandwidth.

text:
Q: This is on a Nexus N5624Q.
I have this TA-queuing service policy :
policy-map type queuing TA-queuing
 class type queuing queue-cos1
  bandwidth percent 20
 class type queuing queue-cos2
  bandwidth percent 10
 class type queuing queue-cos4
  bandwidth percent 15
 class type queuing queue-cos5
  bandwidth percent 5
 class type queuing class-fcoe
  bandwidth percent 0
class-map type queuing queue-cos1
 match qos-group 2
class-map type queuing queue-cos2
 match qos-group 3
class-map type queuing queue-cos4
 match qos-group 4
class-map type queuing queue-cos5
 match qos-group 5
When I want to apply the service policy on an interface, I get an error stating "Bandwidth percent exceeds 100" :
N5624-A-1(config-if)# service-policy type queuing input TA-queuing
ERROR: Bandwidth percent exceeds 100
But the bandwidth parameters do not add up to 100, they only add up to 50, so I don't understand why this error keeps happening?

[Score:0] Note: On any interface only 75% of the bandwidth is available for QoS by default in NX-OS (reserved for system/control traffic).

**[ENRICHED CONTEXT & RESOLUTION]**
The "Bandwidth percent exceeds 100" error in NX-OS queuing policies is almost always caused by an **implicit `class-default`**. If you do not explicitly define a `class class-default` in your queuing policy-map, NX-OS automatically creates one and assigns it 100% of the available interface bandwidth. Since your defined classes already consume 50%, adding the implicit 100% triggers the validation error.

**Fix:** Explicitly define the default class with the remaining bandwidth (or 0%):
```
class class-default
 bandwidth percent 0
```
Alternatively, set it to `bandwidth percent 50` to consume the remaining available bandwidth.

**NX-OS QoS Operational Notes:**
- The 75% limit is controlled by `system bandwidth 75` (default). You can adjust this globally if your interface doesn't run control protocols, but 75% is the recommended baseline.
- `class-fcoe` with `bandwidth percent 0` is valid, but ensure FCoE traffic is explicitly matched via `match qos-group` or `match dscp` if you intend to queue it. Unmatched FCoE traffic will fall into `class-default`.
- Always verify queue allocation with `show policy-map interface <port-channel/ethX>` after application. Queuing policies in NX-OS are applied per-queue, not per-class, so ensure your queue IDs align with the class order if using explicit queue mapping.
- Command syntax is consistent across NX-OS 5.x, 7.x, and 8.x. If migrating to ACI or newer Nexus 9K with Cisco IOS-XE, queuing architectures shift to LLQ/DSCP-based policies with different syntax.

text:
The underlying queuing mechanism (e.g., CBWFQ, LLQ, WRED) is typically consistent across the switch ASIC, but egress QoS policies should differ based on traffic engineering goals:
- **Access Ports:** Handle untrusted user traffic. Egress queues are often simpler, prioritizing latency-sensitive traffic (VoIP/video) with strict priority and using weighted fair queuing for data. Ingress policing/shaping is usually applied here to establish trust boundaries and bound traffic before it enters the fabric.
- **Uplinks:** Act as aggregation points. Egress policies require stricter bandwidth reservation, traffic shaping to match uplink capacity, and potentially more queues or tuned WRED profiles to manage congestion and prevent tail drops.
- **Marking Clarification:** The original claim about "swapping source and destination" is incorrect. QoS marking (DSCP/CoS) involves classifying packets and setting IP DSCP or 802.1p CoS bits, not modifying L3/L2 addresses. Marking is applied via policy-maps based on classification, not address manipulation.
- **Implementation:** Define separate policy-maps for access and uplink ports. Access policies focus on classification and ingress policing, while uplink policies focus on egress shaping, bandwidth guarantees, and congestion avoidance.

It defines the operational scope for managing and optimizing network traffic prioritization and bandwidth allocation across these specific interface types.
