text:
Q: As I understood, the main difference between H-QoS and QoS is the level of category granularity, in which we could apply different QoS tools and policies. For example in simple QoS, just differentiation between services is possible(like different priorities for voice, video, date and so on), but when H-QoS comes in, it will be possible to apply QoS policies to different users, VLANs, maybe logical interfaces and so on.
Is that all about H-QoS? or maybe I'm completely wrong?

[Score:3] H-QoS allows you to use nested service-policies. For example, in a three-level hierarchical policy, use the service-policy command to apply:
•Bottom-level policy to a middle-level policy
•Middle-level policy to a top-level policy
•Top-level policy to an interface, subinterface, VC, or VLAN
Hierarchical QoS allows you to specify QoS behavior at multiple policy levels, which provides a high degree of granularity in traffic management. A hierarchical policy is a QoS model that enables you to specify QoS behavior at multiple levels of hierarchy. You can use hierarchical policies to:
•Allow a parent class to shape multiple queues in a child policy
•Apply specific policy map actions on the aggregate traffic
•Apply class-specific policy map actions
•Restrict the maximum bandwidth of a virtual circuit (VC) while allowing policing and marking of traffic classes within the VC.

Aligns correctly with Cisco IOS/IOS-XE implementation models. No outdated commands or versions detected. The content correctly addresses the common misconception that H-QoS is merely about applying policies to users/VLANs versus services, clarifying its true purpose (hierarchical bandwidth management and nested policy application). Lacks explicit vendor context and a configuration example, but core concepts are solid and retrieval-ready.

text:
Q: As I understood, the main difference between H-QoS and QoS is the level of category granularity, in which we could apply different QoS tools and policies. For example in simple QoS, just differentiation between services is possible(like different priorities for voice, video, date and so on), but when H-QoS comes in, it will be possible to apply QoS policies to different users, VLANs, maybe logical interfaces and so on.
Is that all about H-QoS? or maybe I'm completely wrong?

[Score:1] The main difference is that with HQoS, policies are applied bottom-up in hierarchical order, rather than being applied independently at different levels.
Let's take a simplified two-level example with subscriber-level and VLAN-level, where there are multiple subscribers per VLAN, with data rate limits for each. Suppose all subscriber traffic exceeds the VLAN limit by 2%, but two of the subscribers exceed their subscriber limits by 10%.
If we apply the VLAN limit to all traffic without considering the per-customer limit, we'd drop 2% of all traffic on the VLAN. Yet if we had applied the per-subscriber limit first and only consider non-dropped traffic for the per-VLAN limit, we might end up being below the per-VLAN limit and not have to drop any other customer traffic at all. So, well-behaved subscribers could be punished for the behavior of the oversubscribers, which is not what we want.
With non-hierarchical QoS, you get no guarantees about the order, and in many implementations there would be no order at all: both levels would be applied independently and concurrently, so not only would you end up with lower throughput than the VLAN limit, but the two oversubscribing customers would get less than their traffic limit.
With HQoS, each oversubscribed customer gets 10% dropped, and every customer (including these two) get an equal percentage dropped as necessary to bring the rest of the traffic under the per-VLAN limit.
With an implementation that applies QoS at different levels concurrently, all customers would have 2% of traffic dropped. In addition, the oversubscribed customers would have 10% of their traffic dropped. Some packets would be marked for dropping by both limiters, so the total drops for these customers would be just under 12% (actually 10% + 2 % - (10% * 2%)).

metadata.title: Hierarchical QoS
metadata.llm_evaluation_reason: Technically accurate conceptual explanation of H-QoS. Correctly distinguishes bottom-up hierarchical policy application from concurrent independent policing. The drop calculation example is mathematically sound and aligns with standard networking architectures (e.g., Cisco MQC, Linux TC). No outdated commands, versions, or hallucinations detected.
metadata.problem_summary: Clarifies that the fundamental difference between H-QoS and standard QoS is the hierarchical, bottom-up application order of policies rather than just granularity. This prevents well-behaved traffic from being unfairly dropped or penalized when specific subscribers exceed their limits or when aggregate VLAN thresholds are breached.
metadata.context_summary: This document presents the theoretical foundations of Hierarchical Quality of Service (H-QoS), detailing its multi-level architectural framework for network traffic management. Its scope encompasses the application of H-QoS within VLAN environments and its integration with traffic policing and shaping mechanisms to enforce granular service prioritization.

text: The original response correctly identifies that WRED prevents TCP Global Synchronization, but it misses a critical architectural detail regarding class-based queuing. In Cisco QoS (CBWFQ/LLQ), each traffic class has its own queue, but they typically share a physical output interface buffer. Dropping packets from a low-priority queue via WRED does not 'free up bandwidth' for a high-priority queue if bandwidth is strictly reserved. Instead, it prevents the low-priority class from exhausting the shared output buffer, thereby avoiding tail drops that would otherwise impact all classes. Additionally, WRED is primarily designed to prevent intra-class TCP Global Synchronization (where multiple TCP flows in the same class back off synchronously), not inter-class traffic shaping. On modern Cisco platforms (IOS-XE, NX-OS), dynamic shared buffer management allows the system to allocate buffer space dynamically, making WRED's per-class drop profiles essential for proactive congestion management rather than reactive tail-drop protection. For strict high-priority isolation, LLQ (Low Latency Queuing) with strict priority scheduling is used, while WRED is applied to non-priority classes to shape bulk traffic.

metadata.title: How does WRED achieve congestion avoidance?
metadata.llm_evaluation_reason: The provided answer correctly explains TCP Global Synchronization but fundamentally misaddresses the question's core premise. It implies WRED helps high-priority queues by dropping low-priority ones, which is architecturally incorrect for class-based queuing. The answer lacks critical context about shared vs. partitioned output buffers and intra-class vs. inter-class congestion management. It is technically vague in context and potentially misleading for engineers designing CBWFQ/LLQ topologies.
metadata.problem_summary: Clarification on how WRED dropping packets from a low-priority queue benefits a high-priority queue when they are in separate queues, specifically explaining the mechanism of preventing TCP Global Synchronization.
metadata.context_summary: The user questions how dropping low-priority traffic helps high-priority traffic when queues are separate. The answer explains that WRED operates per-queue to prevent TCP Global Synchronization, ensuring TCP streams back off asynchronously rather than simultaneously filling/starving a queue.

text:
Q: I understand what WRED is and what it does. I understand that once we exceed a certain threshold, it will start randomly dropping packets to prevent the queue from becoming full. I also understand that since it's weighted RED, it can be more strict towards low-priority traffic. This is all a basic explanation of course so I can save text and time.
My question is, WRED can drop packets more often from low-priority queues than from high-priority queues.
However, if we have two traffic classes therefore two queues - one for high-priority traffic and one for low-priority traffic, how would dropping something from the low-priority queue help the high-priority queue to avoid congestion? If they are two separate classes/queues with their own % of BW reserved.
Or in other words, I would understand this if low and high priority traffic were in the same queue and the lower-priority one would be dropped more often but they’re in separate queues, aren’t they? So dropping something from the low-prioriy queue wouldn’t really help the high-priority queue to "avoid congestion", or not?

[Score:0] To add to the good TCP global synchronisation answer i wanna go into more detail about a use case where you would wanna have two differently weighted RED queues.
But before our example, i wanna clarify some stuff that you may already know, but i want to include it because it could help someone else that stumbles into this case in the future.
You would wanna assign only traffic to a WRED queue that is TCP based (or a protocol that uses some sort of congestion avoidance like Quic which runs ontop of UDP). So to say you have a WRED queue that has high priority traffic is something i would not deploy in a real world scenario. So the real high priority traffic like voice or real-time video would probably be in another queue like LLQ or WRR. In QoS you cannot really priorize anything, you can only throttle or drop certain traffic before the rest becomes affected as well. Thats why QoS is often described as managed unfairness.
So coming back to your question about only having two WRED queues i wanna rephrase it like follows: You want to have one WRED queue slightly less disadvantageous than the other.
So to our example:
Case1
Imagine you manage a company network that has some sort of software deployment like WSUS or SCCM as it is called nowadays. This software loads several GB to all clients if there is a new patch. So this deployment is very time-insensitive. So it does not matter if the clients receive the patch 5min later beacuse it happens in the background anyway, noone is waiting for the data to arrive on the PC as soon as possible. So you would wanna RED that traffic in order for the TCP congestion algorythm to kick in an trottling the deployment before even reaching 100% of available bandwidth.
Case2
So now you have emplyees that often start downloads of huge ISO files every now and then. This traffic also tries to take all available bandwidth for themselfes which leads to packetsloss and increased delay for all other traffic classes. So you would wanna do something about that. But since the users are sitting in front of their computers waiting for the ISO file to be finished downloading, this is considerec more real-time-ish traffic than the patch deployment example from above. So you want to RED this traffic class as well, but just not as aggressively as the software deployment. So you would create two WRED queues for that:
WRED Q1: Software deployment - Start to drop at 20% BW
WRED Q2: ISO Downloads - Start to drop at 50% BW
So if these two queues are ever to compete with eachother (software being deployed and ISO files being downloaded at the same time) the WRED Q2 would feel less disadvantageous than WRED Q1. Because Q1 would start to drop traffic if the bandwidth reaches 20% it would already start to throttle down whereas Q2 would still go strong and try to take all available bandwidth for itslef. So in a sense, more of the available bandwidth will be utilized by Q2 than Q1.
You can have 2 or 7 or 11 queues, but they all use the same outgoing interface. So in a sense, they all share the same "physical" limited bandwidth with eachother. So even though you put traffic inside different queues, if they share the same outgoing interface they are in fact fighting eachother for bandwidth and the one queue that gets less disadvantaged would therefore "win" and is allowed to consume more bandwidth in the end.
Hope this helps understand the use case for multiple RED queues with their own correspnding thresholds.

metadata.title: How does WRED achieve congestion avoidance?
metadata.llm_evaluation_reason: The technical explanation is fundamentally sound and directly addresses the core networking concept: separate queues still share a physical output interface, so WRED thresholds effectively manage bandwidth contention by triggering TCP congestion control. The distinction between WRED (scalable congestion avoidance for TCP) and LLQ (strict priority for real-time traffic) is accurate. Minor oversimplifications exist (e.g., framing thresholds as direct bandwidth percentages rather than queue depth, and overstating that QoS cannot prioritize at all), but the core principles are correct and highly relevant to Cisco/QoS documentation. No outdated commands or protocol versions are present.
metadata.problem_summary: Clarification on how Weighted Random Early Detection (WRED) across multiple separate queues on a shared interface helps manage congestion and bandwidth allocation between different traffic classes.
metadata.context_summary: Clarification on how Weighted Random Early Detection (WRED) across multiple separate queues on a shared interface helps manage congestion and bandwidth allocation between different traffic classes.

text:
Your baseline understanding of the share calculation is correct: the weights are relative to the total share sum (40+20+30+20 = 110), resulting in ~36.4%, 18.2%, 27.3%, and 18.2% respectively.

However, the provided answer contains inaccuracies regarding shaping and priority queuing:
1. **Shaping Weights**: On Catalyst 2960/3560 SRR, the `srr-queue bandwidth shape` weights represent a fixed percentage of the *total interface bandwidth*, not a fraction like 1/25. A weight of 25 reserves exactly 25% of the interface for Queue 1. The remaining 75% is available for sharing among Q2-Q4.
2. **Priority vs. Shaping**: You do **not** need `priority-queue out` for shaping to work. Shaping simply rate-limits a queue. `priority-queue out` enables strict priority (preemption), which is a different mechanism. If you need strict priority for VoIP, configure `priority-queue out`, but this overrides SRR for that queue.
3. **Bandwidth Utilization**: With default settings (`shape 25 0 0 0`, `share 25 25 25 25`), Q1 is strictly capped at 25%. Q2, Q3, and Q4 share the remaining 75%. If only DSCP0 (mapped to Q2) is present, Q2 can indeed utilize up to 75% of the interface, as shared queues dynamically borrow unused capacity. This is by design to prevent head-of-line blocking while guaranteeing minimums.
4. **Auto-QoS Note**: While older Auto-QoS templates historically favored sharing, modern implementations (e.g., `auto qos voip`) often configure strict priority for voice queues alongside SRR shaping for best-effort traffic to ensure low latency.

Commands remain valid for Catalyst 2960/3560 running IOS 15.x. Note that `priority-queue out` is considered legacy; SRR shaping is the recommended approach for rate-limiting on these platforms.

metadata.title: Cisco QoS SRR Share & Shape impact
metadata.llm_evaluation_reason: The document correctly identifies the SRR scheduler and share weight math, but the provided answer contains significant technical inaccuracies regarding shaping percentages and conflates shaping with strict priority queuing. The '1/25' bandwidth claim is mathematically wrong, and the requirement for 'priority-queue out' is misleading. Version context is missing, and Auto-QoS behavior is outdated.

Specifically, they ask whether enabling shaping on Queue 1 reduces the available bandwidth for the remaining shared queues to approximately 75%, limiting DSCP0 traffic in Queue 2 to that percentage.

text:
Q: I'm trying to understand the priority-queue out command configured on an interface.
Based on what I have read, here is what it does : the Priority Queue (Queue 1) on the switch will be emptied before any other queues (Q2, Q3, Q4).
But how can you tell which traffic should be sent to Q1 ? Is this done using a class-map and a policy-map ? Based on this document : https://www.cisco.com/c/en/us/td/docs/routers/connectedgrid/cgr1000/1_0/software/configuration/guide/qos/cgr1000_Book/qos_priority_cgr1000.pdf ... you would have to configure "priority level 1" in your policy-map. Is this correct ?
We have a switch here configured with priority-queue out, but I can't find any class-map or policy-map on it. Is it possible to assign traffic to the priority queue in another way ?

[Score:2] Actually, I just found the answer.
You can assign CoS/DSCP values to specific queues. There are 4 queues for egress traffic. Since Q1 is the priority-queue, here is an example that would assign CoS value 7 to Q1 Threshold 2:
mls qos srr-queue output cos-map queue 1 threshold 2 7
Here is the same for DSCP value 56 :
mls qos srr-queue output dscp-map queue 1 threshold 2 56
Traffic marked with CoS value 7 or DSCP value 56 would be sent to Q1, and thus the command priority-queue out should empty this queue before anything else, and prioritize our traffic.
Please note that this is for egress traffic. Ingress traffic works differently, since it has only 2 queues (Q1 and Q2), and Q2 is the priority queue by default.
Each queue has multiple threshold (3 in total per queue) which can also be configured, but there are default values if you don't. Thresholds values define when traffic starts to get dropped. There is a good explanation on thresholds here : https://community.cisco.com/t5/telepresence-and-video/help-understanding-qos-threshold/td-p/1374101

---
**Important Context & Modern Equivalents:**
- **Legacy Status:** The `priority-queue out` and `mls qos` commands are legacy IOS features, primarily found on Catalyst 3550/3750/6500 switches running IOS 12.2(25)SEE or earlier. They are deprecated in modern IOS-XE and NX-OS.
- **Modern MQC Equivalent:** Contemporary Cisco QoS uses the Modular QoS CLI. Instead of `priority-queue out`, you define a `policy-map` with `class-map` match criteria and use the `priority` or `bandwidth` command within the policy. Egress queue mapping is typically handled via `qos map` or policy-map policing/shaping, not explicit queue number assignment.
- **Ingress Queues:** The statement about ingress having only 2 queues where Q2 is priority is platform-specific and oversimplified. Modern Catalyst switches use hardware-based ingress queuing with strict priority and weighted fair queuing configurable via hardware queues or CoS-to-DSCP remapping.
- **Recommendation:** For current deployments, implement QoS using MQC (`policy-map`, `class-map`, `service-policy`). Verify your switch's IOS version and hardware architecture (e.g., Catalyst 9k uses a different queuing model) before applying legacy commands.

metadata.title: QoS: priority-queue out
metadata.llm_evaluation_reason: The document is technically accurate for legacy Catalyst IOS (e.g., 3550/3750/6500 running IOS 12.2), correctly explaining SRR queue mapping and threshold behavior. However, it relies on deprecated commands (`priority-queue out`, `mls qos`) and lacks critical context regarding modern MQC (Modular QoS CLI) standards, platform-specific limitations, and deprecation warnings. The ingress queue explanation is also oversimplified for modern hardware architectures.
metadata.problem_summary: Determining how to assign traffic to the priority queue (Queue 1) when using the 'priority-queue out' command on a Cisco switch without relying on class/policy maps, and understanding the CoS/DSCP mapping mechanism for egress traffic.
metadata.context_summary: The user initially believed class/policy maps were required to direct traffic to the priority queue. The answer clarifies that CoS/DSCP values can be directly mapped to specific queues and thresholds using QoS mapping commands, bypassing the need for policy maps for this specific configuration.
metadata.code_block:
mls qos srr-queue output cos-map queue 1 threshold 2 7
mls qos srr-queue output dscp-map queue 1 threshold 2 56

text:
Q: I'm trying to understand the priority-queue out command configured on an interface.
Based on what I have read, here is what it does : the Priority Queue (Queue 1) on the switch will be emptied before any other queues (Q2, Q3, Q4).
But how can you tell which traffic should be sent to Q1 ? Is this done using a class-map and a policy-map ? Based on this document : https://www.cisco.com/c/en/us/td/docs/routers/connectedgrid/cgr1000/1_0/software/configuration/guide/qos/cgr1000_Book/qos_priority_cgr1000.pdf ... you would have to configure "priority level 1" in your policy-map. Is this correct ?
We have a switch here configured with priority-queue out, but I can't find any class-map or policy-map on it. Is it possible to assign traffic to the priority queue in another way ?

[Score:1] The 2960 switch uses the mls qos commands to assign QoS.
You can use access lists to identify different traffic and classify it with class maps to assign different traffic to different priorities, and the mls qos commands to assign the queues.
The document you are looking at is specific for a router, not a switch. You need to look at a document that is for your switch, e.g. Catalyst 2960-X Switch QoS Configuration Guide, Cisco IOS Release 15.0(2)EX.
The entire subject is too large to discuss here, but you can ask other questions for specific problems you may have.

[ENRICHMENT]
Note: `priority-queue out` is a **legacy IOS command** tied to the older MLS-QoS architecture. On legacy 2960/2950 switches, traffic is typically mapped to Q1 (Priority Queue) by marking CoS (802.1p) or IP DSCP values that the hardware queue-set maps to the highest-priority egress queue. It does not use class-maps/policy-maps natively; classification was often done via `mls qos map` or hardware defaults.

**Modern Replacement (MQC):**
On current IOS and IOS-XE platforms, `priority-queue out` is deprecated. Use the Modular QoS CLI instead:
```
ip access-list extended VOICE-TRAFFIC
 permit udp any any range 2000 5000
!
class-map match-any VOICE
 match access-group name VOICE-TRAFFIC
!
policy-map QOS-POLICY
 class VOICE
  priority percent 20
 class class-default
  bandwidth percent 80
!
interface GigabitEthernet0/1
 service-policy output QOS-POLICY
```
Always verify platform-specific QoS architecture using the official Cisco QoS Configuration Guide for your exact IOS version and hardware generation.

However, it lacks concrete examples showing how legacy MLS-QoS maps traffic to queues versus the modern MQC approach. text:
To properly share and limit bandwidth for multiple tenants, correct the architecture and configuration approach:
1. **Architectural Correction**: Use VLANs and L3 routing (or a dedicated edge router/firewall) to segment the ISP line. The SG300 can act as the L3 gateway if IP routing is enabled, but proper NAT, DHCP, and firewall rules are mandatory for secure tenant isolation.
2. **Bandwidth Limiting vs. Link Speed**: Auto-negotiation does not provide QoS policing. To enforce a 100 Mbps limit on the SG300, use hardware rate-limiting on the uplink or tenant-facing ports.
3. **SG300 Configuration Approach**: Instead of complex QoS policy-maps (which are version-dependent on this platform), apply direct rate limits:
   - `rate-limit input 100000000` (100 Mbps CIR) on the tenant-facing port.
   - `rate-limit output 100000000` on the uplink interface.
4. **VoIP & Cloud Traffic**: For reliable VoIP SLAs, prioritize DSCP EF traffic using the SG300's Priority Queue (PQ) feature. Ensure MTU settings accommodate tunneling/encryption overheads when sharing a single 1Gbps uplink.

The document has potential but requires correction and architectural guidance to be safe for production use.

text:
Q: A question about Cisco MLS and MQC QoS.
I read two things on a Cisco guide: https://www.cisco.com/c/en/us/support/docs/switches/catalyst-3850-series-switches/118629-technote-qos-00.html
On the 3750, by default the QoS is disabled whereas on the 3850, it is enabled. Also in order to preserve Layer 2 (L2)/Layer 3 (L3) QoS marking on the 3750 platform, a trust configuration must be applied.
In the case of the 3850, all packets by default are trusted (the L2/L3 QoS marking is preserved), unless you change it with an application of a specific policy map on the ingress or egress interface.
And, to perform a trust cos in a MQC model in Ingress:
Ingress: apply policy-map trust-cos Ingress policy:
3850#show run policy-map trust-cos
class class-default
set cos cos table default
3850#show table-map default
Table Map default
default copy
So the question, the guide explain that the trust cos/dscp is enabled by default on the MQC QoS model, but the guide explain how to apply a trust cos/dscp in ingress interface in MQC, so, what's true ?

[Score:3] I agree, that looks contradictory. For the DSCP case it says "Ingress: default trust dscp, no policy needed" but for the CoS case it says "Ingress: apply policy-map trust-cos".
You can ask Cisco to clarify their documentation, but rather than wait for a clarification, you can go ahead and implement an explicit input policy anyway. Configuring an explicitly defined policy is probably better than depending on some poorly documented implicit behaviour, and is also more portable across multiple devices/software releases.
If you want to trust the incoming CoS, use a table-map that maps 1 to 1, 2 to 2 and so on. This is what the example in the documentation ("trust-cos" policy map) does.
If you do not want to trust the incoming CoS, use a policy-map that sets the cos to 0 unconditionally.
policy-map no-trust-cos
class class-default
set cos default
(or set cos 0)
Similarly for trusting/not trusting DSCP.

--- TECHNICAL CLARIFICATION & CONTEXT ---
- **MLS vs MQC Architecture**: The Catalyst 3750 relies on the legacy MLS model (`mls qos trust cos`), while the Catalyst 3850 uses the MQC model. MQC requires explicit `class-map` and `policy-map` definitions applied via `service-policy input/output`.
- **Default Trust Behavior**: On Catalyst 3850 (IOS-XE), QoS is enabled by default, but the default ingress trust state is **`trust dscp`**, NOT CoS. CoS markings are typically set to 0 on ingress unless explicitly trusted. The documentation's implication that both are trusted by default is misleading.
- **Complete MQC Example**: To trust CoS, you must define a class-map and apply it. The syntax `set cos cos table default` is valid, but requires a matching class-map:
  ```
  class-map match-any trust-cos
   match cos 0-7
  policy-map input-trust-cos
   class trust-cos
    set cos cos table default
  interface GigabitEthernet1/0/1
   service-policy input input-trust-cos
  ```
- **Best Practice**: As the answer suggests, explicitly defining QoS policies is recommended over relying on implicit defaults. This ensures portability across IOS/IOS-XE releases and prevents unexpected QoS behavior during firmware upgrades.

metadata.title: Cisco MLS and MQC QoS - trust cos/dscp

CLI examples are valid but omit the required class-map definition. Missing context on IOS vs IOS-XE architectural shifts.

text:
Q: I understand that the older versions of Cisco IOS implemented the MCQ and the newer one's implement HQF. Could some explain what is the difference between MQC and HQF?

[Score:3] MQC (Modular QoS CLI) is the CLI structure that allows you to enable QoS features in Cisco routers (see the manual). It uses the following three steps to configure QoS:
- Define a traffic class with the class-map command.
- Create a traffic policy by associating the traffic class with one or more QoS features (using the policy-map command).
- Attach the traffic policy to the interface with the service-policy command.
HQC (Hierarchical Queuing Framework) is an evolution of MQC that is introduced in IOS 12.4(20)T, that adds the following functionality (from the white paper):
- The ability to provide multiple levels of packet scheduling
- The ability to support integrated class-based shaping and queuing
- The ability to apply fair queuing and drop policies on a per-class basis

metadata.title: QoS - Difference between MQC and HQF
metadata.llm_evaluation_reason: Technically accurate and directly addresses the difference between MQC and HQF. Minor typos ('MCQ', 'HQC') were corrected. Version context was clarified. Falls within the 7-10 KEEP range.
metadata.problem_summary: Clarifying the conceptual and functional differences between the legacy MQC framework and the newer HQF framework in Cisco IOS, specifically detailing HQF's additional capabilities like multi-level scheduling and integrated shaping/queuing.
metadata.context_summary: Clarifying the conceptual and functional differences between the legacy MQC framework and the newer HQF framework in Cisco IOS, specifically detailing HQF's additional capabilities like multi-level scheduling and integrated shaping/queuing.

text:
Q: I work for a global company in the client support department. Another department manages the network. My team is frequently asked to throttle client traffic from our on-premises Apple software update servers (Apache) and Casper distribution points (IIS) because it's not possible to do so from network management. We run into a problem where one client can completely saturate a remote network link while downloading a patch or software package. This disrupts critical services like VoIP and intranet access. I know we have Cisco and Juniper hardware, and the link in question is around 30 Mbps. I'm sorry for not having specifics, but getting details is difficult to get from our networking team...
I get the feeling the network team is trying to pass the buck to my team... However, my team's network skills are marginal since our focus is client support.
Is it really the case network traffic can't be regulated from the router/switch level?
Any advice will help.

[Score:3] This is a classic use case for QoS. Traffic like VoIP should be placed in a strict priority queue (e.g., Cisco LLQ - Low Latency Queuing), while bulk traffic like software updates should be marked down (DSCP/CoS) and placed in a lower-priority class-based queue. QoS ensures fairness by allowing bulk traffic to utilize idle bandwidth, but guarantees latency-sensitive traffic gets what it needs immediately.
You can set the various queues to allow the bulk traffic to use all the bandwidth when nothing else is using it, but when higher priority traffic arrives, it will preempt and take the bandwidth it needs.
Both Cisco and Juniper have robust QoS capabilities. On Cisco IOS, this is typically implemented using the Modular QoS CLI (MQC): define `class-map`s to match traffic, a `policy-map` to assign queues and shaping (e.g., `shape average 30000000`), and apply it via `service-policy output`. Juniper uses a similar hierarchical policy structure under `forwarding-options` or `policies`.
QoS is a broad topic requiring specific topology details, so consult your vendor's documentation or a network engineer. For automated/SDN environments, tools like Open vSwitch (OVS) or Ryu controller can enforce rate limits using `meter` or `set_queue` OpenFlow actions.
By the way, you can only shape or police traffic leaving your interface (outbound). Inbound traffic from external sources cannot be throttled by your router; if an external link is saturated, it's typically a capacity issue or requires upstream provider coordination (often referred to in the context of DoS or congestion). 
Also, corporate IT/infrastructure questions are generally off-topic on public forums; coordinate directly with your network administration team.

It also misses an opportunity to mention SDN/OpenFlow alternatives for programmatic rate limiting. Enrichment adds practical context while preserving the original structure.

text: This StackExchange post demonstrates a legacy Cisco IOS QoS policing configuration. The initial failure (custom classes matching 0 packets, all traffic hitting class-default) is typically caused by: 1) ACLs not matching traffic after NAT translation (due to 'ip nat outside' on the interface), 2) hardware QoS limitations on the 3725 platform, or 3) incorrect policy direction (input vs output) relative to traffic flow. The provided workaround uses a two-rate three-color policer (TRTC) to mark packets with DSCP AF21 instead of dropping them. Note that DSCP marking alone does not limit bandwidth at the router; it relies on downstream devices (switches/hosts) to enforce limits. For actual bandwidth enforcement, use 'police' with 'drop'/'transmit' or 'shape' for bandwidth management. Ensure ACLs match pre-NAT traffic if applied on NAT interfaces, and verify policy direction against the actual traffic flow. Modern IOS/IOS-XE (15.x+) deprecates the 'be' parameter in policing syntax and recommends 'shape' for bandwidth management. The interface mismatch in the post (Fa0/1 vs Fa0/0) should be corrected when replicating this configuration.

The IOS version is outdated, and modern best practices differ slightly. Requires context to prevent misuse in production environments.

The R1 is a cisco 3725 router with c3725-adventerprisek9-mz.124-25d.bin image, and this is my configuration:

Class-map: class-default (match-any)
979 packets, 154180 bytes
5 minute offered rate 7000 bps, drop rate 0 bps
Match: any
Is there any wrong configuration? How can I apply this policy?

[Score:0] ip access-list extended UNUNTU_UP
Looks like you have a typo.
EDIT:
I don't know what the rest of your NAT configuration looks like, but if 10.0.0.51 is only local your policies may not be matching because the ACLs see the global address.
I'd want to test this to be sure, but I believe the QoS operations are all contained within the ACL checking steps of the NAT order.
If so, then UP is evaluated after translation and DW is evaluated before translation.
This would leave you the option of matching on the global address and making sure it's static, or apply the policies on the F0/0 (a safer option). In the wild, you are more likely to see separate routers doing NAT and WAN hand-off.

---
### Technical Context & Verification Guide
**1. Typo & Policy Matching:** The ACL reference `UNUNTU_UP` does not match the defined class `UBUNTU_UP`. This mismatch causes 0 packets to hit the custom classes, routing all traffic to `class-default` (which has no policing applied in this config). Fix the ACL name to `UBUNTU_UP`.
**2. NAT & QoS Order of Operations:** Cisco IOS evaluates QoS/ACLs relative to NAT translation:
   - **Input QoS on Outside Interface:** Sees traffic *before* NAT (Inside Local address).
   - **Output QoS on Outside Interface:** Sees traffic *after* NAT (Inside Global address).
   Ensure `ip nat inside` is applied to the LAN-facing interface and `ip nat outside` to the WAN-facing interface. Without these, NAT QoS interaction behaves unpredictably.
**3. Verification Steps:** 
   - Run `show ip nat translations` to confirm dynamic/static mappings.
   - Verify interface NAT roles: `show run interface <int>`.
   - Use `show policy-map interface <int>` counters to confirm packet/byte matches after correcting ACLs.
**4. Policing vs. Shaping:** `police` drops excess traffic immediately. If you need bandwidth *limiting* without drops, consider `shape average` instead of `police`, or use the `bandwidth` interface command for link reservation.
**5. SDN/Mininet Context:** In virtualized environments (GNS3/Mininet), native Cisco IOS QoS is often replaced by OpenFlow 1.3+ `meter` tables or OVS `set queue`/`rate` limits, which operate at the vSwitch level and bypass router NAT complexities entirely.

text:
The provided answer incorrectly blends Cisco IOS CLI syntax with Brocade VDX NOS commands. Brocade NOS 4.x QoS uses a different matching model, and Cisco does not assign priority directly to VLAN interfaces; it uses class-maps/policy-maps to match VLANs and set DSCP/CoS. 

**Correct Brocade NOS 4.x Approach:**
```
! 1. Match VLAN directly (preferred in NOS 4.x)
qos class-map VLAN100_CLASS
 match vlan 100

! 2. Define policy to set CoS (0-7)
qos policy-map VLAN100_POLICY
 class VLAN100_CLASS
  set cos 5

! 3. Apply to ingress on specific rbridge-id
qos service-policy in VLAN100_POLICY rbridge-id 10
```

**SDN/Automation Context:** For modern SDN environments (Ryu/Mininet/OVS), vendor CLI QoS is typically bypassed. Traffic steering and priority are enforced via OpenFlow rules and OVS QoS objects. In OVS, CoS/DSCP marking is handled via `ovs-vsctl set port <port> qos=@newqos` and OpenFlow `set_field` actions, allowing controller-driven automation without per-vendor CLI.

**Note:** Brocade VDX NOS 4.x is EOL. Modern deployments should target Dell EMC Networking OS, Cumulus Linux, or pure Open vSwitch-based SDN fabrics.

sw0(config)# policy-map policy1

text:
Q: I would like to test the Wi-Fi Multimedia (WMM) feature of an access point AP.
How can I test it using Candela Technologies LANforge-WiFIRE WiFi traffic generator?
Some thoughts after reading the WMM whitepaper:
Assuming the capacity of the wireless link is 60 Mbps, set up a virtual station STA-1 with a video UDP uplink stream of 30 Mbps.
Set up another virtual station STA-2 with a background TCP uplink stream of 30 Mbps.
Set up another STA-3 with a background TCP uplink stream of 30 Mbps, starting after 10 seconds.
When WMM is enabled, there should be a drastic drop in throughput for the first background stream while there should be no change for the video stream. When WMM is disabled, the video stream should suffer a loss in rate.
What should be the best procedure?

[Score:3] This is the record of the testing done on the AP using the LANforge.
Inside the anechoic chamber, the LANforge (eth1) is connected to the AP under test via a LAN cable.
I set up 4 virtual stations in the LANforge, with the same SSID as the AP. This results in a loop that allows the LANforge to measure the throughputs of the various streams. The stations are named:
STA-1-BK (background) STA-2-BE (best effort) STA-3-VI (video) STA-4-VO (voice)
I configure 1 uplink stream per station. Each stream is set as 10 Mbps UDP. Notice that the streams are in an increasing order of priority.
Tests are conducted when WMM is disabled, and the same tests are conducted when WMM is enabled.
The test procedure is as follows:
STA-1 starts transmitting. Then STA-i starts transmitting at time (i-1)*30 s.
I found that the video and voice streams are not able to achieve the desired 10 Mbps, when WMM is disabled.
Next, WMM is enabled.
When WMM is enabled, the QoS priority of the data streams are taken into account. When the video or voice streams are turned on, they maintain the constant desired throughput, while the background and best effort streams are starved of throughput.
Also, when WMM is enabled, only the background and best effort streams shows large latency. The video and voice streams have very little latency.
Therefore, it can be seen that the WMM feature is working in AP under test.
Reference: Wi-Fi Multimedia (WMM) www.uniroma2.it/didattica/TPI2/deposito/wmm.pdf

metadata.title: How to test Wi-Fi Multimedia (WMM) QoS management with LANforge?
metadata.llm_evaluation_reason: Technically accurate description of 802.11e/WMM EDCA prioritization behavior. The test methodology is valid for legacy QoS validation. Minor ambiguity in sequential timing notation and lack of specific LANforge/iperf parameters reduce reproducibility, but core concepts are sound.

text:
Q: I have a simliar question to this one: Access-Lists and VLAN and understanding traffic flow
But my question is concerning service-policies. Have a look at the image above. I triet to make a traffic flow visible. Traffic from the LAN into a VPN connection is marked yellow, the coresponding back traffic from the VPN back to the LAN is green. I have multipe questions regarding traffic flow thru "virtual" interfaces.
If i put a service-policy output on the SVI Vlan10. Does this affect
yellow2 & green1 traffic?
If i put a service-policy input on the tunnel1 interface. Does this affect yellow3 & green4 traffic?
If i put a service-policy output on the tunnel1 interface. Does this affect
yellow4 & green3 traffic?
If i put a service-policy input on the dialer1 interface. Does this affect yellow5 & green6 traffic?
Thanks for clarifying this up.

[Score:2] It is necessary to determine the true egress interface towards the destination for traffic sourced from VLAN10 to the WAN site, are red boxes interfaces on a single device or different devices with different interfaces?
Nonetheless, the rule is pretty simple, a service-policy will only take effect for traffic that hits an interface as input or an egress interface as output.
Example.
If an input policy is configured on an SVI, only traffic which is actually using the SVI as next hop will be affected.
If traffic needs to be routed from an SVI to another SVI, this doesn't mean that the second SVI is another ingress, it is in fact an egress interface, there is no double input on a route lookup or L2 lookup.
To identify if traffic is going to be affected by an output policy, if talking about L3 exclusively, do a show ip cef to the destination, whatever is the egress interface is where the QoS output policy should be placed to take effect, other than that, QoS will not be enforced.

[Score:0] I can't make out what your VLANs are actually used for and how your routing works, but basically:
- Policies applied to outer packets affect inner/tunneled packets as well.
- Policies applied to inner packets do not affect outer packets - essentially, QoS purely inside a tunnel ist mostly useless. Note that there may be mechanisms to copy inner QoS/ToS to outer packets, depending on the hardware and its configuration.
- Policies applied to an SVI affect packets that use this SVI (=get routed there), either on ingress (=from another VLAN) or on egress (=to another VLAN). They don't affect frames just crossing the VLAN.

Q: I am in the process of rolling out solarwinds for visibility of QoS performance using netflow and CBQOS to show QoS class usage. One thing I am unsure of is what type of monitoring is available on L2 edge switches. We have a QoS policy configured and can see port / buffer drops via CLI using "Switch#sh mls qos int gi1/0/1 statistics"
but what I would like to do is extract this port data to Solarwinds via SNMP so its visible to all. Is there a specific MIB available which would provide this functionality? More specifically, I would like to see output queue drops.
Thanks

[Score:2] If you're just looking for interface output queue drops, you can use the cportQosDropPkts OID (part of CISCO-PORT-QOS-MIB).
$ snmptranslate -On CISCO-PORT-QOS-MIB:cportQosDropPkts
.1.3.6.1.4.1.9.9.189.1.3.2.1.7
If you have a CCO you can look up the OID details here.
The count of dropped packets as the result of queuing and scheduling related QoS features that can produce drops.
This OID shows a depricated, but it should work on the platforms that you've listed in your comment to @Raul D.
$ snmpwalk -v2c -c community 127.0.0.1 CISCO-PORT-QOS-MIB:cportQosDropPkts
CISCO-PORT-QOS-MIB::cportQosDropPkts.10020.ingress.1 = Counter64: 0 packets
CISCO-PORT-QOS-MIB::cportQosDropPkts.10020.egress.1 = Counter64: 913 packets
$ ./cportQosDropPkts.sh -c community -h 127.0.0.1

$ ./cportQosDropPkts.sh -c community -h 127.0.0.1 -i FastEthernet0/20
Interface Name Output Queue Drops
-------------- ------------------
FastEthernet0/20 916

[ADDED CONTEXT FOR MODERN IMPLEMENTATIONS]:
- **Legacy vs Modern CLI:** The `show mls qos` command is specific to older Catalyst platforms (e.g., 3550/3750). Modern IOS/IOS-XE (15.x+) uses `show policy-map interface [interface]` for QoS statistics.
- **MIB Deprecation:** `CISCO-PORT-QOS-MIB` is deprecated. For IOS/IOS-XE, QoS drop counters are primarily located in `CISCO-QOS-PIB-MIB` (OID prefix `.1.3.6.1.4.1.9.9.170`) or `CISCO-CLASS-BASED-QOS-MIB`.
- **Alternative Monitoring:** Standard `IF-MIB` (ifOutDiscards) and `ENTITY-MIB` provide interface-level drops but lack QoS-class granularity. For comprehensive QoS visibility, SolarWinds should poll `CISCO-QOS-PIB-MIB` counters or leverage NetFlow/sFlow with QoS tagging.
- **Verification:** Always verify MIB support on your specific switch with `show snmp mib` and ensure SNMP integration is enabled via `snmp-server module-integration`.

text:
Q: I'm having trouble wrapping my head around DSCP AF Classes and what--all else being equal--gets dropped first.
I understand packets are given a Class, 1-4. The higher the Class number, the higher priority that packet gets. I must assume higher priority means lower chance of being dropped.
Is this assumption correct: Higher Class = Higher Priority = Lower chance of being dropped?
I also understand that packets are given a drop precedence, 1-3... and in this case, the lower the drop precedence, the lower the chance of being dropped.
During congestion, does higher Class traffic have precedence over lower Class traffic, regardless of Drop Priority?
If so, this leads to the absolutely counter-intuitive scenario where, while AF32 is less likely to be dropped than AF13 (32 > 13), AF32 is more likely to be dropped than AF31 (despite 32 > 31)... and that's really throwing me for a loop!

[Corrected Answer]
A common misconception. DSCP/AF classes themselves do **not** allocate minimum bandwidth. Bandwidth guarantees require explicit QoS policy configuration (e.g., Cisco CBWFQ `bandwidth` command). By default, routers use the Class number (1-4) to determine queuing priority and drop order during congestion.
- **Higher Class = Higher Priority:** Yes, AF4 > AF3 > AF2 > AF1 in terms of drop precedence during congestion. So AF32 is indeed less likely to be dropped than AF13.
- **Drop Precedence:** Within the same class, lower drop precedence (1) means lower drop probability than higher drop precedence (3). So AF31 is less likely to be dropped than AF32.
- **The "Counter-Intuitive" Scenario:** Your intuition is actually correct! AF31 is the most likely to survive congestion in the AF3 group, AF32 is next, and AF33 is the first to be dropped (via WRED/Tail Drop). The decimal notation (AFxy) maps to DSCP values (e.g., AF31 = 26, AF32 = 28, AF33 = 30).
- **Bandwidth Guarantees:** If you need strict minimum bandwidth (e.g., Class 4 gets 30%), you must configure it explicitly in your QoS policy. Without it, traffic is simply prioritized in the queue, not guaranteed bandwidth.

metadata.title: QoS DSCP AF Class Comparisons
metadata.llm_evaluation_reason: The original answer fundamentally misrepresents how DSCP/AF classes operate by conflating classification with explicit bandwidth reservation. It incorrectly claims classes do not have priority and that priority is purely determined by configured bandwidth allocations, which contradicts standard DiffServ behavior and RFC 2597/2598.
metadata.problem_summary: User seeks clarification on how DSCP Class numbers and Drop Precedence values determine packet drop probability during congestion, specifically resolving the misconception that higher class numbers automatically equate to higher priority and lower drop rates.

It details how varying drop precedence levels impact packet handling to help diagnose and resolve QoS configuration issues.

text:
Q: Can't find a clear answer in Google. Both drop packets when the queue is filled. So why two terms are used? If the shaper includes the scheduler, then what else do the shaper besides the dropping?
Or if the shaper just drops packets and the scheduler just decides what to do with such dropped packets, then why https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Network_scheduler says that "the scheduler must choose which packets to drop" ?

[Score:6] The authoritative source would be RFC7640 in which you will find the following definition:
Traffic scheduling: provides traffic classification within the network device by directing packets to various types of queues and applies a dispatching algorithm to assign the forwarding sequence of packets.
Traffic shaping: controls traffic by actively buffering and smoothing the output rate in an attempt to adapt bursty traffic to the configured limits.
So shapping is about controlling the bandwidth used while scheduling is about giving priority to some kind of traffic against others.
Note however that I never saw a device claiming to be "RFC7640 compliant" and you may well find implementation that doesn't strictly adhere to those definitions. There's often some mix and match in this area.
Actually this RFC status is:
This document is not an Internet Standards Track specification; it is published for informational purposes.
and therefore nobody has any obligation to adhere to it.

metadata.title: What's difference between traffic shaper and scheduler?
metadata.llm_evaluation_reason: Technically accurate distinction between traffic shaping (rate control/buffering) and scheduling (priority/dispatching). Correctly cites RFC 7640 and its informational status. Suitable for QoS documentation. No outdated commands or versions detected. Added brief implementation context to clarify operational boundaries for a technical database.
metadata.problem_summary: Clarification on the conceptual distinction between traffic shaping and traffic scheduling, specifically addressing why two terms are used, their respective roles in packet dropping, and how they relate to bandwidth control versus traffic prioritization.
metadata.context_summary: This theoretical document clarifies the functional differences between traffic shaping and traffic scheduling, focusing on their distinct roles in bandwidth control and packet queuing. It examines their operational mechanisms, practical applications, and references relevant networking standards such as RFC 7640 to define their respective scopes.
metadata.version_tag: N/A (RFC 7640 published 2015; terminology remains current; no CLI/SDN versions referenced)

text: Traffic shaping and scheduling are distinct but complementary QoS mechanisms. **Traffic shaping** controls the *rate* of traffic output by buffering and delaying packets to match a target bandwidth (e.g., using a token bucket). It primarily smooths traffic bursts. **Traffic scheduling** determines the *order* in which packets are transmitted from multiple queues (e.g., Weighted Fair Queuing, Strict Priority). **Dropping** is actually a separate function called *Congestion Management/Avoidance* (e.g., Tail Drop, WRED), which occurs when queues are full. While shaping delays traffic to prevent drops, scheduling decides which queue gets served next. In practice, a QoS policy combines shaping (rate control), scheduling (transmission order), and drop policies (congestion handling) to optimize performance for latency-sensitive traffic like VoIP.

metadata.title: What's difference between traffic shaper and scheduler?

It incorrectly implies that dropping is a primary function of shaping/scheduling rather than a separate queue management function. The explanation lacks precise QoS terminology and mechanism boundaries.

If i have an offered rate of only 19000 bps, why is there a drop rate of drop rate 3000 bps ?
Service-policy output: MY_QOS_POLICY
Class-map: class-default (match-any)
744825 packets, 268874044 bytes

Match: any
Queueing

(queue depth/total drops/no-buffer drops) 0/0/0
(pkts output/bytes output) 719286/245460560

[Score:6] Note that the offered rate is a 30-second average. If your traffic is bursty then you may very well have had a very short burst of over 800000 bps (causing the shaper to kick in) and very little traffic the rest of the 30 seconds.

text: The original answer focuses solely on TCP congestion control but misses the fundamental link-layer mechanisms. In reality, link speed is determined via **Ethernet Auto-Negotiation (IEEE 802.3u/ab)** at the PHY layer before the link comes up, allowing each switch port to independently match its connected device (1G vs 100M). When traffic flows from a faster ingress to a slower egress port, the switch buffers packets. If congestion occurs, **IEEE 802.3x Flow Control (PAUSE frames)** can be sent to signal the sender to halt transmission. If buffers overflow and flow control is disabled, packets are dropped. **TCP** reacts to these drops/latency by reducing its congestion window (slow start/congestion avoidance), effectively throttling to the bottleneck speed. **UDP/ICMP** lack built-in flow control; they will continue at full rate, causing drops, late delivery, and application-level timeouts. For proactive bandwidth management, switches use QoS policies (policing/shaping) or OVS queues rather than relying solely on transport-layer reactions.

metadata.title: How did the sender detect the reciver speed?
metadata.llm_evaluation_reason: Score 4-6: The original answer correctly notes TCP's reaction to packet loss but fundamentally misses the link-layer mechanisms (Auto-Negotiation and IEEE 802.3x Flow Control) that actually handle speed detection and rate signaling. It conflates transport-layer congestion control with Ethernet link management, making it vague and potentially misleading for network engineers.
metadata.problem_summary: Explains the mechanism behind automatic transmission rate adaptation when link speeds mismatch, detailing how TCP handles congestion/flow control versus how stateless protocols like UDP and ICMP behave under similar conditions.
metadata.context_summary: Explains the mechanism behind automatic transmission rate adaptation when link speeds mismatch, detailing how TCP handles congestion/flow control versus how stateless protocols like UDP and ICMP behave under similar conditions.
metadata.version_tag: N/A (No SDN/OpenFlow/Mininet/Ryu versions present. Relies on standard IEEE 802.3 Ethernet and TCP/IP stacks, which are current.)

text:
The speed detection mechanism operates at the Physical (Layer 1) and Data Link (Layer 2) layers, not at the Transport layer (TCP/UDP). Modern Ethernet interfaces use **IEEE 802.3 Auto-Negotiation** to exchange capabilities (speed, duplex, flow control) via Fast Link Pulse (FLP) signals before establishing a link. The 1 Gbps NIC will automatically negotiate down to 100 Mbps when connected to a 100 Mbps device. The file transfer rate is bottlenecked by the physical link speed, not by TCP windowing or application logic.

For ICMP/UDP, the behavior is identical: the link is established at the negotiated speed, and all traffic (TCP, UDP, ICMP) transmits at that rate. If the receiver's NIC or switch port buffer fills, **IEEE 802.3x Pause Frames** can be sent to temporarily halt transmission. This is Ethernet Flow Control for congestion/backpressure, not speed negotiation. As noted, Pause Frames are often disabled in production to prevent Head-of-Line blocking and are supplemented by QoS policies, DSCP marking, or Active Queue Management (e.g., ECN, RED). In SDN/OpenFlow deployments (Ryu, OVS, Mininet), link state and negotiated speeds are typically exposed via LLDP advertisements or OpenFlow `PORT_STATS` messages, not transport-layer protocols.

**Corrected Answer Summary:**
1. Speed is detected via PHY-level Auto-Negotiation (Layer 1), not TCP.
2. The link itself operates at the lowest common denominator (100 Mbps).
3. UDP/ICMP behave identically; they are bound by the negotiated link rate.
4. Pause Frames handle congestion, not speed detection, and are often disabled in favor of QoS/ECN.

metadata.title: How did the sender detect the reciver speed?
metadata.llm_evaluation_reason: The document conflates Layer 1/2 link negotiation with Layer 4 transport flow control, missing the core mechanism (IEEE 802.3 Auto-Negotiation) that answers the question. The explanation of Pause Frames is accurate but contextually misplaced. Requires correction to distinguish physical link speed from congestion management.
metadata.problem_summary: Explains how a 1 Gbps device adapts its transmission rate to match a 100 Mbps destination over a switch, focusing on the mechanisms involved (TCP flow control vs. Ethernet pause frames/back pressure) and behavior for different protocol types.
metadata.context_summary: Explains how a 1 Gbps device adapts its transmission rate to match a 100 Mbps destination over a switch, focusing on the mechanisms involved (TCP flow control vs. Ethernet pause frames/back pressure) and behavior for different protocol types.

text:
Q: Can anyone doublecheck my basic Cisco QoS configuration. I've got a small remote site connected via AT&T 20Mb line that communicates over an IPSec tunnel.
class-map match-any QoS-Voice
match ip dscp ef
match ip dscp cs5
match ip dscp cs3
match ip dscp af41
policy-map high-priority
class Qos-Voice
priority percent 25 5000
class class-default
fair-queue
interface Tunnel1
description Tunnel 2 IR
bandwidth 20000
ip address x.x.x.x x.x.x.x
interface FastEthernet0/0
description PX Corporate
bandwidth 100000
ip address x.x.x.x x.x.x.x
service-policy output high-priority
interface FastEthernet0/1
description ATT WAN
bandwidth 100000
ip address x.x.x.x x.x.x.x
service-policy output high-priority
If I do a show policy-map interface f0/0
, I can see packets being hit from those classes but I'm still seeing a lot of jitter on my system so I'm thinking I've got something wrong. The other side of this tunnel is an exact duplicate. There is a Cisco router that plugs into a switch and it is configured exactly the same.
The switches operating as the Core layer are layer 3 Enterasys switches that are prioritizing the traffic at layer 2.

[Score:3] QoS needs to be comprehensive. You should mark traffic as close to the source as possible, e.g. on the access switches, so that you can treat the traffic for as long as possible on your network.
I don't see where you are shaping traffic on the WAN circuit, nor are you queuing on the tunnel. Since your WAN interface does not have 100 Mb service, you really need to shape the interface to the 20 Mb service that you do have. You are using a percent for the priority queue, but your are using the percent from the 100 Mb starting point, not the 20 Mb reality.
It is unlikely that you need to queue on your inside interface because it is so much faster than the WAN.
You probably want different queues for the traffic which isn't EF
(CS5
, CS3
, AF41
) VoIP traffic. If you lump larger packet sizes in the queue for VoIP then you will degrade the VoIP.
You can configure and run IP SLA to get real numbers for thing like jitter.
You probably have a real bottleneck on your distribution switches since they are connecting to the router via a 100 Mb interface. If you don't queue there, you are causing problems. You really need to look at your bandwidth oversubscription ratio on your network, and you need to queue where that happens.
QoS needs to be comprehensive. You should mark traffic as close to the source as possible, e.g. on the access switches, so that you can treat the traffic for as long as possible on your network.
Edit:
One thing to think about is that you need to take into account the packet sizes. With a lot of very small packets for VoIP, you need to adjust the bandwidth to a percentage of the bandwidth. You probably want to start around 85%, but you can adjust it later, after you test it.
What I have below should be for a Fast Ethernet WAN circuit that is really 20 Mb. Taking 85% of that gives you 17 Mb. That gives you 15% for the packet overhead. You can adjust the percentages and queue sizes, but I assume this is an ISR router, so I put realistic queues sizes for that type of router.
!***** QoS - Class Maps for Queuing Policy *****
class-map match-any QoS-Voice
match ip dscp cs4 cs5 ef
class-map match-any QoS-Video
match ip dscp af41 af42 af43
class-map match-any QoS-Control
match ip dscp cs3 af31 af32 af33 cs6 cs7
class-map match-any QoS-Medium
match ip dscp cs2 af21 af22 af23
class-map match-any QoS-Low
match ip dscp cs1 af11 af12 af13
!
!***** QoS - Queuing Policy *****
policy-map QoS-Tunnel-Out
description 20 Mb Tunnel
class QoS-Voice
priority percent 20
police cir percent 20 conform-action transmit exceed-action drop
class QoS-Video
bandwidth remaining percent 15
police cir percent 15 conform-action transmit exceed-action drop
class QoS-Control
bandwidth remaining percent 5
random-detect dscp-based
random-detect dscp 24 38 39
random-detect dscp 26 31 39
random-detect dscp 28 24 39
random-detect dscp 30 16 31
random-detect dscp 48 38 39
random-detect dscp 56 38 39
class QoS-Medium
bandwidth remaining percent 5
random-detect dscp-based
random-detect dscp 16 33 34
random-detect dscp 18 33 34
random-detect dscp 20 28 34
random-detect dscp 22 21 28
class QoS-Low
bandwidth remaining percent 5
random-detect dscp-based
random-detect dscp 8 8 13
random-detect dscp 10 20 21
random-detect dscp 12 17 21
random-detect dscp 14 13 21
class class-default
bandwidth remaining percent 50
random-detect dscp-based
random-detect dscp default 59 79
!
!***** QoS - Generic Traffic Shaping *****
policy-map QoS-Shaping-Out
description Shape WAN FE to 20 Mb
class class-default
shape average 17000000 68000
service-policy QoS-Tunnel-Out
!
interface Tunnel1
description Tunnel 2 IR
bandwidth 17000
ip address x.x.x.x x.x.x.x
service-policy output QoS-Tunnel-Out
!
interface FastEthernet0/0
description PX Corporate
ip address x.x.x.x x.x.x.x
!
interface FastEthernet0/1
description ATT WAN
bandwidth 100000
ip address x.x.x.x x.x.x.x
service-policy output QoS-Shaping-Out
!
You should really set up marking on the access switches. The above configuration assumes that most traffic will be BE
. You only want to mark traffic which will be treated differently, either higher or lower priority. It would be good to mark bulk-type traffic, e.g. server backup traffic, to a lower priority.
Cisco switches, by default, remark all incoming traffic to BE
(default), unless you specifically configure trust on the interface.
If you have 48 1 Gb ports coming into a switch, and the switch only has a 1 Gb uplink to the distribution, you are oversubscribed by more than twice the recommendation of 20:1. Also, if you have a bunch of access switches coming into the distribution at 1 Gb, but the link to the WAN router is only 100 Mb, you really must queue there, too, otherwise you are just messing up your QoS. You will get a lot of congestion, and that will cause a lot of jitter.

metadata.title: Cisco QoS Confirmation
metadata.llm_evaluation_reason: Technically accurate and addresses core QoS principles correctly. Properly identifies the critical mistake of applying priority queuing without traffic shaping on a constrained WAN link. Explains why the `bandwidth` interface command is merely a hint, not a physical limit. The provided MQC configuration is syntactically valid for Cisco IOS and follows standard design practices (shaping at egress, policing/priority alignment, WRED for congestion avoidance, and access-layer marking). Minor ambiguity exists around the necessity of internal `police` commands when `priority percent` is already applied, but this does not detract from the overall accuracy or utility of the document.
metadata.problem_summary: User is experiencing significant jitter on VoIP traffic over a 20 Mbps IPSec WAN link despite applying a basic QoS policy. The root cause is misconfigured bandwidth allocation (using interface speed instead of actual WAN speed), missing traffic shaping on the WAN interface, lack of queuing on the tunnel and distribution switches, and potential network oversubscription causing congestion.

!***** QoS - Class Maps for Queuing Policy *****
class-map match-any QoS-Voice
match ip dscp cs4 cs5 ef
class-map match-any QoS-Video
match ip dscp af41 af42 af43
class-map match-any QoS-Control
match ip dscp cs3 af31 af32 af33 cs6 cs7
class-map match-any QoS-Medium
match ip dscp cs2 af21 af22 af23
class-map match-any QoS-Low
match ip dscp cs1 af11 af12 af13
!
!***** QoS - Queuing Policy *****
policy-map QoS-Tunnel-Out
description 20 Mb Tunnel
class QoS-Voice
priority percent 20
police cir percent 20 conform-action transmit exceed-action drop
class QoS-Video
bandwidth remaining percent 15
police cir percent 15 conform-action transmit exceed-action drop
class QoS-Control
bandwidth remaining percent 5
random-detect dscp-based
random-detect dscp 24 38 39
random-detect dscp 26 31 39
random-detect dscp 28 24 39
random-detect dscp 30 16 31
random-detect dscp 48 38 39
random-detect dscp 56 38 39
class QoS-Medium
bandwidth remaining percent 5
random-detect dscp-based
random-detect dscp 16 33 34
random-detect dscp 18 33 34
random-detect dscp 20 28 34
random-detect dscp 22 21 28
class QoS-Low
bandwidth remaining percent 5
random-detect dscp-based
random-detect dscp 8 8 13
random-detect dscp 10 20 21
random-detect dscp 12 17 21
random-detect dscp 14 13 21
class class-default
bandwidth remaining percent 50
random-detect dscp-based
random-detect dscp default 59 79
!
!***** QoS - Generic Traffic Shaping *****
policy-map QoS-Shaping-Out
description Shape WAN FE to 20 Mb
class class-default
shape average 17000000 68000
service-policy QoS-Tunnel-Out
!
interface Tunnel1
description Tunnel 2 IR
bandwidth 17000
ip address x.x.x.x x.x.x.x
service-policy output QoS-Tunnel-Out
!
interface FastEthernet0/0
description PX Corporate
ip address x.x.x.x x.x.x.x
!
interface FastEthernet0/1
description ATT WAN
bandwidth 100000
ip address x.x.x.x x.x.x.x
service-policy output QoS-Shaping-Out
!

text:
[Original text remains intact] ... The core issue here is IP fragmentation over IPSec, not QoS misconfiguration. When packets exceed the tunnel MTU, they are fragmented. QoS prioritization breaks down on fragmented packets because the first fragment carries the QoS markings, but subsequent fragments are often treated as best-effort or cause reassembly jitter. To properly resolve this:
1. **MTU/MSS Clamping**: Instead of just lowering interface MTU, use `ip tcp adjust-mss <value>` on the ingress interface to force TCP to negotiate smaller payloads, or configure `ip mtu` / `ip tcp adjust-mss` on the tunnel interface.
2. **IPSec MTU Considerations**: IPSec headers (ESP + AH + padding + trailer) typically add 50-60 bytes. Ensure `interface Tunnel1 ip mtu 1400` accounts for this.
3. **Modern QoS Syntax**: The `priority percent 25 5000` syntax is valid but legacy. Modern IOS (15.x+) prefers `priority percent 25` with automatic burst calculation, or explicit `priority queue-limit`. Also, consider `class class-default random-detect` for better congestion management.
4. **Verification**: Use `show policy-map interface` to verify queuing, but also check `show crypto ipsec sa` for fragmentation drops. Enable `debug ip packet detail` or `show interface tunnel1` to monitor MTU issues.
This context bridges the gap between basic QoS configuration and real-world tunnel performance troubleshooting. If you are using IOS XE, consider `queue-limit` under priority classes and `random-detect` for class-default to align with current Cisco best practices for voice over IPsec tunnels.

Original Configuration:
class-map match-any QoS-Voice
  match ip dscp ef
  match ip dscp cs5
  match ip dscp cs3
  match ip dscp af41
policy-map high-priority
  class Qos-Voice
    priority percent 25 5000
  class class-default
    fair-queue
interface Tunnel1
  description Tunnel 2 IR
  bandwidth 20000
  ip address x.x.x.x x.x.x.x
interface FastEthernet0/0
  description PX Corporate
  bandwidth 100000
  ip address x.x.x.x x.x.x.x
  service-policy output high-priority
interface FastEthernet0/1
  description ATT WAN
  bandwidth 100000
  ip address x.x.x.x x.x.x.x
  service-policy output high-priority
show policy-map interface f0/0
The switches operating as the Core layer are layer 3 Enterasys switches that are prioritizing the traffic at layer 2.

[Score:2] Just wanted to circle back and mark the correct answer that fixed my issue. Turns out my system that I was seeing jitter on had a MTU value of 1440 and my Cisco routers were all set to 1400. Once I lowered my value in the 1200 range everything started working as expected.

[Enhanced Context]:
- **MSS Clamping Best Practice**: Replace interface MTU changes with `ip tcp adjust-mss 1360` on the WAN-facing interface. This ensures TCP sessions negotiate payloads that fit within the IPSec tunnel without fragmentation.
- **QoS Over IPSec**: Always apply `service-policy output` on the physical interface (FastEthernet0/1), not the tunnel interface, to avoid double-marking or tunnel header interference. The tunnel interface should only handle routing/IPsec encapsulation.
- **Verification Commands**: Use `show policy-map interface FastEthernet0/1` to confirm class hits, and `show crypto ipsec sa` to verify no fragmented/dropped packets. Enable `service policy debug` if jitter persists.
- **Modern Syntax Note**: `priority percent 25 5000` uses legacy burst sizing. In IOS 15.x/16.x+, use `priority percent 25` and let the system calculate burst, or use `priority level 1 queue-limit 5000` for explicit control.

**Version-Specific Notes**:
- The `priority percent X Y` syntax is deprecated in favor of `priority percent X` (auto-burst) or `priority level X` in IOS XE. The configuration will still work on IOS 12.4-15.2 but may trigger deprecation warnings.
- IPSec MTU/MSS handling is consistent across IOS versions, but IOS XE recommends using `ip tcp adjust-mss` globally or per-interface rather than manual MTU tuning on tunnels.
- No SDN/Mininet/OpenFlow components are present; this is a traditional Cisco IOS QoS/IPSec troubleshooting case.

**Code Blocks**: None modified. Original syntax preserved per instructions.

**Final Assessment**:
- Technical Accuracy: High (MTU fragmentation is the correct root cause)
- Currency: Moderate (uses legacy QoS burst syntax)
- RAG Suitability: Good after enrichment with MSS clamping and modern QoS context
- Action: ENRICH (Score: 6)

The MTU mismatch solution is correct but brief; adding MSS clamping and fragmentation context would significantly improve its value for a RAG database.",

QoS prioritization breaks down on fragmented packets because the first fragment carries the QoS markings, but subsequent fragments are often treated as best-effort or cause reassembly jitter. To properly resolve this:\n1. **MTU/MSS Clamping**: Instead of just lowering interface MTU, use `ip tcp adjust-mss <value>` on the ingress interface to force TCP to negotiate smaller payloads, or configure `ip mtu` / `ip tcp adjust-mss` on the tunnel interface.\n2. **IPSec MTU Considerations**: IPSec headers (ESP + AH + padding + trailer) typically add 50-60 bytes. Ensure `interface Tunnel1 ip mtu 1400` accounts for this.\n3. **Modern QoS Syntax**: The `priority percent 25 5000` syntax is valid but legacy. Modern IOS (15.x+) prefers `priority percent 25` with automatic burst calculation, or explicit `priority queue-limit`. Also, consider `class class-default random-detect` for better congestion management.\n4. **Verification**: Use `show policy-map interface` to verify queuing, but also check `show crypto ipsec sa` for fragmentation drops. Enable `debug ip packet detail` or `show interface tunnel1` to monitor MTU issues.\nThis context bridges the gap between basic QoS configuration and real-world tunnel performance troubleshooting.",

Q: Please explain:
I have two routers with QoS on them.. Both WAN-facing interfaces have a bandwidth command with the same value (100000) but the priority queue (also the other reservations) calculates a different amount of kbps for this queue? I would think that when the bandwidth is the same the calculated result of kbps per queue should be the same?
The fist router (lets call it router a) is at the main office and has a multi-point connection to 3 other routers at branch offices. The real bandwidth at the main office is 300Mbit and at the branches its 100Mbit. I made a HQos script to shape the bandwidth at the main to 100mbit per site (parent) and then the QoS queues (child). I put the bandwidth on the main to 100Mbit to make sure it would calculate the bandwidth (all in percentages) in the correct way (e.g. 100mbit per site). I know i could use a strict amount of bandwidth but I wonder why this is not functioning the way I think it should..
main router (A):
interface GigabitEthernet0/0
description WAN
bandwidth 100000
ip address 172.16.2.1 255.255.255.0
ip flow ingress
ip flow egress
ip ospf message-digest-key 1 md5 7
duplex full
speed 1000
service-policy output 300Mb-forward-qos
end
Service-policy output: 300Mb-forward-qos
Class-map: site1-100Mb-forward-qos (match-all)
97350980 packets, 60719394859 bytes
5 minute offered rate 1891000 bps, drop rate 0 bps
Match: access-group name site1
Queueing
queue limit 64 packets
(queue depth/total drops/no-buffer drops) 0/864/0
(pkts output/bytes output) 97350116/60718176568
shape (peak) cir 97000000, bc 388000, be 388000
target shape rate 194000000
Service-policy : forward-qos
queue stats for all priority classes:
queue limit 64 packets
(queue depth/total drops/no-buffer drops) 0/0/0
(pkts output/bytes output) 148794/7659376
Class-map: forward-qos-class-prec5 (match-all)
148794 packets, 7659376 bytes
5 minute offered rate 0 bps, drop rate 0 bps
Match: ip precedence 5
Priority: 10% (2500 kbps), burst bytes 62500, b/w exceed drops: 0
!^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
Router at branch (B)
interface GigabitEthernet0/0
description WAN
bandwidth 100000
! ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
ip address 172.16.2.4 255.255.255.0
ip ospf message-digest-key 1 md5 7 12480603150C02573E
duplex auto
speed auto
service-policy output 100Mb-forward-qos
end
Service-policy : forward-qos
queue stats for all priority classes:
queue limit 64 packets
(queue depth/total drops/no-buffer drops) 0/0/0
(pkts output/bytes output) 3873239/716718654
Class-map: forward-qos-class-prec5 (match-all)
3873238 packets, 716718440 bytes
5 minute offered rate 123000 bps, drop rate 0 bps
Match: ip precedence 5
Priority: 10% (5000 kbps), burst bytes 125000, b/w exceed drops: 0
!^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^

[Score:5] There is no need to put a bandwidth statement on the router (A) interface. If you're doing H-QoS then you shape to 100Mb on the parent, while the child policies then inherit that shaped value.
Like so:
policy-map parent
class class-default
shape average 100m
service-policy child
policy-map child
class EF
priority percent 5
If you put bandwidth statement on the physical interface you mess up the H-QoS calculation. It's fine for the branches as they are not running H-QoS

metadata.title: QoS bandwith calculation
metadata.llm_evaluation_reason: Score 7-10 (KEEP): The document correctly explains Cisco IOS H-QoS inheritance mechanics. It accurately notes that child policy bandwidth percentages are calculated relative to the parent's shaped rate, making the physical interface 'bandwidth' command redundant for H-QoS calculations. Minor oversimplification regarding the 'bandwidth' command's impact, but no critical technical errors. Lacks explicit IOS version context but remains functionally accurate for standard QoS deployments.
metadata.problem_summary: Two routers with identical interface bandwidth commands (100000) calculate different priority queue bandwidths (2500 kbps vs 5000 kbps). The discrepancy occurs because the main router uses the interface 'bandwidth' command while implementing Hierarchical QoS with parent shaping, which interferes with child policy inheritance and rate calculation.
metadata.context_summary: The user expects matching queue calculations due to identical interface bandwidth settings. The responder explains that the 'bandwidth' command on the physical interface should be removed on the main router when using H-QoS, as shaping on the parent policy should dictate the rate allocation for child policies.

class class-default
shape average 100m
service-policy child
policy-map child
class EF
