text:
Q: I read from this website to understand DSCP and it's corresponding IP precedence value.
For example DSCP value is '000 000' which Drop Probability is N/A(never happen) and it's Equivalent IP Precedence Value is '000'. But in IP Precedence table '000' is least important.
My question is if any packet with DSCP value has very less 'packet drop probability' but why it's less important?

[Score:4] The IPv4 DSCP (IPv6 Traffic Flow) packet header field can be used to mark packets for later treatment in network congestion. There are recommendations and guidelines for interpreting these values a PHBs (Per-Hop Behaviors). It is a best practice to follow the guidelines, but there is no requirement to do so.
By default, network devices do not have QoS enabled, and most packets simply are simply marked BE
(Best Effort). Even if packets are marked, network devices do not by default treat the packets any differently. You can configure your network devices to treat the different DSCP markings as you want them to be treated. This is meaningless unless there is congestion.
You should follow the guidelines, and you should configure a consistent set of QoS policies on all your network devices. Failure to have all your network devices configured to treat packet markings the same can create chaos when there is congestion.
The DSCP markings are ignored on the public Internet because everyone has a different interpretation of QoS and what should be prioritized over what when there is congestion.
Edit for your edit:
What you are really asking about is the AF (Assured Forwarding) interpretation of DSCP. With the AF interpretation, you have two digits of three bits each. The first digit is the priority (1
to 4
) with a higher number being a higher priority. The second digit (1
to 3
) is the drop probability with a higher number being a higher drop probability. There are also two special AF values: BE (Best Effort = 00
) and EF (Expedited Forwarding = 46
) that do not fit in the drop probability of the regular AF values.
There are also other standard ways to interpret the six bits, including CS and Precedence. Cisco has a chart that may help you understand.
The idea is to have some guidelines, recommendations, and best practices about how to interpret the bits, but you can configure the devices to interpret them and assign your own meanings and behaviors to any value. It is not a good idea to buck the guidelines, but there is really nothing to stop you from doing that.

metadata.title: Understand DSCP and Precedence Values in Ipv4 or Ipv6 packet
metadata.llm_evaluation_reason: Technically accurate overview of DSCP, IP Precedence, PHBs, and AF/EF/BE classifications. Covers core QoS principles correctly, including default BE marking, congestion dependence, and enterprise vs. internet handling. No outdated versions or code blocks present. Suitable for RAG indexing as-is.
metadata.problem_summary: Clarification on why a DSCP value with low packet drop probability (000 000) maps to a low IP Precedence value ('000'), explaining that markings only dictate behavior during congestion when QoS is configured, and detailing the AF interpretation where drop probability is a separate metric from priority.
metadata.context_summary: The response explains that DSCP/Precedence values are only meaningful during network congestion and require consistent QoS policy configuration. It clarifies the AF (Assured Forwarding) bit structure, where the first two bits represent priority (1-4) and the next two represent drop probability (1-3), with BE and EF being special cases.
metadata.version_tag: Concepts are based on IETF RFCs (RFC 2474, 2475, 3246, 2597, 2598) and remain current. No CLI commands or software versions mentioned.

text: On Catalyst 3650/3850 running IOS XE, QoS is enabled by default using the Modular QoS CLI (MQC). The legacy `mls qos` command is deprecated; global QoS activation is implicit. By default, ingress interfaces trust the DSCP value (`mls qos trust dscp`), meaning L3 markings are preserved, but L2 CoS is typically rewritten based on internal DSCP-to-CoS mappings unless explicitly configured. The default hardware queue architecture per port consists of four user queues (1–4) plus a dedicated control queue, not two. Traffic classification follows the default QoS queueing profile (typically WRR/SP mix). IP precedence 6/7 remain reserved for network control traffic. To verify current QoS state, use `show qos` and `show policy-map interface <interface>`. Note: IOS XE 16.x Denali is EOL; Cisco recommends migrating to 17.x/3.x for enhanced QoS features. Default trust and queueing behavior can be verified with `show mls qos interface <interface>` (legacy compatibility) or `show qos interface <interface>`.

metadata.title: Default QoS Behavior on Catalyst 3650/3850 Running IOS XE 16.3.6
metadata.llm_evaluation_reason: The answer contains notable technical inaccuracies: it incorrectly states there are only two default queues per port (Catalyst 3650/3850 hardware uses 4 user queues + 1 control queue) and oversimplifies the default trust state (IOS XE defaults to DSCP trust, not blanket trust of all L2/L3 markings). It also relies on deprecated MLS QoS terminology without clarifying IOS XE's native MQC implementation. However, the conceptual direction is correct, making it suitable for enrichment rather than rejection.
metadata.problem_summary: The user wants to understand the default QoS and queuing behavior on Catalyst 3650/3850 switches running IOS XE 16.3.6 Denali, specifically regarding global QoS enablement, equivalent commands to the legacy IOS 15 'mls qos' model, and how unmodified DSCP markings are handled by default without explicit service policies.

text:
The original answer correctly identifies that LAG hash distribution can cause uneven traffic flow across links, potentially leading to microbursts or egress queue exhaustion on individual 40G interfaces, even when aggregate utilization appears low. However, the mathematical explanation of hash distribution is simplified. On Cisco Nexus platforms, the hash algorithm (src-dst ip-l4port) distributes flows pseudo-randomly. With 3 links, certain asymmetric flow patterns (e.g., a few high-bandwidth flows from the same source/dest pair) can indeed saturate one or two links, causing per-port drops while the aggregate link remains underutilized. 

Additional context for Nexus 9300 troubleshooting:
- Verify per-port discard counters: `show interface ethernet 2/1 | include discard`, `show interface ethernet 2/2 | include discard`, etc. This confirms if drops are port-specific.
- Microbursts are common on 40G/100G interfaces. Use hardware counters or a high-speed packet capture to validate.
- The default QoS policy (`default-out-policy`) on N9K may not be optimized for high-speed LAGs. Consider adjusting the egress queue limits or buffer profiles (`hardware qos ns-buffer-profile`).
- To mitigate hash imbalance, you can adjust the load-balancing algorithm or use ECMP-like tuning, though LAG hashing is generally robust. The discard count shown (~3.7k packets out of ~6.3B) is negligible and likely transient noise.

metadata.title: Cisco nexus 9396PX TX output discard on 40G interface without link congestion
metadata.llm_evaluation_reason: The answer correctly identifies flow imbalance and microbursts as potential causes for per-port discards in a LAG, but oversimplifies the hash distribution math and misses Nexus-specific QoS/buffer troubleshooting. The discard count is negligible (~0.00006%), suggesting transient noise rather than a systemic issue. Enrichment adds precise troubleshooting steps, clarifies hash behavior, and contextualizes the discard rate.
metadata.problem_summary: Output packet discards on a 3x40G LACP LAG following a migration from an 8x10G LAG, despite low average utilization, attributed to uneven traffic hashing and microbursts on individual member links.

The responder suggests that hash distribution across only 3 links causes uneven load, leading to microbursts that exceed individual port buffers, unlike the previous 8-link setup where distribution was more balanced.

text:
Q: We have a 24 port NETGEAR managed POE switch that has QoS capabilities.
We also have the following unmanaged switches, 24 port Cisco POE, 48 port Cisco, 48 port NETGEAR, and 24 port tp-link.
If any of the unmanaged switches are between the managed switch and the NETGEAR router (to be replaced by a Ubiquiti EdgeRouter 4) will that make the QoS on the managed router completely pointless?

[Score:3] The 2960 series is managed. Even the Netgear GS348 supports some QoS (its flavor of, check the documentation).
QoS prioritizes traffic - forward important frames, drop less important frames - when that becomes necessary due to link overload = congestion. If none of your links is supposed to handle more traffic then it is capable of, there's no congestion, and no real need for QoS.
So, if those switches where congestion is possible or most likely to happen support QoS features and are configured appropriately, you're basically good. Without deep insight into your network - topology, device configurations, (peak) workloads and flow patterns etc your question cannot be really answered.
Do unmanaged switches honour QoS priorities set by managed switches?
Some do, many don't. Since you cannot configure their QoS features you have to make do with what's there. Some switches may support L2 priorities (priority code points from 802.1p/q), others may support differential services (DSCP) from the L3 IP packets.
You need to make sure that upstream, managed switches set/use the QoS flags that the downstream, unmanaged switches require. Since that can get very hard it's easiest to use managed switches with a common logic (mostly from the same vendor/series).

metadata.title: Do unmanaged switches honour QoS priorities set by managed switches?
metadata.llm_evaluation_reason: Technically accurate and practical for traditional switching environments. Correctly identifies QoS as a congestion-management mechanism and accurately describes the variable behavior of unmanaged switches regarding priority markings. Minor ambiguity exists regarding how unmanaged devices handle Layer 2 CoS vs Layer 3 DSCP markings, which is clarified in the enriched text. No outdated commands or SDN-specific protocols are present, as the scope is limited to legacy Ethernet switching.
metadata.problem_summary: Determining whether unmanaged switches placed between a managed QoS switch and a router will render QoS configuration useless, and how to ensure traffic prioritization is maintained across mixed managed/unmanaged switch topologies.
metadata.context_summary: The user asks if inserting unmanaged switches between a managed QoS switch and a router will negate QoS functionality. The response explains that QoS only matters during congestion, notes that unmanaged switches may or may not respect priority tags (802.1p/q, DSCP), and recommends using managed switches from the same vendor for consistent QoS logic.

text:
Q: It is very hard to find information on this topic. I would like to know: If traffic has a hit inside a class-map, is it immediately forwarded or does it get checked against all remaining match statements first? Example: given following configuration:
ip access-list extended QOS_CITRIX
permit tcp any eq 2598 any
permit tcp any any eq 2598
permit udp any eq 2598 any
permit udp any any eq 2598
class-map match-any QOS_CITRIX
match ip dscp 32
match access-group name QOS_CITRIX
policy-map QOS
class QOS_CITRIX
set dscp 32
bandwidth percent 50
If a packet with DSCP32 arrives to be checked against this class-map, does it process the ACL?

[Score:2] There are 2 different matching variations for class maps. Either match-any or match-all:
With match-any it reads them in order and stops once/if it finds a match.
With match-all it has to match against all the match statements so it needs to read them all as long as it keeps matching ie. it it doesn't match in one of the match statements there is no need to keep trying to match in the following match statements.

metadata.title: C3PL working order
metadata.llm_evaluation_reason: Technically accurate description of Cisco IOS class-map matching behavior. Correctly explains short-circuit evaluation for match-any and AND logic for match-all. Directly answers the question regarding DSCP vs ACL evaluation order in C3PL/QoS configurations. No version-specific quirks affect this fundamental behavior.
metadata.problem_summary: Clarifying the packet evaluation order and matching behavior within a Cisco IOS class-map when multiple match statements are defined with match-any.
metadata.context_summary: A user asks whether a packet matching an early criterion in a class-map is immediately forwarded or continues to be evaluated against subsequent match statements. The answer explains that match-any processes statements sequentially and stops at the first match, while match-all requires all statements to match.
metadata.code_block:
ip access-list extended QOS_CITRIX
permit tcp any eq 2598 any
permit tcp any any eq 2598
permit udp any eq 2598 any
permit udp any any eq 2598
class-map match-any QOS_CITRIX
match ip dscp 32
match access-group name QOS_CITRIX
policy-map QOS
class QOS_CITRIX
set dscp 32
bandwidth percent 50

text:
Q: This question/lab was created with Cisco networking hardware. I have asked this question else where with no responses.
I am new to QoS, but I have spent the last few days researching and I've learned a ton. However, I am still having issues accomplishing what I want. I am trying to configure QoS to prioritize two classes of traffic over all other types of traffic (the first class will have more priority over the second class). To test this theory and my configurations I've set up two routers one switch and two PCs. Here is a picture of the topology I am using... (I am using real hardware, packet tracer is only demonstrating the topology)
My goal is to have all three PCs ping R2 at the same time, and for PC0 to have the easiest/fastest transmission. I set up a choke point between R1 and R2 so I could test and make sure my PC0 packets were the least likely to be dropped. I configured the R1-R2 link to 10mbps, mtu 500, and hold-queue to 10 packets for both 'in' and 'out'. I then had R1 ping R2 with large ICMP packets and R2 ping R1 also with large ICMP packets. The idea is R1's pings, R2's pings and PC2's pings are all routine traffic and should be the traffic that is dropped if the queue is full. PC0's pings should never/rarely be dropped.
Here's an example of my configuration...
ip access-list extended class1-out_acl
permit ip 192.168.1.0 0.0.0.255 any
deny ip any any
ip access-list extended class2-out_acl
permit ip 192.168.2.0 0.0.0.255 any
deny ip any any
ip access-list extended class1-in_acl
permit ip any 192.168.1.0 0.0.0.255
deny ip any any
ip access-list extended class2-in_acl
permit ip any 192.168.2.0 0.0.0.255
deny ip any any
class-map match-all class1-out
match access-group name class1-out_acl
class-map match-all class2-out
match access-group name class2-out_acl
class-map match-all class1-in
match access-group name class1-in_acl
class-map match-all class2-in
match access-group name class2-in_acl
policy-map QOS-OUT
class class1-out
priority percent 20
set precedence 5
class class2-out
priority percent 20
set precedence 3
class class-default
fair-queue
random-detect
set precedence 0
policy-map QOS-IN
class class1-in
police 2000000 400000 400000 conform-action transmit exceed-action drop violate-action drop
class class2-in
police 2000000 400000 400000 conform-action transmit exceed-action drop violate-action drop
class class-default
police 5000000 1000000 1000000 conform-action transmit exceed-action drop violate-action drop
control-plane
service-policy input QOS-IN
interface GigabitEthernet0/1
mtu 500
speed 10
hold-queue 10 in
hold-queue 10 out
service-policy output QOS-OUT
R2's configuration is exactly the same EXCEPT for the ACLs. The ACL for class1-out becomes the ACL for class1-in and vice-versa. The same is true for the class2-in and class2-out ACLs. This is done so the class1 traffic is given 'priority' round trip.
I set PC0 and PC3 to both ping R2 with 5000 size pings for 300 times (didn't use PC1 during this test). I set R1 to ping R2 with 5000 size pings about 15000 times and the same for R2 pinging R1.
During the test, both routers and both PC's were dropping packets (as I expected, slightly surprised PC0 was dropping though). When my test was complete I found that PC2 had the best results, dropping only 5% at a 9ms average, while PC0 dropped 10% with an average of 11ms.
I ran [show policy-map interface] and my class1 packets were being picked out of the flood, but it doesn't seem that they have any priority. Using this command, I also noticed ALL of the packets were being classified under IP Precedence 0 (routine). Which is strange, because I set class1 to 5.
As I mentioned earlier, I am new to QoS so I'm sure I have made a mistake somewhere. Also, could someone please explain a good method/policy to use when setting the 'police' values. I picked these numbers slightly arbitrarily.
Once I have sorted out any configuration errors, my underlying question is... is there a standard or policy I can follow to 'match' for my input and output service policies to provide the same level of QoS to the traffic classes. Referencing my example above, I'm trying to use a priority percent of 20% for class1 on the exit interface while also policing the input interface to 2mbps (20% of 10mbps) for class1. This is my attempt at 'a-lining' the link to use the same prioritization of traffic (both for input and output). I wish I could just use a percentage for both input and output service policies, but my research makes me think this is not possible.

[Score:3] TL;DR:
A. Dont' use Ping, use UDP.
B. Do classification and marking on the ingress side (matching ACLs,Protocols, whatever),
C. Do queuing/scheduling on the egress side, matching just the QoS bits in the IP header's ToS field.
A bit more verbose:
A) Use UDP, not Ping
To start with: ICMP or Ping, respectively, is a particularly unsuitable tool for testing the queuing/scheduling part of a QoS setup for two reasons:
ICMP may do a decent job when testing the classification/marking part of a QoS setup.
Therefore, I suggest to pick a tool that generates unidirectional UDP streams, like iPerf (and certainly others) do, and i run the test cases for each direction individually. You may want to add a PC to the LAN behind R2 to serve as receiving system (with iPerf on UDP, the interesting results are shown on the receiver side, whereas with TCP, either side will show meaningful results).
UDP also has the advantage that you can easily work with ports, making the ACLs for the class-maps LOT easier to write (eg. port udp/5000 is default, 5005 gets precedence 5, port 5003 gets precedence 3) et cetera.
I'm not quite sure what you intended with your configuration - especially w/regards to the role of control plane policing, that is a completely different ballpark. I would advise to leave that aside; CoPP can cause great havoc if badly implemented on a production network.
There's three very important things to remember with QoS:
QoS is an unidirectional thing, and when measuring QoS, always be aware of the direction of the "interesting" traffic. Configurations may be symmetrical and even share the same class-maps/policy maps and ACLs. But the way QoS operates on a router is completely independent for each direction. For that reason, you want to have test scenarios of unidirectional character.
QoS Queuing/Scheduling isn't really happening until the interface (or the parent shaper) gets saturated.
QoS is a system of managed unfairness (not my quote, but for the sake of it, I can't remember where I got this from). Once a link gets saturated, EVERY traffic class WILL suffer to some extent. QoS just sets the rules of unfairness.
I'll give you some hints that follow my experience with QoS setups, picking up the bits as I grasped them from your post.
Usually, you want to perform classification/marking on the ingress interface, possibly together with some policing. Classification/Marking with a policy map bound to an egress interface is sometimes not even possible, or only in a limited fashion.
B) Ingress QoS
Let's start with the ingress side. (Please be aware that I'm composing this freehandedly, not from an actual device. There might be slight errors in syntax or feature support on the given platform)
define class-maps with their ACLs to identify traffic (1):
class-map match-any CMAP_QOS_CLASS1,
match access-group ACL_CLASS1-TRAFFIC
class-map CMAP_QOS_CLASS2
match access-group ACL_CLASS2-TRAFFIC
define a policy-map called PM_QOS_INGRESS, containing the above class-maps
in that policy-map, just set the precedence or DSCP value, optionally police incoming traffic.
policy-map PMAP_QOS_INGRESS
class CMAP_QOS_CLASS1
set precedence 5
class CMAP_QUE_CLASS2
set precedence 3
class class-default
set precedence 0
bind that policy-map to the interface where to-be-QoS'd traffic is coming from (2)
Now packets entering that router get their intended marking in the ToS byte.
C) Egress QoS
Now for the egress side.
define a set of class maps that have matching criteria for ToS byte markings (DSCP or precedence). Do not match on IP address (with an ACL), protocol or port, here.
class-map match-any CMAP_QUE_CLASS1
match precedence 5
class-map match-any CMAP_QUE_CLASS2
match precedence 3
define a policy map that decides on what has to happen to each class (3):
policy-map PMAP_QUE_EGRESS
class CMAP_QUE_CLASS1
priority level 1
police <some upper limit you want to give to that class>
class CMAP_QUE_CLASS2
priority level 2
policy <some upper limit you want to give to that class>
class class-default
random-detect precedence-based
bind that policy map to the interface where the to-be-qos'd traffic is leaving R1.
Then, start some traffic (not yet saturating the link or the upper limits of policing) from all three PCs. Use show policy map interface gig0/1 out
on R1 to see if you get nonzero counters for the different classes.
Then start to increase bandwidth of the udp streams until they start to saturate your choke point. Look at the egress policy map again (this time looking for drop counters per class) and look at the stream receiver's stats for loss and jitter. Be sure to compare jitter for the priority classes vs. jitter of the default class.
CAVEAT You seem to have a Catalyst 2960 in there between your PCs and R1. Even if it's irrelevant in the current scenario (classification/marking happening on R1), I strongly recommend to ...
mls qos
disabledmls qos trust
Else, the catalyst will nullify the ToS byte of all IP packets (yes, even if it's only acting as Layer2 switch), and you might end up with unexpected effects.
(1) On Cisco Nexus platforms, class-maps and policy maps used for (ingress) classification and marking are of type qos
, the ones for (egress) queuing and scheduling are of type queuing
. I happened to like this because it gives clarity, and therefore I chose to reflect the class/policy maps intended use in its name, even on IOS. Your class/policy map naming strategy may be different, of course.
(2) on a campus network, classification and marking would be done on the access switch port where the PCs are connected to. For the sake of exercise, you may want to do this on the router interface towards the (source) PCs.
(3) I'll forego the example of a hierarchical Policy-Map with a parent and child policy map. If needed, I'll edit the answer.

--- TECHNICAL CONTEXT & ENRICHMENT ---
The error 'Match DSCP in IPv4/IPv6 packets is not supported for this interface' on a Cisco ASR 903 service instance is a known platform limitation. Service instances (EVCs) on older IOS-XE releases (pre-16.6/17.x) and certain hardware configurations (e.g., integrated PFC vs. modular SPC cards) do not support DSCP or IP precedence matching in the service-policy context. This is due to how the hardware offloads packet classification for tagged vs. untagged frames.

Your IP-based workaround is valid and commonly used in these scenarios. However, consider the following improvements:
1. Hardware/Version Dependency: DSCP matching on EVCs is fully supported on IOS-XE 16.6+ and later, provided the line card supports it (e.g., SPC3/SPC4). Verify your IOS-XE version with 'show version' and hardware with 'show platform' or 'show hw-module subslot 0/0/0 status'.
2. ACL Optimization: Manually decomposing CIDR blocks (as shown in your object-group) is error-prone. Modern IOS-XE supports 'ip access-list extended' with 'range' or prefix-lists. Alternatively, use 'match ip address prefix-list' if supported.
3. QoS Best Practice: If your goal is traffic shaping/policing, ensure you're using 'police' (hard limit) vs 'shape' (smooths burst). For EVCs, policing is often preferred due to hardware constraints.
4. Alternative Match Criteria: If DSCP matching is still required, try matching on 'match packet-color' (if using MQC with traffic policing) or 'match mac address'/'match destination-mac' as fallbacks, depending on IOS-XE version.

This approach remains a solid workaround for legacy ASR 903 deployments where EVC DSCP matching is restricted.

match dscp af22
!

FiberISP-Cisco(config-if-srv)#service-policy out Cust
Match DSCP in IPv4/IPv6 packets is not supported for this interfac
!
WHY?

[Score:1] DSCP would be part of the IP header as such is processed by layer-3 interfaces. As you have an L2 Ethernet interface you should use CoS to classify your traffic as it doesn't care about nor can it manipulate the IP header.
This document will give you a good overview of how to use CoS in your class-maps and policy-maps.
Based on your provided config it would be something like this.
class-map match-all GGC-OUT
match cos 3
!
policy-map google-cap
class google
set cos 3

text: The original text correctly identifies that QoS must be applied at the bottleneck (typically your egress interface or the ISP edge). While TCP congestion control (RFC 5681) theoretically limits single flows, high Bandwidth-Delay Product (BDP) links and TCP window scaling often allow one connection to saturate available bandwidth. To enforce fairness, implement per-IP or per-subnet rate limiting using Cisco IOS Modular QoS CLI (MQC). Instead of a blanket artificial bottleneck, use policing to drop/exceed traffic above a defined threshold per user. Example implementation context: Define a class-map to match traffic, then apply a policy-map with `police cir <kbps>` and `conform-action transmit exceed-action drop`. For advanced traffic management, combine CBWFQ for bandwidth guarantees and LLQ for latency-sensitive traffic. Note: Cisco PIX is EOL; modern equivalents use ASA or ISR G3/G4 with IOS-XE. QoS remains effective only if you control the egress interface; upstream ISP congestion requires carrier-grade QoS or dedicated circuits. SDN/Mininet/Ryu/OVS are not applicable to this traditional QoS scenario but could be leveraged in lab environments to dynamically program per-flow rate limits via OpenFlow.

metadata.title: How do I prevent any one user from hogging all the bandwidth?

The author questions why this congestion did not occur at lower speeds and whether QoS configurations can prevent it, particularly given that the ISP-side router configuration is inaccessible.

text: The answer correctly identifies QoS as the solution but lacks implementation specifics. To enforce per-user bandwidth limits on Cisco IOS/IOS-XE, use Class-Based QoS (CBQoS) with policing or shaping rather than prioritization alone. Typical workflow: 1) Create a class-map to match the user's source IP/subnet. 2) Define a policy-map containing a police or shape command (e.g., `police cir 5m conform-action transmit exceed-action drop` or `shape average 5m`). 3) Apply the policy to the egress ISP-facing interface using `service-policy output <policy-name>`. Note: Policing drops excess traffic immediately, while shaping buffers it to smooth bursts. Prioritization mechanisms (LLQ, WFQ) handle congestion among traffic classes, whereas policing/shaping enforces hard limits. The Cisco PIX is EOL; ASA is recommended for modern deployments. This scenario does not involve SDN/Mininet/OpenFlow/OVS, as it relies on traditional enterprise CLI-based QoS.

text:
Q: Wondering if anyone has ran PTP traffic along side high volume UDP traffic on the same switch running at 100 Mbps. Would it be expected to get massive (15 us) jitter on the PTP packets without setting QoS, especially if the link is not being saturated? The high volume traffic would be characterized as one packet every 125 us with a size of roughly 250 bytes.
Thanks!

[Score:3] An IP(?) packet of 250 bytes translates to ~23 μs link time on Fast Ethernet (=maximum additional latency with gapped packets). Half that somewhat matches the jitter you see, so it's to be expected.
For more precision you'll need dedicated or faster links. QoS wouldn't improve this as a frame isn't terminated in the middle of a transmission for the sake of a higher prioritized frame.
QoS helps with a (near) saturated link in that it can limit the additional latency to that of a frame's transmission time, independent of the queue length.

[Additional Context for RAG/Modern Deployments]: The answer correctly describes classical store-and-forward Ethernet behavior. However, PTP (IEEE 1588) over shared Ethernet today relies on IEEE 802.1TSN (Time-Sensitive Networking) rather than traditional 802.1p/DSCP QoS. To mitigate jitter without link saturation:
• IEEE 802.1Qbv (Time-Aware Shaper): Configures time windows to guarantee bandwidth and isolate PTP traffic from best-effort UDP.
• IEEE 802.1AS-Rev: Defines the TSN profile for PTP, optimizing synchronization over switched networks.
• Hardware Timestamping & PTP Snooping: Ensures switches handle PTP packets at line rate without OS/queue delay.
• IEEE 802.3br (Frame Preemption): Allows high-priority frames to interrupt low-priority transmissions mid-frame (modern TSN switches).
Recommendation: For deterministic sub-microsecond jitter, deploy TSN-capable switches with hardware timestamping and configure 802.1Qbv schedules. Classical QoS alone cannot guarantee PTP precision on shared media.

metadata.title: PTP traffic over 100Mbps switch with high volume traffic
metadata.llm_evaluation_reason: The original answer is technically accurate for classical Ethernet but lacks modern context relevant to PTP deployments. It correctly identifies transmission delay and QoS limitations but omits IEEE 802.1TSN standards, which are the industry solution for deterministic latency on shared links.

[Original Text]
Q: I'm having great difficulty transferring a QoS configuration from a Procurve HP switch to an HP switch running Comware v7. The documentation lists all available configuration parameters, but fails to explain how they all fit together.
Each blade in our rack has only two physical interfaces, so different types of traffic are separated by VLAN tags.
Each VLAN basically needs to be classified into one of five groups, with descending priority and a guaranteed minimum bandwidth for each group. If there is no other traffic on the wire, each group should be able to claim the full capacity of the link.
The Procurve configuration is as follows:
[...]
I have been experimenting with wfq configuration for the Comware switch, but I cannot seem to fit the pieces together.
What I think I want to do is:
[...]
Although the configuration seems to work, I cannot confirm that the traffic is marked correctly and weighted fair queuing is actually taking place.
Can anyone point me in the right direction?

[Score:2] What's the reason you want to drop all traffic of a VLAN into a queue? It's not common. You can use DSCP and prioritize on IP packet level. You can be much more specific in what you want to prioritize. If it's VoIP, your hardphone's probably send out DSCP 46. On Comware do a 'qos trust dscp' command on the interfaces, and that's all. VoIP will be thrown in 'ef', queue 5.
The reason traffic is coming into queue's 0, 2 and 7 is because of background chatting of switches and routing protocols.
2 = default queue on Comware
7 = VRRP, OSPF, etc.

[Enrichment & Corrections]
- Comware 7 QoS Architecture: Follows a strict pipeline: `traffic classifier` (match conditions) -> `traffic behavior` (actions like remark/priority) -> `qos policy` (bind classifier to behavior) -> `qos vlan-policy` or `qos traffic-policy` (apply to interface/VLAN).
- Syntax Correction: The user's WFQ and bandwidth commands are incorrect for Comware 7. Use:
  `qos wfq enable`
  `qos wfq queue <0-7> weight <1-128>`
  `qos queue <id> bandwidth min <kbps>`
- DSCP Trust: The answer's advice to use `qos trust dscp` on ingress interfaces is industry best practice. It offloads classification to the IP header, allowing granular per-flow QoS instead of blunt VLAN-based grouping.
- Queue Mapping Clarification: Queue 7 is typically reserved for Network Control Traffic (NCT) on HP/Comware, not strictly VRRP/OSPF. Queue 2 is indeed the default data queue. EF (DSCP 46) typically maps to Queue 5 by default.
- Verification Commands: Add `display qos statistics interface <interface>`, `display qos policy applied`, and `display traffic classifier <name>` to confirm markings and queue utilization.
- Note: For guaranteed minimum bandwidth with strict priority, consider using a combination of PQ (for control/voice) and WFQ/CBWFQ (for data) rather than pure WFQ.

text:
### Technical Context & Corrections (HP Comware v7)

The original post attempts to configure QoS using HP's Modular QoS CLI (MQC), which is the standard architecture for Comware v7. However, the WFQ and bandwidth commands provided are syntactically incorrect for this platform. Comware v7 requires explicit weight configuration for WFQ and uses kbps for minimum bandwidth guarantees.

**Corrected MQC Configuration Flow:**
1. **Classify:** Match traffic by VLAN, DSCP, or 802.1p.
2. **Behavior:** Remark priority and assign WFQ weights.
3. **Policy:** Bind classifiers to behaviors.
4. **Apply:** Bind policy to VLAN or interface.

**Standard Comware v7 Syntax Example:**
```text
# Classifiers
traffic classifier VLAN10 operator or
 if-match vlan-id 10

traffic classifier VLAN20 operator or
 if-match vlan-id 20

# Behaviors (WFQ weights sum to 100)
traffic behavior BEHAVIOR_HIGH
 remark local-precedence 7
 qos wfq weight 40

traffic behavior BEHAVIOR_MED
 remark local-precedence 3
 qos wfq weight 30

# QoS Policy
qos policy POLICY_QOS
classifier VLAN10 behavior BEHAVIOR_HIGH
classifier VLAN20 behavior BEHAVIOR_MED

# Apply to VLAN (inbound classification)
qos vlan-policy POLICY_QOS vlan 10-20 inbound
```

**Interface Queue & Bandwidth Configuration:**
Comware v7 maps `local-precedence` to hardware queues automatically. To set minimum bandwidth:
```text
interface Ten-GigabitEthernet 1/1/8
 qos wfq enable
 qos bandwidth queue 7 min 40000 max 100000
 qos bandwidth queue 3 min 20000 max 100000
```
*(Note: Bandwidth values are in kbps. Ensure min values sum to ≤ link speed.)*

**Verification & Troubleshooting:**
- **Check policy binding:** `display qos policy vlan-policy`
- **Monitor queue statistics:** `display qos statistics interface Ten-GigabitEthernet 1/1/8`
- **Verify DSCP/802.1p mapping:** `display qos map-table dscp-to-local-precedence`
- **Real-time queue depth:** `display qos queue interface <interface>`

**Key Notes:**
- Comware v7 classifies → marks → schedules. Ensure policy direction (`inbound` vs `outbound`) matches your traffic flow.
- The original Wireshark/DSCP advice is valid for L3 verification, but Comware v7 internal queue utilization is best verified via `display qos statistics`.
- WRR and WFQ are both supported; WFQ is preferred for dynamic traffic. Ensure `qos wfq enable` is active on the interface.

text:
As usually, big (modular) devices and switches have hardware accelerated QoS. So you cant see load of CPU, because qos is implemented in ASIC/NPE. Of course, it is limited, but limit is in packet per second (1mpps of CEF switching NPE G1, 2mpps of CEF switching NPE G2), not in bit per second.

Note: While data-plane forwarding is hardware-accelerated, the Route Processor (RP) still handles policy evaluation, policing, and queue management, which does consume CPU cycles. To monitor QoS resource usage and drops, use the following IOS commands:
- `show policy-map interface <interface>`: Displays queue drops, policing statistics, and bandwidth utilization per policy.
- `show queueing interface <interface>`: Shows buffer allocation and queue thresholds.
- `show platform qos`: Provides hardware-level QoS resource usage (NPE/ASIC).
- `show resource`: Monitors system-wide memory and queue allocations.

Regarding SNMP, you can track QoS drops and buffer usage via IF-MIB counters (e.g., `ifInDiscards`, `ifOutDiscards`) or vendor-specific OIDs like `CISCO-QOS-PAYLOAD-MIB`. Monitoring drops is generally sufficient; if drops occur, troubleshoot and tune buffers/queues/thresholds accordingly.

http://www.cisco.com/en/US/prod/collateral/routers/ps341/product_data_sheet09186a0080116113.html

metadata.title: Cisco: Can (and should) I monitor the processing and memory load of QoS on a router?
metadata.llm_evaluation_reason: The original answer correctly identifies that QoS on the 7200 series is hardware-accelerated and appropriately suggests monitoring drops over raw CPU metrics. However, it oversimplifies the Route Processor's role (CPU is still required for policy evaluation, policing, and queue management) and lacks specific IOS commands or SNMP OIDs for practical QoS monitoring. The enriched version adds actionable monitoring commands, clarifies the hardware vs. control-plane split, and provides SNMP context.
metadata.problem_summary: User seeks methods to monitor the computational and resource strain (CPU cycles, memory/buffers) imposed by QoS policies on subscriber interfaces (PPP/L2TP and Ethernet/VLAN) on a Cisco 7206VXR router, and whether standard CPU/memory SNMP monitoring is sufficient or if specific QoS/metric tracking is required.

Instead, packet processing is limited by CEF switching rates (e.g., 1-2 MPPS depending on NPE), and monitoring should focus on packet drops rather than direct QoS resource metrics. Tuning buffers/queues/thresholds is recommended only if drops occur.

text:
The original answer correctly identifies ingress policing and egress shaping/classification as valid approaches for limiting NetApp sync traffic. However, the CLI syntax requires correction and platform context. Cisco IOS/IOS-XE requires rates in bits/sec, and modern switches use standard MQC instead of legacy `srr-queue` commands. Below is a corrected, platform-agnostic MQC example for a modern Catalyst 9300/9500 or IOS-XE switch. Note: Adjust the class-map match criteria to align with your actual NetApp sync protocol (iSCSI, FCoE, or proprietary). Always verify QoS policies against your specific switch hardware and IOS version.

```text
! Define class to match NetApp sync traffic (replace with actual ACL/protocol match)
class-map match-any NETAPP_SYNC
 match protocol iscsi
 ! Or: match access-group name NETAPP_ACL

! Ingress policing policy (hard limit to 800 Mbps)
policy-map INGRESS_LIMIT
 class NETAPP_SYNC
  police 800000000 1000000 exceed-action drop transmit
 class class-default
  police 200000000 1000000 exceed-action drop transmit

! Apply to access interface
interface GigabitEthernet1/0/1
 description NetApp-Controller-Link
 service-policy input INGRESS_LIMIT

! Egress shaping policy (congestion-based throttling)
policy-map EGRESS_SHAPE
 class NETAPP_SYNC
  shape average 800000000
 class class-default
  shape average 200000000

interface GigabitEthernet1/0/23
 description Uplink
 service-policy output EGRESS_SHAPE
```

**Version/Platform Context:** Legacy `srr-queue` commands are only valid on specific Catalyst platforms running IOS 12.2(50)SE or early 15.x. Modern IOS-XE (16.x+) and NX-OS use standard MQC (`shape average`, `police`, `dscp-marking`). For L2 LAG/bonded links, ensure QoS is applied to the port-channel interface, not individual member ports, to avoid asymmetric queuing.

metadata.title: QOS Layer 2 Link

However, the provided CLI syntax contains critical errors (`police 800M` is invalid; Cisco IOS/IOS-XE requires rate in bits/sec, e.g., `800000000`). The `srr-queue` commands are platform-specific legacy features (Catalyst 3750/3850) and are largely deprecated in modern IOS-XE in favor of standard Modular QoS CLI (MQC). The lack of switch model and IOS version context makes this potentially misleading for current deployments.

text:
Q: There is a command to increase the SoftMax Buffers on 3850 switches:
qos queue-softmax-multiplier 200
This is done in percent which means i am doubling the amount of softmax buffer.
Before, the SoftMax buffers for 10Gig interface are at 2000:
switch#sh platform hardware fed switch 2 qos queue config interface Te2/0/37
DATA Port:1 GPN:1425 AFD:Disabled QoSMap:0 HW Queues: 8 - 15
DrainFast:Disabled PortSoftStart:3 - 4500
----------------------------------------------------------
DTS Hardmax Softmax PortSMin GlblSMin PortStEnd
----- -------- -------- -------- -------- ---------
0 1 6 500 7 2000 7 1280 0 0 5 6000
1 1 4 0 8 3000 8 1920 3 750 5 6000
2 1 4 0 5 0 5 0 0 0 5 6000
3 1 4 0 5 0 5 0 0 0 5 6000
4 1 4 0 5 0 5 0 0 0 5 6000
5 1 4 0 5 0 5 0 0 0 5 6000
6 1 4 0 5 0 5 0 0 0 5 6000
7 1 4 0 5 0 5 0 0 0 5 6000
After the command, they increased to 4000:
swRZ101#sh platform hardware fed switch 2 qos queue config interface Te2/0/37
DATA Port:1 GPN:1425 AFD:Disabled QoSMap:0 HW Queues: 8 - 15
DrainFast:Disabled PortSoftStart:5 - 7500
----------------------------------------------------------
DTS Hardmax Softmax PortSMin GlblSMin PortStEnd
----- -------- -------- -------- -------- ---------
0 1 6 500 12 4000 7 1280 0 0 7 10000
1 1 4 0 13 6000 8 1920 3 750 7 10000
2 1 4 0 5 0 5 0 0 0 7 10000
3 1 4 0 5 0 5 0 0 0 7 10000
4 1 4 0 5 0 5 0 0 0 7 10000
5 1 4 0 5 0 5 0 0 0 7 10000
6 1 4 0 5 0 5 0 0 0 7 10000
7 1 4 0 5 0 5 0 0 0 7 10000
two questions arise from this:

[Score:2] Are these bytes or queues or potatoes? I couldnt find anyting online.
Cisco maintains documentation on its devices. For example, it took me about 30 seconds to find this by searching for cisco 3850 softmax buffer
. I have higlighted the relavant text from Catalyst 3850: Troubleshooting Output drops:
The Default Buffer allocation for a 1GB port is 300 buffers and for a 10GB port, it is 1800 buffers (1 buffer = 256 bytes).
What about the ressources if i increase this to the max (12x) on the switch? Im guessing, these buffers are comming from the RAM. Is there a process where i can observe the memory usage not to overcommit anything? This is a stack of switches with 246 interfaces total.
Cisco has many different command variations to show the memory usage, from very detailed to general. For example:
show memory statistics
will show the processor and I/O (what you want) memory statistics in a condensed format. You can use the ?
to see all the variations of the show memory
command.

--- ENRICHMENT ADDED ---
Clarification on Buffer Units & Memory Monitoring:
The values shown (e.g., 2000, 4000) are **buffer units**, where 1 unit = 256 bytes. Therefore, 2000 units = 512 KB. The `qos queue-softmax-multiplier` adjusts the dynamic soft limit for these units, not raw bytes or queue counts.

Increasing the multiplier (up to the platform max of 1200/12x) allocates more memory from the switch's DRAM to the Forwarding Engine Driver (FED) buffer pools. While this reduces tail drops, it increases latency and consumes system RAM that could otherwise be used for routing tables or process memory.

To monitor buffer pool utilization accurately, avoid `show memory statistics` (which shows overall system memory). Instead, use:
- `show platform hardware fed switch active qos buffer` (shows global buffer pool usage and fragmentation)
- `show platform hardware fed switch active interface te2/0/37 qos` (shows per-interface queue stats and drops)
- `show platform hardware fed switch active qos statistics` (monitors drop counters and buffer thresholds)

Adjust buffer multipliers incrementally in lab environments first, as overcommitting can lead to increased latency or FED process instability.

However, it fails to clarify that the output values are in 256-byte units (not raw bytes or queue counts), and incorrectly suggests `show memory statistics` for monitoring dedicated QoS/FED buffer pools. The technical core is sound but lacks precision for production troubleshooting.

text:
Q: I am looking out for a practical usage of WRED. After reading/searching I found that it is more suitable for TCP traffic. When the queue is full, all the TCP session at that time will start with slow window size. And they may grow linearly. However, by the time there window size goes to maximum, the queue might be empty. Thus, all will be running with a small window size. Thus, there is a slow start for all the TCP connections and the bandwidth is under utilized.
My questions is how significant it is. Let it grow linearly. How does it matter. Why bandwidth is still under-utilized? It will grow eventually. Why for a few seconds it matters so much. Is there any other use for WRED? Am I missing some main application here?

[Score:2] I think you are missing the point that there are likely multiple TCP connections trying to use the queue.
RED (or Cisco's version called WRED), fixes a problem particular to TCP. When a queue fills up, no other packets can be enqueued, and any arriving packets will then be dropped (tail drop). If there are multiple TCP connections, they will then all be subject to loss, and they can all slow down, then ramp up, synchronously (TCP global synchronization). This can alternately fill and starve the queue, and this is a poor use of the queue.
The "R" in RED stands for "Random", and it randomly drops packets to keep from filling the queue. This tends to even out the TCP flows, and helps prevent them from becoming synchronized.

metadata.title: Is WRED configured for TCP traffic only?
metadata.llm_evaluation_reason: The text accurately explains the core networking problem WRED addresses (TCP global synchronization caused by tail drop) and correctly describes how probabilistic dropping prevents flow synchronization. It lacks modern context such as the 'Weighted' mechanism (DSCP/IP Precedence-based drop thresholds) and the industry shift toward ECN (Explicit Congestion Notification), but the foundational explanation is technically sound. No commands or protocol versions are referenced, so no outdated technical debt is present.
metadata.problem_summary: User questions the practical necessity of WRED, specifically misunderstanding how TCP global synchronization occurs and why slow linear window growth negatively impacts queue utilization and bandwidth efficiency.
metadata.context_summary: This theoretical document examines Weighted Random Early Detection (WRED) configuration, clarifying whether it is exclusively applied to TCP traffic while detailing its relationship to Random Early Detection, tail drop, and global synchronization. It outlines how WRED proactively manages congestion by selectively dropping packets based on traffic class and queue depth to optimize network throughput and prevent performance degradation.

text:
Q: Regarding our current Cisco QoS implementation...
[Answer 1] Yes. When `mls qos trust dscp` is configured, the switch classifies the packet based on the incoming DSCP. On egress from a trunk port, the switch automatically maps the internal DSCP value to a CoS (802.1p) value using the DSCP-to-CoS mapping table. This mapping occurs regardless of whether a custom table is applied; if none is configured, the Cisco default table is used. The incoming CoS value is overwritten by this mapped value.
[Answer 2] Yes. When `mls qos trust cos` is configured, the switch classifies based on incoming CoS. On egress, it maps the internal CoS to a DSCP value using the CoS-to-DSCP mapping table (default or custom). The incoming DSCP is overwritten accordingly.
[Answer 3] No. The `trust` command only affects ingress classification and internal queuing; it does not strip or erase IP headers. The DSCP field (46) will remain intact in the packet unless explicitly modified by a policy-map (e.g., `set dscp` or `remark`). Trust mode has no effect on header preservation.
[Answer 4] Clarification on Default Trust & Fallback: On Catalyst 3850/4500 and Nexus platforms, the default trust mode is indeed DSCP. However, the 'fallback' behavior applies strictly to pure Layer 2 frames (e.g., ARP, untagged traffic with no IP header) that lack a DSCP field. For IP packets, even if DSCP=0, the switch will trust DSCP 0. Consequently, when egressing a trunk, DSCP 0 will map to CoS 0 (per the default DSCP-to-CoS table), effectively overwriting the original CoS marking. To preserve an incoming CoS value on IP packets, you must explicitly configure `mls qos trust cos` or use a policy-map to remark CoS/DSCP on egress.

Key Commands for Verification:
- `show qos maps dscp-to-cos` / `show qos maps cos-to-dscp`
- `show mls qos interface <int> trust`
- Note: IOS/IOS-XE uses `mls qos trust dscp`, while Nexus NX-OS uses `mls qos trust dscp` or `qos trust dscp`. Both platforms support dynamic DSCP-to-CoS mapping via `qos map dscp-to-cos`.

metadata.title: Cisco QoS trust and cos-dscp dscp-cos Mapping behaviors
metadata.llm_evaluation_reason: The original answers contain fundamental misconceptions about Cisco QoS trust behavior. Specifically, trust modes only dictate ingress classification and internal queuing; they do not strip or erase DSCP/CoS headers. The claim that mapping only occurs 'if a table map is defined' is incorrect because Cisco switches always use default DSCP-to-CoS and CoS-to-DSCP tables unless explicitly overridden. The explanation of the 'fallback' behavior is also muddled regarding pure Layer 2 vs. IP packets. The text requires correction to prevent misconfiguration in production environments.
metadata.problem_summary: Clarification on how Cisco switches handle DSCP and CoS trust modes, value mapping, and preservation during ingress/egress on Catalyst and Nexus platforms.
metadata.context_summary: Clarification on how Cisco switches handle DSCP and CoS trust modes, value mapping, and preservation during ingress/egress on Catalyst and Nexus platforms.

text:

In following command output, what exactly its saying, My Plan is to use this blade center for VMWare do you think following config will create any issue in my VMWare traffic? I have little knowledge of QoS so trying to understand impact.
swt-blade-1-a# show qos traffic-template egressdt
TRAFFIC-TEMPLATE: egressdt
Status : Active
Queue Egress Discard
Number Priorities Threshold Name
------ -------------- -------------- --------------------
1 0,1,2,3 Low best-effort-tcg
2 4,5,6,7 High control-tcg

A: Removing the QoS settings usually involves the statement with a no prepended, similar to Cisco devices.
qos dscp-map 111000 priority 7
qos dscp-map 110000 priority 6
qos dscp-map 101000 priority 5
qos dscp-map 100000 priority 4
qos dscp-map 011000 priority 3
qos dscp-map 010000 priority 2
qos dscp-map 001000 priority 1
qos dscp-map 000000 priority 0
These lines map IP ToS diffserve codepoints to the switch's (802.1p) priorities. Note that other codepoints may already have a default priority. You can check the current table with show qos dscp-map.
qos queue-config 2-queues
Configures the switch to use 2 queues (4 is default, 8 is also possible).
qos traffic-template "egressdt"
map-traffic-group 1 egress-discard-threshold low
exit
Creates a template with low priority for queue 1.
interface 1
policy traffic-template "egressdt"
...
exit
...
Applies the template to the desired interfaces.

**[Enrichment Added]**
**Exact Removal Commands:** To fully revert this configuration on this platform, you must explicitly negate each applied command:
- Under each interface: `no policy traffic-template "egressdt"`
- To delete the template: `no qos traffic-template "egressdt"`
- To remove maps/queues: `no qos dscp-map <dscp> priority <pri>` and `no qos queue-config`

**Platform & VMware Context:**
- The firmware `Z.14.44` belongs to the legacy HP ProCurve OS family. HP rebranded this line to Aruba (AOS-SW) in 2014, and the CLI syntax differs significantly from modern ArubaOS-CX or Cisco IOS-XE.
- For VMware traffic, this configuration only defines egress discard thresholds. It does not enforce strict prioritization or policing. VMware management and vMotion traffic generally perform well on best-effort queues, but if you observe latency or jitter, consider mapping vMotion (TCP 443) to a strict priority queue and enabling bandwidth limits if congestion occurs.
- Reference: HP ProCurve 6120 Blade Switches Advanced Traffic Management Guide (legacy documentation). Keep in mind that OpenFlow/SDN features are not supported on this legacy hardware family.

text:
**Answer:**

1. **Applying QoS on a Tunnel Interface:** Yes, you can apply a `service-policy` on a tunnel interface in Cisco IOS. However, for WAN prioritization, best practice is to **match and mark traffic** (e.g., set DSCP) before it enters the tunnel, and apply the queuing/scheduling `service-policy` on the **physical egress WAN interface**. Tunnel interfaces handle classification/encapsulation, while physical interfaces handle actual queuing.

2. **ISP Prioritization:** Unless you are using a managed service (MPLS, SD-WAN overlay, or paid DSCP transit), **ISPs do not honor QoS markings** (DSCP/CoS) on the public Internet. Packets will be treated on a best-effort basis across transit networks. Your local QoS will only guarantee priority within your own network and up to the ISP edge.

3. **Verification/Testing:**
   - Classification/Marking: `class-map match-any TUNNEL_TRAFFIC` -> `match access-group name ACL_PRIORITY` -> `policy-map WAN_PRIORITY` -> `class TUNNEL_TRAFFIC` -> `set dscp ef` (or `cs5`).
   - Application: `interface Tunnel0` -> `service-policy output TUNNEL_TRAFFIC` (for marking) OR `interface GigabitEthernet0/0` -> `service-policy output WAN_PRIORITY` (for queuing).
   - Test: `ping 1.1.1.1 dscp ef size 1500 repeat 1000` and monitor with `show policy-map interface Tunnel0` or `show policy-map interface <WAN-IF>`.
   - Verify marking with Wireshark/tcpdump on the tunnel interface before encapsulation.

*Note: For modern deployments, consider SD-WAN (e.g., Cisco Viptela/Catalyst Center) which handles application-aware QoS and dynamic path selection more effectively than static GRE+QoS.*

metadata.title: Qos implementation
metadata.llm_evaluation_reason: The original answer is technically accurate regarding ISP QoS limitations and the feasibility of local tunnel classification. However, it lacks actionable configuration steps, testing procedures, and best practices for WAN prioritization. It scores a 6 due to missing context needed for practical implementation in an enterprise environment.
metadata.problem_summary: Determining the feasibility, scope, and testing methodology for applying QoS on a GRE tunnel to prioritize traffic destined for a specific IP over a WAN link, particularly regarding ISP handling of QoS markers.
metadata.context_summary: The user asks if service-policy can be applied to a tunnel interface, whether ISP routers will honor QoS markings for the specified destination IP, and how to validate the configuration. The response clarifies that QoS is generally ignored by public Internet routers unless under a managed/MPLS agreement, but can be effectively used within the tunnel to prioritize internal flows.

text:
Q: It might be a stupid question, but as far as I understood the CWND dictates the rate of a TCP flow.
I created a "load-balancer" that split single packets on two paths of 10Mb/s. This introduces some reordering which prevents the growth of the congestion window. When I measure the CWND for a single path (through captcp) it is around 600KB and the final goodput (with iperf3) is ~9Mb/s, if I do it with the combined paths the CWND is around 200KB, but the final goodput is ~17Mb/s!
So how comes that the goodput is higher even if the cwnd is limiting only 200kb outstanding in the network?

A: TCP throughput is not dictated solely by CWND; it follows the relationship: Throughput ≈ (CWND × MSS) / RTT. A smaller CWND can still achieve higher throughput if the Round-Trip Time (RTT) is significantly lower or if the alternative path avoids congestion-induced drops. In your scenario, splitting traffic across two paths likely reduces per-flow RTT and distributes load, preventing queue buildup on a single link. Even with a 200KB CWND, if the RTT is halved or if the single 10Mb/s path suffers from congestion drops (e.g., via RED or tail drop), the effective throughput can exceed the reordered path's performance. Additionally, TCP reordering can trigger spurious retransmissions or slow-start behavior, artificially suppressing CWND growth. To properly manage bandwidth allocation, QoS mechanisms (like CBWFQ or Policing) should be used alongside TCP congestion control, but note that QoS does not override TCP's end-to-end AIMD algorithm—it merely shapes or marks traffic. For multipath scenarios, consider MPTCP or explicit load-balancing strategies that preserve packet order per flow to avoid TCP head-of-line blocking.

metadata.title: How can a TCP Flow with smaller congestion window have higher throughput?
metadata.llm_evaluation_reason: The original answer contains partially correct concepts but fails to address the core TCP mechanics. It incorrectly states that QoS dictates TCP flow rates, which is false; TCP's AIMD algorithm self-regulates based on end-to-end congestion signals. The answer lacks the critical relationship between CWND, RTT, and Bandwidth-Delay Product (BDP), and does not explain how multipath routing or reduced RTT can yield higher throughput with a smaller CWND. It is vague and potentially misleading regarding QoS and TCP dynamics.
metadata.problem_summary: The user observes that load balancing a single TCP flow across two paths causes packet reordering, which reduces the TCP congestion window (CWND) from ~600KB to ~200KB. Despite the smaller CWND, the combined path achieves higher goodput (~17Mb/s vs ~9Mb/s). The question asks why throughput increases when the CWND appears to restrict the amount of data in flight.
metadata.context_summary: This troubleshooting guide examines network scenarios where a TCP flow achieves higher throughput despite a smaller congestion window, focusing on the impact of RED queue management and QoS policies. It provides diagnostic steps to identify and resolve throughput anomalies caused by router-level congestion control and traffic shaping mechanisms.

text:
Q: It might be a basic question, but as far as I understood, the CWND dictates the rate of a TCP flow. I created a "load-balancer" that splits packets across two 10Mb/s paths. This introduces reordering, which prevents the congestion window from growing. When I measure the CWND for a single path (using captcp), it is around 600KB, and the final goodput (with iperf3) is ~9Mb/s. If I use the combined paths, the CWND drops to around 200KB, but the final goodput increases to ~17Mb/s! How can the goodput be higher even if the CWND is limiting only 200KB outstanding in the network?

A: The CWND does not directly dictate throughput; it works in conjunction with RTT and the TCP throughput formula: Throughput ≈ (CWND × MSS) / RTT. Your observation stems from three key factors:
1. **Per-Connection vs. Aggregate Metrics**: CWND is tracked per TCP connection, not per physical path. If your load balancer splits traffic without proper flow affinity, a single TCP flow experiences severe packet reordering. TCP interprets reordering as potential congestion, triggering duplicate ACKs and Fast Retransmit, which halves the CWND. However, the underlying links still deliver data at their combined rate.
2. **RTT and Link Utilization**: The ~17Mb/s goodput indicates both 10Mb/s paths are active. Even with a reduced CWND, if the RTT remains low and the network can absorb the reordering, the aggregate throughput can exceed a single path's capacity. The CWND drop is TCP's conservative reaction to reordering, not a hard cap on what the network can physically carry.
3. **Measurement Nuances**: Tools like `captcp` analyze pcap files per stream. If packets are out-of-order, `captcp` may misattribute retransmissions or late ACKs, artificially lowering the measured CWND. For accurate multi-path analysis, ensure flow-based load balancing (e.g., 5-tuple hashing) or use MPTCP, as packet-level splitting severely degrades standard TCP performance due to reordering penalties.

For QoS and SDN implementations, always use flow-aware load balancing (e.g., OpenFlow group tables with consistent hashing) to prevent reordering-induced CWND suppression.

metadata.title: How can a TCP Flow with smaller congestion window have higher throughput?
metadata.llm_evaluation_reason: The original text presents a valid networking scenario but contains a critical typo (CNWD) and a technically incomplete answer. It fails to explain the TCP throughput formula, the per-connection nature of CWND, or how packet reordering from naive load balancing triggers TCP's congestion control mechanisms. The enriched version corrects terminology, clarifies the underlying TCP mechanics, and adds context relevant to load-balancing best practices and SDN/QoS implementations.
metadata.problem_summary: Why does a TCP flow split across two 10Mb/s paths achieve higher total goodput (~17Mb/s) despite packet reordering limiting the congestion window (CWND) to ~200KB, compared to a single path with a 600KB CWND and ~9Mb/s goodput?
metadata.context_summary: Why does a TCP flow split across two 10Mb/s paths achieve higher total goodput (~17Mb/s) despite packet reordering limiting the congestion window (CWND) to ~200KB, compared to a single path with a 600KB CWND and ~9Mb/s goodput?

text:
Q: I'm running new IOS 15.4.3(M2) on 2911 and 15.4.3(S3) on ISR4331s. I have the following QoS policy:
policy-map WAN-EGRESS-POLICY
class CLASSIFY-AF41
bandwidth remaining percent 25
class CLASSIFY-AF31
bandwidth remaining percent 30
class CLASSIFY-AF21
bandwidth remaining percent 15
class class-default
random-detect dscp-based
In prior IOS versions, Cisco would allocate 25% to the default class, which leaves you to allocate 75% of the remaining bandwidth (or changeable with the max reserved bandwidth command). Now, Cisco allows you to configure up to 100%, however, I'm not clear on what "percentage" that leaves the default class.
For example, I have 70% assigned to classes above. My questions is:
Assuming full 100% allocation by default, what is allocated to the default class if it's not specified?

[Score:2] You are not assigning 70% of the bandwidth because you are using the remaining
keyword.
You are not reserving or dedicating the bandwidth you assign. If a class is not using all its bandwidth, other classes can use it, too. The default class gets to use any unreserved bandwidth that is not used. It could use up to 100% of the bandwidth, as can any of the classes, if it is not reserved. The bandwidth percent
command doesn't exclusively reserve bandwidth.
Cisco has a paper describing how this works:
How Is Unused Bandwidth Allocated?
This section explains how the queueing system distributes any remaining bandwidth. Here is how the Class-Based Weighted Fair Queueing Feature Overview describes the allocation mechanism: "If excess bandwidth is available, the excess bandwidth is divided amongst the traffic classes in proportion to their configured bandwidths. If not all of the bandwidth is allocated, the remaining bandwidth is proportionally allocated among the classes, based on their configured bandwidth." Let's look at two examples.
In the first example, policy-map foo guarantees 30 percent of the bandwidth to class bar and 60 percent of the bandwidth to class baz.
policy-map foo class bar bandwidth percent 30 class baz bandwidth percent 60
If you apply this policy to a 1 Mbps link, it means that 300 kbps is guaranteed to class bar, and 600 kbps is guaranteed to class baz. Importantly, 100 kbps is leftover for class-default. If class-default does not need it, the unused 100 kbps is available for use by class bar and class baz. If both classes need the bandwidth, they share it in proportion to the configured rates. In this configuration, the sharing ratio is 30:60 or 1:2.

metadata.title: Cisco QoS - Default Class Allocation
metadata.llm_evaluation_reason: Technically accurate explanation of CBWFQ weighted distribution. Commands and IOS versions are current and valid. Minor historical ambiguity does not impact core accuracy.
metadata.problem_summary: Clarification on how 'class-default' bandwidth allocation behaves when using 'bandwidth remaining percent' in Cisco IOS QoS policies, specifically addressing whether the default class automatically receives leftover bandwidth or requires explicit percentage configuration.

text:
Q: The context of my question is actually another question which is "Why isn't ingress shaping possible?". So yes, I get the whole "you can't affect packets you haven't received yet" idea, but we can't drop a packet before we get it either, yet we are able to do so with policing.
Thanks.

[Score:2] I get the whole ...
Apparently not. Your router cannot do anything with or about a packet until it has crossed the wire and arrived at the router. At that point, it can drop it. However, at that point, bandwidth has already been used. "Policing" doesn't change that.

metadata.title: Signals wise, how does dropping an ingress packet due to policing look like?
metadata.llm_evaluation_reason: Technically accurate for traditional QoS networking. Correctly addresses the core misconception that policing affects bandwidth consumption before packet arrival. The explanation is concise and correct, but lacks depth on hardware implementation (e.g., token bucket mechanisms, ASIC ingress classification, and how policing differs from shaping in practice). No version-specific commands or outdated references present. Suitable for a QoS-focused RAG database as-is.
metadata.problem_summary: Clarifies the architectural limitation of ingress traffic shaping versus policing by explaining that routers can only process packets after they have arrived and consumed bandwidth, making pre-arrival shaping impossible.
metadata.context_summary: Explains the architectural limitation of ingress traffic shaping versus policing by explaining that routers can only process packets after they have arrived and consumed bandwidth, making pre-arrival shaping impossible.

[Score:2] Sorry I don't have a system actually in front of me to get you actual output, but look for "show qos interface ..." and that should have the configured hardware values for all the QoS parameters.
The CLI is going to be something like:
show qos interface gigE 0/0/0/0.101 ingress
(it might actually be "input" or "output" instead of "ingress" or "egress" but with a little question-mark magic you'll be able to find it. You'll have a whole bunch of output that shows you all the policer rates, actions, and token bucket depths. It's actually a very useful little command. ;-)

[ENRICHMENT]
- **Syntax Correction**: IOS-XR exclusively uses `ingress` and `egress`. `input`/`output` are legacy IOS terms and will result in a syntax error in IOS-XR.
- **Retrieving Configured Rates**: `show qos interface` primarily displays hardware QoS statistics and policy bindings. To view the explicitly configured policer rate, use `show policy-map name <policy-name>` or `show running-policy-map`.
- **Platform & Version Context**: IOS-XR 5.x through 7.x maintains consistent QoS CLI syntax. However, policer rate visibility in `show policy-map interface` depends on the specific QoS template and forwarding plane (e.g., ASR9K QFP vs. NCS 5500/585 ASICs). For hardware-specific token bucket depths and actual enforced rates, platform commands like `show hardware <slot> <subslot> <card> qfp` or `show policy-map class <class-name>` may be required.

text:
Q: I have some ME3800's running 15.3(3)S which aggregate layer 2 circuits from customers. They all have a similar configuration of an incoming trunk on Gi0/1 and then Gi0/23 & Gi0/24 are layer 3 p-t-p links to neighbouring ME3x00 switches;
gi0/23--SW1---gi0/23 gi0/24---SW2---gi0/23 gi0/24---SW3---gi0/23
| | |
| | |
gi0/1 gi0/1 gi0/1
I am trying to apply some QoS on this network. I can apply my policies to the L3 interfacings linking the switches together but I can't apply the same QoS policy to the L2 trunks.
SW1#show run int gi0/1
Building configuration...
Current configuration : 93 bytes
!
interface GigabitEthernet0/1
description ****ACCESS-UPLINK****
switchport mode trunk
end
The reason is because the QoS policies are written for layer 3 interfaces, when I try to apply them to this trunk interface I get the following error;
SW1(config-if)#service-policy input PE-QOS-OUT
QoS: Configuration failed. 'match qos-group' not allowed in an input policy-map
QoS: Configuration failed. 'match qos-group' not allowed in an input policy-map
QoS: Configuration errors for policymap PE-QOS-OUT
I am wondering how I can write a policy for layer 2 trunk interfaces; I could apply the layer 3 policy map to each SVI on this switch that terminates each VLAN being trunked up but surely the QoS policy on an SVI doesn't look at the ingress/egress interface of a frame and examine the congestion for that interface? How coule the SVI policy manage the layer 2 turnk?
Short question: To QoS a layer 2 interface is the only (best?) way to break it down into service instances?
Long question: Would I have to convert those interfaces to use service instances and then add a service instance for each VLAN being trunked in; Then I could apply a parent policy to the interface which has various classes defined, each class matches one of the VLANs/service instances being trunked in and then apply the same child policy to each VLAN/service instace which would be the layer 3 one I originally tied to apply?
A snippest of the layer 3 policy I am using;
class-map match-any NC
match mpls experimental topmost 6
match mpls experimental topmost 7
match dscp cs6
match dscp cs7
match cos 6
match cos 7
class-map match-any REALTIME
match mpls experimental topmost 5
match dscp ef
match dscp cs5
match cos 5
class-map match-any NC-QG
match qos-group 6
match dscp cs6
match dscp cs7
class-map match-any REALTIME-QG
match qos-group 5
policy-map PE-QOS-IN
class NC
set qos-group 6
class REALTIME
set qos-group 5
policy-map PE-QOS-OUT
class NC-QG
bandwidth percent 2
queue-limit 1024000 bytes
class REALTIME-QG
police cir percent 10
conform-action transmit
exceed-action drop
priority
queue-limit 1024000 bytes

[Score:2] It seems I knew the answer to my own question already essentially;
To aggregate layer 2 links and QoS them I must define the service insances to match each VLAN (rather than one VLAN per cusomer the same set of VLANs are being trunked down to all customers so they all fall under one class-map);
class-map match-any tripple-play
match service instance ethernet 10
match service instance ethernet 20
match service instance ethernet 30
policy-map PE-QOS-CPE-OUT-PARENT
class tripple-play
service-policy PE-QOS-CPE-OUT
interface gi0/1
switchport trunk allowed vlan none
switchport mode trunk
service-policy input PE-QOS-CPE-IN
service-policy output PE-QOS-CPE-OUT-PARENT
service instance 10 ethernet
description voice
encapsulation dot1q 10
rewrite ingress tag pop 1 symmetric
bridge-domain 10
service instance 20 ethernet
description video
encapsulation dot1q 20
rewrite ingress tag pop 1 symmetric
bridge-domain 20
service instance 30 ethernet
description data
encapsulation dot1q 30
rewrite ingress tag pop 1 symmetric
bridge-domain 30

text: The Cisco 877 series (ISR G1) does not support Hierarchical QoS (nested policy-maps) in hardware. The parent-policy wrapping service-policy RDP will cause the router to ignore the nested class or default to class-default. Simplify the configuration by applying the RDP policy directly to the interface: service-policy output RDP. On the 877, QoS is typically applied to the physical WAN interface (e.g., FastEthernet 0) rather than an SVI, unless you are using PPPoE or NAT where the SVI represents the external interface. Ensure your IOS version supports priority percent (IOS 12.4+), and verify ACL 145 is correctly matching the intended traffic using show access-lists 145.

metadata.title: QoS not matching traffic
metadata.llm_evaluation_reason: The original answer correctly identifies the core architectural limitation (HQoS) but incorrectly implies it is specific to SVIs. The Cisco 877 series lacks hardware support for hierarchical QoS entirely. The enrichment clarifies the platform limitation, corrects the interface placement recommendation, and adds practical verification steps for ACL matching and IOS syntax compatibility.
metadata.problem_summary: QoS traffic matches the default class instead of the prioritized class due to applying hierarchical QoS on physical interfaces of a Cisco 877, which does not support HQoS on physical ports or SVIs.
metadata.context_summary: User is trying to prioritize RDP traffic using nested policy-maps (HQoS) on a Cisco 877 router but traffic falls to class-default. The solution indicates HQoS is unsupported on physical interfaces of this platform and SVIs also lack HQoS capability, requiring a simplified single-level QoS configuration on the SVI.
metadata.code_block:
class-map match-any RDP
match access-group 145
policy-map RDP
class RDP
priority percent 70
class class-default
fair-queue
policy-map parent-policy
class class-default
shape average 458752
service-policy RDP
int FastEthernet 0
description WAN
service-policy output parent-policy
int FastEthernet 1
description LAN
access-list 145 permit ip any host XXX.XXX.XXX.XXX

text:
The original answer is technically sound for a Catalyst 3750 running classic Cisco IOS. For a RAG database, it benefits from a concrete configuration example and clarification on how multilayer switches handle DSCP marking despite operating primarily at Layer 2. Below is an enriched version with a typical IOS QoS policy example and context on trust boundaries.

**Key Context:**
- Catalyst 3750 switches are multilayer devices capable of inspecting and marking L3 headers (DSCP/ToS) even when ports operate as access/trunk L2 interfaces.
- IP phones typically mark their own traffic (EF/46 for voice, AF31/26 for signaling). The switch should usually `trust dscp` rather than remap it, to avoid double-marking or policy conflicts.
- If strict VLAN-based remapping is required, use `mls qos vlan-based` with class/policy maps.

**Example IOS Configuration:**
```cisco
! Enable QoS globally
mls qos

! Trust DSCP on the access port connected to the IP Phone
interface GigabitEthernet0/1
 mls qos trust dscp
 spanning-tree portfast
!

! VLAN-based QoS remapping example (VLAN 30 -> DSCP EF)
mls qos vlan-based
!
class-map match-any VLAN30_VOICE
 match access-group name 100
!
access-list 100 permit ip any any
!
policy-map VLAN30_DSCP_EF
 class VLAN30_VOICE
  set dscp ef
!
interface Vlan30
 service-policy input VLAN30_DSCP_EF
```

**Version Note:** The `mls qos` architecture is specific to older Catalyst IOS (12.2(50)SE and similar). Modern platforms (Catalyst 9000, Nexus) use a unified QoS model (`policy-map` applied directly to interfaces or `system qos`). Always verify the exact IOS version with `show version` before applying QoS policies.

metadata.title: QoS - Add DSCP EF tag with L2 switches?
metadata.llm_evaluation_reason: Technically accurate for the specified hardware but lacks configuration examples and explicit version context, making it less ideal for direct RAG ingestion.
