Q: I'm trying to understand the basic mechanics of how throttling works. Here are two scenarios:
Scenario 1: A hotel offers a fast and slow connection option to its guests. Let's say the slow plan is 500Kbps. But the hotel's ISP connection is 1Gbps. If there is only one guest accessing the network and that guest is using the slow plan his data is presumably reaching the hotel router at a faster rate than the LAN is allowing it to be conveyed to the guest's device. To me this implies the packets are queuing up in a sense or being cached somewhere. Is this accurate? If so where does this queuing occur? Does it happen on the hotel network or is it more likely the ISP would be doing the throttling on the hotel's behalf
Scenario 2: If an ISP decides to throttle its users I would expect a similar queuing to occur at the edge of its network if the sum total of the permitted throughput on the ISP's own network is slower than the rate at which packets arrive from backbone providers or networks it is peered with.
For both scenarios I'm trying to wrap my head around how the queuing or caching (not sure what is the correct term) of packets works. I'm assuming that the packets are forced to sit in memory on networking equipment. But that must carry a cost and overhead that I've never heard mentioned when people talk about bandwidth throttling. And furthermore it looks to me like an external cost that the throttled network imposes on the adjacent unthrottled networks.
I'm not a network engineer so doubtless I am not grasping some concepts about how throttling works. I would be very grateful if someone would enlighten me.

[Score:5] Mostly, the packets get dropped. Some packets may get buffered in queues, but the queues aren't very big. Policing is probably what the hotel uses, and policing is simply dropping traffic in excess of the allowed bandwidth. With TCP (HTTP runs on TCP), the TCP will react to lost packets and slow down, shrinking the window size.
Even with queuing, you probably run into some variant RED (Random Early Detection), which randomly drops queued packets in order to prevent queues from filling, triggering TCP to slow down. If buffers fill and drop packets based on full buffers, different TCP flows can become synchronized (not good).
The ISP probably just limits the actual link speed (DSL and cable can limit the number of channels available to a customer). This is the easiest thing to do.

metadata.title: How does bandwidth throttling work
metadata.llm_evaluation_reason: The response accurately covers fundamental QoS mechanics for bandwidth throttling. It correctly distinguishes between policing (drop excess) and shaping (buffer/delay), accurately describes TCP congestion control behavior (window reduction on packet loss), and properly identifies RED/WRED and TCP global synchronization. The explanation of ISP throttling is slightly oversimplified (physical channel limitation vs. logical QoS policing/shaping at the CMTS/DSLAM), but remains conceptually sound for a non-engineer audience. No code blocks or version-specific claims require validation.
metadata.problem_summary: Understanding the mechanical process of network throttling, specifically whether packets are queued/cached or dropped, where this occurs in the network path, and how TCP protocols adapt to throttled bandwidth.
metadata.context_summary: A user asks whether throttling works by queuing/caching packets or dropping them, where this happens (hotel router vs ISP), and how TCP reacts. The answer explains that throttling primarily relies on policing (dropping excess traffic) and TCP's congestion control (shrinking window size), with queuing being minimal due to small buffers and RED mechanisms to prevent synchronization issues.

text:
Q: I have the following Questions regarding Delays and throughput
- Is transmission rate same as Bandwidth?
- What is throughput and while calculating throughput why do we
consider transmission delay. Shouldn't we also consider the
propagation delay?
I know the question might sound silly,but please help.

[Score:3] Is transmission rate same as Bandwidth?
In essence yes, with some small adjustments for the difference between the clocking of the media ("bandwidth") and the actual rate at which packets are accepted onto the media (ie, minus minor overheads like ethernet headers, SDH segmentation, forward-error control, tunnelling like VLANs, MPLS, GRE).
We can do "traffic shaping" in the router so that the packet transmission rate presented to the media is substantially less than the available transmission rate of the media. This is how you can order a 200Mbps service over a gigabit ethernet fibre.
What is throughput
Throughput is the speed visible to the application.
Often we aren't so much interested in the throughput of a link as the "end-to-end throughput" between two applications communicating over a path consisting of multiple links and routers. End-to-end throughput can be affected by the lowest transmission rate of the links on the path; the path error rate, packet re-ordering, latency and jitter affecting the TCP congestion-control algorithm; the path MTU and packet re-ordering affecting operating system efficiency; the choice of TCP congestion control algorithm used.
why do we consider transmission delay
We usually don't. We used to, since getting a 1.5KB of packet onto the wire took some time at 9600bps. But at the high data rates in modern networks the number is so small as to be safely ignored outside of weird situations.
Shouldn't we also consider the propagation delay?
Also called "latency". Latency remains important as it's the one performance factor in a global network which isn't improving rapidly. As everything else improves then avoiding latency becomes increasingly important to improving performance. This has effects from avoiding round-trips packet exchanges in application and protocol designs; fielding new protocols which move data closer to their endpoint; and architectural responses such as content distribution networks.

metadata.title: Delays and throughput
metadata.llm_evaluation_reason: Technically accurate and practical explanation of fundamental networking concepts. Correctly addresses the distinction between bandwidth and transmission rate, outlines key throughput bottlenecks (TCP congestion control, path MTU, reordering), and accurately contextualizes why propagation delay dominates modern networks. The statement that transmission delay is 'usually not' considered is a valid practical simplification for high-speed links, though it slightly oversimplifies its formal role in the total delay equation (L/R). No outdated versions, commands, or hallucinations detected. Meets KEEP criteria (7-10).
metadata.problem_summary: Clarification on fundamental networking performance metrics: the distinction between transmission rate and bandwidth, the definition of end-to-end throughput, and why transmission delay is often negligible compared to propagation delay (latency) in modern high-speed networks.
metadata.context_summary: Clarification on fundamental networking performance metrics: the distinction between transmission rate and bandwidth, the definition of end-to-end throughput, and why transmission delay is often negligible compared to propagation delay (latency) in modern high-speed networks.

text:
Q: I have the following Questions regarding Delays and throughput
- Is transmission rate same as Bandwidth?
- What is throughput and while calculating throughput why do we consider transmission delay. Shouldn't we also consider the propagation delay?

A: In data networking, 'bandwidth' and 'transmission rate' are often used interchangeably, but they have distinct technical meanings. Strictly speaking, bandwidth refers to the frequency range (Hz) a physical channel can carry, while transmission rate (or data rate) is the speed of bit transfer (bps). Channel capacity (maximum data rate) is determined by bandwidth and signal-to-noise ratio per the Shannon-Hartley theorem.

Throughput is the actual rate of successful data delivery over a period, calculated as: Throughput = Data Volume / Total Transfer Time. Total transfer time includes transmission delay (L/R), propagation delay (d/s), processing delay, and queuing delay. While transmission delay is directly tied to link capacity, propagation delay primarily affects latency, not raw throughput. However, in TCP/IP, high propagation delay reduces effective throughput unless the TCP receive window is large enough to keep the pipe full (Bandwidth-Delay Product). Therefore, both delays impact end-to-end performance, but throughput is fundamentally bottlenecked by the slowest link's transmission rate and protocol efficiency.

metadata.title: Delays and throughput
metadata.llm_evaluation_reason: Generally correct but conceptually vague. The distinction between signal bandwidth (Hz) and data rate (bps) is accurate, but the explanation of throughput vs. delay lacks critical networking context (e.g., bottleneck links, TCP windowing, and the bandwidth-delay product). No outdated commands or versions are present.
metadata.problem_summary: Clarification of fundamental networking concepts: distinguishing transmission rate from bandwidth, defining throughput, and determining whether propagation delay is factored into throughput calculations.
metadata.context_summary: Clarification of fundamental networking concepts: distinguishing transmission rate from bandwidth, defining throughput, and determining whether propagation delay is factored into throughput calculations.

text:
Q: I have the following Questions regarding Delays and throughput
- Is transmission rate same as Bandwidth?
- What is throughput and while calculating throughput why do we consider transmission delay. Shouldn't we also consider the propagation delay?
I know the question might sound silly,but please help.

[Score:0] In networking theory, **transmission rate** (or data rate) refers to the speed at which bits are pushed onto the physical medium (e.g., 1 Gbps link speed), while **bandwidth** typically refers to the theoretical maximum capacity of the channel or a guaranteed/committed rate in QoS configurations. In practice, they are often used interchangeably, but technically differ when traffic management policies are applied.

**Throughput** is the actual rate of successful data delivery over a period. It is influenced by all four types of delay: transmission, propagation, queuing, and processing. You should absolutely consider propagation delay. In TCP networks, throughput is heavily constrained by Round-Trip Time (RTT), which includes propagation delay. This relationship is quantified by the **Bandwidth-Delay Product (BDP)**: `BDP = Max Bandwidth × RTT`. The BDP represents the maximum amount of unacknowledged data that can be "in flight." If the TCP congestion/window size is smaller than the BDP, throughput will be limited by latency (propagation delay) rather than the link's transmission capacity.

Regarding your QoS example: If you contract for 200 Mbps over a 1 Gbps fiber link, the provider uses a **policer** to immediately drop or remark excess traffic, while a **shaper** would buffer and delay packets to smooth bursts and conform to the 200 Mbps rate. Both mechanisms directly impact observed throughput and QoS compliance.

Q: Today I was pinging random websites, and I decided to ping a website, from which I was downloading at the speed of 2mb/s, and the ping(latency) was 10 ms.
My question is, if the answer from the server comes for around 10ms, how are these 2mb/s calculated? Any hints, links to explanations or similar are highly appreciated :)

[Score:1] Your ping time latency is how long it takes to get from one end to the other, and it has nothing to do with bandwidth which is how many bits are transferred per second.
Think of latency as the distance between two cities. It takes a fixed amount of time to drive between the two cities. Bandwidth is like how many people you can fit in each vehicle traveling between the cities; a bus has more bandwidth than a two-seater car. It takes the bus and the car the same amount of time (latency) to get from one city to the other, but the bus delivers more people (bandwidth) in that same amount of time.

text:
Q: Today I was pinging random websites, and I decided to ping a website, from which I was downloading at the speed of 2mb/s, and the ping(latency) was 10 ms.
My question is, if the answer from the server comes for around 10ms, how are these 2mb/s calculated? Any hints, links to explanations or similar are highly appreciated :)

[Score:1] Everything that has been written is true, but if you're using TCP, there is a strong correlation between latency and bandwidth.
TCP does not wait for an acknowledgement to send the next packet. It's using a window. When the window is full, the peer must wait for an ack. Moreover, the faster ACKs are received, the faster the window can grow (it's the slow start algorithm).
The stability of the latency can also come into play.

[Added Context for RAG]:
The theoretical maximum throughput is constrained by the Bandwidth-Delay Product (BDP): `BDP = Bandwidth × RTT`. The TCP receive window must be ≥ BDP to saturate the link. If the window is smaller, throughput is artificially capped.
- **Slow Start**: Congestion window (cwnd) doubles per RTT. Lower latency = shorter RTT = faster window growth in wall-clock time.
- **TCP Window Scaling (RFC 7323)**: Default 16-bit window caps at 64KB. Modern stacks enable scaling (up to 1GB) to support high-BDP links.
- **Practical Calculation**: 2 MB/s at 10 ms RTT requires a window of ~20 KB (2,048,000 bytes/s × 0.01 s). This fits within default TCP limits, explaining why the connection performs well.
- **Stability Note**: Jitter or packet loss triggers TCP congestion control (reducing cwnd), immediately dropping throughput regardless of raw bandwidth.

text:
Q: Different types of Ethernet (standard Ethernet, Fast Ethernet, Gigabit Ethernet) have different speed of data transfer (10 Mbit, 100 Mbit, 1 Gbit). Why is that?
On the physical layer, the speed of electrical signals (voltage change) stays the same. The cable (twisted pair) is also the same (I think). So why does the maximum bandwidth differ? If the reason is that in the past, we didn't have so much processing power in network cards, why doesn't the protocol allow for bandwidth negotiation (that would suit both devices that communicate)?

[Score:8] Different types of Ethernet (standard Ethernet, Fast Ethernet, Gigabit Ethernet) have different speed of data transfer (10 Mbit, 100 Mbit, 1 Gbit). Why is that?
Progress - technology advances.
Including obsolete and brand new physical layers, Ethernet ranges from 1 Mbit/s to 800 Gbit/s.
On the physical layer, the speed of electrical signals (voltage change) stays the same.
The wave propagation speed depends on the medium, not the signaling rate. Cat-3 is slightly slower than Cat-5 and fiber. Coax (from ancient 10BASE5) and Cat-7/8 are the fastest - they're all close though, 60% to 80% speed of light in vacuum.
However, the frequencies that pass over the medium differ considerably - from 10 MHz for 10BASEx to ca. 1.6 GHz for 40GBASE-T. Of course, higher frequency means faster voltage change. On fiber, the current generation runs 100 Gbit/s per lane.
The cable (twisted pair) is also the same (I think).
Well, its 'twisted pair' but there are several cable grades, differing in frequency response, attenuation and crosstalk mostly. Cat-3 only supports 10 Mbit/s, Cat-5e up to 1 Gbit/s, 10 Gbit/s requires Cat-6A, and 25/40 Gbit/s require Cat-8 cable rated at 2 GHz. The cable is only the same (Cat-5e) for 100BASE-TX and 1000BASE-T due to a leap in encoding technology.
So why does the maximum bandwidth differ? If the reason is that in the past, we didn't have so much processing power in network cards, why doesn't the protocol allow for bandwidth negotiation (that would suit both devices that communicate)?
Yes. Faster data rates mean faster processing and more elaborate encoding using more silicon (=transistors). Also, back in the 80s and early 90s, computers lacked the internal bandwidth to make use of faster data rates - they even struggled with 10 Mbit/s for a while. Literally nobody would have had a use for 10+ Gbit/s back then.
Ethernet does allow for speed negotiation on twisted pair - faster (and some intermediate) rates have been added for a while:
Fiber variants currently add 50, 100, 200, 400, and 800 Gbit/s (2024).

text:
Q: Different types of Ethernet (standard Ethernet, Fast Ethernet, Gigabit Ethernet) have different speed of data transfer (10 Mbit, 100 Mbit, 1 Gbit). Why is that?
On the physical layer, the speed of electrical signals (voltage change) stays the same. The cable (twisted pair) is also the same (I think). So why does the maximum bandwidth differ? If the reason is that in the past, we didn't have so much processing power in network cards, why doesn't the protocol allow for bandwidth negotiation (that would suit both devices that communicate)?

[Score:4] Well, most devices that support 1000Base-T will also negotiate to 100Base-TX and 10Base-T.
Older devices that are 10Base-T cannot negotiate to 100Base-TX or 1000Base-T, nor can devices that are 100Base-TX do 1000Base-T (although they can probably do 10Base-T), because those standards did not exist when the older devices were built.
There is a lot more to this than just the speed. For example, the encoding used by each is different. It is fairly simple to put in backwards compatibility, but how would you propose to build in for a standard that does not yet exist?
The cable (twisted pair) is also the same (I think).
There are actually different cable categories. The currently registered cable categories are 3, 5e, 6, 6a and 8 (new). Each category can handle up to a certain frequency that determines the bandwidth you can get for ethernet.

It outlines the technical specifications and performance capabilities that define each networking generation.

text: The bandwidth differences between Ethernet generations stem from three main factors: encoding schemes, cable specifications, and hardware evolution. First, the 'speed of electrical signals' (baud rate) is not uniform. 10BASE-T uses Manchester encoding (1 bit per baud), 100BASE-TX uses 4B/5B line coding, and 1000BASE-T uses PAM-5 signaling with advanced DSP. This allows higher data rates without proportionally increasing the raw signal frequency. Second, the claim that 'the cable is the same' is incorrect. 10BASE-T operates on Cat3, 100BASE-TX requires Cat5, and 1000BASE-T requires Cat5e/6 due to stricter near-end crosstalk (NEXT) and attenuation limits. Third, auto-negotiation was standardized in IEEE 802.3u (1997) for Fast Ethernet. Early coaxial standards (10BASE2/5) used a shared bus topology with 50-ohm terminations; attempting higher speeds would cause severe impedance mismatches, signal reflections, and EMI, making reliable communication impossible. Modern NICs leverage advanced CMOS and ASICs to handle complex encoding, which was prohibitively expensive and power-intensive in the 1980s.

text:
Q: I dont know where to ask this question. So i think its best suited for networking community.
As fiber optic transmit data using light. So we are getting/sending data at the speed of light ??
I did some simple distance and speed calculations but whatever the pings that we get does not add up to the pure calculations.
So, what are the hurdles ? What slows down Internet ?

[Score:5] From Wikipedia:
A typical singlemode fiber used for telecommunications has a cladding made of pure silica, with an index of 1.444 at 1500 nm, and a core of doped silica with an index around 1.4475.[48] The larger the index of refraction, the slower light travels in that medium. From this information, a simple rule of thumb is that a signal using optical fiber for communication will travel at around 200,000 kilometers per second. To put it another way, the signal will take 5 milliseconds to travel 1,000 kilometers in fiber.
By the way, pings are not a reliable way to measure latency. The process on a host that responds to echo requests is usually a low priority process, so there can be significant delays on a busy device.

text:
Q: I dont know where to ask this question. So i think its best suited for networking community.
As fiber optic transmit data using light. So we are getting/sending data at the speed of light ??
I did some simple distance and speed calculations but whatever the pings that we get does not add up to the pure calculations.
So, what are the hurdles ? What slows down Internet ?

[Score:2] As fiber optic transmit data using light. So we are getting/sending data at the speed of light ??
The speed of light in fiber is not the same as the speed of light in free space. Typically the speed of light in fiber is about two thirds of the speed of light in free space.
So, what are the hurdles ? What slows down Internet ?
Many many things, but two main ones.
Firstly your traffic rarely goes in a straight line from A to B, networks operate in a limited number of locations, interconnected by a limited number of paths, furthermore those paths themselves. The paths themselves are also likely to not be straight. In general Internet providers route for cost and bandwidth, not for minimum latency.
Secondly your packet may spend some time waiting in queues to be forwarded or processed.
There is also the time to actually serialise the packet onto the fiber, but at modern network speeds this is fairly small. At 1 gigabit per second a max-size packet is about 2.4km long, at 10 gigabit per second this drops to about 240 meters long.

text:
Q: I dont know where to ask this question. So i think its best suited for networking community.
As fiber optic transmit data using light. So we are getting/sending data at the speed of light ??
I did some simple distance and speed calculations but whatever the pings that we get does not add up to the pure calculations.
So, what are the hurdles ? What slows down Internet ?

[Score:1] You seem to be confusing some things.
Yes, the light in a fiber optic cable travels at the speed of light in the medium (glass fiber), which is slower than the speed of light in a vacuum.
Where I think you are confused is that a sending device will only serialize data onto the fiber at a given rate. A 1 Gbps interface will serialize 1,000,000,000 bits of information in one second, so the first bits are arriving at the destination while the last bits have not yet been serialized on the fiber.
When sending data on a network, the data must be framed so that the receiver knows where the data start, and where they end. The data must then go up through the network stack at the receiver, be processed, and a reply generated and sent back in the same way the data were sent in the first place. All that adds a lot of time when comparing the latency of a ping request/reply.
The request data end up going through two network stacks and the length of the cable, being serialized/deserialized at whatever speed the interface does, and travel the length of the fiber. The request is processed, a reply is generated, and it must do the same thing the request did. The reply is processed, and the time difference noted. The speed of light plays only a very small part in all that.
Also, using copper cabling will result in basically the same time as fiber optic cabling with the same serialization rate because electrons move through the copper at the speed of light in the medium, and that is very close to the speed of light in a glass fiber.

text:
Q: According to my textbook, with TDM, each circuit gets all of the bandwidth periodically during brief intervals of time (that is, during slots).
If we have a circuit switched network where each link uses TDM with 24 slots and has a bit rate of 1.536 Mbps. Also suppose we have to send a file of 640,000 bits.
How come we divide the bit rate (bandwidth) by 24? I thought each slot gets the full 1.536 Mbps?

[Score:5] According to my textbook, with TDM, each circuit gets all of the bandwidth periodically during brief intervals of time (that is, during slots).
Yes, that's correct.
If we have a circuit switched network where each link uses TDM with 24 slots and has a bit rate of 1.536 Mbps.
That's typical for T1 line, in which line is made from 24 DS0 64kbit/s channels.
How come we divide the bit rate (bandwidth) by 24? I thought each slot gets the full 1.536 Mbps?
No, the line would be then 24x1.5Mbit/s, not 1.5Mbit/s. The whole idea of TDM is that you take a line, clocked appropriately (for T1 it's usually Stratum 3 class clock), to fit a defined number of DS0 channels. Your specific service can get one DS0 (64kbit/s), two (128kbit/s), three (192kbit/s) - and up to full bandwidth of the link (1.5Mbit/s).
Think about it this way: each second, 1.5Mbit is sent over the link according to clocking of the line. Depending on how many channels (DS0's) you were allocated in your service specification, you'll be able to send that much of bits over the link.
BTW, in Europe we use E1's, that have 30 channels, each at 64kbit/s for 2Mbit/s, but again, the service can get anything from one channel up to full set of channels - to the level of 2Mbit/s.

No code blocks or version-specific commands to update. While outside the primary focus areas (SDN, Mininet, etc.), the networking fundamentals are sound and require no changes.

text:
Q: According to my textbook, with TDM, each circuit gets all of the bandwidth periodically during brief intervals of time (that is, during slots).
If we have a circuit switched network where each link uses TDM with 24 slots and has a bit rate of 1.536 Mbps. Also suppose we have to send a file of 640,000 bits.
How come we divide the bit rate (bandwidth) by 24? I thought each slot gets the full 1.536 Mbps?

[Score:3] The transmission rate 1.536 MBps is the full transmission rate of the link. In TDM, each link is divided up into time slots for each channel. In this example, there are 24 slots so each channel's transmission rate is 1/24 * the full transmission rate.
I just came across this example in Kurose and Ross so here's some explicit working out in case anyone still has this question and isn't familiar with all the terminology in the accepted answer:
Time taken, T = time to establish connection + time to transmit the file
T = 500 msec + 640,000 bits / (1.536 Mbps / 24)
= 0.5 s + 640,000 bits / (1.536 (Mb/s) / 24)
= 0.5 s + (640,000 * 24 / 1.536) b/(Mb/s)
= 0.5 s + (640,000 * 24 / 1.536) b/(Mb) s
= 0.5 s + (640,000 * 24 / 1.536)/1,000,000 s
= 0.5 s + (640 * 24 / 1.536)/1,000 s
= 0.5 s + (640 * 24 / 1536) s
= 0.5 s + 10 s = 10.5s

text:
The bottleneck principle is correct: the 10 MBps (80 Mbps) internet link is the slowest component compared to the Gigabit NIC (~125 MBps), PCI bus (~133 MBps), and disk (~70 MBps). However, 100 seconds is a theoretical maximum. Real-world throughput will be lower due to:
- **Protocol Overhead:** TCP/IP headers (~40 bytes) reduce effective throughput by ~10-15% with standard 1500-byte MTU.
- **TCP Window & Latency:** Throughput is capped by (Window Size / RTT). High latency or default TCP window settings will likely cap speed below 10 MBps.
- **Disk I/O & Caching:** Modern OSes use page cache, which masks raw disk bottlenecks but can introduce write-back latency.
- **Unit Consistency:** 10 MBps = 80 Mbps. Ensure calculations use consistent units.
A more realistic estimate would be 7-8 MBps, extending transfer time to ~125-143 seconds for a 1000 MB file, depending on RTT and TCP tuning.

text:
The bottleneck principle is correct: end-to-end throughput is limited by the slowest link or component in the path. However, your calculation requires critical clarifications:
1. **Units Matter**: Networking bandwidth is universally measured in *bits* per second (Mbps/Gbps), not bytes (MBps). If the link is truly 10 MBps (80 Mbps), 1000 MB / 10 MBps = 100 seconds is theoretically correct for raw payload.
2. **Protocol Overhead**: Ethernet framing, IP/TCP headers, and TCP windowing reduce effective throughput. Real-world WAN transfers typically achieve 80-90% of the theoretical bottleneck.
3. **Server & Path Factors**: Actual transfer time depends on the server's upstream/downstream capacity, disk I/O (sequential vs random), OS network stack tuning, and TCP congestion control. Latency (RTT) and QoS policies also significantly impact large file transfers.
For accurate network engineering assessments, always specify units, account for ~10-15% protocol overhead, and model the full path including MTU and buffer sizes.

text:
Q: Assuming the internet link is 10 Mbps (not MBps), the NIC is 1 Gbps, PCI bus is ~1.06 GB/s, and disk I/O is 70 MB/s, what limits the transfer time for a 1000 MB file?

A: Throughput is constrained by the slowest component in the path (the bottleneck principle). However, networking uses bits per second (Mbps), while storage uses bytes per second (MBps). 1000 MB = 8000 Mb. At 10 Mbps, the theoretical transfer time is ~800 seconds, not 100. If the link is actually 10 MBps (80 Mbps), then ~100 seconds is correct. In practice, actual throughput will be lower due to protocol overhead, TCP window size, and disk write speeds. For high-bandwidth, long-latency links (Long Fat Networks), you must tune TCP buffer sizes and enable window scaling to fully utilize the available bandwidth. Without tuning, TCP will throttle throughput regardless of the physical link speed.

text:
The bandwidth on a physical interface is fixed. To increase aggregate bandwidth between two Cisco 3750 switches, configure an EtherChannel (port-channel) across multiple available uplinks. Note that EtherChannel load-balances based on flow hashing (src/dst MAC/IP, TCP/UDP ports), so a single flow will still use only one physical link. Example configuration:

```
interface range GigabitEthernet0/1 - 4
 channel-group 1 mode active
 switchport mode trunk
```
Use LACP (`active`/`passive`) for standards compliance and cross-vendor compatibility. The 3750 series supports up to 8 physical ports per port-channel, but verify your specific hardware revision and IOS version for exact limits. If no spare ports exist, increasing bandwidth without rewiring is impossible; consider upgrading to 10G interfaces, implementing QoS/policing for traffic prioritization, or using traffic shaping instead.

text:
Q: 2 Cisco 3750 switches are connected via port 22(Trunk). First switch consists of 3 VLANs(each vlan have 7 ports such as 1-7,8-14,15-21) and 2nd switch consists of 2 VLANs. what solution can i use to increase the bandwidth between the two switches without rewiring?

[Score:2] Even the non-G, Fast Ethernet models feature SFP ports at gigabit speed. Those require running a fiber in between or alternatively, using 1000BASE-T transceivers that only need Cat-5e cabling - I believe GLC-T would fit.
If you're already on gigabit, the only alternatives are either aggregating multiple ports as Ron's described or replacing the switches with faster models.

**Added Context:** The correct solution is Link Aggregation (EtherChannel). On Cisco IOS, configure a port-channel interface to bundle physical links. Example:
```
interface range GigabitEthernet0/1 - 4
 channel-group 1 mode active
```
Use LACP (`mode active`/`passive`) as industry standard. Ensure all member ports share identical VLAN/trunk settings and MTU. Note: The Catalyst 3750 is End-of-Life, but EtherChannel principles and GLC-T transceiver compatibility remain accurate for legacy deployments. For modern equivalents, consider Catalyst 9300/9400 with native 10G/25G uplinks or SDN controllers (e.g., Ryu/ONOS) for automated bandwidth provisioning in OpenFlow environments.

Q: While being on the same channel as another network is not ideal, is it better than being on a neighboring overlapping channel because 802.11 specification has methods for dealing with co-channel interference?
Or in other way, is it better to be in the non overlapping channels 1, 6, and 11 (besides they will be probably shared between many other networks) or choose some channel between them containing less networks?
I am asking about the 2.4GHz ISM band.

[Score:6] It is better to be in the same channel as your interfering signals (if you can't find a free channel) rather than in overlapping channels that are not the same channel - if you are in a nearby channel, there is interference, but no communication. On the same channel, the spec requires some cordination to share the channel.
Often your best actual bet is to run more wire, and place your APs at lower power nearer your end use devices. Using the 5GHz band is also very helpful as there are more non-overlapping channels in most of the world, and it does not penetrate walls so well, keeping neighboring signals out of your space (or at least greatly reducing them.)

Q: In a spine and leaf architecture with Link Aggregation between spine and leaves (active-active) like this:
- Do we have the same active-atcive link aggregation between the leaf and the server?
- Is the bandwidth in the aggregated link full used? So if I have two 10 Gbps links do I get 20 Gbps full used by the server? Or the link between the server and the leaf is active-passive so we use only 10 Gbps even if we have two cables?

[Score:3] - Do we have the same active-atcive link aggregation between the leaf and the server?
From the diagram, you've got a single link to each server. Of course, you can also use LACP LAG, static LAG, or multiple L3 links (with any kind of load balancing that your installation supports). L3 multilinks provide more options on load balancing but are harder to fail over.
- Is the bandwidth in the aggregated link full used? So if I have two 10 Gbps links do I get 20 Gbps full used by the server? Or the link between the server and the leaf is active-passive so we use only 10 Gbps even if we have two cables?
That depends on whether you've got redundant links or aggregated links to the servers. That depends on the hardware, software and the setup.
Redundancy holds a spare in case the active link fails (active-passive), aggregation uses both links (active-active). As Ron has already pointed out, each flow to/from the server (or across the spine for that matter) can only use a single link's bandwidth. Multiple flows might utilize all links - if the load balancing/traffic distibution matches the workload. If you need more bandwidth in a flow you need to use faster links.

text:
Q: In a spine and leaf architecture with Link Aggregation between spine and leaves (active-active) like this:
- Do we have the same active-atcive link aggregation between the leaf and the server?
- Is the bandwidth in the aggregated link full used? So if I have two 10 Gbps links do I get 20 Gbps full used by the server? Or the link between the server and the leaf is active-passive so we use only 10 Gbps even if we have two cables?

[Score:2] With LACP aggregating multiple links, a single flow will only use a single link, but you can use the full channel bandwidth in aggregate. That means that you can have multiple flows, and each flow can only use one of the links, but all the links could be used with multiple flows. There is a hashing algorithm that determines which flow uses which link of the channel.
You do not want to split a single flow across multiple links because that with result in dropped packets and out of order packet delivery, which could result in degraded performance. Connection-oriented protocols can handle something like that, but it burdens the protocol to request missing packets, and to reorder packets. Connectionless protocols cannot handle that, and it will absolutely kill real-time protocols.
In the event of a link failure, the algorithm will move any flows using the failed link to the remaining links. This happens very rapidly.

metadata.title: Spine and Leaf architecture with LACP

metadata.problem_summary: Understanding bandwidth utilization and flow distribution behavior in server-to-leaf link aggregation within a spine-and-leaf topology, specifically addressing whether active-active aggregation provides full bandwidth to a single flow or distributes multiple flows across links.
metadata.context_summary: Clarifying that LACP distributes multiple flows across aggregated links using a hashing algorithm, while a single flow is restricted to one link to prevent packet reordering and drops. Link failures are handled rapidly by redistributing affected flows.

text: The observed behavior is primarily driven by TCP's end-to-end congestion control mechanism. When the ISP router egresses traffic capped at 4Mbps, packets exceeding this rate are dropped or queued. TCP senders detect this loss via duplicate ACKs or retransmission timeouts and reduce their congestion window, naturally throttling the flow to match the available bandwidth. Additionally, SNMP counters (e.g., ifOutOctets on the switch port) measure successfully transmitted bytes over the wire, not raw transmit attempts. Since the ISP's egress policing drops excess traffic, the sustained throughput observed via SNMP aligns with actual successful delivery (~4Mbps), not the physical 1Gbps link capacity. To accurately monitor line-rate transmit attempts, correlate switch SNMP counters with router interface drop counters (output drops, police exceed, or late collisions). Implementing explicit QoS policing/shaping on the router ingress or enabling ECN can provide more predictable bandwidth utilization and reduce TCP retransmission overhead in automated traffic engineering scenarios.

metadata.title: LAN side 1Gb link showing WAN 4Mbps throughput in SNMP

metadata.context_summary: The ISP's 4Mbps egress cap causes packet drops, triggering TCP's congestion control to throttle transmission rates, which is why the 1Gbps LAN-side link never exceeds 4Mbps in traffic capture.

text: Bisection Bandwidth is the usable bandwidth across a network cut that divides the infrastructure into two equal halves. In modern data centers, this is typically calculated across the spine layer of a Clos or Fat-Tree topology. (Note: STP blocking is largely obsolete in modern DCs, which rely on ECMP and VXLAN; bisection calculations now assume all parallel paths are active). Full Bisection Bandwidth implies a non-blocking fabric where the aggregate bandwidth of all hosts in one half matches the available uplink/bisection capacity to the other half (a 1:1 ratio). Over-subscription occurs when host aggregate bandwidth exceeds available bisection capacity (e.g., 4:1 or 2:1 ratios are common in leaf-spine designs to optimize cost, assuming traffic patterns aren't simultaneously saturated). Under-subscription is the inverse. In a k-ary Fat-Tree, full bisection is achieved when the total bandwidth of spine links crossing the cut equals the total bandwidth of leaf switch downlinks. Modern SDN controllers (like Ryu or ONOS) can dynamically monitor and adjust forwarding paths to mitigate congestion in over-subscribed topologies.

metadata.title: Significance of Bisection Bandwidth, Full Bisection Bandwidth, Subscription and their differences

It also misses the requested Fat-Tree/Clos topology example. The core concepts are correct but need modernization and brief contextual examples to align with current DC/SDN design practices.

Q: I use the CoS method limited the EX3300's switch port bandwidth to 200m.
But I have a question, how to check whether it works?
Is there the Juniper Switch EX3300's switch port have a bandwidth property intuitively? or should I use a tool in the Dedicated Server for checking?

[Score:4] As I said in previous question, you can check that shaping is working by transfering data to target (shaped) host by either copying something (via NetBIOS, SCP/FTP, etc.) or by running simple speedtest (link).
Interface statistics can be retrieved by running > show interface ge-0/0/0 extensive | match bps
Here is example. Target host interface shaped under CoS section to 50 Mbps limit.
set class-of-service interfaces ge-5/0/13 shaping-rate 50m
I'm then started speedtest on target computer to check results. Here is speed results without shaping:

[ADDED CONTEXT] While `show interface ... | match bps` shows current throughput, verifying CoS shaping specifically in Junos is best done by checking output rate counters and shaping drops. Run `show class-of-service interfaces ge-0/0/0` to confirm the shaping policy is applied, or `show interfaces ge-0/0/0 statistics` and look for the `output rate` (should reflect the 50/200 Mbps limit) and `shaping` drop counters. For accurate validation, use a traffic generator like iPerf rather than file transfers, as OS-level overhead and TCP windowing can skew results.

[VERSION NOTE] The `set class-of-service interfaces ... shaping-rate` syntax and `show interface ... extensive` commands are stable across Junos 12.1+ releases and apply directly to EX3300 running Junos OS 14.x through 20.x.

text:
Q: If you check show int tranceiver command on Nexus Switch 10Gbit Ethernet port, the output shows the nominal bitrate is 12000 MBits/sec instead of 10000 MBits/sec. As I know 12 bytes Interframe Gap + 8 bytes preamble + 18 bytes frame header and FCS are all included in bandwidth 10000 MBits/sec of 10Gig Ethernet. So what is 2000 Mbit/sec overhead used for?
switch# show interface Ethernet1/5 transceiver
Ethernet1/5
sfp is present
name is CISCO-MOLEX INC
part number is 74752-9025
revision is A
serial number is MOC12302468
nominal bitrate is 12000 MBits/sec

[A] The nominal bit rate reported by NX-OS refers to the SFP+ physical layer signaling rate, not the payload bandwidth. While 10GBASE standards use 64b/66b encoding (compared to 8b/10b for 1G Ethernet), the actual serial line rate for 10G Ethernet is 10.3125 Gbps. The SFP+ form factor was standardized to support up to 12.5 Gbps signaling (originally for 12G Fibre Channel). Cisco displays this maximum signaling capability as 12000 Mbps. The 'extra' 2 Gbps is not Ethernet frame overhead (IFG, preamble, and FCS are already factored into the 10G payload rate); it represents the physical layer signaling margin and electrical/optical interface headroom required by the transceiver standard. To verify the actual active line rate, check the interface counters or use `show interface Ethernet1/5` to observe the configured speed and actual throughput.

The answer is technically sound in principle but lacks vendor-specific context and precise terminology.

text:
Q: If you check show int transceiver command on Nexus Switch 10Gbit Ethernet port, the output shows the nominal bitrate is 12000 MBits/sec instead of 10000 MBits/sec. As I know 12 bytes Interframe Gap + 8 bytes preamble + 18 bytes frame header and FCS are all included in bandwidth 10000 MBits/sec of 10Gig Ethernet. So what is 2000 Mbit/sec overhead used for?
switch# show interface Ethernet1/5 transceiver
Ethernet1/5
sfp is present
name is CISCO-MOLEX INC
part number is 74752-9025
revision is A
serial number is MOC12302468
nominal bitrate is 12000 MBits/sec

A: The 'nominal bitrate' reported by the transceiver refers to the module's maximum supported line rate or MSA standard rating, not the pure Ethernet payload rate. 10GBASE Ethernet uses 64b/66b line coding, which adds ~3.125% overhead, pushing the actual wire rate to 10.3125 Gbps. Furthermore, the SFP+ Multi-Source Agreement (MSA) standardizes a nominal bitrate of 12 Gbps to accommodate this encoding overhead, physical layer framing, and to ensure multi-protocol compatibility (e.g., Fibre Channel 12G). The 2000 Mbps difference accounts for line coding, preamble/SFD, IFG, and manufacturing margin, not user data throughput.

Q: Can some one please help me understand the below QOS setting on our MPLS router the service policy.. Policy map and class map
I know the basic understanding behind how these work .. I need to in detail, how in this particular scenario ,,traffic rate that is being allowed , policed or dropped .
I am kind of confused ..

class ENHANCED-PLUS

please help .
The port capacity is 100 Mb.
need help undersanding how these commands work in respect to each other ..
priority 10000
police 10000000 conform-action transmit exceed-action transmit
shape average 50000000
on policy map
bandwidth 50000
on interface

[Score:4] When decoding QOS policies I like to reverse engineer it. Start with the last command that was configured in regards to the QOS. This is usually the service-policy statement on the interface.
service-policy output QOS-SHAPER-GI0/0
The points to a shaping parent policy with nested LLQ scheduler (QOS-SCHEDULER-GI0/0) child policy. The shaper is configured for 50Mbps. And the LLQ has one priority traffic classes with a policer.
The bandwidth statement on the interface is referenced when bandwidth statements exist in the parent policy. For example if you had configured two classes (one being class default) the bandwidth statements for those classes can't exceed the max_reserve x bandwidth statement on the interface. In your case the max res is 100% for a result of 50Mbps. In your actual configuration you have a shaper in the parent policy and it effectively ignores the bandwidth statement. However the bandwidth statements in the child policy, if any were present, are now bound to the bandwidth of the shaper. Hopefully your able to follow this.
With regard to the priority queue in the LLQ scheduler you have something called conditional policing and unconditional policing. During periods of congestion the priority bandwidth command polices the traffic to the configured CIR. During periods of non-congestion the priority queue is able to exceed the CIR. The is call conditional policing. The police command provides an unconditional policer and polices any excess traffic during periods of congestion and non-congestion. Typically you would choose one or the other.
With both configured the unconditional takes precedence. I had to test to confirm. And with your configuration they both are configured at the same rate. Priority is configured in kbps and policers/shapers are configured in bps.
I don't understand why you are configuring the exceed action to transmit and remark to ef. I think this defeats the purpose of the LLQ.

text: The original answer correctly contrasts shared cable vs. dedicated fiber contention models but misses the core technical issue: **bufferbloat** and **router queue management**. When multiple high-bandwidth TCP flows saturate a link, large router/NAT buffers fill up, causing severe latency and packet loss. This starves TCP ACKs, triggering congestion control (e.g., CUBIC/Reno) and drastically reducing throughput or causing timeouts that appear as 'connectivity ends'. Additionally, consumer-grade CPEs often lack hardware-accelerated QoS or sufficient CPU/RAM to handle sustained multi-flow NAT processing. To mitigate: 1) Enable router QoS to prioritize control traffic (DNS, TCP ACKs, VoIP) over bulk data. 2) Diagnose bufferbloat using tools like `caper` or `curl` latency monitoring. 3) Verify ISP provisioning vs. CPE bottlenecks. TCP self-balances via congestion windows, but relies on low-latency feedback; saturated buffers break this loop. Proper QoS implementation or upgrading to enterprise-grade CPE with hardware offloading (e.g., Cisco ISR, MikroTik CRS, or SDN-enabled gateways) resolves the issue without throttling actual throughput.

metadata.title: What are the implications of 'saturating' a broadband connection?

text:
Q: We recently switched from a business-class cable connection (100/20) to a symmetrical (100/100) fiber one. This serves a front office, a media department, and several client computers on any given day.
While on the former service we up/downloaded many multi-gig media files from client and graphics computers, with no problems except what you'd expect when several people are competing for limited bandwidth. It all worked, it just slowed down.
But with this new service we get periods where (the supplier says) we "saturate" the connection and all connectivity ends for everyone. What is really going on here? I would guess most traffic is 'web-based' in that it's mediated through ports 80/443, with some FTP.
And some of the file transfers do proceed at amazing speeds -- the remote ends can easily keep up with our puny 100Mb/s, where in the past it was rare to see any given connection exceed 30Mb/s. It's just that when three or more of these up/down transfers are going on, it eventually, to put it crudely, 'clogs the pipes'.
So what's really going on? Why would a faster service with a more dedicated channel be susceptible to this sort of degradation? I've always thought of TCP as somewhat self-balancing in that multiple connections would degrade together, that one or two connections couldn't 'hog' the bandwidth. Is that wrong?

[Score:1] TCP will send packets at an increasing rate until it starts to see packet loss, then it will back off. Normally this works pretty well, however there are a couple of things that can frustrate it.
One is oversized buffers, this can easilly occour when a relatively slow link is terminated on a peice of big high end equipment. TCP will fill up the large buffer leading to high latency for all traffic.
Another is poorly implemented artificial rate limits. If the link allows an initial burst of high speed traffic then suddenly imposes a limit then again TCP can respond badly.
Really your provider should be sorting this out, but if they can't/won't then the soloution is to introduce an artificial rate limit on your end that is slightly slower than the provider's limit. You can then control the buffer size and prioritisation rules (if any) at that artificial limit). The buffers at the provider's end should never fill up.

metadata.title: What are the implications of 'saturating' a broadband connection?
metadata.llm_evaluation_reason: Technically accurate explanation of TCP congestion control, bufferbloat, and ISP traffic policing. Correctly identifies that oversized buffers on high-end CPE routers cause latency spikes under saturation. The recommendation to implement customer-edge traffic shaping is standard QoS best practice. No outdated versions or commands. Minor room for improvement: explicitly naming 'bufferbloat' and providing a concrete implementation example (e.g., Linux `tc` or router QoS policies) would enhance clarity for RAG retrieval, but the core networking principles are sound and current.
metadata.problem_summary: Network connectivity degrades or drops when multiple high-speed file transfers saturate a newly upgraded 100/100 fiber connection. Despite TCP's congestion control mechanisms, the degradation is caused by oversized buffers leading to high latency (bufferbloat) and/or ISP-imposed artificial rate limits that disrupt TCP's self-balancing behavior.
metadata.context_summary: This troubleshooting guide examines the network implications of saturating a broadband connection, focusing on how full bandwidth utilization triggers congestion and bufferbloat. It analyzes the resulting performance degradation for TCP, FTP, and HTTP/HTTPS traffic to provide a framework for diagnosing and resolving latency and throughput issues.

text:
Q: How is bandwidth managed in an unmanaged network switch?
Suppose that port 1 is connected to a a very busy server. If suddenly machines connected to ports 2, 3 and 4 decide to send a massive amount of packets (in an extreme scenario: at maximum theoretical throughput) to the server in port 1, how will the switch handle this?
Will the switch perform any sort of flow control, ensuring that each sending port gets a fair share of the available bandwidth to port 1, or will they simply be served in a "first come, first served" manner (obviously, dropping packets as necessary)?

[Score:2] In your scenario, most ethernet frames will be dropped, and it is up to the upper-layer protocols, e.g. TCP, to handle that. There is a rudimentary ethernet flow control, but it is poorly supported. Switches only have tiny buffers, and a 3:1 bandwidth over-subscription will really cause a 3:1 frame drop rate.
Ethernet (layer-2 frames), and IP (layer-3 packets) will really play no part in this. TCP (layer-4 segments) guarantees delivery by requesting dropped segments be resent and shrinking the TCP windows. UDP, or other connectionless protocols, will need an application to request lost data to be resent.

metadata.title: Switch bandwidth management
metadata.llm_evaluation_reason: Technically accurate for unmanaged Ethernet switches. Correctly identifies that limited internal buffers lead to frame tail-drop under oversubscription, and that congestion control falls to TCP. Accurately notes that IEEE 802.3x Ethernet flow control is poorly supported and ineffective for bandwidth management. The answer is current and directly addresses the scenario. Minor improvement would be contrasting this with managed switch QoS mechanisms for broader applicability.
metadata.problem_summary: How an unmanaged switch handles bandwidth over-subscription and congestion when multiple ports attempt to send traffic to a single busy port, specifically addressing frame dropping mechanisms and the reliance on upper-layer protocols (TCP/UDP) to manage packet loss.

text: In an unmanaged switch, bandwidth management is handled entirely at the hardware level without software-defined policies or QoS. When multiple ports transmit at line rate toward a single congested port, the switch relies on shared egress buffer memory. If the buffer fills faster than it can drain, packets are dropped (tracked via hardware drop counters). Modern switches use terms like 'tail drops' or 'buffer exhaustion' rather than legacy 'overruns'. Unmanaged switches lack 802.3x PAUSE flow control, traffic shaping, and fair queuing, so they operate on a strict first-come, first-served basis. While enterprise switches often use shared ASIC buffers to improve utilization, unmanaged devices typically use fixed or limited memory pools. If ingress traffic exceeds the internal switching fabric (backplane) capacity, macroscopic congestion occurs, leading to sustained packet loss. For deterministic bandwidth allocation, managed switches implement QoS mechanisms (e.g., WFQ, rate limiting, priority queuing), which are entirely absent in unmanaged hardware.

metadata.title: Switch bandwidth management
metadata.llm_evaluation_reason: The original answer contains outdated terminology ('overruns', 'puntings') and incorrectly applies control-plane concepts to data-plane forwarding. text:
Q: I thought a bandwidth is a number represent maximum amount of traffic. But it seems not.
Let me show the Network map in my company.
In this image. WAN has a 155Mbps bandwidth and there are a lot of PC and mobile devices. I tested network benchmark from 6 PCs at the same time. The result of min download speed is 91.2 Mbps and min upload speed is 88.7 Mbps. For this reason I wonder how this is possible or it works even though WAN has 155 Mbps.
Addition
I used Mbps as mega bit not byte.
It seems I gave you confuse what I want to know. I'm not good at English so It was my best But let me try more to explain specific.
I did't mean why the speed less than 155 Mbps even though the bandwidth of WAN is 155 Mbps.
The thing is how 6 hosts can speed almost 100 Mbps at the same time. WAN is the Internet bandwidth which is ISP(Internet Service Provider) gave our company and that moment may be more than 100 hosts use the Internet when I tested. So How can 100 hosts have speed over than 90 Mbps at the same time even ISP gave us the 155 Mbps for the Internet?

[Score:2] There are many possibilities and substantial further testing is needed to narrow down which ones apply in your case.

**Technical Context & Enhanced Analysis:**
1. **QoS Policing & Burst Allowances (Token Buckets):** ISPs typically enforce limits using traffic policing (e.g., CIR/PIR configuration). Policers use token buckets that allow short-term bursts above the committed rate before dropping or shaping packets. If multiple clients test simultaneously, aggregate burst capacity can temporarily exceed 155 Mbps.
2. **Speed Test Methodology:** Most web-based speed tests run for <30 seconds and use multiple TCP/UDP streams. This captures the burst window rather than sustained throughput. Run a 60+ second `iperf3` test with 6 concurrent streams (`iperf3 -c <server> -P 6 -t 60`) to measure true sustained bandwidth.
3. **ISP Peering & Local Caching:** If the benchmark server resides within your ISP's network or a nearby CDN edge, traffic may be routed via local peering or cached locally. This bypasses the WAN uplink entirely, delivering wire-speed results regardless of the external cap.
4. **Soft Limits vs. Hard Limits:** Many business/ISP contracts specify 'soft limits' enforced only after sustained violation (e.g., >5 minutes). Temporary spikes are often permitted to accommodate TCP window scaling and congestion control dynamics.
5. **Configuration Verification:** Check your edge router/firewall QoS policies. Misapplied rate-limiters, disabled policing, or incorrect interface bandwidth statements can cause the device to forward traffic without enforcing the ISP's contracted limit.

**Recommendation:** Use `iperf3` for controlled testing, monitor queue drops (`show queueing` on Cisco or `ovs-appctl dpif/show` for OVS), and verify token bucket settings on your ISP's edge device.

metadata.title: How the Internet speed on PCs can be almost 100Mbps although the bandwidth of WAN is 155Mbps?

While it does not directly address SDN/OpenFlow/OVS, the underlying QoS and traffic policing concepts are foundational to SDN flow rule enforcement and OVS qdisc/policer configurations. The response is generally accurate but vague for a technical RAG database.

text: The original answer correctly identifies that the premise lacks critical data (actual concurrent users, QoS policies). However, it misses key ISP provisioning concepts. Modern WAN circuits (like the 155Mbps mentioned) often operate on statistical multiplexing and oversubscription ratios (e.g., 10:1 or 20:1). ISPs typically provision a Committed Information Rate (CIR) with a Peak Information Rate (PIR) that allows bursting. During low contention, hosts can temporarily exceed the CIR. Additionally, enterprise networks use QoS (DiffServ, WFQ, or SDN-based traffic steering via OpenFlow) to prioritize or shape traffic, allowing bursty downloads to achieve near-link speeds when the WAN is underutilized. The mention of a hub causing half-duplex collisions is technically valid for legacy setups but would be replaced by full-duplex switches in modern environments. To accurately diagnose, one would need the oversubscription ratio, QoS configuration, and real-time utilization metrics. For SDN/automation contexts, controllers like Ryu or OVS can dynamically adjust flow tables to enforce fair queuing or traffic shaping based on real-time WAN utilization metrics.

text:
Q: How can 6 PCs achieve ~100 Mbps when the WAN is only 155 Mbps?

A: The scenario describes a common misunderstanding of bandwidth allocation and testing. A 155 Mbps WAN link (typically STM-1/SDH) is a shared pipe. It cannot support 6 hosts simultaneously downloading at 100 Mbps (which would require ~600 Mbps aggregate throughput). The observed results are likely due to:
1. **Local Caching/CDNs:** Modern ISPs and CDNs cache content locally. If your benchmark tests against a nearby server, traffic may not traverse the WAN link.
2. **TCP Overhead & Window Scaling:** TCP requires ACKs, retransmissions, and header overhead. On a 100 Mbps Ethernet link (100Base-T), max TCP throughput is typically 90-95 Mbps due to these factors. UDP can approach 100 Mbps but lacks congestion control.
3. **ISP Oversubscription & Shaping:** ISPs often guarantee minimum speeds but allow burst traffic or oversubscribe aggregate uplinks. However, sustained per-host speeds exceeding WAN capacity indicates measurement error or local breakout.
4. **Testing Methodology:** To accurately test WAN bandwidth, use `iperf3` with multiple concurrent streams (`-P 4`) between two external endpoints outside your LAN. Ensure MTU settings are correct and disable local caching/CDNs during testing.

Bottom line: 155 Mbps is the total shared capacity. Per-host speeds will scale down as concurrent users increase, unless traffic is locally cached or the WAN link is aggregated/upscaled. For QoS enforcement or traffic shaping in enterprise environments, consider DSCP/CoS marking (Cisco IOS) or OpenFlow-based scheduling (Ryu/Mininet).

metadata.title: How the Internet speed on PCs can be almost 100Mbps although the bandwidth of WAN is 155Mbps?
metadata.llm_evaluation_reason: The original answer correctly identifies physical layer speeds (100Base-T vs STM-1/SDH) and TCP overhead, but fails to address the core networking concept: aggregate bandwidth vs. per-host allocation. The scenario described (6 hosts simultaneously achieving ~100 Mbps on a 155 Mbps WAN) is physically impossible without local caching, CDN usage, ISP oversubscription, or flawed test methodology. The answer lacks context on how ISPs provision bandwidth, TCP window scaling/ACK overhead, and proper WAN benchmarking techniques.

text: [Score:5] Multicast is a one-to-many mechanism where the sender transmits a single stream, and the network replicates packets only toward interested receivers. On a Layer 2 switch, multicast is *not* flooded like broadcast if IGMP snooping is enabled; the switch forwards it only to ports with active group members. However, the core issue is Layer 3: routers do not forward multicast traffic by default (TTL is typically 1). End-to-end multicast across the public internet is effectively impossible because transit providers do not run PIM (Protocol Independent Multicast) and block multicast routing to prevent abuse and routing table bloat. If multicast *were* supported end-to-end, your upload bandwidth would remain at 0.1 Mb/s, as the network handles replication. Since it won't work, you'd need to use unicast scaling, CDNs, or WebRTC SFUs/MCUs, where upload bandwidth scales with the number of clients or depends on the media server's architecture. Note: Enterprise/ISP LANs sometimes use IGMP proxying or dedicated multicast routing within their managed networks, but this never crosses the public internet.

metadata.title: If I UDP multicast to 10 listeners in a multicast group, will my upload bandwidth usage increase by 10 fold?
metadata.llm_evaluation_reason: The response correctly identifies that end-to-end IP multicast is unsupported on the public internet due to TTL restrictions and the absence of PIM routing across transit networks. However, it inaccurately equates L2 multicast handling with broadcast without mentioning IGMP snooping, and it fails to directly answer the bandwidth question. The explanation is conceptually sound on routing limitations but lacks precision on packet replication mechanics and modern alternatives.
metadata.problem_summary: Clarifies whether a sender's upload bandwidth is multiplied when transmitting a single UDP multicast stream to multiple recipients over the public internet, explaining that multicast is a one-to-many mechanism requiring explicit router configuration and is not natively routed across public networks.
metadata.context_summary: This document explains that UDP multicast transmits a single data stream which network infrastructure replicates for multiple receivers, meaning the sender's upload bandwidth remains constant regardless of the number of listeners. It clarifies multicast scaling behavior, contrasts it with unicast transmission, and addresses routing and wireless network implications.

text:
The sender's upload rate will remain ~0.1 Mb/s. In IP multicast, packet replication occurs at router branching points (managed via IGMP for group membership and PIM for routing), not at the source. However, native IP multicast is almost never routed across the public Internet due to administrative policies, TTL restrictions, and routing table scaling limits. To span the public Internet, you must use an overlay or tunnel:
1. Application-layer multicast (e.g., WebRTC SFU, Mesh, or MCU) handles replication at a central server or peer-to-peer, bypassing ISP restrictions and scaling efficiently.
2. If strictly required at the network layer, GRE or IPsec tunnels can encapsulate multicast traffic across unicast paths, though this adds encapsulation overhead and requires manual route/multicast forwarding configuration.
For modern deployments, cloud-native SFU architectures or managed SD-WAN with multicast support are recommended over raw GRE tunnels. Note: This concept applies universally regardless of SDN controllers (Ryu/ONOS) or hypervisors (OVS), which can emulate multicast forwarding in lab environments like Mininet using `--switch ovsk --controller remote` and appropriate flow rules.

metadata.title: If I UDP multicast to 10 listeners in a multicast group, will my upload bandwidth usage increase by 10 fold?
metadata.llm_evaluation_reason: The core technical premise is correct: IP multicast replicates packets at router branching points (via IGMP/PIM), not at the source, so the sender's upload bandwidth remains ~0.1 Mb/s. However, the answer is overly brief and lacks critical context. It omits why native multicast cannot simply be used on the public internet (IGMP/PIM is generally blocked on transit networks due to scaling, TTL limits, and administrative policies). GRE tunneling is technically valid but often impractical for modern deployments. The text requires enrichment to explain network-layer replication mechanics, internet routing constraints, and contemporary application-layer alternatives (SFU/Mesh) for reliable RAG retrieval.
metadata.problem_summary: Determining whether sending UDP multicast traffic to multiple recipients over the public internet increases the sender's upload bandwidth consumption beyond the individual packet rate.

Q: I know that a non-blocking switch is a switch that can manage the sum of the maximum theoretical speeds of all its ports.
However, as the term suggests, "theoretical" maximum speed is just a theory and it rarely happens in real-life conditions.
Given this infrequent scenario, why is there a need for a non-blocking switch??

[Score:9] However, as the term suggests, "theoretical" maximum speed is just a theory and it rarely happens in real-life conditions.
That depends on your life, I guess.
In my day job, we try to maximize utilization of our switch ports. Why would you pay for a 400G switch and then not use it? Our products support both 25G, dual-25G, and 100G interfaces precisely so that you can use the switch port that matches your requirements most closely. Our next products will support everything from 25G to 400G, again, for the same reason: so you can maximize utilization of your switch ports.

metadata.title: Why non-blocking switch needed?
metadata.llm_evaluation_reason: Technically accurate and correctly addresses the practical necessity of non-blocking switches. The answer effectively counters the misconception that line-rate traffic is rare by emphasizing modern data center utilization practices. While slightly informal, it correctly implies that switching fabric capacity must match aggregate port bandwidth to avoid internal bottlenecks. No outdated commands or versions are present.
metadata.problem_summary: Clarifying the practical necessity and ROI of non-blocking switches, addressing the misconception that theoretical maximum speeds are required for real-world utility by emphasizing port utilization and interface matching.
metadata.context_summary: This theoretical document explains the necessity of non-blocking network switches for modern high-speed Ethernet architectures, specifically addressing the bandwidth demands of 100G, 25G, and 400G standards. It outlines the technical rationale for eliminating contention-induced packet loss and latency in scalable, high-throughput network deployments.

text:
Q: I know that a non-blocking switch is a switch that can manage the sum of the maximum theoretical speeds of all its ports.
However, as the term suggests, "theoretical" maximum speed is just a theory and it rarely happens in real-life conditions.
Given this infrequent scenario, why is there a need for a non-blocking switch??

[Score:8] By being able to handle the sum of the maximum speeds of all ports, a non-blocking switch guarantees that the internal switching logic can never cause a delay in the network traffic.
Suppose two servers (A and B) have a dedicated connection to each other that runs through one or more non-blocking switches. This connection only handles traffic that goes between A and B.
Because all the switches in-between are non-blocking, you have the guarantee that the connection between A and B will never be impacted by the other traffic that the switches handle as well. This can be useful if you need to have a guaranteed throughput or a guaranteed latency on that connection.

metadata.title: Why non-blocking switch needed?
metadata.llm_evaluation_reason: The answer is technically accurate and directly addresses the core misconception about 'theoretical' switch capacity. It correctly explains that non-blocking fabrics prevent internal contention, ensuring guaranteed throughput and latency regardless of aggregate traffic patterns. The explanation is concise and conceptually sound.
metadata.problem_summary: Addresses why non-blocking switches are necessary despite theoretical max speeds rarely occurring: they prevent internal switching contention, guaranteeing dedicated paths maintain consistent throughput and latency regardless of other network traffic.
metadata.context_summary: This document examines the theoretical rationale and operational scope of non-blocking switches in network architecture, detailing how their design eliminates internal contention to guarantee simultaneous, high-bandwidth data transmission across all ports.

text:
Q: I know that a non-blocking switch is a switch that can manage the sum of the maximum theoretical speeds of all its ports.
However, as the term suggests, "theoretical" maximum speed is just a theory and it rarely happens in real-life conditions.
Given this infrequent scenario, why is there a need for a non-blocking switch??

[Score:2] Non-blocking is a quality in switches that tells you that a switch doesn't lose frames unless a destination/egress port reaches its maximum bandwidth. That knowledge provides peace of mind and simplifies network design.
In a blocking switch there's a (sometimes unknown) total bandwidth that you cannot exceed. The bad thing is that it's not very obvious and might be really hard to detect that you're hitting the backplane forwarding limit and losing frames.
While small, 1U switches are all non-blocking today (except for really high-speed ones), larger chassis switches usually have a maximum backplane bandwidth and, more specifically, a limited module-to-module throughput that you need to know about when maxing out your network. Not all vendors properly document those limits - and not all network engineers have them on their radar at all times.

metadata.title: Why non-blocking switch needed?
metadata.llm_evaluation_reason: Technically accurate explanation of non-blocking vs. blocking switch architectures. Correctly identifies backplane/fabric oversubscription as the root cause of frame loss in blocking designs and accurately notes that modern 1U switches are typically non-blocking while chassis switches may have internal module-to-module throughput limits. No outdated protocols, commands, or version references are present. Minor ambiguity exists regarding real-world aggregate traffic patterns, but the core networking concepts are sound.
metadata.problem_summary: Explains the practical necessity of the 'non-blocking' switch specification despite theoretical maximum speeds rarely being reached, emphasizing how it prevents hidden backplane/forwarding limits, avoids undetected frame loss, and simplifies network design.
metadata.context_summary: This theoretical document explains the architectural necessity of non-blocking switches by contrasting them with blocking designs, focusing on how backplane capacity and forwarding limits dictate performance in chassis switches. It outlines the fundamental principles that determine when a non-blocking configuration is required to prevent traffic congestion and ensure consistent data throughput.

text:
Q: Will transferring over PPP data stream containing only reserved bytes 0x7E, 0xFF, 0x03 reduce bandwidth by 50% because of escaping?
Then it seems to be very stupid if not say moronic. Such poor design still widely used in PPPOE, PPTP, L2TP and many other linked network stacks. Am I missing something? The example with transferring only one of magic bytes may seem to be kind of extreme case but it is still possible in some time periods. Magic bytes may occur in data stream more or less frequently but the principle itself is depressing.

[Score:20] You might care to read RFC 1547 "Requirements for an Internet Standard Point-to-Point Protocol" which explains how the PPP was chosen. The thing I'd suggest you are missing is that interoperability is one of the principal driving forces in the internet protocols, and efficiency is much less important. You do the highly talented engineers who designed PPP a disservice in describing their work as "stupid/moronic/depressing". They solved an extremely large class of problem with a very general, extensible, protocol -- and, much more difficult, got everyone to agree on it. It's a tribute to its quality that many systems still use PPP, and many new uses are found for it, after 30-odd years.
From the motivation section of of RFC 1134, "The Point-to-Point Protocol: A Proposal for Multi-Protocol Transmission of Datagrams Over Point-to-Point Links":
One reason for the small number of point-to-point IP links is the lack of a standard encapsulation protocol. There are plenty of non- standard (and at least one defacto standard) encapsulation protocols available, but there is not one which has been agreed upon as an Internet Standard. By contrast, standard encapsulation schemes do exist for the transmission of datagrams over most popular LANs.
To directly answer your question, PPP over asynchronous serial does do octet-stuffing (ie, 0x7e is encoded as 0x7d, 0x5e). It does this for the Flag Sequence (0x7e), the Control Escape (0x7d) and control characters (0-0x1f). This is defined in RFC 1549 "PPP in HDLC-Like Framing". The control character which need escaping can be negotiated by the Link Control Protocol option Async-Control-Character-Map
.
Yes: A frame consisting entirely of octets which require escaping would indeed have a bandwidth reduction approaching 50% on large frames. It's not clear to me why you describe this in such disparaging terms: it's a perfectly ordinary framing mechanism designed for compatibility with very many different serial links, interoperability across implementations, and reliability. How else would you propose to do it?
But No: Over ethernet there is no octet-stuffing. See RFC 2516 "A Method for Transmitting PPP Over Ethernet (PPPoE)"

metadata.title: Worst-case efficiency of PPP escaping mechanism
metadata.llm_evaluation_reason: Technically accurate with correct RFC references (1134, 1547, 1549, 2516). Clearly distinguishes between async serial PPP (uses octet-stuffing) and PPPoE (no octet-stuffing). No outdated versions or commands present. Content is well-structured and directly answers the question. While outside the primary SDN/automation focus areas, it maintains high protocol accuracy and requires no modifications.
metadata.problem_summary: Clarifies whether PPP's byte-escaping mechanism causes ~50% bandwidth reduction for specific data patterns and explains that the protocol design prioritizes interoperability, extensibility, and reliability over raw transmission efficiency.
metadata.context_summary: The original poster questions the efficiency of PPP's octet-stuffing design, citing a hypothetical 50% bandwidth loss when transmitting only reserved/escape bytes. The response references multiple RFCs to explain that PPP's framing mechanism is intentional for serial link compatibility and interoperability, noting that efficiency was deliberately traded for standardization and reliability across diverse implementations.
metadata.version_tag: N/A - RFC standards are current. No CLI or protocol versions mentioned.

text:
Q: I have been trying to diagnoise this problem for a while (slow 10Gb connection). I thought it might be firmware or settings related(MTU). How ever while looking through some SNMP stuff I discovered that the switch (Quanta LB4M) is reporting the speed as much lower then 10Gb. Why is it doing this? The switch is attached to a Dell PE2950 using a DAC. The Dell is reporting a 10Gb link, as is the switch in the port config section. Only in the SNMP am I seeing the 4.3 Gbps, and in real world testing that is where were hitting a wall, about at 550MB's. This was with raw packet generators and testing file transfer speeds with RAM disk on each system.
Thanks, Levi

[Score:11] Just to address the SNMP portion of your question, I'm guessing that you are seeing the 4.3Gbps result from querying the SNMP object 'ifSpeed'. If so, you are simply getting back the maximum value that is possible from a 32-bit object (i.e. 2^32-1). This is the expected behaviour for a 10Gbps interface. You will need to query 'ifHighSpeed' for the interface instead; more explanation is in RFC2863 & RFC3635.

metadata.title: Why is 10Gb SFP+ port showing 4.3Gbps Speed?
metadata.llm_evaluation_reason: Technically accurate explanation of the SNMP reporting limitation. Correctly identifies the 32-bit gauge constraint of ifSpeed and points to the standard RFC solution. Slightly lacks hardware context for the legacy Dell platform and missing OID examples, warranting an 8/10.
metadata.problem_summary: User observed a ~4.3 Gbps speed in SNMP queries despite configuring a 10Gb link between a Dell server and Quanta switch, suspecting a hardware or configuration fault. The issue was identified as a limitation of the 32-bit SNMP 'ifSpeed' object, with the solution being to query 'ifHighSpeed' instead.
metadata.context_summary: User observed a ~4.3 Gbps speed in SNMP queries despite configuring a 10Gb link between a Dell server and Quanta switch, suspecting a hardware or configuration fault. The issue was identified as a limitation of the 32-bit SNMP 'ifSpeed' object, with the solution being to query 'ifHighSpeed' instead.
