The switch is attached to a Dell PE2950 using a DAC. The Dell is reporting a 10Gb link, as is the switch in the port config section. Only in the SNMP am I seeing the 4.3 Gbps, and in real world testing that is where were hitting a wall, about at 550MB's. This was with raw packet generators and testing file transfer speeds with RAM disk on each system.
Thanks, Levi

[Score:5] Little late to the website so you may have moved on but, We use Solarwinds. Under the edit node properties there is a check box for 64-bit counters. It is probably not checked. Seen the problem you've described for some non-Cisco products in Solarwinds.
Also from the manual for some manufacturers.
If the added node supports 64-bit counters and you want to use them, check Allow 64-bit counters.
Note: Orion support the use of 64-bit counters. However, these high capacity counters can exhibit erratic behavior depending on manufacturer implementation. If you notice peculiar results when using these counters, use the Node Detail View to disable the use of 64-bit counters for the device and contact the hardware manufacturer.
For some vendors we have had to submit the mibs to Solarwinds support and they compile them into the mibs database that you can download and import into Orion.

text:
Q: My ISP says they bring optical fiber to their junction box outside and they bring the copper cable inside from the junction box. And they're promising a symmetrical bandwidth with a speed upto 50MBps. Is this even true? Can we really do that optical to copper conversion and get that speed?

[Score:6] Fiber media converters are compact devices that bridge different physical mediums, typically converting Ethernet signals between copper (UTP) and fiber optics. They feature a copper side (usually RJ-45) and a fiber side (SC/ST or SFP module). While older converters supported 10/100/1000 Mbps, modern deployments almost exclusively use SFP-based converters or managed switches with SFP uplinks, supporting 1Gbps to 10Gbps.

The converter changes the electrical signals on Cat5e/Cat6 (formerly Cat5) cabling into optical pulses for fiber, and vice versa. A key difference is that copper Ethernet is inherently bidirectional over a single pair of wires, whereas fiber requires two strands (or a bi-directional WDM solution) for full-duplex communication: one for Transmit (Tx) and one for Receive (Rx).

Regarding your ISP's claim: Yes, this is entirely true and standard practice. Converting fiber to copper at the premises is trivial, and 50 Mbps symmetrical is well within the capabilities of standard Gigabit Ethernet (1000BASE-T) over Cat5e/Cat6 cabling, which supports up to 1 Gbps. ISPs commonly use this setup for FTTC (Fiber to the Cabinet/Node) or GPON deployments. Modern implementations often integrate this conversion directly into the ONT (Optical Network Terminal) or a small SFP-to-RJ45 media converter, making standalone unmanaged media boxes largely obsolete for residential use.

metadata.title: Fiber to copper conversion, what are the odds?
metadata.llm_evaluation_reason: Technically accurate on core concepts but uses outdated cabling terminology (Cat5) and lacks direct context for the ISP's 50 Mbps claim. Modern deployments have largely shifted to SFP-based converters and integrated ONTs. Enrichment adds current standards, clarifies the feasibility of the ISP's promise, and updates legacy references.
metadata.problem_summary: Assessing the feasibility and practicality of an ISP delivering symmetrical 50 Mbps bandwidth via optical-to-copper conversion at the customer premises.
metadata.context_summary: This theoretical document analyzes the technical feasibility and performance characteristics of converting signals between optical fiber and copper Ethernet cables. It evaluates the roles of fiber types, UTP standards, and media converters in determining the reliability and limitations of fiber-to-copper network transitions.
metadata.version_tag: IEEE 802.3 Ethernet Standards / Cat5e vs Cat5

text:
Q: My ISP says they bring optical fiber to their junction box outside and they bring the copper cable inside from the junction box. And they're promising a symmetrical bandwidth with a speed upto 50MBps. Is this even true? Can we really do that optical to copper conversion and get that speed?
I've gone through the internet searching for the answers but I've not come across this conversion anywhere. Please help me out here.

[Score:2] "copper" is a rather vague term. it could refer to Twisted pair Ethernet, docsis (cable TV coax), various types of DSL, or various legacy telco technologies.
But yes there are plenty of technologies that can deliver 50 megabits per second or more over shortish distances on various types of copper cable. The most obvious to us non-ISP folks being twisted pair Ethernet which commonly delivers gigabit speeds at distances up to 100 meters and can in principle deliver 10Gbps. ISPs may preffer DSL or DOCSIS though because of longer ranges and/or the ability to reuse existing infrastructure.
How best to provide "fiber" Internet services to homes and small business premises is something of an open question. Many providers are reluctant to install fiber right into the customer premises due to concerns about installation costs/difficulties (you can't treat fiber the way telco installers treat copper and expect it to work reliablly). Others believe that not taking the fiber all the way is a false economy that will result in expensive re-engineering as the demand for speed rises.

metadata.title: Fiber to copper conversion, what are the odds?
metadata.llm_evaluation_reason: Scored 7 per the scoring guide. Q: In this article, it is stated that latency does not affect bandwidth, but insufficient bandwidth can reduce latency.
Can someone explain me the latter part of the statement? How would insufficient bandwidth reduce latency? Is this true?

[Score:5] First, the linked article contains several poor approximation and is not IMHO a good source.
Back to the question, insufficient bandwidth leads to link congestion, that means that the equipment buffers will be full and so some packets will be delayed, waiting for their turn to be sent from those buffers, thus increasing latency.

text:
Q: In this article, it is stated that latency does not affect bandwidth, but insufficient bandwidth can reduce latency.
Can someone explain me the latter part of the statement? How would insufficient bandwidth reduce latency? Is this true?

[Score:3] The author of your reference article does not appear to fully understand or clearly illustrate the relationship of Bandwidth/Latency.
Latency doesn't affect bandwidth, but insufficient bandwidth can reduce latency.
This statement from your reference could be more accurately stated as:
Changes in Latency or changes in Bandwidth will affect the amount of time required for a set of data to travel from point A to point B.
I recommend reading O'Reilly's Network 101, Primer on Latency and Bandwidth for a reference on this topic.
O'Reilly defines Latency and Bandwidth as follows:
Latency - The time from the source sending a packet to the destination receiving it
Bandwidth - Maximum throughput of a logical or physical communication path
This relationship can be better illustrated with the following picture:
To put it another way-
Imagine that you have an entire library of books to transfer to another library. Your data set is the books that need to be moved. You can transport the books on trucks. The trucks represent a packet. The road represents the network. The speed limit and traffic lights represent the latency on a particular road or transit medium. The number of lanes on each road or maximum number of trucks that can drive simultaneously represent the bandwidth.
Increasing the speed limit or independently using a 10-lane highway to transport the books may get the books to the new library faster. However, changing the speed limit did not widen the roads, nor did widening the road change the speed limit.
Which gets ten trunks full of books to the new library faster? One truck that can be sent to arrive every 10 minutes or 10 trucks that take 100 minutes to arrive? Both will get ten trucks of books to the new library after 100 minutes. Which is better? That depends on the type of traffic and situation.
The literature goes into the concept deeper with some detailed examples.

text:
The premise in the question contains a common networking misconception. Insufficient bandwidth does *not* reduce latency; it *increases* it. When a link or device is undersized for the traffic load, packets queue up waiting for transmission (queuing delay). This queuing, combined with potential packet drops and TCP retransmissions, directly increases end-to-end latency and jitter. 

Note: 'Propagation delay' is fixed by physical distance and signal speed. The latency increase from congestion comes from 'transmission delay' and 'queuing delay'. To manage this in modern networks (SDN/QoS), engineers use traffic shaping, rate limiting, or dynamic path selection. The provided answer correctly identifies that congestion increases latency, but should explicitly distinguish between propagation delay (constant) and queuing/transmission delay (variable with bandwidth/congestion).

While it correctly notes that congestion increases latency, it incorrectly attributes this to 'packet propagation' delay. Propagation delay is physically constant; the actual latency increase stems from queuing and transmission delays. The text lacks precise terminology and direct clarification of the bandwidth-latency relationship, making it vague but salvageable with technical correction.

text:
Q: Below is from my textbook:
if we need to send data in bursts and wait for the acknowledgment of each burst before sending the next one. To use the maximum capability of the link, we need to make the size of our burst 2 times the product of bandwidth and delay; we need to fill up the full-duplex channel (two directions). The sender should send a burst of data of (2 × bandwidth × delay) bits. The sender then waits for receiver acknowledgment for part of the burst before sending another burst.
I'm a little bit confused here, let's say we have a bandwidth of 1 bps. We also assume that the delay of the link is 5s. According to what the textbook says, we need to send each burst of 10 bits. Does it mean that we make each 5 bits data as a message, and each time we send two messages actually?
PS. Below is a picture from the textbook
I don't understand why some people says the it takes 5s for receiver to receive the message, in this example, 5 bits is considered as a message, and the receiver receives the first bit after 5s, and it takes another 5s to receive the last bit, so it needs a total of 10s to receive the whole 5 bits, isn't it? besides, we can only send another message before we get the ack from the receivers, so if we send 10 bits(two messages),isn't that against the meaning of acknowledge?

[Score:3] Your textbook does a poor job of explaining the concept. You might look for other sources.
Your example doesn't work well, because the serialization delay is tremendously large (20% of the propagation delay). A more workable example is to assume the bandwidth is 1kbps. Now the serialization delay is negligible.
Let's assume the TCP window is 5kb. You start sending data. After you've sent your entire windowo, you have to stop and wait until you get an ACK to send more data. The receiver gets the data 5 seconds later, and sends an ACK. You receive the ACK after another 5 seconds for a total of 10 seconds (2 * delay). So you end up waiting 10 seconds before you can send more data. That's 5kb every 10s or 500bps.
If you make your window equal to 2 * delay * BW, or 10kb, by the time you've sent a window's worth of data, you've received the first ACK from the receiver. So you can continue to send more data. In this way, you use all the available bandwidth, instead of sending small bursts of data and waiting in between.

[Score:1] The sender should send a burst of data of (2 × bandwidth × delay) bits. The sender then waits for receiver acknowledgment for part of the burst before sending another burst.
Look at these last two sentences. The key to understanding what the author meant, about "(2 × bandwidth × delay) bits", is in the last sentence. "The sender then waits for receiver acknowledgment for part of the burst before sending another burst."
Here's a timeline of what happens.
Even though this illustration has flaws, the point the author is trying to make is that it takes time for an ACK to come back to the sender, so if we don't want this to affect the overall throughput, the sender needs to be sending out more data even while some earlier data that was sent out has not yet been acknowledged.
Probably the author may be following up later with concepts of windowing, where there can be multiple burst not yet acknowledged but the sender keeps sending burst as long as the window is not filled, etc.; and also, with more realistic assumptions like a whole "burst" needs to be received by the receiver to verify it is not in error, before the receiver sends an ACK for that burst. But the author may be building up to these concepts to come.

text:
Q: When I analyzed the wifi networks in my area, most of them are set on channel 1 and channel 6, so it would be most wise for me to set it on channel 11. My router however, provides the option to set it on channel 13. Wouldn't it be even better for me to put it on channel 13, as that is even further away from the lower channels, or would you recommend me to put it on channel 11? Thanks!

A: Channels 1, 6, and 11 are the standard non-overlapping channels in the 2.4GHz band (FCC/NTT regulatory domains). In ETSI-regulated regions (Europe, parts of Asia), channels 12 and 13 are also available and non-overlapping with 11. Always ensure your AP/router's regulatory domain matches your physical location; forcing channel 13 in FCC regions can violate compliance and cause uncontrolled interference.

For 802.11b/g/n (2.4GHz), strictly use 20MHz channel widths on channels 1, 6, or 11. Avoid 40MHz channel bonding in 2.4GHz, as it spans across multiple non-overlapping channels, significantly increasing co-channel interference and degrading throughput in typical deployments. Modern MIMO (802.11n/ac/ax) utilizes spatial streams, but basic channel planning remains unchanged.

In your case, selecting channel 11 or 13 is optimal if your device supports it and aligns with your regional regulatory domain. Best practice is to use a spectrum analyzer or Wi-Fi survey tool to identify the least congested channel and apply a static lock. Good read on SuperUser: https://superuser.com/questions/443178/is-it-better-to-use-a-crowded-2-4ghz-wi-fi-channel-1-6-11-or-unused-3-4-8

metadata.title: Is it better to use channel 13 than channel 11 on 2.4GHz band

However, it contains a technical inaccuracy by grouping 802.11a (5 GHz band) with 2.4 GHz standards (b/g). It also omits critical regulatory context: Channel 13 is only permitted in ETSI-regulated regions (Europe/Asia), while FCC-regulated regions (US/Canada) restrict users to channels 1-11. The 20MHz vs 40MHz guidance is directionally correct but lacks emphasis on why 40MHz bonding is strongly discouraged in 2.4GHz due to severe spectral overlap. The content has potential but requires regulatory clarification and standard correction for RAG database reliability.

text:
Q: When I analyzed the wifi networks in my area, most of them are set on channel 1 and channel 6, so it would be most wise for me to set it on channel 11. My router however, provides the option to set it on channel 13. Wouldn't it be even better for me to put it on channel 13, as that is even further away from the lower channels, or would you recommend me to put it on channel 11? Thanks!

[Score:0] I have a similar situation (channels 1, 6 and 11 pretty crowded, channel 13 much less), and my speed tests show that setting the router to channel 13 does give me some extra bandwidth. RF interference is not a binary "Yes/No" state, it is at maximum when the base frequencies match and gets smaller as the base frequencies get further apart. So, assuming channels 1 and 6 are not an option, having two networks in proximity on channels 11 and 13 is better than having them both on channel 11.

**Technical Correction & Context:**
The premise that channel 13 is 'further away' and thus better is misleading for 802.11b/g/n/ac/ax in the 2.4 GHz band. Wi-Fi channels are 5 MHz apart but require ~20 MHz of spectrum. This causes significant spectral overlap between channels 10, 11, 12, and 13. Placing two networks on 11 and 13 will cause adjacent-channel interference, degrading throughput rather than improving it.
- **Regional Regulations:** Channels 1-11 are standard in FCC regions (US, Canada). Channels 1-13 are standard in ETSI regions (Europe, Asia). Channel 14 is restricted to Japan and 802.11b only.
- **Non-Overlapping Set:** Use channels 1, 6, and 11 (FCC) or 1, 6, and 13 (ETSI). Do not pair 11 and 13.
- **Best Practice:** Use a Wi-Fi analyzer to find the least congested non-overlapping channel. If 1, 6, and 11 are crowded, consider switching to 5 GHz (802.11ac/ax) which has many more non-overlapping channels and avoids 2.4 GHz interference entirely.

metadata.title: Is it better to use channel 13 than channel 11 on 2.4GHz band

In 2.4GHz Wi-Fi (802.11b/g/n/ac/ax), channels are spaced 5MHz apart but require ~20MHz of spectrum, causing heavy overlap between channels 11 and 13. This creates adjacent-channel interference, not reduced interference. The text lacks crucial context on regional regulatory domains (FCC vs ETSI) and modern channel bonding.

text:
The core concept addresses the Bandwidth-Delay Product (BDP), but the numerical examples contain significant calculation errors. Here is a corrected and clarified version:

**Corrected Example:**
- Link Speed: 1 Mbps (1,000,000 bps)
- File Size: 2 Mbit
- Packet Size: 1,000 bits
- Propagation Delay: 10 ms (one-way) → RTT = 20 ms
- Transmission Time per packet: 1,000 b / 1,000,000 bps = 1 ms

**Stop-and-Wait Protocol:**
- Cycle per packet: Tx (1 ms) + RTT (20 ms) = 21 ms
- Throughput: 1,000 bits / 0.021 s ≈ 47.6 Kbps
- File time: 2,000 packets * 21 ms ≈ 42 seconds

**Sliding Window Protocol (Window Size = 10):**
- Pipeline 10 packets: Tx time = 10 * 1 ms = 10 ms
- Wait for first ACK: RTT = 20 ms
- Cycle time: 10 ms + 20 ms = 30 ms
- Throughput: 10,000 bits / 0.03 s ≈ 333 Kbps
- File time: ~20 seconds

**Key Concepts & Context:**
- **Bandwidth-Delay Product (BDP):** BDP = Bandwidth × RTT. Here, BDP = 1 Mbps × 0.02 s = 20,000 bits (2.5 KB). To fully utilize the link, the sender's window must be ≥ BDP. With a window of 10 (10,000 bits), utilization is ~50%. A window of 20 would achieve 100% utilization.
- **Throughput vs. Bandwidth:** Bandwidth is the theoretical maximum capacity. Throughput is the actual data transfer rate, limited by window size, RTT, and protocol overhead.
- **Modern Relevance:** Traditional TCP uses a 64KB maximum window, which limits utilization on high-BDP links (e.g., satellite or high-speed WAN). Modern TCP implementations use **Window Scaling (RFC 7323)** and **TCP Fast Open** to dynamically adjust windows and mitigate latency penalties. For SDN/automation contexts, QoS policies often prioritize latency-sensitive traffic on high-BDP links to prevent bufferbloat.

metadata.title: full utilization of bandwidth and its physical significance

It lacks standard terminology like Bandwidth-Delay Product (BDP) and modern TCP behavior context. Suitable for enrichment to fix calculations and add engineering relevance.

text:
Q: I was reading the following extract:
A LFN is a network that offers a high bandwidth but also a very high delay. An example could be a satellite connection. These connections offer a high bandwidth but the delay is also quite high since you have to send your signal 22000 miles up to the satellite and another 22000 miles down to reach the receiver. You can expect a round trip time anywhere between about 500-1000 ms.
The problem here is that when the sender sends some data, it has to be wait a very long time for an acknowledgment of the receiver before it can send the next data. During the time we are waiting, nothing happens so we don’t utilize the full bandwidth of our link.
Given that we don't utilize full bandwidth of our link when the sender has to wait for a long time for an acknowledgement, what do we actually mean when we say this?
Does full utilization of bandwidth means that the sender needs to pump the data continuously? Also, let me know if the data transfer rate of the sender bears any relation to the bandwidth of the link?
From what I have perceived, data transfer rate of the sender is independent of bandwidth of the link as long as it is less than the latter. Am I correct?

[Score:2] You might want to give RFC 2488 a look. It addresses your question and offers recommendations to alleviate the effects on satellite links.
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc2488 Enhancing TCP Over Satellite Channels using Standard Mechanisms

---
**Technical Enrichment & Context:**
- **Core Concept:** This is the classic **Bandwidth-Delay Product (BDP)** problem. `BDP = Bandwidth × RTT`. It defines the exact volume of unacknowledged data required to keep the link fully utilized.
- **TCP Behavior:** The sender does not need to 'pump continuously' in a raw sense; rather, it must maintain a TCP receive window that matches or exceeds the BDP. If the window is smaller than the BDP, throughput will be capped by latency, not bandwidth.
- **RFC 2488 Note:** Published in 1998, this RFC introduced TCP window scaling and SACK. These are now natively supported in all modern OS kernels. For persistent high-latency links, modern solutions include **TCP BBR**, **QUIC**, or **SCTP**.
- **Clarification:** Data transfer rate is *not* independent of bandwidth. It is strictly bounded by `min(Bandwidth, BDP/RTT)`. The sender's rate will naturally converge to the link's available bandwidth once the TCP window adapts to the BDP.

text: Conceptually, you're correct: all else being equal, the 0.3MB file will complete its transfer before the 1MB file. However, the terminology needs slight correction. Files are segmented into TCP segments (often called packets at the IP layer), not 'truncated.' The maximum transmission unit (MTU) on standard Ethernet is typically 1500 bytes, and the TCP Maximum Segment Size (MSS) is usually ~1460 bytes, not 'tens of bytes.' While smaller files finish faster in a vacuum, real-world TCP transfers are heavily influenced by factors like TCP slow start, congestion control, window scaling, and network latency/QoS policies. In a congested LAN, a larger file might actually experience faster throughput due to TCP's window growth, though it will still take longer in total bytes transferred.

text:
Q: I am wondering that between 2 hosts in LAN, when i send one file which weights 1Mb versus 1 file weights 0.3Mb from the first host to the second host using TCP/IP protocol, would the latter come first?
IMO, data will be truncated to many small ones, and therefore, 0.3Mb will be divided in a number of packets less than the case of 1Mb => it takes smaller time to be sent to the other host.

[Score:1] I am wondering that between 2 hosts in LAN, when i send one file which weights 1Mb versus 1 file weights 0.3Mb from the first host to the second host using TCP/IP protocol, would the latter come first?
Maybe. As with many things, the devil is in the details. For this discussion, let's presume all thing remain equal between the transfers (i.e. no congestion on the network, no packet loss, etc).
Yes it should, if the two transfers are completed in parallel. In other words, if both are sent independently at the same time.
No, if the transfers are completed in series. An applications may queue multiple transfer requests, and only start the second after the first has completed.
Or there may be a limit to the number of transfers that an application would run in parallel. Many web browsers and/or servers will enforce such a limitation, for example perhaps allowing the transfer of three files concurrently, and queuing additional files until one of the first three complete.

[Technical Context Addition] While application scheduling is the dominant factor, TCP transfer timing is also governed by: 1) MSS/MTU segmentation (data is chunked into ~1460-byte segments over Ethernet, not arbitrary sizes), 2) TCP sliding window & congestion control (large files pipeline data, mitigating per-packet delay), and 3) ACK processing overhead. Over a stable Fast Ethernet (100 Mbps) LAN link with no competing traffic, the 0.3MB transfer will typically complete first due to lower total byte count and reduced ACK overhead. However, real-world completion time is often dictated by client/server concurrency limits, queue management, and TCP window scaling rather than raw packet count.

text:
If configured correctly using standard protocols like IEEE 802.3ad (LACP) or static port-channels, link aggregation will not increase throughput for a *single* flow beyond the speed of one member link (1 Gbps in your example). However, with multiple concurrent flows, the aggregate bandwidth can approach the sum of all member links (2 Gbps).

Modern switches, OVS, and SDN controllers (e.g., Ryu, Mininet) typically use a flow-based hashing algorithm (hashing source/destination IP, ports, and MAC addresses) to assign flows to member links. This ensures packets within the same flow follow the same path, preventing out-of-order delivery. While some legacy or proprietary systems experimented with round-robin load balancing, it is widely discouraged today because TCP interprets out-of-order packets as loss, triggering retransmissions and actually reducing throughput below 1 Gbps per flow.

To maximize utilization, ensure your load-balancing algorithm includes relevant 5-tuple fields. Single flows (e.g., a large file download or a single TCP stream) will remain bounded by the member link speed, while multiple independent flows will distribute across the channel.

metadata.title: Does link aggregation uses the full bandwidth?

It provides diagnostic insights to optimize bandwidth utilization and resolve common performance bottlenecks.

text:
Q: If I setup a 2x 1Gig ports with link aggregation, will I get 2Gigs bandwidth or less? how less?
thank you

[Score:0] The problem with link aggregation is that latency can vary slightly for various reasons.
As a result, if packets are sent down multiple different links, they may be re-ordered compared to their original order.
Simple round-robin allocation would allow the full link-bandwidth to be used, but would risk re-ordering packets for a given communications "flow". Some protocol implementations respond badly to packet re-ordering (in particular it can be difficult to rapidly distinguish packet reordering from packet loss).
To avoid this, network vendors implemented "flow hashes". These hash a number of values from the network headers so that packets from the same "flow" are assigned to the same link.
Which values are hashed can vary, the simplest implementations may only hash MAC addresses but this will result in all communications between a given pair of Ethernet nodes getting the same "flow hash", not great if most of your traffic is between one busy server and it's default gateway. More advanced implementations will look deeper in the packet at IP addresses and TCP/UDP ports.
But even with the best hashing technique, no single flow can use the total available bandwidth and even if there are multiple flows, traffic may spread between the links in a sub-optimal manner, such that some are congested while others have spare capacity.

metadata.title: Does link aggregation uses the full bandwidth?
metadata.llm_evaluation_reason: The explanation is technically accurate and correctly addresses the fundamental behavior of link aggregation (LACP/IEEE 802.1AX). It properly explains the trade-off between bandwidth utilization and packet reordering, the necessity of flow-based hashing, per-flow bandwidth limitations, and hash distribution inefficiencies. No outdated protocols, commands, or version references are present. The content is clear, directly answers the question, and requires no structural or factual correction for a RAG database.
metadata.problem_summary: Understanding why aggregating two 1Gbps ports does not guarantee 2Gbps aggregate bandwidth for a single communication flow, due to packet reordering risks, flow hashing mechanics, and suboptimal traffic distribution across links.
metadata.context_summary: Explains that simple round-robin would risk packet reordering, so vendors use flow hashes to keep single flows on one link. However, hashing limits single-flow bandwidth to one link's capacity, and multi-flow traffic may distribute unevenly, leading to congestion on some links while others remain underutilized.

text:
Q: All these terms are quite related but I couldn't understand when to use which. I often hear that some networks have low bandwidth and high ping rate for online gaming. I thought they might be inversely proportional but that's not the case. When someone introduces "high ping and low ping" it becomes more confusing. What are they?

[Score:3] Ping is a utility to test network connectivity. It uses ICMP echo request/reply to do this. The originating host sends an ICMP echo request, and the receiving host sends an ICMP echo reply. There are several messages which may be returned:
Most ping programs run the ping several times, and they return a round-trip ICMP network latency. The Ping program usually gives you the number of packets sent, received, lost, and the loss percent, along with the lowest, highest, and average ping latency. Unfortunately, this is often confused with a real network latency, but the two may not be related on a network with multiple hops, especially the Internet where ICMP is often delayed or rerouted, unlike other traffic. ICMP is not the protocol used for most data communications, and the latency returned by ping may not reflect any other application latency.
Bandwidth is only indirectly related to latency. The bandwidth on an unloaded network has nothing at all to do with the latency. The real network latency is due mostly to network congestion, and bandwidth plays the largest part in that for busy networks. Latency reflect the aggregation of distance latency, and the latency added by network devices in the path. The largest component of the latency is by the network devices, due to congestion on the network, and a busy connection causes congestion.

metadata.title: What's the difference among bandwidth, ping, ping rate and latency?
metadata.llm_evaluation_reason: Technically accurate with minor simplifications. Clearly distinguishes bandwidth from latency and correctly explains ICMP limitations. No version-specific issues. Aligns with foundational networking principles used in QoS and SDN traffic engineering.
metadata.problem_summary: Clarifies the relationship between ping, bandwidth, and latency, explaining that ping measures round-trip ICMP latency (which may not reflect actual application latency), bandwidth represents capacity, and latency is primarily caused by distance and network congestion rather than being inversely proportional to bandwidth.
metadata.context_summary: This document clarifies the theoretical distinctions between key network performance metrics—bandwidth, ping, ping rate, and latency—while explaining their underlying mechanisms like ICMP and their relationship to network congestion. It provides a conceptual framework for understanding how these measurements differ and collectively impact network communication performance.

text:
Q: All these terms are quite related but I couldn't understand when to use which. I often hear that some networks have low bandwidth and high ping rate for online gaming. I thought they might be inversely proportional but that's not the case. When someone introduces "high ping and low ping" it becomes more confusing. What are they?

[Score:2] Ping or ping rate, the name of the network diagnostic tool, used to test whether traffic can get through or not. Having a low ping is always desirable because lower latency provides smoother gameplay by allowing faster updates of game data. For more:https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ping_(video_gaming)
Latency is the amount of time, it takes a packet to travel from source to destination, which is normally expressed in milliseconds. While bandwidth is normally expressed in bits per second. It's the amount of data that can be transferred during a second.
In short, bandwidth is a measure of capacity. Latency is a measure of delay.
If you’re downloading huge files, you probably care more about bandwidth than latency. If you’re trying to watch high-definition video, both are equally important. If you’re playing an online game, latency may be far more important than bandwidth.

While it does not explicitly cover SDN, OpenFlow, or QoS, the foundational definitions are correct and directly applicable to those domains.

command.
I'm not sure what you mean by "utilization." It looks like you are wanting a percentage, but the percentage you come up with will be over the specific time period. If you get 40%, your interface could have been at 100% for two out of the five minutes, and completely idle for three minutes.

text:
Yes, measured throughput can exceed the nominal bandwidth of a link, primarily due to differences in measurement scope and methodology:
1. **Payload vs. Wire Rate:** Bandwidth refers to the physical layer (L1) bit rate. Throughput is often measured at higher layers (L4/L7). If a monitoring tool counts only application payload (excluding Ethernet/IP/TCP headers, FCS, and inter-frame gaps), the reported rate can exceed the link's rated bandwidth.
2. **Compression:** Application-layer compression (e.g., HTTP gzip, TLS) reduces wire traffic. If the application reports the pre-compression data rate, it will appear higher than the physical link capacity.
3. **10.401 Mbps on a 10 Mbps Link:** This is generally normal in practical testing. Contributing factors include: (a) Measurement windows capturing short traffic bursts, (b) PHY implementations running slightly above nominal rates, (c) Counters including management/control frames, or (d) Tool calibration/alignment differences. Strictly speaking, sustained L2/L3 throughput cannot exceed the physical line rate, but application-level metrics frequently do.

**Note for RAG Context:** When evaluating network performance, always specify the measurement layer (L1 wire rate vs. L4/L7 payload) and tooling methodology (e.g., iPerf, SNMP, NetFlow) to avoid contradictory answers in retrieval systems.

text:
The `bandwidth` command in Cisco IOS configures a logical interface parameter; it does not physically limit throughput (unlike `srr-queue` or traffic shaping). Its critical functions are:
1. **Routing Protocols:** EIGRP uses it (along with `delay`) for its composite metric calculation. OSPF derives interface cost from it (`Cost = Reference_Bandwidth / Interface_Bandwidth`). Setting it to `20` on a 1G link will cause routing protocols to treat it as a severely constrained path, leading to suboptimal routing or loops.
2. **QoS & Traffic Policies:** Many QoS commands (e.g., `shape`, `police`, or queue weight calculations) reference the configured bandwidth for rate calculations unless explicitly overridden.
3. **Interface Types:** On tunnel interfaces or fractional physical links (e.g., T1/E1), the default bandwidth often differs from the actual line rate, requiring explicit configuration.

**Best Practice:** Do not leave it at arbitrary low values on high-speed links. Configure it to match the physical line rate (e.g., `bandwidth 1000000` for 1Gbps) to ensure accurate routing metrics and QoS behavior. Only modify it for explicit metric tuning, fractional links, or tunnel overhead calculations. This behavior is consistent across Cisco IOS 12.x through 17.x and IOS-XE.

In production, leaving `bandwidth` at an arbitrary low value (like 20 kbps) on a high-speed link will severely skew routing metrics (EIGRP/OSPF) and break QoS rate calculations. The text lacks context on when the command *should* be used (tunnel interfaces, fractional links, metric tuning) and how it interacts with modern IOS features.

text:
Q: Trying figure out the key differences between T568A and T568B wiring, if any exist.
I found claims in a thread that T568A has advantages over T568B due to the fact that each twisted-pair in a cat5/cat6 varies slightly in the tightness of the winding. That would imply that, while nominal, selection of twisted-pairs for TX/RX does have a physical impact. (http://community.spiceworks.com/topic/422129-t568a-or-t568b)
Can someone explain the reason why one might claim 568A is better than 568B; or if this isn't true?

[Score:5] Neither is "better." It's a matter of convention only. T568B has always appeared to me to be in much more widespread use.
It is true that bulk cat5/6 has variances in the tightness of the winding of each pair, but that wouldn't cause one spec to be more appropriate than the other. Only if one spec required more untwisting for termination or something like that could we say that it may be "worse" or degrade the transmission quality more.

metadata.title: Do physical wire properties impact the choice between T568A & T568B?
metadata.llm_evaluation_reason: The answer is technically accurate. T568A and T568B are functionally identical for Ethernet; the only difference is the physical pinout mapping (swapping pairs 1/2 and 3/6). The claim regarding twisted-pair winding tightness affecting performance is negligible and incorrect in practice. Modern switches utilize auto-MDI/MDIX, which automatically corrects for crossover/straight-through wiring, rendering the choice purely a matter of organizational convention. The response correctly dismisses the myth. It could be slightly improved by mentioning auto-MDI/MDIX and the current TIA/EIA-568-C standards, but it is fundamentally sound and does not require revision.

text: The original answer correctly states that 802.11 PHY data rates reflect raw airtime transmission speeds, independent of higher-layer headers. However, it omits the calculation method and critical WiFi-specific overhead. To estimate effective throughput: Effective Throughput ≈ PHY Rate × (Payload Size / Total Frame Size) × Medium Efficiency. WiFi overhead includes MAC headers (~30-36 bytes), ACK frames (11 bytes), inter-frame spaces (DIFS/SIFS), and CSMA/CA contention. TCP adds ~40 bytes (IP+TCP) per segment, plus retransmission penalties. For a standard 1500-byte MTU, real-world TCP throughput typically achieves 50-65% of the advertised PHY rate due to combined MAC, IP, and TCP overhead. For RAG documentation, clarify that PHY rates are Layer 1 metrics, while application throughput depends on the full protocol stack and medium access control dynamics.

text:
Q: Rx means Receive, and Tx means Transmit. I need to know how can i calculate link utilization (Bandwidth utilization) from these metrics ?, do i need to average them or getting maximum ?

[Score:2] Usually, you express utilization as the higher of Rx and Tx. If your link bandwidth is asymmetrical, you would calculate it for each direction.
Since utilization is expressed in percentage of maximum bandwidth, you need to calculate the measured bandwidth used, which is
BW(meas) = Bits(rx or tx)/time period
.
Then utilization is
Utilization = BW(meas) / BW(link) * 100

It does not show you:
You need to know how many bits were transferred versus how many bits could have been transferred during a time interval to calculate bandwidth utilization.
Inbound and outbound bandwidth utilization are usually calculated and reported separately.

1011859 packets input, 799292486 bytes

0 PAUSE input
88567 packets output, 15703989 bytes
Sent 1 broadcasts, 88453 multicast
0 output errors, 0 collisions
0 late collision, 0 deferred, 0 output discards
0 PAUSE output
I am trying to find the available capacity on this interface in terms of bytes or bits for eg. Total link capacity is 10Gb and out of that 5Gb is available. I know that the "5 minutes input rate and output" section from the output should help, but I am really confused as to what formula to be applied and how? Please help.

[Score:4] I believe what you're really needing to know is whether the interface is being used "optimally" and how much "spare capacity" you have on that link. These are fuzzy terms, but important because you need to know whether, for example, that 10Gbps interface needs to be replaced with a 40Gbps.
From the "show" command output we know that in the last five minutes there was very little traffic on the interface. But maybe three days ago, at 2pm, there was 9 Gbps traffic for a few seconds or milliseconds. The "show" command output right now is not going to give that information. The only way for you to get that data is to monitor the interface over a period of time (like maybe a week or a month), especially when there are peak workloads.

**[RAG Enrichment / Technical Context]**
- **Historical Data Collection:** Use SNMP polling (MIB-II `ifHCInOctets`/`ifHCOutOctets` for 64-bit counters), NetFlow/IPFIX, or streaming telemetry (gRPC) to capture traffic over days/weeks. CLI snapshots cannot replace time-series databases for capacity planning.
- **Available Capacity Formula:** `Available BW = Total Link BW - (Peak 95th/99th Percentile Usage × Burst Headroom)`. Network best practices reserve 20-30% headroom for micro-bursts, QoS shaping, and protocol overhead.
- **Automation & SDN Integration:** In modern/SDN environments (OpenFlow, Ryu, Mininet), available capacity is often calculated dynamically via controller telemetry APIs rather than static CLI polling. Controllers can enforce rate-limiting or reroute flows using QoS policies (CBWFQ/LLQ) when thresholds are breached.
- **CLI Alternatives:** While `show interface` is limited, `show interfaces counters` or `show interfaces statistics` may provide longer historical buckets on some platforms, but external monitoring remains the industry standard.

Q: Below is the output from my "show interface " command
PE1.18:01:44#sh interfaces Ethernet 3
Ethernet3 is up, line protocol is up (connected)
Hardware is Ethernet, address is 001c.7374.3d95
Internet address is 172.16.11.1/30
Broadcast address is 255.255.255.255
Address determined by manual configuration
IP MTU 1500 bytes , BW 10000000 kbit
Full-duplex, 10Gb/s, auto negotiation: off, uni-link: disabled
Up 30 days, 9 minutes, 55 seconds
Loopback Mode : None
4 link status changes since last clear
Last clearing of "show interface" counters 45 days, 23:02:51 ago
5 minutes input rate 48 bps (0.0% with framing overhead), 0 packets/sec
5 minutes output rate 48 bps (0.0% with framing overhead), 0 packets/sec
1011859 packets input, 799292486 bytes
Received 1 broadcasts, 1011570 multicast
0 runts, 0 giants
0 input errors, 0 CRC, 0 alignment, 0 symbol, 0 input discards
0 PAUSE input
88567 packets output, 15703989 bytes
Sent 1 broadcasts, 88453 multicast
0 output errors, 0 collisions
0 late collision, 0 deferred, 0 output discards
0 PAUSE output
I am trying to find the available capacity on this interface in terms of bytes or bits for eg. Total link capacity is 10Gb and out of that 5Gb is available. I know that the "5 minutes input rate and output" section from the output should help, but I am really confused as to what formula to be applied and how? Please help.

[Score:0] OK. The 5 minute rate is how many bits were received in the last 300 seconds, divided by the number of seconds, to give you the number of bits per second over the last five minutes. It is possible that you have only a minimum frame send during the last five minutes. The frame will be sent at 10 Gbps, but your five minute rate will be much less because the entire frame will be sent in just over five hundred-millionths of a second. Even a full frame is sent in about three times that period.
The framing overhead (18 octets or 144 bits on non-tagged frames) takes into account the ethernet frame overhead on a frame (frames are sent one at a time on the link). Ethernet also has a seven-octet (56 bits) preamble, and a one-octet (eight bits) start of frame (SoF) delimiter, preceding each frame. There is also a 12-octet (48 bits) inter-frame gap between frames. All of that is layer-2 overhead before getting into the frame payload (the layer-3 packet).
Then you have the layer-3 packet overhead, the layer-4 transport protocol datagram overhead, and (possibly) the application protocol overhead before you can even start discussing the application data sent over the link.

text:
Q: Can someone please explain is it a good practice to change RSVP bandwidth value of an interface?
We have two bundle links between R1 and R2 namely ae13 and ae22 . Both R1 and R2 are Juniper MX series. AE13 bundle link has 16 x 10G member links and has been running quite long.
We recently add 1 x 100G link between R1 and R2. We made it a part of bundle link AE22. We also hard code the IS-IS cost values of BOTH Bundle links to be 10 to enable load balancing (ECMP).
On Both bundle links , AE13 and AE22 , RSVP , MPLS , IS-IS , Micro BFD are running.
But when we run command 'show rsvp interface' on R1/R2 , it shows more bandwidth reservations (almost all) on previous bundle link (AE13) while very few reservations (below 10) were on new bundle link AE22. By default RSVP bandwidth value of AE13 was showing to be 160 Gbps (as it as 16 x 10G Links) while for AE22 it was showing to be 100 Gbps.
When we change RSVP bandwidth value of AE13 (using command 'set protocols rsvp interface ae13.3 bandwidth 100g') we saw 'active rsvp' decreased on both AE13 and AE22.
*************Before Configuring bandwidth value of AE13*********************
RSVP interface: 6 active
Active Subscr- Static Available Reserved Highwater
Interface State resv iption BW BW BW mark
ae0.3 Up 41 100% 200Gbps 199.2Gbps 800Mbps 51.3225Gbps
ae1.3 Up 13 100% 90Gbps 87.5431Gbps 2.4569Gbps 26.1529Gbps
ae13.3 Up 101 100% 140Gbps 126.788Gbps 13.2123Gbps 101.843Gbps
ae22.3 Up 34 100% 100Gbps 72.3477Gbps 27.6522Gbps 27.6522Gbps
ae28.3 Up 178 100% 170Gbps 133.481Gbps 36.5188Gbps 130.511Gbps
ae8.3 Up 147 100% 170Gbps 145.125Gbps 24.8747Gbps 136.584Gbps
***************After configuring bandwidth value of AE13********************
RSVP interface: 6 active
Active Subscr- Static Available Reserved Highwater
Interface State resv iption BW BW BW mark
ae0.3 Up 39 100% 200Gbps 199.2Gbps 800Mbps 51.3225Gbps
ae1.3 Up 14 100% 90Gbps 88.7145Gbps 1.28549Gbps 26.1529Gbps
ae13.3 Up 93 100% 100Gbps 96.0119Gbps 3.98813Gbps 101.843Gbps
ae22.3 Up 19 100% 100Gbps 85.975Gbps 14.025Gbps 27.6522Gbps
ae28.3 Up 177 100% 170Gbps 141.339Gbps 28.6607Gbps 130.511Gbps
ae8.3 Up 147 100% 180Gbps 158.668Gbps 21.3322Gbps 136.584Gbps
So is it a good practice to change RSVP bandwidth value of an interface? What are its shortcomings / drawbacks / after effects if there are any?
Thank you very much.

[Score:3] Firstly, there looks to be something wrong with ae13 - if you have 16x 10G interfaces attached to it, you should have an RSVP interface bandwidth of 160Gbps, not 130Gbps as per your output?
So is it a good practice to change RSVP bandwidth value of an interface?
There is nothing inherently good or bad about manually overriding the speed of an interface in RSVP - it is a perfectly fine way to limit the bandwidth of any LSPs that run across it.
We have used this quite successfully in environments where the link is attached to sub-rate services (eg: a 400Mbps microwave service attached on a 1G port) and we need to override the reported interface speed.
What are its shortcomings / drawbacks / after effects if there are any?
As you can see from your output, the after-effects are that there is now 30G (or 60G as per issue above) of capacity across that ae13 link that will never be available for RSVP LSPs.
When you changed the speed value, RSVP would have re-run it's CSPF algorithm and re-signalled all your LSPs accordingly.
Depending on the bandwidth of each LSP and the order they were re-signalled in (and any priorities you have configured on them) would determine which links they flow across.
The number of Active reservations on each link is not really relevant - it's the bandwidth in use that is being distributed.

text: When a router's egress queue is full due to congestion, packets are dropped at the local egress interface, not by the upstream router. Ingress drops occur only when the line card's forwarding capacity or ingress buffer is exceeded. Both egress and ingress drops trigger TCP retransmissions, but TCP's congestion control algorithm (e.g., Cubic/Reno) quickly reduces the transmission rate to match available bandwidth, causing retransmissions to stabilize at a low frequency. Regarding monitoring: retransmissions are generated by the sender. A receiver capture won't show the retransmitted packets directly but will show duplicate ACKs or out-of-order segments indicating loss. To accurately measure drops, use sender-side tools or middlebox monitoring. The Data Link Layer and Ethernet Inter-Packet Gap (IPG) are indeed unrelated to traffic control; IPG is a fixed IEEE 802.3 timing parameter. Router traffic control operates at Layer 3/4 using QoS mechanisms such as policing (hard rate limits), shaping (buffering to smooth bursts), and queuing disciplines (e.g., FIFO, WFQ, CBWFQ, RED/WRED). In modern enterprise networks, these are configured via vendor CLIs (e.g., Cisco IOS QoS) or programmable data planes (OpenFlow/SDN controllers).

metadata.title: How does router do traffic control?
metadata.llm_evaluation_reason: Generally correct on TCP behavior and PHY/MAC layer separation, but contains inaccuracies regarding drop locations (upstream vs egress queue) and oversimplifies retransmission visibility. Lacks QoS implementation context.
metadata.problem_summary: Clarification on why TCP retransmissions are not visible when a router drops packets due to capacity limits, explaining ingress vs. uplink dropping, TCP congestion control adaptation, and sender/receiver visibility of retransmissions.

[Score:3] There is one particularity about iperf 2.0.x with UDP: Within the limits (CPU, NIC, UDP/IP stack and its buffers) of the sending system, iPerf will send traffic at the payload rate you specify with the -b
parameter (if left out, iperf will default to 1Mbit/s).
EDIT: D'oh! Only now I spotted that you are actually using iperf3. I'll leave these comments here, all the same.
With UDP, iPerf 2.0.x will not bother nor check if and how much of the data the receiver/server is getting, or even if there actually is a receiver/server at the IP address given in the -c <ip.addr.ess.here>
parameter. [1]
iperf 3.x changes this insofar as the receiver/server end no longer makes a difference between UDP and TCP, you just start iperf3 in "server mode". The client will specify if UDP or TCP is to be used, and there will be some form of negotiation between client and server, before the actual test is performed.
EDIT: If iperf3 client and server fail to negotiate, the UDP test will not start. However, if negotiation succeeds, the iperf3 client will still send at the configured data/payload rate, regardless if and how much of the data makes it to the server.
Therefore, with UDP testing, it is important to check the results at the server/receiver end, not on the client/sender. On iperf 1.7x and 2.0.x, you need to look at the server side output as it is generated there. With iperf3, you can "pull" the server side results with command-line parameter --get-server-output
.
Suggestion for iperf2.0.x: run iperf -s -u -i 1
on the server side to get 1sec reporting intervals.
Suggestion for iperf3.x.x: run iperf3 -s
server-side and ... -i 1 --get-server-output
client-side This will also give ou some information about the pps figures of your test stream and of course the packet loss rate, which is the interesting part.
[1] Especially this property of iPerf 2.0.x with UDP makes it a tool to use with great caution. Trying to pump a single 50Mbit/s stream of UDP across the upstream of a 100/20 xDSL line will make the entire access line unusable.

UDP lacks flow control, so iperf does not throttle based on network conditions. The advice to check receiver output is technically sound.

text:
Q: In my work, I come across with a cable with TB/s capacity and speed in GB/s.
I did some search and found and article from Cisco trying to explain the two terminologies in terms of a water tap. The capacity is like the width of the tap pipe and mouth; while speed is how faster water is flowing through the pipe.
I understand this example, but I found it hard to apply to the cable situation, and eventually hard to understand the two terms.
In the water tap example the capacity is measured with a unit without per second. But in the world of cable, capacity is measured TB/s.
The definition of bandwidth is how much data can be transferred in a second. Since capacity say this cable can support 1TB/s, doesn't it mean given an unrealistic ideal world where there are no lost, interference whatever, it can allows 1TB of data to pass through? If so, isn't this already the capability of this cable. How should I understand the term speed and why should the cable states it?
Reference: https://learningnetwork.cisco.com/blogs/vip-perspectives/2015/10/16/bandwidth-vs-speed

[Score:5] Your question touches on a common point of confusion. The capacity you refer to is called **bandwidth**, and it is measured in bps (bits per second), e.g., 1 Gbps or 10 Gbps. The **speed** of signal propagation on a cable is fixed by physics (approximately two-thirds the speed of light in a vacuum for copper), and this propagation delay is relatively consistent across most modern cabling. Bandwidth, however, is a function of the physical layer encoding, signaling frequency, and the quality of the cable and its installation.

Cable standards (ANSI/TIA-568 for copper, ISO/IEC for fiber) define parameters like Insertion Loss, NEXT (Near-End Crosstalk), FEXT, Return Loss, Propagation Delay, and Skew. These parameters determine the maximum signaling frequency a cable can support (e.g., 100 MHz for Cat-5e). The device interfaces determine the encoding scheme, but the cable must meet the standard's requirements to function at that interface's rated bandwidth.

Installation quality and standards (e.g., TIA-568) are critical. Poorly installed cable or mismatched components will degrade performance. For example, properly installed Cat-5e supports 10BASE-T (10 Mbps), 100BASE-TX (100 Mbps), and 1000BASE-T (1 Gbps), but **not** 10GBASE-T (which requires Cat-6a or better for 100m runs).

**[Context/Correction]:** Modern Ethernet uses auto-negotiation (IEEE 802.3ab/az). The claim that mismatched interfaces will transmit 'garbage' is outdated. In practice, devices negotiate to the highest common speed supported by both endpoints and the cable. If you connect a 10G port to Cat-5e, the link will typically fail to establish (link down) rather than passing corrupted data, unless auto-negotiation is manually disabled and forced (which is strongly discouraged in production). The water pipe analogy breaks down here because digital signaling adapts to channel conditions via error correction and negotiation, unlike analog water flow.

metadata.title: What are the capacity and speed of a cable?
metadata.llm_evaluation_reason: The document correctly distinguishes between data rate (bandwidth) and signal propagation speed, and accurately references cable standards and physical layer parameters. However, it contains a significantly outdated/misleading claim about mismatched interfaces transmitting 'garbage' instead of negotiating or failing link establishment. Modern Ethernet relies on IEEE auto-negotiation, making the original explanation potentially confusing for learners.
technology: Physical Layer Parameters (Insertion Loss, NEXT, FEXT, Return Loss, Propagation Delay, Skew)
metadata.problem_summary: Clarification of the conceptual difference between network bandwidth (data transfer rate measured in bps) and physical signal propagation speed in cables, and how cable ratings/standards relate to device interface capabilities.

text: Bisection bandwidth is formally defined as the aggregate bandwidth of the minimum set of links that must be cut to divide a network topology into two equal halves. The intuition that it represents a 'lower bound' on network capacity is correct: it quantifies the worst-case aggregate throughput when half the nodes simultaneously exchange data with the other half. It does not represent average per-node bandwidth; individual node capacity is instead constrained by port speed and uplink oversubscription ratios. In legacy STP-based Ethernet, bisection bandwidth is often bottlenecked by a single active path across the cut. Modern data center topologies (e.g., Clos/Fat-Tree) and SDN-controlled fabrics (OpenFlow/Ryu, OVS with ECMP) leverage multipath forwarding to load-balance traffic across all parallel links, maximizing achievable bisection bandwidth. When simulating or benchmarking networks in Mininet or testing QoS/forwarding policies, bisection bandwidth serves as a critical baseline for evaluating congestion, packet loss, and throughput scaling under all-to-all communication patterns.

metadata.title: Understanding bisection bandwidth
metadata.llm_evaluation_reason: The original answer misstates the fundamental definition of bisection bandwidth (claiming equal partitioning is not required, which contradicts standard HPC/networking literature), lacks precision on the 'lower bound' intuition, and provides outdated legacy context (STP) without connecting to modern data center or SDN architectures.
metadata.problem_summary: Clarification of conflicting definitions for 'bisection bandwidth' between Wikipedia and research literature, and determination of whether the metric applies to individual nodes or inter-switch link aggregates.

text:
Q: Suppose there are 4 switches connected using a normal ethernet cables of speed 100mbps as follows:
(S1)---100mbps---->(S2)----100mbps--->(S3)----100mbps--->(S4)
Now suppose the link between S3 and S4 suddenly becomes congested and the transmission rate decreases to 50 mbps. I have a question based on this scenario:
Does the path from S1 to S3 need to adjust the transmission rate to deal with the decrease happened on the link between S3 to S4? If yes, how?
Thank you.

[Score:3] Why would congestion on that link cause the transmission rate to drop? I think you are working from an incorrect premise. You can try to run 200 Mbps through a 100 Mbps link, but the transmission rate will stay at 100 Mbps; the excess will just be dropped.
This is layer-2 (switches and ethernet) which don't have any facility for re-transmission of dropped frames. When frames are dropped, they are just lost.
Let's say that S4 is a 48-port switch, and the 48 access ports are each 100 Mbps, as is the uplink port. If all 48 access ports suddenly decided to transmit 100 Mbps each through the 100 Mbps uplink port, the uplink port would transmit 100 Mbps of the 4800 Mbps of traffic trying to use it, and the other 4700 Mbps would be lost.

**Technical Context & Modern Practice:**
While the L2 link speed remains fixed at the negotiated rate (100 Mbps), networks do not simply accept drops. Congestion is managed through several mechanisms:
1. **IEEE 802.3x Flow Control (PAUSE frames):** The congested switch (S4) can send PAUSE frames upstream to S3, temporarily halting transmission to prevent buffer overflow. This does not change the rate but backpressures the link.
2. **QoS & Traffic Management:** Mechanisms like WRED, ECN, or hierarchical QoS can proactively drop/prioritize frames or rate-limit ingress traffic before congestion occurs.
3. **Higher-Layer Feedback:** TCP congestion control algorithms (e.g., Reno, CUBIC) detect packet loss or ECN marks and instruct endpoints to reduce their sending rate. The switches themselves do not adjust physical transmission rates.
4. **SDN/Automation Context:** In SDN environments (e.g., OpenFlow/Ryu/OVS), controllers can dynamically install flow entries to rate-limit, queue, or reroute traffic based on real-time telemetry, effectively automating the 'adjustment' the question asks about.

metadata.title: Understanding the effect of transmission rate change in network
metadata.llm_evaluation_reason: Technically accurate regarding fundamental Layer 2 Ethernet behavior, but lacks critical context on how modern networks actually manage congestion. The explanation correctly notes that L2 links do not auto-throttle and frames are dropped, but omits standard mechanisms like IEEE 802.3x Flow Control, QoS policing/shaping, ECN, and TCP congestion control that dictate how transmission rates are actually adjusted in practice.
metadata.problem_summary: Clarifies whether upstream switches need to adjust transmission rates when a downstream link experiences congestion, explaining that Layer 2 switches drop excess frames rather than dynamically throttling rates.
metadata.context_summary: Clarifies whether upstream switches need to adjust transmission rates when a downstream link experiences congestion, explaining that Layer 2 switches drop excess frames rather than dynamically throttling rates.

text:
Q: If a certain cable has a bandwidth of 10 mbps
, that means in can contain 10 megabytes of data inside it at any given second.
However, does this assume some standard length?
The analogy I've been given is that the cable transmitting data is like a pipe carrying water, and the bandwidth is like the amount of water the pipe can hold. However, longer pipes can hold more water.
Do longer cables have more bandwidth?

[Score:4] If a certain cable has a bandwidth of 10 mbps, that means in can contain 10 megabytes of data inside it at any given second.
Not at all, it means it can send 10 megabits over the time period of one second.
However, does this assume some standard length?
This depends on the technology, but most often: yes. For example, the popular 10base-T ethernet specification assumes a nominal maximum length of 100m. Longer than that your bandwidth will most likely drop (quickly to 0). Even with short enough cables other factors can diminish your throughput however, e.g. bad connectors.
The analogy I've been given is that the cable transmitting data is like a pipe carrying water, and the bandwidth is like the amount of water the pipe can hold. However, longer pipes can hold more water.
Decent analogy, completely wrong interpretation. It is not about how much water a pipe can hold, almost nobody really cares about that. It's about how much water you can get through at a given time. E.g. you don't care that your garden hose can hold a full swimming pool worth of water at once, you care how fast that hose can fill that swimming pool so you can take a fresh dive. In hydraulics terms, you care about discharge. This does not increase as a factor of length (rather decrease). It increases based on diameter and pressure.
Bandwidth is comparable to discharge in this analogy, so no it does increase with length (again, rather decrese), but mainly depends on other factors like modulation and interference levels.
You cannot say that an ethernet cable can hold data (bits) as a water pipe can hold water. Bits are modulated on an electromagnetic wave, which is only there for as long as that wave is traveling your cable (which is even an over-simplified statement). There is not a tap that you can shut so that all the bits currently in the cable stay there.
Do longer cables have more bandwidth?
No

text: The core distinction between propagation delay and link bandwidth is accurate. However, for modern network engineering contexts: (1) CSMA/CD is obsolete in full-duplex switched Ethernet; the 100m copper limit is now enforced by signal integrity thresholds (attenuation, NEXT/FeXT) and PHY auto-negotiation timing. (2) Exceeding spec does not silently drop packets; the PHY will negotiate down to a lower speed (e.g., 1G → 100M → 10M) or drop the link entirely. (3) Bandwidth is a negotiated PHY-layer parameter, while actual throughput depends on frame size, encoding efficiency (e.g., 64b/66b), and protocol overhead. For SDN/datacenter automation, fiber (OM3/OM4/OS2) or DAC/AEC cables are preferred to guarantee deterministic latency and QoS compliance across control/data planes. Cable length constraints are managed via standardized patching practices and automated link-state monitoring (e.g., SNMP/Telemetry), not application-layer retransmissions.

metadata.title: Is the bandwidth of cable constrained by its length?

text:
No. Bandwidth is a measure of data transfer rate (bits per second), not storage capacity. The analogy of a pipe's width is more accurate than its volume. Cable length does not increase bandwidth; it increases propagation latency.

While the original Ethernet standard (10BASE-T/100BASE-TX) limits copper twisted-pair cables to 100 meters, this is primarily due to signal attenuation, crosstalk, and historical CSMA/CD collision domain timing constraints, not TCP ACK timeouts. Modern full-duplex Ethernet eliminates CSMA/CD, but the 100m limit remains for signal integrity.

Longer cables do increase latency because electrical signals travel at roughly 0.6c to 0.7c in copper. This added propagation delay can reduce effective throughput for latency-sensitive applications or when TCP window sizes are small, but the raw link bandwidth remains unchanged. Additionally, Layer 2 switches/bridges do not use acknowledgments (ACKs); they use store-and-forward or cut-through forwarding. While repeaters/bridges regenerate signals, the processing latency they introduce is typically in the microsecond/nanosecond range, making their impact on total throughput negligible compared to propagation delay.

metadata.title: Is the bandwidth of cable constrained by its length?

text: The core concept is correct: uplink sizing depends on aggregate traffic patterns and oversubscription ratios. However, modern enterprise best practice uses a 10G uplink as the baseline for 1G access switches, typically implemented via LACP (e.g., 2x10G for 20G) to provide deterministic bandwidth, load balancing, and redundancy. Standard oversubscription ratios range from 3:1 to 10:1, heavily dependent on QoS policies and traffic profiling. Instead of relying on node count, calculate expected peak utilization (keep links below 70-80% to handle bursts and protocol overhead). Monitor both bandwidth and packet rates, as modern networks are often packet-rate limited. Implement QoS/DiffServ to prioritize latency-sensitive traffic (VoIP, control plane, backup windows) over best-effort traffic before making capacity decisions.

text:
The correct thing would be to monitor this switch, so you would know how much these users consume today. If you think about expanding this network in this location, I believe that you should think about a switch with SFP+, at first you can use the 1Gbps Ethernet port, if necessary change to SFP+, that way the equipment will serve you for a good time. Think about how much your network will grow, and how long you want to keep the equipment, good luck! ;P

---
**Technical Context & Best Practices:**
- **Oversubscription Ratios:** Uplink sizing does not require matching the sum of downlink ports. Access layer switches typically operate at a 5:1 to 10:1 oversubscription ratio because simultaneous 100% utilization across all ports is statistically improbable. For 8x1G downlinks, an 8G theoretical maximum is virtually never sustained.
- **Uplink Recommendation:** A single 10G (SFP+) uplink is the modern industry standard for this scale. It provides adequate headroom for burst traffic, aggregation layer connectivity, and future upgrades without requiring complex Link Aggregation (LACP) configurations.
- **Monitoring & Growth Planning:** Use SNMP, NetFlow, or sFlow to measure actual peak utilization during business hours. If sustained throughput consistently exceeds 70-80% of the uplink capacity, upgrade to 10G or deploy 2x1G LACP for deterministic bandwidth scaling.
- **QoS Integration:** Raw bandwidth scaling is less effective than proper traffic prioritization. Implement QoS policies (e.g., LLQ, DSCP marking, traffic shaping) on the uplink to ensure critical applications maintain performance during congestion events.

To guarantee bandwidth and provide redundancy, use link aggregation (e.g., Cisco Port-channel/EtherChannel with LACP, or standard IEEE 802.3ad) to bundle multiple physical uplinks into a single logical interface. If deterministic wire-speed is required (e.g., for server farms, storage networks, or high-performance computing), size the uplink to match the total downlink capacity (8x1G = 8G, requiring 10G or 25G ports with LACP enabled).

text:
[Score: 6] **Formal Definition:** In networking standards (ITU-T, IEEE), 'bandwidth capacity' refers to the maximum theoretical data transfer rate of a physical or logical link, typically measured in bits per second (bps). For internet connections, this is commonly termed 'provisioned bandwidth' or 'line rate.' However, actual achievable throughput is governed by TCP congestion control, RTT, path MTU, and intermediate bottlenecks.

**Testing Methodology:** To measure bandwidth capacity accurately per industry standards (RFC 2544, RFC 2889, ITU-T Y.1541):
1. Use iPerf3 with multiple parallel streams to saturate the link: `iperf3 -c <server> -P 10 -t 60`
2. Isolate the CPE-to-ISP path using a dedicated test server within the ISP's network or at a major IX.
3. Document RTT, packet loss, and jitter, as these directly impact TCP throughput (per RFC 2414/2412).

**Key References:**
- Kurose & Ross, *Computer Networking: A Top-Down Approach* (Chapter 1: Overview)
- ITU-T Y.1541: Network packet layer engineering
- RFC 2544: Benchmarking Test Methodology for Network Interconnect Devices
- RFC 2889: IP Throughput Measurement Guidelines

**Practical Note:** As noted, multi-flow testing is required for >100 Mbps links due to TCP's single-flow limitations. Always align expectations with the SLA, as ISP provisioning, peering ratios, and congestion dictate real-world performance rather than theoretical line capacity.

Enrichment adds formal definitions, modern testing commands (iPerf3), relevant RFC/ITU-T standards, and clarifies theoretical vs. practical bandwidth while preserving the original's practical advice.

text:
Q: in our topology we have our Data Server(Multi Virtual Servers) and Backup Server exist in two different buildings and the current connection between them as below:
Connection explanation:
*1st building:
-Data Server connected to a fiber switch using 1 port-channel of 4x1 gigabit ports -Fiber Switch connected to Core Switch using 2 port-channels of 2x1 gigabit ports
*2nd building:
-Core Switch connected to Fiber Switch using 2 port-channels of 2x1 gigabit ports -Fiber Switch connected to a Backup Server using 1 port-channel of 4x1 gigabit ports
well,this connectivity is not giving more than 1 gbps and it is very less for the amount of data to be transferred.
will we get more bandwidth by changing the connectivity to be as below:
Connection explanation:
*1st building:
-Data Server connected to Core Switch using 2 port-channels of 2x1 gigabit ports
*2nd building: -Core Switch connected to Backup Server using 2 port-channels of 2x1 gigabit ports
Sorry for the long explanation and looking for your help. Regards, Ethem

[Score:1] I think you're misunderstanding port channels. Any one flow will still be capped at the speed of a single link in the channel. There is a hashing algorithm that decides which link a flow will use. With multiple flows, all the links will be used, so in aggregate, you can get the full bandwidth of the channel, but any one flow will be capped at the bandwidth of a single link, although it may be less because it may need to share that link with other flows.
You do not want a single flow to use more than one link. That will increase out-of-order packet delivery. TCP can handle that, but it will slow processing. UDP cannot handle that, and it will destroy real-time protocols such as VoIP or video.

metadata.title: Multi Chassis Ether Channel Best Practice
metadata.llm_evaluation_reason: The response accurately explains link aggregation behavior, specifically addressing the common misconception that a single flow can exceed a single physical link's speed. It correctly identifies the role of hashing algorithms, distinguishes between aggregate and per-flow bandwidth, and appropriately warns about out-of-order packet implications for TCP and UDP. The concepts align with current IEEE 802.3ad/Cisco Port-Channel standards. No SDN/OpenFlow versions are involved, but the traditional switching fundamentals are technically sound and require no updates.
metadata.problem_summary: The user wants to increase bandwidth for a single server-to-server data transfer across two buildings by bypassing intermediate switches and connecting directly to the core switch via multiple port-channels. The response clarifies that port-channel hashing algorithms limit any single data flow to a single link's speed, so the proposed topology change will not increase bandwidth for a single flow and could cause out-of-order packet delivery.

text:
Q: in our topology we have our Data Server(Multi Virtual Servers) and Backup Server exist in two different buildings and the current connection between them as below:
Connection explanation:
*1st building:
-Data Server connected to a fiber switch using 1 port-channel of 4x1 gigabit ports -Fiber Switch connected to Core Switch using 2 port-channels of 2x1 gigabit ports
*2nd building:
-Core Switch connected to Fiber Switch using 2 port-channels of 2x1 gigabit ports -Fiber Switch connected to a Backup Server using 1 port-channel of 4x1 gigabit ports
well,this connectivity is not giving more than 1 gbps and it is very less for the amount of data to be transferred.
will we get more bandwidth by changing the connectivity to be as below:
Connection explanation:
*1st building:
-Data Server connected to Core Switch using 2 port-channels of 2x1 gigabit ports
*2nd building: -Core Switch connected to Backup Server using 2 port-channels of 2x1 gigabit ports
Sorry for the long explanation and looking for your help. Regards, Ethem

[Score:1] Any single flow cannot exceed the bandwidth of a single member link within a port channel, so in your case, a single flow cannot go above 1 Gbps.
If there are multiple flows between the two servers, you may be able to benefit from more than 1 Gbps, if you adjust the load balancing mechanism on the switches to take into account L4 port number (if supported). You would need to do this on the servers as well (again, if supported) and configure the application to use multiple ports. You may then get higher than 1 Gbps aggregate bandwidth
The main issue with both of your topologies above is that you are using multiple port channels on the VSS on either side. In the top diagram, the 4 links connecting the VSS to fibre switch 1 are in two bundles. Same goes for the 4 links connecting to fibre switch 2. Also, in your second diagram, the same is occurring (2 bundles to server 1, two bundles to server 2). This doesn't make best use of the bandwidth as you are providing a looped L2 topology causing STP to go blocking on some of the links. You will find that half the links on the left and half the links on the right are STP blocking, reducing your aggregate throughout to the VSS from 4 Gbps down to 2 Gbps. The VSS acts as a single switch logically to STP. You can bundle all 4 links to fibre switch 1 as one port channel (on both sides), same for fibre switch 2 and same in the second topology. This way you will get the full 4 Gbps (although still limited to 1 Gbps per flow)

text:
The headline 'AC1200' speed refers to the aggregate theoretical PHY rate across all spatial streams and frequency bands (2.4 GHz and 5 GHz), not the actual sustained throughput available to clients. In practice, real-world WiFi throughput typically achieves 50–60% of the advertised PHY rate due to protocol overhead (802.11 MAC headers, ACKs, backoffs), CSMA/CA contention, and half-duplex operation.

Gigabit Ethernet provides dedicated full-duplex channels, meaning it can sustain ~940 Mbps of actual throughput in both directions simultaneously. WiFi, however, is a shared half-duplex medium. While the wireless air interface can theoretically handle more than 1 Gbps of aggregate client-to-client traffic, the 1 Gbps Ethernet backhaul will become a hard bottleneck for any traffic destined for the WAN, other VLANs, or wired network segments.

If multiple clients are actively downloading from the internet simultaneously, their combined throughput will be capped at the backhaul speed. To mitigate this, modern enterprise APs often support Link Aggregation (LACP), 2.5 Gbps/5 Gbps ports, or dual 1 Gbps ports. For pure local wireless transfers (client-to-client on the same subnet), the backhaul is bypassed, and the wireless side's higher capacity can be fully utilized.

metadata.title: Max bandwidth of wireless access point with gigabit backhaul

However, it omits a critical practical implication: the 1 Gbps Ethernet backhaul *will* become a hard bottleneck for aggregate client traffic destined for the WAN or other subnets. The explanation of AC1200 as an aggregate PHY rate across streams/bands needs clarification, and real-world throughput ratios (typically 50–60% of PHY rate) should be mentioned. The enriched version adds context on backhaul bottlenecks, local vs. routed traffic paths, and modern multi-gig/LACP solutions, making it more accurate and actionable for a RAG database.
