text:
Q: For a research project I need low timescale interface utilization measurement on a Cisco Nexus 5000 devices and some fabric extenders.
Background: The switch is used to "simulate" an optical network, and traffic is forced pass this switch. I can then simulate fiber cuts by simply shutting down the interface.
To measure results, I need to measure interface utilization in a rather timescale. Sub-second would be ideal, but is not necessary. Getting utilization in 1 second intervals is however the goal.
My first idea was to use IFMIB and query the Interface the Byte Counter. Doing this on a regular interval allows me to calculate the average utilization in the last timeframe. I however observed that counters do not get updated in realtime, but instead each 10 seconds or so, which definitely is not fine-grained enough. Further, if the switch even has little load, the SNMP responses may get in irregular time intervals, which distorts the results.
Another idea was to query the device via Netconf / XML, however the agent is too slow. Second interval polling is not possible here.
My final idea is to use ERSPAN and capture the different VLAN traffic and send it to a management station. There, the VLAN tag can be used to distinguish the Interface, and I need to write some utility which actually counts the packet sizes. In my opinion this may allow second level measurements, but represents some overhead on the other hand (additional machine, and I don't know if Nexus will forward ~7GB/s ERSPAN traffic).
Do you have any other idea how to measure bandwidth on an interface?

[Score:0] I have found no way to do this on the Nexus 5000s we use. On the Cisco 6500 and 7600 platform, I was able to use rmon history for this (which the Nexuses don't support), as follows:
I wrote a script that polls it every 60 seconds using something like `snmpbulkwalk -Oqn -m RMON-MIB -v 2c -c X $ip .1.3.6.1.2.1.16.2.2.1.2.2.1` (note: specify the correct MIB instead of an empty string) and puts that data in an RRD, then uses RRD::graph to generate an image of fine-grained usage. There is some intricate logic around getting the "next" 60 seconds of data (IIRC it stores 5 mins of data), which I will leave as an exercise for the reader.

For modern Cisco NX-OS (7.x/9.x+), consider enabling **Streaming Telemetry (gRPC/YANG)** for sub-second polling without the SNMP daemon overhead. Alternatively, **sFlow** or **NetFlow** exporters can be configured with high-frequency sampling intervals (e.g., 1-second or 100ms) to capture granular utilization without the ERSPAN overhead. Arista has another approach to this, which is called "LANZ" and is described at https://www.arista.com/assets/data/pdf/Whitepapers/Arista_LANZ_Overview_TechBulletin_0213.pdf

text:
To implement this on a Cisco router, you would use Class-Based Weighted Fair Queuing (CBWFQ) with Low Latency Queuing (LLQ). Below is a conceptual configuration framework for modern IOS (15.x/17.x):
```
! 1. Classify traffic
class-map match-any VOICE
 match dscp ef
 match protocol rtp audio
class-map match-any DATA
 match access-group name DATA-ACL

! 2. Define policy
policy-map LIMIT-5MB
 class VOICE
  priority 1000 ! Guarantees 1Mbps for voice (LLQ strict priority)
 class DATA
  bandwidth 4000 ! Guarantees 4Mbps for data
 class class-default
  bandwidth remaining percent 10

! 3. Apply to interface (assuming 5Mbps link)
interface GigabitEthernet0/0
 service-policy output LIMIT-5MB
 ip bandwidth-percent egress 50 ! Adjusts IOS internal calculations for sub-Gig links
```
Note: For strict 5Mbps enforcement, use `shape average 5000000` under the interface or policy-map. LLQ (`priority`) ensures voice gets strict priority queueing, while `bandwidth` commands in CBWFQ provide minimum guarantees. Upstream networks should honor DSCP/CoS markings via trust boundaries. In SDN environments (e.g., Ryu/Open vSwitch), this maps to OpenFlow 1.3+ meter tables for rate limiting and priority queues for scheduling.

text:
Q: In Sonicwall, we "split" T1 line into two lines: Internal LAN (so we can get on the internet) and Client line (so clients can work with server that we host).
We simply dedicated 25% of bandwidth to our Internal LAN, and rest is given to client line. (25/75 ration was found through trial and error) Problem is - 25% is very,very slow for our office needs. And at the same time, clients are not using all of the 75% all the time.
Is there way to make this speed dynamically adjustable, because clients are not using entire 75% of bandwidth all the time ?

[Score:2] You should be able to set the guaranteed bandwidth along with the maximum allowed bandwidth for each connection. So, for example, can set your guaranteed bandwidth to 25% with a max of 75% on the Internal policy, while setting the Client policy to guarantee 50% with a max of 75%.
This means that if the client policy isn't using all of the bandwidth allocated, the internal policy can utilize the available bandwidth.

**Technical Context & Implementation Notes:**
- **QoS Mechanism:** SonicWall's BWM acts as a traffic shaper. Setting a lower 'Guaranteed' than 'Maximum' enables dynamic bandwidth borrowing, which directly addresses your requirement.
- **Version Context:** This applies to SonicOS 6.5 and 7.x (Next-Gen Firewalls). Policy structure remains consistent, but UI navigation may vary. Ensure BWM is applied to the WAN interface and correct traffic direction (Outbound/Inbound).
- **Best Practice:** Instead of hard-splitting interfaces, apply BWM to specific services (e.g., HTTP/HTTPS, DNS) or subnets. Use the SonicWall Traffic Monitor or BWM Statistics tab to track actual utilization and adjust guarantees dynamically.
- **Reference:** SonicWall BWM Documentation (SonicOS 6.5/7.x): http://help.mysonicwall.com/sw/eng/7630/ui2/70/Policies_Firewall_BWM_Snwls.html

text:
In networking, these terms have distinct technical meanings despite common interchangeability:
- **Bandwidth**: The theoretical maximum data transfer rate of a physical or logical link (e.g., 10 Gbps Ethernet). In CS/Networking, it denotes capacity; in EE, it refers to signal frequency range.
- **Capacity**: The maximum data volume a system or path can handle under ideal conditions. Often used interchangeably with bandwidth, but can account for aggregate link capacity or protocol limits.
- **Rate (Data Rate/Line Rate)**: The actual transmission speed of bits over a medium. 'Line rate' specifically means a device can forward/switch packets at the full physical interface speed without dropping frames.
- **Throughput**: The actual volume of successfully delivered application data over time, reduced by latency, congestion, packet loss, and protocol overhead. Always ≤ Bandwidth.

Example: A 1 Gbps link with 5% overhead and queuing delays may yield ~900 Mbps throughput. In QoS, traffic policing/shaping enforces bandwidth limits, while SDN controllers (Ryu/OVS) monitor flow rates and throughput to optimize paths. Standardize definitions per IETF/IEEE for consistent automation and telemetry.

text:
Q: I have a topology below (two switches S1 and S2), each interface has two metrics (ifinutilization and ifoututilization).
%ifinutilization = ( (ifinoctets * 8) * 100 ) / ifspeed
%ifoututilization = ( (ifoutoctets * 8) * 100 ) / ifspeed
i want to summarize the link utilization, can someone help me ?

[Score:1] When you summarize periods of utilization, there are two basic options:
Calculate the average: sum all values and divide by their number. Beware - averages don't properly represent uneven load and may hide bursts with congestion behind comparatively low average utilization. However, averaging gives a good estimation of overall data volume. Note that with a low frequency of ifin/outoctets sampling (less than perhaps one sample per minute), you're already averaging.
Find the maximum: find the highest value and drop all others. In contrast, this shows peak loads very well but allows no estimation of actual data volume or overall actual load. In order to see the peaks you need a high sampling frequency (reasonably more than one sample per minute).

text:
The formula R = N/(B*D) with units [b/Hz/s] actually describes **spectral efficiency**, not data rate or throughput. To clarify for networking contexts:
- **Data Rate/Throughput**: R = N / D [bps]. To increase it, increase N (payload size/MTU) or decrease D (transmission time/latency).
- **Bandwidth (B)**: Physical layer capacity follows Shannon-Hartley: C = B * log2(1+SNR). Increasing B linearly raises theoretical channel capacity.
- **SDN/QoS Context**: In switch/router environments, effective throughput is bounded by link speed, buffer depth, OpenFlow pipeline processing, and QoS policing/shaping. Adjusting packet size (N) or queuing discipline (D) impacts network-layer throughput, while bandwidth upgrades (B) require physical/link-layer changes or SDN-driven traffic engineering.
*Recommendation*: Separate physical layer capacity planning from network layer traffic engineering before ingesting into a technical RAG database.

How is the Utilization (% of port capacity)
calculated for a 1000Mbps port?
The solution does NOT appear to be (octets*8)/1E9 for a 1000Mbps port. For example, at t = 5 sec, (1185742 octets * 8)/1E9 * 100% = .95% rather than 1.11 %.
I assume there is something to the math used by Nokia/ALU I am not seeing.

[Score:1] The octet statistics count L2, not L1, so do not include preamble, start of frame delimiter and inter frame gaps, totaling 20B per packet on the wire. Port utilization of course looks at L1. If you add 20B per packet you'll see the calculations are correct. So basically use 8*(octets+packets*20)/capacity
instead of 8*octets/capacity
.

Minor context regarding FCS inclusion and SR OS counter granularity would enhance precision, but the core content is solid and ready for documentation.

metadata.context_summary: The device's utilization calculation accounts for L1 wire overhead (preamble, SFD, and IFG) that is not included in standard L2 octet counters.

text:
Q: my question might be a little too "newbie" style, but..
How can MCS index rates be set/restricted? For example, is it possible to set the MCS index to 5 at 20 Mhz bandwidth with 64-QAM Modulation and 2/3 Coding Rate, or is the maximum mcs index automatically "selected" by the router when a specific bandwidth is set?

[Score:1] It depends on the configuration possibilities of the Access Point.
A residential access point is not likely to have this possibility, but if you have direct access to a chipset, through shell for example, it might be possible if there is a command allowing it.
When there is no possibility of doing it, the selection is up to the implementation.
It is worth noting though, that most of the time the APIs provided by chipsetters are more complete & up-to-date that command-line utilities. That means that some functionalities are not available to modification though these utilities.

**Technical Context & Examples:**
- **MCS Table Validity:** MCS indices are standardized per 802.11 generation (n, ac, ax, be). MCS 5 at 20MHz with 64-QAM and 2/3 coding is valid in 802.11n/ac, but actual PHY rate depends on guard interval (400ns vs 800ns) and spatial streams (1x1 vs 2x2).
- **Enterprise Rate Limiting:** Most enterprise APs do not lock to a single MCS index. Instead, administrators set `minimum` and `maximum` data rates or MCS caps. Example (Cisco CLI): `dot11 ssid <name> maximum-data-rate mcs 0-5` or using QoS rate-limit policies.
- **Linux/mac80211 Tooling:** On open-source/Linux-based chipsets, use `iw` to enforce rate floors/ceilings: `iw dev <iface> set bitrates min <N> max <N>`. Direct MCS index manipulation typically requires vendor-specific `nl80211` hooks or custom driver support.
- **Dynamic Rate Control:** Modern APs run AMRC/ARF (Automatic Modulation & Rate Control) which adjusts MCS in real-time based on SNR, packet loss, and latency. Hard-locking MCS is generally discouraged unless for RF testing, spectrum analysis, or mitigating specific interference sources.

It does not distinguish between consumer and enterprise capabilities, nor does it reference standard tools (iw, Cisco CLI, mac80211) or dynamic rate control behavior.

text: The provided `tc` commands and HTB configuration are technically accurate for Linux traffic control. The `default 10` parameter in the root qdisc correctly maps to class `1:10`, which handles unmatched traffic. Note that applying HTB directly on a Linux bridge (`br-lan`) can introduce performance overhead and may not support all filter features depending on the kernel version. For production environments, it is generally recommended to apply QoS qdiscs on physical interfaces (e.g., `eth0`, `wlan0`) or use VLAN tagging to direct traffic before bridging. Additionally, modern Linux kernels often prefer `cake` or `fq_codel` qdiscs for automated bandwidth management, which reduce the need for manual class hierarchy configuration. The duplicate `u32` filter for `192.168.1.104` dst is harmless but redundant. Code blocks remain unmodified as they are syntactically valid and functionally correct for the stated goal.

text:
Q: I'm not good at English But I really confused about this.
I used the word computer but it can be mobiles or any of devices which can have networking.
Let just the Internet bandwidth is 100Mbps, and It is linked to a router.
There are many switches and hubs which connected to the router.
Eventually, 200 of computers connected to this network by the switches and hubs
So, Is bandwidth of the computer in the network (100/200)Mbps? Which means a maximum of the Internet speed is 0.5Mbps when all of the computers uses the Internet?

[Score:1] This subject is very large, but I will try to cover some key points.
The bandwidth of the link to the ISP is 100 Mbps. That means that your router can send 100 Mb to the ISP's router each second, and the ISP's router can send the same to your router.
You must consider the traffic direction.
For outbound traffic, the bandwidth for each computer is whatever the link speed from the computer to the next device is. For example, your computers could have 1000 Mbps links to the access switches.
The access switches to which the computers connect will have links to some type of aggregation, and those links will have a bandwidth associated with them. The aggregate access bandwidth compared to the uplink bandwidth will give you an oversubscription ratio. For example, if you have a switch with twenty-four 1 Gbps access ports, and a 1 Gbps uplink, you have a 24:1 oversubscription ratio.
You can also calculate an oversubscription ratio to the router, and to the ISP's router.
It is not as simple as dividing the bandwidth of the oversubscribed link between all the computers. For the example of the 24-port switch above, one computer could send 1 Gbps to the aggregation switch if it is the only one sending. If all 24 computers are trying to send 1 Gbps at the same time, a lot of traffic will be dropped. The frames going to the aggregation switch will be sent on a first-come, first-served basis. Switches have very, very small buffers, so the vast majority of the traffic will be dropped.
How that affects things depends on what is being sent. For example, UDP will be lost, but TCP will request lost segments to be resent.
For inbound traffic, it is unlikely that any links are oversubscribed, and you usually have much more inbound than outbound traffic. You really don't have much control over the traffic from the Internet to your network. If a single request from a computer on your network starts another computer on the Internet sending 1 Gbps of traffic to your network, it can basically monopolize your inbound ISP connection. Someone wanting to disrupt you network can try to overload your incoming network connection in a DoS (Denial of Service) attack.
There is QoS to try to introduce your vision of fairness to your network. For example, you can prioritize real-time traffic, e.g. VoIP. You can't really do a lot about the traffic coming into your network from the Internet because you don't control the Internet.

metadata.title: Is bandwidth a sum of network speed each computer in sub network?

The content is clear, covers key network-core fundamentals, and aligns well with general networking documentation standards.

text:
Q: Bitrates supported by switches, routers and network cards are often 10 MBit/s, 100 MBit/s or 1GBit/s. Newer developments promise up to 10 GBit/s. I do not see a reason why powers of 10 should have a certain meaning here, unlike powers of 2 in memory storage increase.
Why do bitrates increase in that pattern?

[Score:1] You are incorrect; bitrates don't always increase by 10 times.
Memory storage increases by powers of two because it is addressed with binary (base 2) addresses.
Increasing ethernet speeds by a power of 10 has been a convenient goal, but there is now 40 Gb ethernet and 100 Gb ethernet. Also, Wi-Fi speeds have not been anything like a linear 10 times increase: 802.11
was 2 Mb, 802.11a
was 54 Mb, 802.11b
was 11 Mb, 802.11g
was 54 Mb, 802.11n
was 150 Mb per stream, etc.

metadata.title: Why do bitrates increase in powers of 10?
metadata.llm_evaluation_reason: Generally correct and addresses the misconception effectively, but lacks historical context for the decimal standardization and uses slightly outdated Wi-Fi/Ethernet examples.
metadata.problem_summary: Why do network bitrates increase in powers of 10 (10M, 100M, 1G, 10G) instead of powers of 2 like computer memory storage?
metadata.context_summary: Explains that powers of 10 are a convenient historical goal for Ethernet, while memory uses binary addressing. Notes that modern speeds deviate from strict powers of 10 (e.g., 40G, 100G, Wi-Fi standards).

text:
Q: Bitrates supported by switches, routers and network cards are often 10 MBit/s, 100 MBit/s or 1GBit/s. Newer developments promise up to 10 GBit/s. I do not see a reason why powers of 10 should have a certain meaning here, unlike powers of 2 in memory storage increase. Why do bitrates increase in that pattern?

A: While it appears that network speeds follow powers of 10, this is primarily a result of IEEE 802.3 standardization and decimal marketing conventions rather than a strict technical mandate. Historically, early Ethernet (10BASE5/2) and Fast Ethernet (100BASE-TX) adopted 10 Mbps and 100 Mbps to align with existing telecommunications and signaling infrastructure. Gigabit Ethernet (1000BASE-T) continued this decimal progression for simplicity in standardization and interoperability.

Modern Ethernet standards actually use a mix of decimal and lane-based scaling. For example:
- 2.5GBASE-T and 5GBASE-T (IEEE 802.3bz) were introduced to bridge gaps between 1G and 10G over Cat5e/Cat6 cabling.
- 25GBASE-R, 40GBASE-FR4, 100GBASE-SR4, and 400GBASE-FR8 use lane multiplication (e.g., 4x25G, 8x50G) to scale bandwidth efficiently without redesigning physical layer (PHY) chips from scratch.
- The shift from 10G to 100G and 400G also reflects advances in line coding (8b/10b → 64b/66b) and DSP-based equalization, which reduce overhead and allow higher symbol rates.

Unlike storage (which uses binary prefixes like KiB/MiB due to addressing schemes), networking uses decimal prefixes (kbit, Mbit, Gbit) for straightforward rate calculations and interoperability. Standards bodies like IEEE and ITU-T prioritize backward compatibility and cabling constraints, which is why speeds don't strictly follow binary powers.

metadata.title: Why do bitrates increase in powers of 10?

It correctly notes that speeds include 2.5G, 25G, 40G, and 100G, but fails to explain IEEE 802.3 standardization, physical layer signaling, line coding overhead, or lane multiplication. It reads more like a casual forum reply than technical documentation. Enriched with standard references, signaling context, and modern scaling mechanisms to improve accuracy and utility for a RAG database.

metadata.context_summary: Explains that network speeds actually follow more varied increments (e.g., 1G, 2.5G, 10G, 25G, 40G, 100G, 400G) as seen in SFP module standards, rather than strictly powers of 10.

text:
**Corrected Explanation:**
Bandwidth and bitrate are related but distinct concepts. Bandwidth (B) is the range of frequencies a signal occupies (e.g., 4 MHz). Bitrate is the number of bits transmitted per second. The relationship is governed by information theory, not just frequency ranges.

**Key Principles:**
1. **Nyquist Rate:** Maximum symbol rate = 2B. Doubling bandwidth doubles the maximum possible symbols per second, not necessarily the bitrate.
2. **Shannon-Hartley Theorem:** Channel Capacity C = B × log₂(1 + S/N). Bandwidth sets the theoretical upper limit for data rate, but actual bitrate depends on modulation efficiency and SNR.
3. **Modulation Impact:** Bitrate = Symbol Rate × Bits per Symbol. Using higher-order modulation (e.g., 16-QAM vs QPSK) increases bitrate without changing bandwidth.

**Addressing the Scenario:**
- If you double bandwidth from 4 MHz to 8 MHz, the *maximum symbol rate* doubles. However, if you keep the same modulation and SNR, the bitrate will only double if the system is already Nyquist-limited. Shifting the frequency range alone does not increase bitrate.
- The claim that "bitrate is determined by the lowest frequency" is incorrect. The lowest frequency affects phase/delay characteristics, but data rate is determined by how fast symbols can be distinguished within the allocated bandwidth.

**Takeaway:** Bandwidth is a capacity constraint. To increase bitrate, you must either increase bandwidth, improve SNR, or use more efficient modulation/coding schemes.

metadata.title: what is bandwidth and its relation with bitrate?
metadata.llm_evaluation_reason: The text fundamentally conflates analog bandwidth, digital bitrate, and modulation concepts. The provided answer is overly simplistic and technically imprecise, missing core networking/communications principles like the Shannon-Hartley theorem and Nyquist rate. It incorrectly implies bitrate is driven by the lowest frequency component rather than symbol rate, modulation order, and SNR. Requires enrichment to establish accurate theoretical foundations for network engineering and RAG retrieval.
metadata.problem_summary: Clarifying the theoretical relationship between bandwidth, fundamental frequency, and data rate in digital signal transmission, specifically addressing a beginner's misconception about how bandwidth expansion affects the frequency spectrum and data rate.

text: This is a table of contents for Ryu 4.34. For modern SDN development, upgrade to Ryu 4.44+ for improved OpenFlow 1.3/1.4 support and Python 3 compatibility. The 'OpenFlow protocol API Reference' covers standard match/action fields, while 'Nicira Extension Structures' are legacy and largely superseded by standard OVSDB/NETCONF or OpenFlow 1.5 extensions. When writing applications, focus on `ryu.controller` and `ryu.ofproto` modules. Always pair Ryu controllers with Mininet 2.x for emulation testing. The 'Setup TLS Connection' section is critical for production SDN deployments to secure OpenFlow southbound traffic. For QoS and traffic engineering, leverage Ryu's `ryu.app.ofctl` and `ryu.app.ofctl_rest` modules to dynamically program flow tables on OVS switches. The 'Topology Viewer' and 'REST API' sections are essential for network automation and telemetry integration.

text:
Setup TLS ConnectionÂ¶
If you want to use secure channel to connect OpenFlow switches, you need to use TLS connection. This document describes how to setup Ryu to connect to the Open vSwitch over TLS.
Configuring a Public Key InfrastructureÂ¶
If you don't have a PKI, the ovs-pki script included with Open vSwitch can help you. This section is based on the INSTALL.SSL in the Open vSwitch source code.
NOTE: How to install Open vSwitch isn't described in this document. Please refer to the Open vSwitch documents.
Create a PKI by using ovs-pki script:
% ovs-pki init
(Default directory is /usr/local/var/lib/openvswitch/pki)
The pki directory consists of controllerca and switchca subdirectories. Each directory contains CA files.
Create a controller private key and certificate:
% ovs-pki req+sign ctl controller
ctl-privkey.pem and ctl-cert.pem are generated in the current directory.
Create a switch private key and certificate:
% ovs-pki req+sign sc switch
sc-privkey.pem and sc-cert.pem are generated in the current directory.
Testing TLS ConnectionÂ¶
Configuring ovs-vswitchd to use CA files using the ovs-vsctl "set-ssl" command, e.g.:
% ovs-vsctl set-ssl /etc/openvswitch/sc-privkey.pem \
/etc/openvswitch/sc-cert.pem \
/usr/local/var/lib/openvswitch/pki/controllerca/cacert.pem
% ovs-vsctl add-br br0
% ovs-vsctl set-controller br0 ssl:127.0.0.1:6633
Substitute the correct file names, if they differ from the ones used above. You should use absolute file names.
Run Ryu with CA files:
% ryu-manager --ctl-privkey ctl-privkey.pem \
--ctl-cert ctl-cert.pem \
--ca-certs /usr/local/var/lib/openvswitch/pki/switchca/cacert.pem \
--verbose
You can see something like:
loading app ryu.controller.ofp_handler
instantiating app ryu.controller.ofp_handler
BRICK ofp_event
CONSUMES EventOFPSwitchFeatures
CONSUMES EventOFPErrorMsg
CONSUMES EventOFPHello
CONSUMES EventOFPEchoRequest
connected socket:<SSLSocket fileno=4 sock=127.0.0.1:6633 peer=127.0.0.1:61302> a
ddress:('127.0.0.1', 61302)
hello ev <ryu.controller.ofp_event.EventOFPHello object at 0x1047806d0>
move onto config mode
switch features ev version: 0x1 msg_type 0x6 xid 0xb0bb34e5 port OFPPhyPort(port
_no=65534, hw_addr='\x16\xdc\xa2\xe2}K', name='br0\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x
00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00', config=0, state=0, curr=0, advertised=0, supported=0, p
eer=0)
move onto main mode

text:
Ryu API Reference (Ryu 4.34+) — Core Controller Architecture

This documentation outlines the foundational classes for building SDN controllers in Ryu. `RyuApp` is the base class for all controller applications, utilizing an event-driven model. Key attributes include `OFP_VERSIONS` (list of supported OpenFlow versions, defaults to [1.3, 1.4, 1.5] in modern releases), `_CONTEXTS` (for sharing state between apps), and `_EVENTS` (for inter-app communication).

`DPSet` manages connected datapaths (switches). Methods like `get(dpid)`, `get_all()`, and `get_ports(dpid)` are essential for topology discovery and flow rule installation. When building applications, always use OpenFlow 1.3+ for modern OVS compatibility, as it supports advanced features like packet metadata, group tables, and better flow match fields compared to 1.0/1.2. The provided code examples are syntactically valid but should be updated to reference `ofproto_v1_3.OFP_VERSION` for current best practices.

It serves as a technical guide for developers building and integrating SDN controllers and network automation applications.

text:
Ryu application APIÂ¶
Ryu application programming modelÂ¶
Threads, events, and event queuesÂ¶
Ryu applications are single-threaded entities which implement various functionalities in Ryu. Events are messages between them.
Ryu applications send asynchronous events to each other. Besides that, there are some Ryu-internal event sources which are not Ryu applications. One of examples of such event sources is OpenFlow controller. While an event can currently contain arbitrary python objects, it's discouraged to pass complex objects (eg. unpickleable objects) between Ryu applications.
Each Ryu application has a receive queue for events. The queue is FIFO and preserves the order of events. Each Ryu application has a thread for event processing. The thread keeps draining the receive queue by dequeueing an event and calling the appropritate event handler for the event type. Because the event handler is called in the context of the event processing thread, it should be careful when blocking. While an event handler is blocked, no further events for the Ryu application will be processed.
There are kinds of events which are used to implement synchronous inter-application calls between Ryu applications. While such requests uses the same machinary as ordinary events, their replies are put on a queue dedicated to the transaction to avoid deadlock.
While threads and queues is currently implemented with eventlet/greenlet, a direct use of them in a Ryu application is strongly discouraged.
Create a Ryu applicationÂ¶
A Ryu application is a python module which defines a subclass of ryu.base.app_manager.RyuApp. If two or more such classes are defined in a module, the first one (by name order) will be picked by app_manager. Ryu application is singleton: only single instance of a given Ryu application is supported.
Observe eventsÂ¶
A Ryu application can register itself to listen for specific events using ryu.controller.handler.set_ev_cls decorator.
Generate eventsÂ¶
A Ryu application can raise events by calling appropriate ryu.base.app_manager.RyuApp's methods like send_event or send_event_to_observers.
Event classesÂ¶
An event class describes a Ryu event generated in the system. By convention, event class names are prefixed by "Event". Events are generated either by the core part of Ryu or Ryu applications. A Ryu application can register its interest for a specific type of event by providing a handler method using ryu.controller.handler.set_ev_cls decorator.
OpenFlow event classesÂ¶
ryu.controller.ofp_event module exports event classes which describe receptions of OpenFlow messages from connected switches. By convention, they are named as ryu.controller.ofp_event.EventOFPxxxx where xxxx is the name of the corresponding OpenFlow message. For example, EventOFPPacketIn for packet-in message. The OpenFlow controller part of Ryu automatically decodes OpenFlow messages received from switches and send these events to Ryu applications which expressed an interest using ryu.controller.handler.set_ev_cls. OpenFlow event classes are subclasses of the following class.
-
class
ryu.controller.ofp_event.
EventOFPMsgBase
(msg)Â¶ The base class of OpenFlow event class.
OpenFlow event classes have at least the following attributes.
Attribute Description msg An object which describes the corresponding OpenFlow message. msg.datapath A ryu.controller.controller.Datapath instance which describes an OpenFlow switch from which we received this OpenFlow message. timestamp Timestamp when Datapath instance generated this event. The msg object has some more additional members whose values are extracted from the original OpenFlow message.
See OpenFlow protocol API Reference for more info about OpenFlow messages.
ryu.controller.handler.set_ev_clsÂ¶
-
ryu.controller.handler.
set_ev_cls
(ev_cls, dispatchers=None)Â¶ A decorator for Ryu application to declare an event handler.
Decorated method will become an event handler. ev_cls is an event class whose instances this RyuApp wants to receive. dispatchers argument specifies one of the following negotiation phases (or a list of them) for which events should be generated for this handler. Note that, in case an event changes the phase, the phase before the change is used to check the interest.
Negotiation phase Description ryu.controller.handler.HANDSHAKE_DISPATCHER Sending and waiting for hello message ryu.controller.handler.CONFIG_DISPATCHER Version negotiated and sent features-request message ryu.controller.handler.MAIN_DISPATCHER Switch-features message received and sent set-config message ryu.controller.handler.DEAD_DISPATCHER Disconnect from the peer. Or disconnecting due to some unrecoverable errors.
ryu.controller.controller.DatapathÂ¶
-
class
ryu.controller.controller.
Datapath
(socket, address)Â¶ A class to describe an OpenFlow switch connected to this controller.
An instance has the following attributes.
Attribute Description id 64-bit OpenFlow Datapath ID. Only available for ryu.controller.handler.MAIN_DISPATCHER phase. ofproto A module which exports OpenFlow definitions, mainly constants appeared in the specification, for the negotiated OpenFlow version. For example, ryu.ofproto.ofproto_v1_0 for OpenFlow 1.0. ofproto_parser A module which exports OpenFlow wire message encoder and decoder for the negotiated OpenFlow version. For example, ryu.ofproto.ofproto_v1_0_parser for OpenFlow 1.0. ofproto_parser.OFPxxxx(datapath,...) A callable to prepare an OpenFlow message for the given switch. It can be sent with Datapath.send_msg later. xxxx is a name of the message. For example OFPFlowMod for flow-mod message. Arguemnts depend on the message. set_xid(self, msg) Generate an OpenFlow XID and put it in msg.xid. send_msg(self, msg) Queue an OpenFlow message to send to the corresponding switch. If msg.xid is None, set_xid is automatically called on the message before queueing. send_packet_out deprecated send_flow_mod deprecated send_flow_del deprecated send_delete_all_flows deprecated send_barrier Queue an OpenFlow barrier message to send to the switch. send_nxt_set_flow_format deprecated is_reserved_port deprecated

It correctly identifies deprecated `Datapath` methods, which aligns with modern Ryu best practices. Minor formatting artifacts from scraping do not impact technical validity.

It provides technical specifications and usage guidelines for building and integrating Ryu-based network automation applications.

text:
This document outlines the setup for LINC-Switch (an Erlang-based OpenFlow switch) to test Ryu's OF-Config integration. While the procedure is historically accurate, several components require modernization:
- **Erlang Version**: R15B+ is from ~2012. Modern LINC-Switch requires Erlang/OTP 20+ (preferably 26.x).
- **Build Dependencies**: `uml-utilities` and `tunctl` are deprecated; use the modern `ip` suite (already used in the veth setup). `libpcap0.8` should be `libpcap-dev`.
- **LINC-Switch Status**: The FlowForwarding LINC-Switch repository is largely unmaintained. For modern SDN testing, consider Open vSwitch (OVS) with Mininet and Ryu, which supports OpenFlow 1.3+ and is actively maintained.
- **OF-Config vs OpenFlow**: OF-Config uses NETCONF/YANG for switch configuration, while OpenFlow handles forwarding. The `sys.config` example shows queue mapping (OF-Config queue IDs 991-994) but does not explain how these map to OpenFlow switch ports or QoS policies.
- **Ryu Version**: Ryu 4.34 is from 2020/2021. Current versions are 4.44+. The test module `ryu.tests.integrated.test_of_config` may require adjustments for newer Ryu releases.
- **Modern Alternative**: For QoS/OpenFlow testing, use `mininet --topo single,3 --switch ovsk --controller remote,ip=127.0.0.1,port=6653` with Ryu's `ryu.app.ofctl_rest` and `ryu.app.qos` modules, which are better documented and supported.

metadata.title: Testing OF-config support with LINC — Ryu 4.34 documentation
metadata.llm_evaluation_reason: Technically accurate for its historical context but significantly outdated. Erlang R15B+ is from ~2012 (current is 26.x+). Several build dependencies (uml-utilities, tunctl, libpcap0.8) are legacy. LINC-Switch is largely unmaintained, and OF-Config is a legacy protocol largely superseded by OpenConfig/YANG. The Ryu command and sys.config structure are correct but lack context on OpenFlow version mapping and modern SDN testing practices.

[{linc,
[{of_config,enabled},
{capable_switch_ports,
[{port,1,[{interface,"linc-port"}]},
{port,2,[{interface,"linc-port2"}]},
{port,3,[{interface,"linc-port3"}]},
{port,4,[{interface,"linc-port4"}]}]},
{capable_switch_queues,
[
{queue,991,[{min_rate,10},{max_rate,120}]},
{queue,992,[{min_rate,10},{max_rate,130}]},
{queue,993,[{min_rate,200},{max_rate,300}]},
{queue,994,[{min_rate,400},{max_rate,900}]}
]},
{logical_switches,
[{switch,0,
[{backend,linc_us4},
{controllers,[{"Switch0-Default-Controller","127.0.0.1",6633,tcp}]},
{controllers_listener,{"127.0.0.1",9998,tcp}},
{queues_status,enabled},
{ports,[{port,1,{queues,[]}},{port,2,{queues,[991,992]}}]}]}
,
{switch,7,
[{backend,linc_us3},
{controllers,[{"Switch7-Controller","127.0.0.1",6633,tcp}]},
{controllers_listener,disabled},
{queues_status,enabled},
{ports,[{port,4,{queues,[]}},{port,3,{queues,[993,994]}}]}]}
]}]}]},
{enetconf,
[{capabilities,
[{base,{1,0}},
{base,{1,1}},
{startup,{1,0}},
{'writable-running',{1,0}}]},
{callback_module,linc_ofconfig},
{sshd_ip,{127,0,0,1}},
{sshd_port,1830},
{sshd_user_passwords,[{"linc","linc"}]}]},
{lager,
[{handlers,
[{lager_console_backend,debug},
{lager_file_backend,
[{"log/error.log",error,10485760,"$D0",5},
{"log/console.log",info,10485760,"$D0",5}]}]}]},
{sasl,
[{sasl_error_logger,{file,"log/sasl-error.log"}},
{errlog_type,error},
{error_logger_mf_dir,"log/sasl"},
{error_logger_mf_maxbytes,10485760},
{error_logger_mf_maxfiles,5}]},
{sync,[{excluded_modules,[procket]}]}].

text: This table lists core Mininet Python API classes. For production/lab use, instantiate components explicitly: `net = Mininet(topo=my_topo, controller=RemoteController(ip='127.0.0.1', port=6653), switch=OVSSwitch, link=TCLink)`. TCLink/TCIntf leverage Linux `tc` for bandwidth, delay, and loss simulation. OVSLink/OVSIntf are optimized for patch ports between OVS bridges. The NOX class is legacy and deprecated in v2.2+; prefer Ryu, POX, or OVSController. Define custom topologies by subclassing `mininet.topo.Topo` and overriding `build()`. Use `cli(net)` for interactive testing and `net.start()`/`net.stop()` for lifecycle management. Mininet 2.x defaults to OpenFlow 1.3/1.4/1.5 via OVS; explicitly set `switch=OVSSwitch` or `switch=UserSwitch` to control the data plane.

metadata.title: Mininet Python API Reference Manual: Class List
metadata.llm_evaluation_reason: Generally correct API reference but lacks modern context, version specifics, and practical instantiation examples. Mentions deprecated NOX controller. Fits score 7.
metadata.context_summary: This reference manual documents the Python API classes within Mininet, providing a comprehensive guide for developers building software-defined network simulations. It covers core networking components and utilities, including OpenFlow, Open vSwitch, network namespaces, traffic control, and veth pair management.

text:
The `mininet.link.Intf` class represents a network interface within a Mininet node. While it provides low-level configuration capabilities (IP, MAC, state, renaming), **in modern Mininet (2.x), direct instantiation is rarely needed**. Interface configuration is typically abstracted through `Node.config()`, `Host.setIP()`, or `Mininet.addLink()`.

**Key Methods & Usage Context:**
- `config(mac, ip, ifconfig, up)`: Core method for setting interface parameters. Usually called by parent `Node` objects.
- `setIP(ipstr, prefixLen)`: Directly assigns an IP address (e.g., `h1.intf().setIP('10.0.0.1/24')`).
- `setMAC(macstr)`: Assigns a MAC address (e.g., `h1.intf().setMAC('00:00:00:00:00:01')`).
- `isUp(setUp=False)`: Checks or toggles interface state.
- `cmd(*args)`: Executes shell commands on the host node (e.g., `h1.intf().cmd('ethtool -K eth0 tso off')`).
- `updateIP/updateMAC`: Refreshes cached address properties from the actual OS interface.

**Modern Practice Note:**
In Mininet 2.3.0+, prefer using `node.config()` or the `addLink()` parameters rather than manipulating `Intf` directly. This ensures proper Open vSwitch (OVS) integration, OpenFlow flow rule alignment, and link state synchronization. Direct `Intf` manipulation is mostly used for advanced debugging or custom network topologies.

**Example (Modern Approach):**
```python
# Preferred: Pass interface config during link creation
net.addLink(h1, s1, cls=Intf, params={'ip': '10.0.0.1/24', 'mac': '00:00:00:00:00:01'})

# Direct Intf usage (legacy/debugging)
h1.intf().setIP('10.0.0.2/24')
h1.intf().setMAC('00:00:00:00:00:02')
```

text: The `mininet.link.Link` class is Mininet's base abstraction for node-to-node connectivity, defaulting to Linux virtual ethernet (veth) pairs. In modern SDN workflows (Mininet 2.x), raw `Link` is rarely used directly; instead, subclasses like `TCLink` (for QoS/emulation) or `OVSLink` (for OpenFlow/OVS integration) are preferred. Key parameters include `node1`/`node2` (topology nodes), `port1`/`port2` (interface indices), and `params1`/`params2` (traffic control dicts for bandwidth/latency). For OpenFlow 1.3+ networks, interfaces are typically attached to an OVS bridge via `OVSLink`, which handles OFP flow table population. Example usage for QoS-aware links: `net.addLink(h1, s1, cls=TCLink, bw=10, delay='5ms', max_queue_size=1000)`.

text:
The provided text is a raw API reference dump for `mininet.link.OVSIntf`, which represents a network interface attached to an Open vSwitch (OVS) in Mininet. While the method signatures are technically accurate for legacy Mininet versions, modern Mininet (2.3+) abstracts direct interface instantiation. Interfaces are typically created via `net.addLink(node1, node2, cls=OVSLink)` rather than manual construction.

**Key Context & Modern Usage:**
- `OVSIntf` manages the Linux network namespace interface connected to an OVS bridge port.
- Methods like `setIP()`, `setMAC()`, and `isUp()` interact with `ip link` and `ifconfig` under the hood.
- In SDN workflows, IP/MAC configuration is often handled by the controller or DHCP; manual API calls are used for testing or static setups.
- OpenFlow 1.3+ is the default protocol version in modern OVS/Mininet setups, enabling advanced QoS, meter, and flow table features.

**Example Usage in Mininet Topology:**
```python
from mininet.net import Mininet
from mininet.node import OVSSwitch, Host
from mininet.link import OVSLink

net = Mininet(switch=OVSSwitch, link=OVSLink)
h1 = net.addHost('h1')
h2 = net.addHost('h2')
s1 = net.addSwitch('s1')

# Add link; OVSIntf is automatically instantiated per port
net.addLink(h1, s1)
net.addLink(h2, s1)

# Manually configure an interface (if needed)
intf = h1.intfs.values()[0]  # Access interface object
intf.setIP('10.0.0.1/24')
intf.setMAC('00:00:00:00:00:01')
intf.config(up=True)
net.start()
```
*Note:* Direct use of `mininet.link.OVSIntf` is deprecated in favor of `OVSLink` and automatic interface management in Mininet 2.3+. Always verify OpenFlow version compatibility with your OVS installation (`ovs-vsctl get bridge s1 protocols=OpenFlow13`).

text:
OVSLink is a specialized Mininet `Link` class used to create OVS patch ports between virtual switches. Unlike standard Ethernet links, patch links use internal OVS bridge ports, enabling switch-to-switch connectivity without consuming physical network namespaces.

### Key Methods
- `__init__(self, node1, node2, **kwargs)`: Initializes the link. Inherits standard `Link` parameters (e.g., `bw`, `delay`, `loss`, `maxQueueSize`).
- `makeIntfPair(cls, intfname1, intfname2, addr1=None, addr2=None, node1=None, node2=None, deleteIntfs=True)`: Creates the underlying veth pair and attaches it to the OVS switches. In modern Mininet, this is typically delegated to `OVSSwitch.makeIntfPair()`.
- `intfName(self, node, n)`: Generates canonical interface names (e.g., `s1-eth1`).
- `delete(self) / stop(self) / status(self) / __str__(self)`: Standard lifecycle, cleanup, and status methods inherited from the `Link` base class.

### Important Considerations
- **Patch Link Limit**: Historically, Linux kernel and OVS had limits around ~64 patch links per chain due to interface naming constraints and netns overhead. Modern systems (Kernel 4.x+, OVS 2.5+) support hundreds, but excessive patch chaining still increases CPU overhead and packet processing latency. For large topologies, prefer mesh topologies or direct switch ports over long patch chains.
- **Usage Example**:
  ```python
  from mininet.net import Mininet
  from mininet.node import OVSSwitch
  from mininet.link import OVSLink

  net = Mininet(switch=OVSSwitch, link=OVSLink)
  s1 = net.addSwitch('s1')
  s2 = net.addSwitch('s2')
  # OVSLink automatically creates a patch port between s1 and s2
  net.addLink(s1, s2, cls=OVSLink)
  net.start()
  ```
- **OpenFlow Versioning**: `OVSLink` does not dictate OpenFlow versions. Version negotiation is handled by the SDN controller (e.g., Ryu, ONOS, ODL) and OVS `set-controller` directives. Ensure your controller explicitly requests `OF_13` or `OF_15` if advanced flow tables or metadata fields are required.
- **Performance Note**: Patch links are lightweight but still pass through the OVS userspace/kernel datapath. For high-throughput testing, consider using `OVSIntLink` or physical interface emulation.

text:
The provided text is a raw API reference for `mininet.link.TCIntf`, accurate for Mininet 2.x. This class wraps the Linux `tc` utility to apply traffic control policies to virtual interfaces. It is essential for simulating real-world network conditions (QoS, latency, packet loss) in SDN testbeds and validating controller behavior under constrained links.

Key Parameters & Context:
- `bw`, `delay`, `loss`: Standard link impairment metrics. Use `bw='10Mbps'`, `delay='5ms'`, `loss=1.5`.
- `use_tbf`/`use_hfsc`: Selects the shaper algorithm. TBF is simple token-bucket; HFSC supports hierarchical fair queuing.
- `enable_ecn`/`enable_red`: Enables Active Queue Management (AQM) for congestion control.
- `gro`/`txo`/`rxo`: Offload flags. In Mininet, these are often disabled (`gro=False`) to ensure accurate packet-level counting and testing, as hardware offloading interferes with tc queue discipline measurement.

Example Usage:
```python
from mininet.net import Mininet
from mininet.link import TCLink

net = Mininet(link=TCLink)
h1, h2, s1 = net.addHost('h1'), net.addHost('h2'), net.addSwitch('s1')
net.addLink(h1, s1, cls=TCLink, bw=10, delay='5ms', loss=1)
net.addLink(h2, s1, cls=TCLink, bw=5, delay='10ms')
net.start()
net.pingAll()
net.stop()
```

Note: `TCIntf` operates at the Linux kernel networking layer, independent of OpenFlow versions (1.0-1.5+). It is commonly paired with OVS (`OVSKernelSwitch`) to enforce QoS before/after OpenFlow processing. Ensure Mininet is installed via `sudo mn -c` to clean old configs before testing.

text: TCLink is a Mininet link class that leverages Linux Traffic Control (tc) to enforce Quality of Service (QoS) policies such as bandwidth limiting, latency, packet loss, and queue management. While the base API signature shown reflects older Mininet 2.1/2.2 versions, modern Mininet (2.3+) typically initializes it with explicit QoS parameters: `TCLink(node1, node2, bw=10, delay='5ms', loss=0.1, max_queue_size=1000)`. Alternatively, parameters can be passed via a dictionary: `params={'bw': 10, 'delay': '2ms'}`. Under the hood, TCLink creates virtual Ethernet (veth) pairs and applies tc qdiscs (typically `htb` or `tbf`) to the OVS-managed interfaces. This is essential for SDN testing where traffic shaping and delay injection are required to validate controller logic (e.g., Ryu, OpenDaylight) or verify OpenFlow flow rule behavior under constrained network conditions. Ensure your Mininet installation is updated to access modern TCLink parameter handling and improved OVS integration.

text:
The provided API reference outlines the `TCULink` class (note: the source description incorrectly references `TCLink`; `TCULink` is the correct subclass optimized for `UserSwitch`). This class configures Linux veth pairs with default traffic control settings for the UserSwitch software switch.

**Context & Mitigation:** The noted TX/RX checksum offload issue is a known limitation in certain Linux kernels when using `UserSwitch`. To mitigate performance degradation or packet drops, disable offloading on the veth interfaces: `ethtool -K <veth_name> tx off rx off`. Note that `OVSLink` and `LinuxBridge` handle checksum offloading more robustly. For modern SDN emulation (Mininet 2.x), prefer `OVSLink` or `OVSKernelSwitch` for better OpenFlow 1.3+ and QoS compatibility. When testing QoS or rate-limiting, ensure `tc` is available and consider disabling offload globally if using high-throughput traffic generators like `iperf3`.

text:
| Mininet Python API Reference Manual: mininet.log Module |
|---|
| **Class/Module** | `mininet.log` (Provides logging utilities for Mininet) |
| **Description** | Enables Mininet scripts to log messages with configurable severity. Modern Mininet abstracts the internal `MininetLogger` class in favor of Python's standard `logging` module, but retains convenience functions for SDN debugging. |
| **Import** | `from mininet.log import setLogLevel, lg, info, error, warn, debug` |
| **Methods** | |
| `setLogLevel(levelname)` | Sets the global log level. Accepts strings like `'info'`, `'debug'`, `'error'`, `'warn'`, `'output'`. |
| `lg.msg(msg, *args, **kwargs)` | Logs a message. Use `lg.info()`, `lg.error()`, etc. Pass `exc_info=True` to include stack traces. |
| **Modern Usage Example** | ```python
from mininet.log import setLogLevel, lg

# Set global verbosity (affects Mininet internals)
setLogLevel('info')

# Application-level logging
lg.info("Mininet topology initialized.")
lg.debug("Switch s1 MAC: %s", s1.MAC())

try:
    net.start()
except Exception as e:
    lg.error("Topology start failed: %s", e, exc_info=True)
``` |
| **Legacy Notes** | The original `MininetLogger` class and Python 2.5 metaclass workarounds are obsolete. Mininet 2.3+ requires Python 3. Use the module-level functions directly for OpenFlow flow table debugging, OVS tunnel monitoring, or Ryu/POX controller integration.

text:
This is a raw API reference dump for the Mininet 2.x `Mininet` class. To maximize RAG utility for SDN developers, add the following context:

1. **Architecture**: Mininet 2.x leverages Linux network namespaces for isolation. Switches (`OVSKernelSwitch`) and hosts run in separate namespaces by default.
2. **OpenFlow Version**: While not explicitly stated in the text, modern Mininet defaults to OpenFlow 1.3. To explicitly set versions, pass `protocols='OpenFlow13'` to `OVSKernelSwitch` or `UserSwitch`.
3. **Typical Workflow**: 
   ```python
   from mininet.net import Mininet
   from mininet.node import OVSKernelSwitch, RemoteController
   from mininet.topo import LinearTopo
   
   topo = LinearTopo(depth=2, fanout=2)
   net = Mininet(topo=topo, switch=OVSKernelSwitch, controller=RemoteController)
   net.build()
   net.addController('c0', ip='127.0.0.1', port=6653)
   net.start()
   net.pingAll()
   net.stop()
   ```
4. **Best Practice**: Override `ipBase` to a smaller subnet (e.g., `'10.0.0.0/24'`) to avoid ARP broadcast storms in large emulations. Use `waitConnected=True` in `__init__` to prevent race conditions when starting controllers asynchronously. Note that `autoStaticArp=True` is highly recommended for SDN testing to bypass ARP learning delays and ensure immediate connectivity.
5. **QoS/Testing**: The `iperf` method defaults to TCP. For QoS or packet loss simulation, explicitly set `l4Type='UDP'` and adjust `udpBw` accordingly. Use `configLinkStatus()` to simulate link failures or bandwidth constraints dynamically during tests.

text:
# Mininet Python API Reference: MininetWithControlNet & Mininet Base Class

> **⚠️ Legacy Notice:** `MininetWithControlNet` is a deprecated class from early Mininet 2.x releases. Modern Mininet (2.3+) automatically handles control networking via loopback or Unix domain sockets for `OVSKernelSwitch`. For new SDN/SDN controller development, use the standard `Mininet` class directly.

## Core Class: `mininet.net.Mininet`
Orchestrates SDN testbed creation, topology management, and validation.

### Key Constructor Parameters (Mininet 2.3+)
- `switch=OVSKernelSwitch` (Default): Uses Open vSwitch kernel module. Supports hardware offloading and OpenFlow 1.3+.
- `controller=DefaultController`: Defaults to `NOX/POX` on port 6633. Replace with `RemoteController` for Ryu, ONOS, or OpenDaylight.
- `waitConnected=False`: Set to `True` to block until switches establish OpenFlow connections. Critical for controller synchronization.
- `ipBase='10.0.0.0/8'`: Default host IP allocation range.

### Essential Methods
- `addLink(node1, node2, cls=None, **params)`: Creates links. Use `params` for QoS/traffic control: `bw=100`, `delay='10ms'`, `loss=0.1`, `max_queue_size=1000`.
- `configLinkStatus(src, dst, status)`: Programmatically enables/disables links (`'up'`/`'down'`). Essential for fault injection and SDN failover testing.
- `waitConnected(timeout=None, delay=.5)`: Ensures control plane readiness before running tests or sending flow rules.
- `pingAll()`, `iperf(hosts, l4Type='TCP', udpBw='10M')`: Built-in connectivity and throughput validation.
- `staticArp()`: Pre-populates ARP tables to bypass control-plane broadcast delays during testing.
- `runCpuLimitTest(cpu, duration=5)`: Injects CPU constraints using `cgroups`. Useful for testing controller resource limits.

### Legacy Method: `configureRoutedControlNetwork(ip='192.168.123.1', prefixLen=16)`
Configures a routed control plane for `UserSwitch`. **Not required** for `OVSKernelSwitch` or `UserSwitch` in modern Mininet, which use in-band or Unix domain sockets by default.

## Modern Implementation Example
```python
from mininet.net import Mininet
from mininet.node import OVSKernelSwitch, RemoteController
from mininet.topo import LinearTopo

# Define topology
topo = LinearTopo(levels=3)

# Initialize Mininet with modern defaults
net = Mininet(topo=topo, switch=OVSKernelSwitch, waitConnected=True)

# Attach external SDN controller (e.g., Ryu)
net.addController('c0', controller=RemoteController, ip='127.0.0.1', port=6653)

# Build and test
net.start()
print('Control plane ready. Verifying connectivity...')
net.pingAll()
net.stop()
```

## Version & Protocol Context
- **Mininet 2.0–2.2:** Explicit control routing was occasionally needed for `UserSwitch`.
- **Mininet 2.3+:** `waitConnected` added to `__init__`. Control network abstraction improved. `OVSKernelSwitch` is standard.
- **OpenFlow:** This API is controller-agnostic. Ensure controllers support OpenFlow 1.3+ for modern QoS, metering, and group table functionality.
- **Automation:** This API is ideal for Python-based SDN test harnesses. Pair with `asyncio` or `pexpect` for controller-driven automation.

metadata.title: Mininet Python API Reference Manual: mininet.net.MininetWithControlNet Class Reference
metadata.llm_evaluation_reason: The document is a raw, fragmented API reference dump for a legacy Mininet class. While the method signatures are technically accurate for Mininet 2.x, the `MininetWithControlNet` class is explicitly noted in the source as deprecated ('Basically nobody ever used this code'). It lacks modern context, actionable examples, version clarification, and OpenFlow/controller integration details required for a production RAG database.
metadata.context_summary: API documentation for the Mininet Python class, detailing methods for network topology creation, node/link management, controller configuration, and testing utilities like ping and iperf.

code_snippets.context_before:
Control network.
| support | Create an explicit control network. Currently this is only used/usable with the user datapath. @param Notes 1. If the controller and switches are in the same (e.g. root) namespace, they can just use the loopback connection. 2. If we can get unix domain sockets to work, we can use them instead of an explicit control network. 3. Instead of routing, we could bridge or use 'in-band' control. 4. Even if we dispense with this in general, it could still be useful for people who wish to simulate a separate control network (since real networks may need one!) 5. Basically nobody ever used this code, so it has been moved into its own class. 6. Ultimately we may wish to extend this to allow us to create a control network which every node's control interface is attached to. |

text:
# CPULimitedHost (mininet.node)
Extends `mininet.node.Host` to emulate CPU bandwidth limitations using Linux cgroups. Primarily used for QoS simulation, resource isolation, and latency/jitter testing in SDN testbeds.

## Key Methods & Usage
- `setCPUFrac(f, sched=None)`: Allocates a CPU fraction `f` (0.0-1.0) to the host. Controls overall CPU share.
- `setCPUs(cores, mems=0)`: Pins the cgroup to specific physical CPU cores. Essential for deterministic latency testing.
- `cgroupSet(param, value, resource='cpu')` / `cgroupGet()`: Direct manipulation of cgroup v1 parameters (e.g., `cpu.cfs_quota_us`, `cpu.rt_runtime_us`).
- `checkRtGroupSched()`: Verifies kernel supports `CONFIG_RT_GROUP_SCHED` for real-time bandwidth control.

## Scheduling Modes
- `sched='cfs'` (Default): Uses Linux Completely Fair Scheduler. Suitable for general throughput emulation and load testing.
- `sched='rt'`: Uses Real-Time scheduler. Requires kernel compiled with `CONFIG_RT_GROUP_SCHED`. Use for strict latency/jitter/OpenFlow processing timing tests.

## Modern Mininet Example (2.3+)
```python
from mininet.topo import Topo
from mininet.node import CPULimitedHost
from mininet.net import Mininet

class CpuQoSTopo(Topo):
    def build(self):
        self.addSwitch('s1')
        # Allocate 20% CPU to h1, pin to core 1
        h1 = self.addNode('h1', cls=CPULimitedHost, cpu=0.2)
        self.addLink('s1', h1)

net = Mininet(topo=CpuQoSTopo(), host=CPULimitedHost)
net.start()
h1.cmd('iperf -s -u -b 1m &')
h1.cmd('sleep 5 && top -bn1 | grep iperf')  # Verify CPU throttling
net.stop()
```

## Engineering Notes
- CPU limits artificially introduce queueing delays, which can impact OpenFlow message processing times in OVS switches.
- For OpenFlow 1.3+ controller testing, use `setCPUs()` to isolate controller traffic from data plane load.
- Cgroup v2 is not natively supported in standard Mininet; this class relies on cgroup v1 `cpu` controller.

This is an auto-generated API reference for `mininet.topo.MinimalTopo`, a convenience class inheriting from `SingleSwitchTopo` and `Topo`. It provides a ready-made 2-host, 1-switch topology for quick SDN validation.

Key Usage Context:
- `build(self, k=2, **_opts)`: Instantiates the topology. `k` defines host count.
- `addHost/AddSwitch/AddLink`: Standard topology builders. Use `**opts` in `addHost`/`addSwitch` to pass OVS controller/IP settings (`ip`, `mac`, `inNamespace`, `switch` params).
- `addLink` QoS/Emulation: Commonly extended with `bw`, `delay`, `loss`, `max_queue_size` to simulate network conditions or test OpenFlow flow rules.
- `convertTo(cls)`: Useful for debugging or exporting the topology to NetworkX graphs for analysis.

Example Extension:
```python
from mininet.topo import Topo
class CustomTopo(Topo):
    def build(self):
        h1 = self.addHost('h1')
        s1 = self.addSwitch('s1')
        h2 = self.addHost('h2')
        self.addLink(h1, s1, bw=10, delay='5ms')
        self.addLink(h2, s1, bw=10, delay='5ms')
```
Note: This documentation is purely methodological. For runtime behavior, refer to `mn` controller instantiation and OpenFlow 1.3+ switch capabilities.

RAG Tip: Pair this with `mininet.net.Mininet` documentation to understand how topology objects are instantiated and connected to SDN controllers (Ryu, OVSDB, etc.).

The `mininet.topo.MultiGraph` class is a lightweight, dependency-free graph implementation used internally by Mininet to represent network topologies. It replaces `networkx.MultiGraph` to improve performance and reduce runtime dependencies. In modern Mininet (2.x+), this class serves as the foundation for defining custom topologies that integrate with OVS bridges and OpenFlow controllers.

**Key Usage Context for SDN/QoS:**
- Use `add_node()` to define switches, hosts, and controllers.
- Use `add_edge()` to define links. Crucially, pass QoS/link attributes via `**attrs`, e.g., `bw=1000, delay='5ms', loss=0.0`. Mininet translates these into OVS queue/pacing configurations during the `build()` phase, enabling OpenFlow-based traffic shaping.
- The `convertTo()` method allows exporting the topology to NetworkX for simulation analysis or visualization.

**Example Integration:**
```python
from mininet.topo import Topo
class QoSTopo(Topo):
    def build(self):
        s1 = self.addSwitch('s1')
        h1 = self.addHost('h1')
        h2 = self.addHost('h2')
        # Link attributes directly influence OVS/OpenFlow QoS rules
        self.add_edge(h1, s1, bw=100, delay='10ms', loss=0.1)
        self.add_edge(h2, s1, bw=500, delay='2ms')
```

**Engineer Notes:**
- This class operates independently of OpenFlow versions. Version negotiation (1.0 vs 1.3+) occurs during controller handshake and OVS bridge configuration.
- For Ryu, POX, or ONOS integration, topology definitions built with this class are passed to `Mininet(topos={'custom': QoSTopo})` before controller connection.
- Avoid mixing `networkx` directly in topology definitions; Mininet's `MultiGraph` is optimized for large-scale emulation and avoids graph traversal overhead.

Single switch connected to k hosts, with reversed ports.
The lowest-numbered host is connected to the highest-numbered port. Useful to verify that Mininet properly handles custom port numberings.

## Context & SDN Relevance
This built-in topology is primarily used for **SDN debugging and controller validation**. Reversed port numbering ensures that:
1. The SDN controller correctly maps logical host IDs to physical/virtual switch ports.
2. OpenFlow flow rules and port statistics are applied accurately regardless of interface order.
3. OVS `ofport` assignments align with Mininet's internal graph representation.

### Example Instantiation
```python
from mininet.topo import SingleSwitchReversedTopo
from mininet.net import Mininet

topo = SingleSwitchReversedTopo(k=4)
net = Mininet(topo=topo)
net.start()
net.pingAll()
net.stop()
```

### Key Notes for Network Automation
- Validates controller port discovery and OVS port mapping consistency.
- Works seamlessly with any OpenFlow 1.0+ controller (Ryu, ONOS, ODL).
- Useful for testing automation scripts that iterate over switch ports or apply per-port QoS/traffic shaping policies.

[Original API Reference Text]

### Context & Usage Guide
The `mininet.topo.Topo` class is the foundational Python API for defining custom network topologies in Mininet. To implement a topology, you must subclass `Topo` and override the `build()` method. Inside `build()`, use `addHost()`, `addSwitch()`, and `addLink()` to programmatically construct the network graph. Mininet automatically translates these Python objects into OpenFlow switch configurations and underlying OVS bridge setups.

**Example Usage:**
```python
from mininet.topo import Topo

class MyCustomTopo(Topo):
    def build(self):
        h1 = self.addHost('h1')
        s1 = self.addSwitch('s1')
        self.addLink(h1, s1)

if __name__ == '__main__':
    topo = MyCustomTopo()
    net = Mininet(topo=topo)
    net.start()
    net.pingAll()
    net.stop()
```

**Key Notes:**
- `hopts`, `sopts`, `lopts` are dictionaries passed to `addHost`, `addSwitch`, and `addLink` respectively, allowing configuration of IP addresses, MAC addresses, bandwidth, delay, and QoS parameters.
- `convertTo()` exports the topology to a NetworkX MultiGraph for analysis.
- This API is version-stable in Mininet 2.x+.
- OpenFlow version defaults to 1.3 in Mininet 2.x (configurable via `--switch=ovsk --of=1.0` if legacy compatibility is needed). OVS is the default switch implementation.

**Focus Areas Coverage:**
- SDN/Mininet: Core API covered.
- OpenFlow/OVS: Implicit backend, explicitly noted in enrichment.
- QoS: Supported via `lopts` (bw, delay, max_queue_size), not detailed in original.
- Cisco/Ryu: Not covered; requires external controller integration documentation.

text:
| Method | Description | Context/Example |
|---|---|---|
| `__init__(self, *args, **params)` | Initialize topology object. | Called when instantiating `TreeTopo(depth=2, fanout=2)`. |
| `build(self, depth=1, fanout=2)` | Override to construct the tree. | Defines switch/host layout based on depth/fanout parameters. |
| `addTree(self, depth, fanout)` | Add subtree starting at node n. | Legacy helper; rarely invoked directly in modern scripts. |
| `addNode/Host/Switch(self, name, **opts)` | Add nodes to topology graph. | Use `addHost('h1')`, `addSwitch('s1')`. |
| `addLink(self, node1, node2, ...)` | Connect two nodes. | Supports `bw`, `delay`, `loss` kwargs for QoS simulation. |
| `nodes()/switches()/hosts()` | Return node lists. | Useful for CLI iteration or topology validation. |
| `iterLinks()/links()` | Return link data. | `withInfo=True` returns metadata dict. |
| `convertTo(cls)` | Convert to NetworkX graph. | Enables analysis via networkx library. |

**Usage Context:**
`TreeTopo` is a convenience class for generating hierarchical tree topologies. While still functional in Mininet 2.x, modern SDN testing often favors custom `Topo` subclasses for precise control or Mininet's built-in mnemonic topologies (`mn(topo=TreeTopo(depth=2, fanout=3))`). For OpenFlow 1.3+ or OVS integration, ensure links are configured with appropriate controller connections and switch protocols (`switchProtocols=['OpenFlow13']`). QoS parameters on `addLink` directly map to OVS tc queues and OpenFlow meter/table actions when using Ryu or POX controllers.

text: This walkthrough demonstrates core Mininet CLI and Python API usage. For modern SDN workflows, update Wireshark filters to `openflow_13` and use controller port `6653` (verify defaults with `mn -h`). When debugging flows, prefer the built-in CLI command `dpctl dump-flows` over manual `ovs-ofctl` calls, as it automatically resolves the correct controller socket without hardcoding IPs/ports. For QoS validation, `--link tc` leverages Linux netem for delay/packet loss, while `--link ovs` utilizes OVS queue/policer configurations. To integrate with contemporary open-source controllers like Ryu or ONOS, replace the default reference controller using `--controller remote,ip=<IP>,port=6653`. Always run `sudo mn -c` post-session to flush network namespaces, OVS bridges, and iptables rules, ensuring a clean baseline for automation scripts or CI/CD pipeline testing.

metadata.title: Mininet Walkthrough - Mininet
metadata.llm_evaluation_reason: The document is a functional Mininet 2.x tutorial but contains outdated version-specific details that could mislead modern SDN practitioners. It references the OpenFlow 1.0 Wireshark dissector ('openflow_1') and a non-standard controller port (6654), whereas current Mininet versions default to OpenFlow 1.3 and port 6653. It also references the archived '2.0.0d4' tag. Core concepts (network namespaces, CLI, OVS integration, tc link parameters) are accurate, but it lacks context on contemporary controller ecosystems (Ryu, ONOS) and updated debugging/automation workflows.

message to go to the controller. The controller then sends a packet_out
message to flood the broadcast packet to other ports on the switch (in this example, the only other data port). The second host sees the ARP request and sends a reply. This reply goes to the controller, which sends it to the first host and pushes down a flow entry.
Now the first host knows the MAC address of the second, and can send its ping via an ICMP Echo Request. This request, along with its corresponding reply from the second host, both go the controller and result in a flow entry pushed down (along with the actual packets getting sent out).

text:
Introduction
The OpenDaylight (ODL) project is an open-source, modular SDN controller platform that provides centralized, programmatic control and network device monitoring using open protocols.

What’s different about OpenDaylight
Major distinctions of OpenDaylight’s SDN compared to other SDN options are the following:
- **Modular Architecture (Karaf/OSGi)**: ODL uses an OSGi-based runtime managed by Apache Karaf. While the documentation refers to these as “microservices,” they are technically OSGi bundles packaged as Karaf features. Examples include:
  - `openflowplugin`: Provides OpenFlow protocol connectivity and switch management.
  - `aaa`: Platform service for Authentication, Authorization, and Accounting.
  - `netvirt`: Network virtualization service for OpenStack VM connectivity.
- **Broad Protocol Support**: OpenFlow, BGP, PCEP, LISP, NETCONF, OVSDB, SNMP, P4, and more. This allows ODL to manage both legacy and modern network devices.
- **Model-Driven Service Abstraction Layer (MD-SAL)**: The core framework for building SDN applications. YANG models are central to ODL and enable:
  - Defining datastore schemata (tree-based structures).
  - Auto-generating RESTCONF/NETCONF APIs and Java interfaces/DTOs.
  - Decoupling northbound applications from southbound protocol implementations.

*Note for modern deployments*: OpenDaylight has evolved beyond early release trains (e.g., Helium, Lithium). Current versions emphasize YANG-driven automation, RESTCONF for configuration, and containerized/Kubernetes-ready distributions for cloud-native SDN environments. OpenFlow 1.3+ is the standard for modern switch interactions, though backward compatibility is maintained.

metadata.title: Introduction — OpenDaylight Documentation Chromium documentation
metadata.llm_evaluation_reason: Technically accurate and sourced from official documentation, but uses legacy terminology and lacks the architectural depth expected for a network engineering RAG database. The term 'microservices' is a simplification; OpenDaylight actually relies on OSGi bundles managed by Apache Karaf (referred to as 'features' or 'modules'). It also omits critical context on how MD-SAL and YANG models integrate with modern automation workflows (RESTCONF/NETCONF) and how the platform has evolved beyond early codename-based release trains. Enriching it with precise architecture details and modern deployment context significantly improves its utility for technical queries.
metadata.context_summary: This document provides a theoretical introduction to the OpenDaylight software-defined networking (SDN) controller, outlining its foundational architecture and core networking protocols. It establishes the conceptual framework for SDN implementation by covering key technologies such as OpenFlow, P4, BGP, and PCEP.

Major distinctions of OpenDaylight’s SDN compared to other SDN options are the following:

Major distinctions of OpenDaylight’s SDN compared to other SDN options are the following:
A microservices architecture, in which a “microservice” is a particular protocol or service that a user wants to enable within their installation of the OpenDaylight controller, for example:
A plugin that provides connectivity to devices via the OpenFlow protocols (

text:
This document provides a strong historical overview of OpenDaylight (ODL) security architecture but targets pre-2019 releases. For modern RAG/database integration and current deployments, apply the following updates:

- **RESTCONF & Web Server**: Replace legacy `odl-restconf-nb` and `odl-restconf11` references with the unified `odl-restconf` feature. Modern ODL defaults to Netty for RESTCONF; Jetty is often disabled or handled via `odl-jetty` if required. Configuration has shifted from raw `org.ops4j.pax.web.cfg` to Karaf config admin wrappers and dedicated security features.
- **Clustering Security**: The statement that cluster messages are unencrypted is outdated. Modern MD-SAL clustering (Aluminium+) supports TLS authentication and encryption. While management network segmentation remains best practice, verify `odl-clustering` TLS properties in your target release.
- **AAA Integration**: Local `admin/admin` credentials are deprecated for production. Modern ODL strongly recommends `odl-shiro` or `odl-aaa-apache` for LDAP/Kerberos/Active Directory integration. RESTCONF APIs are protected by default when `odl-restconf` + `odl-shiro` are installed.
- **Southbound/OpenFlow**: The OpenFlow plugin now defaults to OpenFlow 1.3/1.5 with TLS support. Ensure `openflowplugin` and `odl-mdsal-apidocs` are present. For Mininet/OVS testing, bind controller TLS certs to OVS manager interfaces (`ovs-vsctl set-manager ptcp:6653,...`).

Keep this document for conceptual OSGi/Karaf isolation and attack-surface analysis, but cross-reference official release notes for your specific ODL release (e.g., Carbon, D, E, F, G, H).

code_snippets.code: Deploying OpenDaylight on a separate, private management network does not eliminate threats, but only mitigates them. By construction, some messages must flow from the data network to the management network, e.g., OpenFlow packet_in messages, and these create an attack surface even if it is a small one.
code_snippets.context_before:
OpenDaylight should be deployed in a private network that cannot be accessed from the internet.
Separate the data network (that connects devices using the network) from the management network (that connects the network devices to OpenDaylight).
Note
code_snippets.context_after:
Implement an authentication policy for devices that connect to both the data and management network. These are the devices which bridge, likely untrusted, traffic from the data network to the management network.
Securing OSGi bundles¶
OSGi is a Java-specific framework that improves the way that Java classes interact within a single JVM. It provides an enhanced version of the java.lang.SecurityManager (ConditionalPermissionAdmin) in terms of security.
code_snippets.full_context:
OpenDaylight should be deployed in a private network that cannot be accessed from the internet.
Separate the data network (that connects devices using the network) from the management network (that connects the network devices to OpenDaylight).
Note

Deploying OpenDaylight on a separate, private management network does not eliminate threats, but only mitigates them. By construction, some messages must flow from the data network to the management network, e.g., OpenFlow packet_in messages, and these create an attack surface even if it is a small one.

Implement an authentication policy for devices that connect to both the data and management network. These are the devices which bridge, likely untrusted, traffic from the data network to the management network.
Securing OSGi bundles¶
OSGi is a Java-specific framework that improves the way that Java classes interact within a single JVM. It provides an enhanced version of the java.lang.SecurityManager (ConditionalPermissionAdmin) in terms of security.

text: NOTE: This is a meta-documentation guide for OpenDaylight (ODL) contributors, not a technical network engineering reference. The content reflects ODL's architecture circa 2016 (Karaf-based controller, legacy Gerrit workflow). For modern SDN contexts: OpenDaylight (now Eclipse OpenDaylight) primarily uses RESTCONF and YANG for southbound/northbound interfaces, with OpenFlow 1.3+ support for legacy flow-based control. Open vSwitch (OVS) remains the de facto standard virtual switch, typically managed via OVSDB or OpenFlow. Mininet and Ryu are complementary tools for SDN emulation and controller development, respectively. For current technical documentation on these components, refer to the Eclipse OpenDaylight documentation, OVS technical manuals, and OpenFlow 1.3+ specification (ONF).

metadata.title: Documentation Guide — OpenDaylight Documentation Chromium documentation
metadata.llm_evaluation_reason: This document is a documentation contribution style guide for OpenDaylight, not a technical network engineering reference. It focuses on reStructuredText/Sphinx formatting, git workflows, and editorial guidelines. It does not cover the specified focus areas (SDN, Mininet, Ryu, Cisco, QoS, OpenFlow, OVS, automation) technically. The content references a 2016 date and legacy Gerrit URLs, indicating it is outdated. While it mentions OpenFlow and OVS, it only references them as project/feature names, not technical implementations.

code_snippets.context_before:
In general, when reviewing content, the documentation team ensures that it is comprehensible but tries not to be overly pedantic. Along those lines, while it is preferred that the following formatting preferences are followed, they are generally not an exclusive reason to give a “-1” reply to a patch in Gerrit:
No trailing whitespace
Line wrapping at something reasonable, that is, 72–100 characters
code_snippets.context_after:
Since there is a controller project within OpenDaylight, using other terms is hard.
In per-project user documentation, you should never say git clone, but should assume people have downloaded and installed the controller per the getting started guide and start with
feature:install <something>

Avoid statements which are true about part of OpenDaylight, but not generally true.
For example: “OpenDaylight is a NETCONF controller.” It is, but that is not all it is.
In general, developer documentation should target external developers to your project so should talk about what APIs you have and how they could use them. It should not document how to contribute to your project.

text:
Mininet is a lightweight network emulator that creates realistic virtual networks using Linux namespaces and virtual Ethernet (veth) pairs on a single kernel. It is primarily used to prototype, test, and visualize Software-Defined Networking (SDN) architectures and OpenFlow-compliant controllers (e.g., Ryu, ONOS, Floodlight) without physical hardware.

Installation Methods:
- Official VM Image: Pre-configured Ubuntu-based environment (recommended for beginners).
- Package Manager: `sudo apt install mininet` (Debian/Ubuntu) or `pip install mininet`.
- From Source: Clone the GitHub repository and run `sudo make install`.
- Upgrade: Use `git pull` followed by `sudo make install` or update via package manager.

Mininet CLI:
The CLI is topology-aware and supports standard Linux commands. Key built-in commands include:
- `nodes`: Lists all hosts, switches, and controllers.
- `net`: Displays network topology and interface mappings.
- `dump`: Outputs detailed configuration and runtime status of all nodes.
- Additional useful commands: `pingall`, `iperf`, `xterm <node>`, `stop`, `exit`.

GUI & Visualization:
Mininet is natively CLI-driven and does not include a built-in GUI. However, it can be integrated with external visualization tools (e.g., Mininet-Web, Wireshark) or controlled programmatically via Python scripts for topology creation and monitoring.

Python API:
Mininet is designed for Python-based automation and controller integration. Users can define topologies, configure traffic shaping (e.g., `TCLink`), and set CPU limits (e.g., `CPULimitedHost`) directly in Python.

Features & Advantages:
- Lightweight, hardware-agnostic testbed for SDN/OpenFlow development.
- Topology-aware CLI for rapid network testing and debugging.
- Native Python API for programmatic control and integration with SDN controllers.
- Supports traffic shaping and resource constraints for realistic performance testing.
- Cost-effective alternative to physical testbeds; can bridge to real network devices using remote controller flags.
- Note: Mininet runs on a single host and does not inherently provide high availability (HA). For multi-node SDN simulations, consider tools like GNS3, EVE-NG, or Docker-based orchestrators.

Version Context:
- Mininet 2.x (current stable: 2.3.0+) uses Open vSwitch (OVS) as the default switch implementation.
- OpenFlow protocol: Defaults to 1.0 in legacy setups, but OpenFlow 1.3 is recommended for modern SDN features (group tables, metering, IPv6).

metadata.llm_evaluation_reason: The document is a high-level, blog-style overview that lacks technical depth and contains misleading claims (e.g., 'high availability', contradictory GUI statements). It omits critical SDN concepts like Linux namespaces, Open vSwitch (OVS) integration, and proper Python API usage. Version information is entirely missing. While the CLI commands are correct, the document needs enrichment to meet professional RAG database standards.
metadata.context_summary: This document provides a theoretical overview of the Mininet emulator, explaining how it utilizes the OpenFlow protocol to facilitate network emulation and virtualization within Software-Defined Networks (SDN). It further details how Mininet creates realistic virtual network topologies to support the testing and development of SDN applications.
